  • Identify and interpret urinary casts and crystals
  • Apply quantification methods (ACR, PCR, 24h)
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP clinical vignettes

2. Urinalysis — The "Renal Biopsy of the Poor"

Dipstick Analysis

ParameterNormalAbnormal Significance
pH4.5–8.0Low: acidosis, UTI (urea-splitters); High: alkalosis, urea-splitters
Specific Gravity1.005–1.030Low: DI, CKD; High: dehydration, glycosuria, proteinuria
ProteinNegativeProteinuria (glomerular > tubular > overflow); false +ve: alkaline, bloody
GlucoseNegativeGlycosuria (DM, renal glycosuria, pregnancy, SGLT2i)
KetonesNegativeKetonuria (DKA, starvation, low carb diet)
BloodNegativeHaematuria (RBC >3/HPF); false +ve: myoglobin, Hb
LeucocytesNegativeLeucocyturia (UTI, sterile pyuria = TB, stones, interstitial nephritis)
NitritesNegativeNitrituria (Gram-negative UTI, sensitivity ~50%)
BilirubinNegativeLiver disease, haemolysis
UrobilinogenNormalIncreased: haemolysis, haemolytic anaemia
[!key]
Proteinuria: Dipstick detects albumin; ACR = gold standard for quantification.
Haematuria: Dipstick +ve + microscopy RBC >3/HPF = true haematuria.

3. Urine Microscopy

ElementNormalAbnormal Significance
RBC0–2/HPF>3/HPF = haematuria; dysmorphic = glomerular; isomorphic = non-glomerular
WBC0–5/HPF>5/HPF = pyuria; sterile pyuria = TB, interstitial nephritis, stones, carcinoma
Epithelial cellsFewSquamous = contamination; Transitional = bladder/TCC; Renal tubular = ATN, interstitial nephritis
CastsHyaline fewRBC cast = GN; WBC cast = pyelo/interstitial nephritis; Granular/muddy brown = ATN; Waxy/broad = advanced CKD; Fatty = nephrotic syndrome
CrystalsFew uratesCystine (hexagonal = cystinuria), Uric acid (needles/rosettes), Calcium oxalate (envelopes), Struvite (coffin-lid = struvite/Proteus)
MicroorganismsNoneBacteria = UTI; Yeast = Candida; Parasites (Schistosoma)

4. Proteinuria Quantification

TestMethodNormalUse
ACRAlbumin:creatinine ratio (spot)<3 mg/mmolGold standard; corrects for concentration
PCRProtein:creatinine ratio (spot)<15 mg/mmolIncludes non-albumin; overestimates if tubular protein
24h urine protein24h collection<150 mg/dayGold standard but cumbersome
DipstickSemi-quantitativeNegativeScreening only
ACR CategoryRange (mg/mmol)Term
A1<3Normal
A23–30Microalbuminuria
A3>30Macroalbuminuria

5. Haematuria

TypeDefinitionCause
Microscopic>3 RBC/HPF on microscopyDipstick +ve → confirm microscopy
MacroscopicVisible blood in urineStone, tumour, trauma, severe GN
TypeRBC MorphologyCause
GlomerularDysmorphic (>50% dysmorphic), RBC castsGN, vasculitis, IgA nephropathy
Non-glomerularIsomorphic (uniform), no castsStones, tumours, UTI, trauma, exercise
[!key]
Dysmorphic RBCs + RBC casts = Glomerular haematuria
Isomorphic RBCs + no casts = Non-glomerular (urological)

6. Urinary Casts

Cast TypeAppearanceSignificance
HyalineClear, low refractiveNormal (dehydration, fever, exercise)
RBCRed-brown, retain RBC shapeGlomerulonephritis (pathognomonic)
WBC / PusWhite, granularPyelonephritis, acute interstitial nephritis
GranularBrown, granularATN, severe glomerular disease
Muddy brownDark, granularATN (classic)
Waxy / BroadHigh refractive, broadAdvanced CKD, chronic tubular damage
Fatty / Oval fat bodiesMaltese crossesNephrotic syndrome (lipiduria)

7. Urinary Crystals

CrystalShapeClinical Association
CystineHexagonal (flat, hexagonal plates)Cystinuria (autosomal recessive)
Uric acidNeedles, rosettes, rhombicGout, tumour lysis, acidic urine
Calcium oxalateEnvelope-shaped (bipyramidal), dumbbellsHyperoxaluria, ethylene glycol, stones
Calcium phosphateAmorphous, stellateAlkaline urine, hyperparathyroidism
Struvite (Triple phosphate)Coffin-lid / coffin-lid shaped (prismatic)Struvite stones (Proteus, Klebsiella - urea-splitting organisms)
Ammonium urateThorny apples, cactus-likeLiver disease, laxative abuse
Leucine / TyrosineYellow-brown, fine/silkySevere liver disease, tyrosinaemia
CholesterolRectangular, notchedNephrotic syndrome (lipiduria)
[!key]
Cystine = Hexagonal; Uric acid = Needles/rosettes; CaOx = Envelopes; Struvite = Coffin-lid;
Cholesterol = Rectangular/notched (nephrotic); Tyrosine/Leucine = liver failure.

8. Proteinuria Quantification

TestMethodNormalBest For
ACRAlbumin:creatinine ratio (spot)<3 mg/mmolGold standard
PCRProtein:creatinine ratio<15 mg/mmolTotal protein (incl. non-albumin)
24h urine protein24h collection<150 mg/dayGold standard (cumbersome)
DipstickSemi-quantitativeNegativeScreening only
ACR CategoryRange (mg/mmol)TermSignificance
A1<3Normal—
A23–30MicroalbuminuriaEarly diabetic nephropathy, hypertensive nephropathy
A3>30MacroalbuminuriaOvert proteinuria; >220 = nephrotic range
PCR vs ACR: PCR measures total protein; ACR specific for albumin. PCR > ACR if tubular proteinuria (Fanconi, myeloma).

9. Haematuria Investigation

StepAction
1Confirm: Repeat dipstick + microscopy (>3 RBC/HPF)
2Morphology: Dysmorphic (>50%) = glomerular; Isomorphic = non-glomerular
3Proteinuria: ACR >30 + haematuria = glomerular disease
4Renal function: eGFR, BP
5Imaging: US KUB (stone, tumour, cyst); CT urogram (haematuria >40yr)
6Cystoscopy: If >40yr or risk factors (smoker, analgesic abuse)
7Serology: ASOT (post-strep), ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, C3/C4, HIV, Hep B/C

10. Urine Culture & Sensitivity

IndicationMethod
Symptomatic UTIMidstream urine (MSU)
Catheter specimenCatheter specimen (CSU)
Suprapubic aspirateInfants, contaminated samples
Quantitative culture>10⁵ CFU/mL = significant bacteriuria
OrganismCommon Context
E. coli70–80% community UTI
KlebsiellaHospital, catheter, stones
ProteusStones (urease → struvite), catheter
EnterococcusHospital, catheter, instrumentation
PseudomonasHospital, catheter, immunocompromised
Staph saprophyticusYoung women, community UTI

11. Special Urine Tests

TestIndicationMethod
Bence Jones ProteinMyeloma, amyloidosisUrine immunofixation, heating (precipitates at 50–60°C)
MyoglobinRhabdomyolysisDipstick +ve for blood, no RBCs on microscopy
HaemoglobinIntravascular haemolysisDipstick +ve blood, no RBCs on microscopy
PorphyrinsPorphyriaFluorescence, chromatography
CatecholaminesPheochromocytoma24h urine VMA, metanephrines
CortisolCushing's24h urinary free cortisol
VMA/HVANeuroblastoma24h urine VMA, HVA
5-HIAACarcinoid syndrome24h urine 5-HIAA

12. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Urinalysis = renal biopsy of the poor; first test in renal disease
- Proteinuria: ACR >30 = proteinuria; ACR >300 = nephrotic range
- Haematuria: >3 RBC/HPF + dysmorphic = glomerular; isomorphic = non-glomerular
- Casts: RBC = GN; WBC = pyelo/interstitial; granular = ATN; waxy = advanced CKD; fatty = nephrotic
- Crystals: Cystine (hex), urate (needles), Ca oxalate (envelopes), struvite (coffin-lid)
- ACR: <3 (A1), 3–30 (A2), >30 (A3) — nephrotic if >220 mg/mmol
- Casts: RBC = GN; WBC = pyelo/interstitial; granular = ATN; waxy = CKD
- Crystals: Cystine (hex), urate (needles), Ca oxalate (envelopes), struvite (coffin-lid)
- ACR categories: A1 <3, A2 3–30, A3 >30 mg/mmol
- Glomerular haematuria: dysmorphic RBCs + RBC casts
- Non-glomerular: isomorphic RBCs, no casts

13. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Dipstick protein = all proteinDipstick = albumin only; misses tubular proteins (Bence Jones)
Microalbuminuria = diabetic onlyAlso hypertensive, early CKD, heart failure
Red urine = haematuriaMyoglobin, haemoglobin, beetroot, rifampicin, porphyria
Proteinuria = always glomerularTubular (Fanconi, drugs), overflow (myeloma), postural
Casts = only in GNWBC = pyelo/interstitial; granular = ATN; waxy = CKD
All crystals = stonesCrystalluria ≠ stones (can be normal in concentrated urine)
ACR = PCRACR = albumin only; PCR = total protein (incl. tubular)
Microalbuminuria = only diabetesAlso hypertension, early CKD, heart failure

14. Mnemonics

  • Cast types: RBC = GN; WBC = pyelo/interstitial; Granular = ATN; Waxy = CKD; Fatty = nephrotic = RWGWF
  • Crystals: Cystine = Hexagonal; Uric acid = Needles; Ca Oxalate = Envelopes; Triple Phosphate = Coffin-lid = HUNECTPC
  • Proteinuria: A1 <3, A2 3–30, A3 >30 = A1-A2-A3
  • Casts: RBC = GN; WBC = pyelo; Granular = ATN; Waxy = CKD = RWGW
  • Crystals: Cystine Hex; Uric Needles; CaOx = Envelope; Struvite = Coffin = CHUCEC

15. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. ACR categories (A1, A2, A3)
  2. Cast types and significance (RBC, WBC, granular, waxy, fatty)
  3. Crystal shapes (cystine, urate, calcium oxalate, struvite)
  4. Haematuria: dysmorphic vs isomorphic
  5. Cast types: RBC, WBC, granular, waxy, fatty
  6. Crystal shapes: cystine, urate, Ca oxalate, struvite
  7. ACR categories
  8. Bence Jones protein detection
  9. Cast significance table

16. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

17. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Dipstick + microscopy, ACR categories, cast types, crystal shapes, haematuria differentiation, proteinuria quantification
Should Know 🟡Bence Jones protein, special urine tests, 24h urine collection, urine culture interpretation
Nice to Know 🟢Novel biomarkers (NGAL, KIM-1), proteomics, urinary metabolomics

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Gold standard for quantifying proteinuria:

A. Dipstick protein

B. Albumin:Creatinine Ratio (ACR)

C. 24h urinary protein

D. Protein:Creatinine Ratio (PCR)

E. Dipstick protein

  1. Microalbuminuria ACR range:

A. <3 mg/mmol

B. 3–30 mg/mmol

C. 30–300

D. >300

E. 30–3000

  1. RBC casts in urine indicate:

A. Pyelonephritis

B. Glomerulonephritis

C. ATN

D. Interstitial nephritis

E. Tumour

  1. Waxy casts indicate:

A. Acute tubular necrosis

B. Nephrotic syndrome

C. Advanced chronic kidney disease

D. Acute interstitial nephritis

E. Minimal change disease

  1. Cystine crystals are:

A. Needle-shaped

B. Hexagonal

C. Envelope-shaped

D. Coffin-lid

E. Amorphous

  1. Cystine crystals are:

A. Needle-shaped

B. Hexagonal

C. Envelope-shaped

C. Coffin-lid

E. Amorphous

  1. Best imaging for suspected renal colic:

A. IVU

B. Non-contrast CT KUB

C. Ultrasound

D. IVP

E. MRI

  1. Gold standard for measuring GFR:

A. Serum creatinine

B. eGFR (CKD-EPI)

C. Inulin clearance (DTPA/MAG3 clinical)

D. Cockcroft-Gault

E. Cystatin C

  1. Cystine crystals are:

A. Needle-shaped

B. Hexagonal

C. Envelope-shaped

C. Coffin-lid

E. Amorphous

  1. Microalbuminuria ACR range:

A. <3 mg/mmol

B. 3–30 mg/mmol

C. 30–300

D. >300

E. 30–3000

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 65-year-old diabetic, HbA1c 8.2%, urine ACR 45 mg/mmol. Classification:

A. Normal

B. Microalbuminuria (A2)

C. Macroalbuminuria (A3)

D. Normal

E. Nephrotic range

  1. Child with macroscopic haematuria after URTI. Urine: RBC casts+, proteinuria. Diagnosis:

A. IgA nephropathy

B. Post-streptococcal GN

C. Thin basement membrane

D. Alport syndrome

C. Vasculitis

  1. Adult with macroscopic haematuria, smoker, 55 years. Best initial imaging:

A. Ultrasound

B. CT urogram

C. CT KUB

D. IVU

E. MRI

  1. Patient with nephrotic syndrome. Urine microscopy: oval fat bodies, fatty casts. Mechanism:

A. Lipiduria from glomerular leak

B. Hyperlipidaemia + glomerular leak → lipiduria

C. Tubular secretion

D. Dietary fat malabsorption

E. Chyluria

  1. Patient with macroscopic haematuria, smoker, 60 years. CT urogram: bladder mass. Next step:

A. Radical cystectomy

B. TURBT (Transurethral Resection of Bladder Tumour)

C. Radical cystectomy

D. BCG instillation

E. Radiotherapy

  1. Patient with recurrent UTIs, ultrasound shows normal kidneys. Next investigation:

A. CT KUB

B. Cystoscopy

C. IVU

D. CT urogram

E. MCU

  1. Patient with haematuria, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, proteinuria ACR 150. Most likely:

A. Bladder cancer

B. Glomerulonephritis

C. Stone disease

D. UTI

E. Prostatitis

  1. Cystine crystals in urine. Inheritance:

A. Autosomal dominant

B. Autosomal recessive

C. X-linked

C. Mitochondrial

E. Sporadic

  1. Patient with recurrent calcium oxalate stones. Urine crystal: envelope-shaped. Prevention:

A. Low calcium diet

B. High fluid intake, low oxalate, normal calcium, citrate supplementation

C. High calcium diet

C. Thiazide only

E. Allopurinol only

20. Flashcards

  • Q: ACR normal?

A: <3 mg/mmol

  • Q: Microalbuminuria range?

A: 3–30 mg/mmol

  • Q: Macroalbuminuria?

A: >30 mg/mmol

  • Q: RBC casts = ?

A: Glomerulonephritis

  • Q: WBC casts = ?

A: Pyelonephritis / interstitial nephritis

  • Q: Granular casts = ?

A: ATN

  • Q: Waxy casts = ?

A: Advanced CKD

  • Q: Fatty casts = ?

A: Nephrotic syndrome

  • Q: Cystine crystals?

A: Hexagonal

  • Q: Uric acid crystals?

A: Needles/rosettes

  • Q: Ca oxalate crystals?

A: Envelope-shaped

  • Q: Struvite crystals?

A: Coffin-lid / coffin-lid shaped

  • Q: Calcium phosphate?

A. Amorphous, stellate

  • Q: Bence Jones protein?

A. Free light chains, myeloma

  • Q: Proteinuria gold standard?

A: ACR (albumin:creatinine ratio)

▸ 18.1.6 Blood Tests Renal Disease
Topic: Blood Tests in Renal Disease — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Investigation of Renal and Urinary Tract Disease]], [[Functional Anatomy and Physiology of the Kidney and Urinary Tract]], [[Glomerular Filtration Rate]], [[Urine Investigations]], [[Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)]], [[Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Core renal bloods: Creatinine, eGFR, Urea, Electrolytes (Na, K, HCO3, Cl, Anion Gap), Phosphate, Calcium (corrected), Magnesium, Uric Acid, Albumin, Hb, PTH, Vit D, HbA1c, Lipids. Key: Creatinine/eGFR for CKD staging; K+ & HCO3- for AKI/CKD; Phos/Ca/PTH for CKD-MBD; Albumin/Hb for malnutrition/anaemia; PTH/Vit D for CKD-MBD.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Interpret core renal blood tests (creatinine, eGFR, urea, electrolytes)
  • Apply acid-base interpretation (anion gap, metabolic acidosis/alkalosis)
  • Assess mineral bone parameters (Ca, PO4, PTH, Vit D) in CKD-MBD
  • Interpret haematology and nutritional markers in renal disease
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP clinical scenarios

2. Renal Function Tests

Creatinine & eGFR

TestNormal RangeInterpretation
Serum Creatinine60–110 μmol/L (M), 45–90 (F)↑ = ↓ GFR; muscle mass dependent
eGFR (CKD-EPI)>90 mL/min/1.73m²CKD staging: G1 (>90), G2 (60-89), G3a (45-59), G3b (30-44), G4 (15-29), G5 (<15)
Cystatin C0.5–1.0 mg/LLess muscle-mass dependent; better at low GFR
Urea2.5–7.5 mmol/L↑ in CKD, dehydration, high protein, GI bleed, catabolism
BUN:Creatinine ratio10–20:1>20: pre-renal; <10: liver disease, malnutrition
[!key]
Creatinine = muscle-mass dependent; eGFR (CKD-EPI) = standard for staging; Cystatin C = better in extremes of muscle mass.

3. Electrolytes & Acid-Base

Sodium & Water Balance

TestNormalRenal Significance
Na+135–145 mmol/LHyponatraemia: SIADH, diuretics, CKD, heart failure, cirrhosis; Hypernatraemia: dehydration, diabetes insipidus
Osmolality285–295 mOsm/kg↑ in dehydration, hyperglycaemia; ↓ in SIADH, overhydration

Potassium

TestNormalRenal Significance
K+3.5–5.0 mmol/LHyperkalaemia: CKD, AKI, ACEi/ARB, K-sparing diuretics, acidosis; Hypokalaemia: diuretics, RTA, diarrhoea, insulin, alkalosis
Management>6.5 or ECG changes: Ca gluconate, insulin+dextrose, salbutamol, kayexalate, dialysis

Bicarbonate & Acid-Base

TestNormalInterpretation
HCO3-22–29 mmol/L↓ = metabolic acidosis; ↑ = metabolic alkalosis
Anion Gap8–12 mmol/LHAGMA: DKA, AKI, CKD, lactate, toxins (MUDPILES); NAGMA: diarrhoea, RTA, pancreatic fistula
Venous pH7.35–7.45<7.35 = acidaemia; >7.45 = alkalaemia
pCO24.5–6.0 kPaRespiratory compensation (Winter's formula: pCO2 = 1.5 × HCO3 + 8 ± 2)
[!key]
Metabolic acidosis in CKD: High anion gap (uraemic acids) + normal anion gap (NH4+ excretion defect).
Winter's formula: Expected pCO2 = 1.5 × HCO3 + 8 ± 2.

Chloride & Anion Gap

ParameterNormalSignificance
Cl-95–105 mmol/LHyperchloraemia: normal AG metabolic acidosis (RTA, diarrhoea, saline infusion)
Anion Gap8–12 mmol/LHAGMA = Na - (Cl + HCO3); MUDPILES: Methanol, Uraemia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Infection (lactate), Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates

4. Mineral Bone Parameters (CKD-MBD)

ParameterNormalCKD SignificanceTarget in CKD
Phosphate (PO4)0.8–1.4 mmol/LHyperphosphataemia = CKD (↓ excretion), rhabdo, tumour lysisG3–5: <1.13 mmol/L (KDIGO)
Calcium (total)2.2–2.6 mmol/LHypocalcaemia = CKD (↓ Vit D, ↑ Phos), hypopara; Hypercalcaemia: sarcoid, malignancy, tertiary HPTTarget: lower normal range (2.1–2.3)
Corrected CaAlbumin-adjustedFormula: Ca_corr = Ca + 0.02 × (40 - Albumin)Use corrected Ca for interpretation
PTH1.6–6.9 pmol/L↑ in CKD (secondary HPT); target: 2–9 × upper limitG3a–G5: 2–9 × ULN
Vit D (25-OH)>50 nmol/LDeficiency common in CKD (↓ 1α-hydroxylase)>50 nmol/L; treat if <30
FGF-23—↑ early in CKD (phosphate homeostasis)Not routinely measured
[!key]
CKD-MBD triad: ↑ Phosphate, ↓ Calcium, ↑ PTH, ↓ Vit D, ↑ FGF-23.
Treatment: Phosphate binders, Vit D analogues (alfacalcidol, calcitriol), calcimimetics (cinacalcet).

5. Haematology & Nutritional Markers

TestNormalRenal Significance
Haemoglobin (Hb)130–180 (M), 115–165 (F)Anaemia of CKD (↓ EPO, ↓ survival, blood loss); target 100–120 g/L
MCV80–100 fLNormocytic (CKD), microcytic (Fe def), macrocytic (B12/folate, AZT)
Ferritin30–400 μg/LFerritin >500 = overload; <100 (or <200 if inflammation) = Fe deficiency
TSAT20–50%<20% = absolute Fe deficiency; >50% = overload
Albumin35–50 g/L↓ in nephrotic syndrome, malnutrition, inflammation
HbA1c<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)Diabetic nephropathy monitoring; target <53 (7%) in DM
LipidsTC <5, LDL <3Nephrotic: ↑ TC, TG, LDL; CKD: CVD risk
Uric acid200–420 (M), 140–360 (F)↑ in gout, tumour lysis, CKD, diuretics; ↓ in Fanconi, Wilson's

6. Inflammatory & Immunological Markers

TestUse in Renal Disease
CRP / ESRInflammation (vasculitis, infection, amyloidosis)
ANA, dsDNA, C3/C4SLE nephritis, lupus activity
ANCA (p-ANCA, c-ANCA)Vasculitis (MPA, GPA, EGPA)
Anti-GBMGoodpasture's syndrome
ANA, ENASLE, other CTDs
C3, C4Low in MPGN, SLE, post-strep GN
Anti-PLA2RPrimary membranous nephropathy
Hepatitis B/C, HIVViral-associated GN (MPGN, HIVAN)
Serum free light chainsMyeloma cast nephropathy, amyloidosis
Serum protein electrophoresisParaprotein (myeloma, MGUS, amyloidosis)

7. Specialised Renal Tests

TestIndication
Cystatin CeGFR estimation (extremes of muscle mass)
Complement (C3, C4)Low in MPGN, SLE, post-strep GN, C3G
Anti-GBM antibodyGoodpasture's syndrome
Anti-PLA2RPrimary membranous nephropathy
Anti-dsDNA, C3/C4SLE activity
ANCA (MPO, PR3)Vasculitis (MPA, GPA, EGPA)
Serum free light chainsMyeloma cast nephropathy, AL amyloidosis
Serum protein electrophoresisParaprotein (myeloma, MGUS, amyloidosis)
CryoglobulinsCryoglobulinaemic vasculitis (HCV, lymphoma)
Urinary electrophoresisBence Jones protein (myeloma)
C3/C4Low in MPGN, SLE, post-strep GN
Hepatitis B/C, HIVViral-associated GN
CalprotectinIntestinal inflammation (IBD-associated nephropathy)

8. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Creatinine/eGFR: CKD staging (G1–G5)
- K+ / HCO3-: AKI/CKD monitoring
- Phos/Ca/PTH/Vit D: CKD-MBD triad
- Anaemia of CKD: EPO deficiency → target Hb 100–120
- Anaemia workup: Ferritin, TSAT, B12, folate
- PTH target: 2–9 × ULN (KDIGO)
- Phosphate target: <1.13 mmol/L (CKD G3–5)
- Vit D target: >50 nmol/L
- Acidosis in CKD: Normal AG (NH4+ defect) + HAGMA
- Albumin/Hb: Nutritional status, anaemia severity

9. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Creatinine = GFRCreatinine = marker; eGFR = estimate
Urea = GFR markerUrea affected by hydration, protein, catabolism
eGFR = true GFReGFR = estimate; ±30% accuracy
Phosphate = only CKDAlso rhabdo, tumour lysis, pseudohypoparathyroidism
PTH = only CKDAlso primary HPT, Vit D deficiency, FHH
Corrected Ca = measured CaMust correct for albumin (Ca + 0.02 × (40 - Alb))
PTH = only CKD markerAlso in Vit D deficiency, malabsorption
Ferritin = iron status onlyAcute phase reactant; ↑ in inflammation
TSAT = only iron testNeed ferritin + TSAT + CRP for full picture
Acidosis in CKD = only HAGMAAlso NAGMA (RTA, NH4+ defect)

10. Mnemonics

  • Renal bloods: Cr, EGFR, Urea, Electrolytes, Ca, PO4, PTH, Vit D, Alb, Hb, PTH = CUEECPAD
  • CKD-MBD: Phosphate, Calcium, PTH, Vit D, FGF-23 = PCPVD
  • Anaemia CKD: EPO deficiency → Anemia; Fe def → TSAT <20% = EATF
  • Acidemia CKD: Normal AG (NH4+ defect) + High AG = NAH
  • PTH target: 2–9 × ULN = 2–9

11. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Blood Tests in Renal Disease))
    Renal_Function[Cr, eGFR, Urea, Cystatin C]
    Electrolytes
      Na_K[Na 135-145, K 3.5-5.0]
      Acid_Base[HCO3 22-29, AG 8-12]
      MBD[PO4, Ca, PTH, VitD, FGF23]
    Haematology[Hb, Ferritin, TSAT, B12, Folate]
    Nutrition[Albumin, HbA1c, Lipids, Uric Acid]
    Inflammation[CRP, ESR, ANA, ANCA, C3/C4]
    Special[Anti-PLA2R, ANCA, Anti-GBM, FLC, Cryoglobulins]
    Monitoring
      AKI[Cr, K, HCO3, UO q6h]
      CKD[Cr, eGFR, K, HCO3, PO4, Ca, PTH, Hb q3-6mo]
      Dialysis[Pre/post Cr, K, PO4, Hb, PTH q1-3mo]

12. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Key renal bloods (Cr, eGFR, Urea, U&E, Ca, PO4, PTH, Vit D, Hb, Albumin)
  2. CKD-MBD targets (PO4, Ca, PTH, Vit D)
  3. Anaemia of CKD (EPO, target Hb, iron studies)
  4. Acid-base in CKD (NAGMA + HAGMA)
  5. PTH target in CKD (2–9 × ULN)
  6. Phosphate target in CKD (<1.13)
  7. Vit D target (>50 nmol/L)
  8. Anaemia workup (Ferritin, TSAT, B12, Folate)

13. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

14. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Core renal panel, electrolytes/acid-base, CKD-MBD (Ca/PO4/PTH/VitD), anaemia workup, PTH targets
Should Know 🟡Cystatin C, FGF-23, specialised antibodies (anti-PLA2R, ANCA, anti-GBM), novel biomarkers
Nice to Know 🟢Novel biomarkers (NGAL, KIM-1, suPAR), genetic testing, proteomics

15. MCQs (10)

  1. Serum creatinine is used to estimate GFR because:

A. It is freely filtered and not reabsorbed

B. It is freely filtered, not reabsorbed, minimally secreted

C. It is completely secreted

D. It is completely reabsorbed

E. It is produced at a constant rate by liver

  1. CKD-MBD triad includes:

A. High Ca, Low PO4, Low PTH

B. Low Ca, High PO4, High PTH

C. High Ca, High PO4, Low PTH

D. Normal Ca, Normal PO4, Normal PTH

E. Low Ca, Low PO4, Low PTH

  1. Target PTH in CKD G3–5 (KDIGO):

A. Normal range

B. 2–9 × upper limit of normal

C.

D. >10 × upper limit

E. Not monitored

  1. Target phosphate in CKD G3–5 (KDIGO):

A. <1.78 mmol/L

B. <1.13 mmol/L

C. <2.0 mmol/L

D. <0.8 mmol/L

E. Normal range

  1. Anaemia of CKD — primary cause:

A. Blood loss

B. Iron deficiency

C. Erythropoietin deficiency

D. Folate deficiency

E. Haemolysis

  1. Target Hb in CKD on ESA:

A. >130 g/L

B. 100–120 g/L

C. >120 g/L

D. >140 g/L

E. >110 g/L

  1. Metabolic acidosis in CKD — primary type:

A. High anion gap only

B. Normal anion gap (impaired NH4+ excretion) + some HAGMA

C. Respiratory acidosis

D. Metabolic alkalosis

E. Pure HAGMA

  1. PTH target in CKD G5 on dialysis (KDIGO):

A. Normal range

B. 2–9 × ULN

C.

D. >10 × ULN

E. Not applicable

  1. Phosphate binder first-line in CKD:

A. Sevelamer

B. Calcium acetate / carbonate (if Ca normal/low)

C. Lanthanum

D. Aluminium hydroxide

E. Ferric citrate

  1. Vitamin D deficiency in CKD — cause:

A. ↑ 1α-hydroxylase

B. ↓ 1α-hydroxylase (loss of renal mass)

C. ↑ FGF-23

C. ↓ PTH

E. Malabsorption only

  1. Anaemia of CKD — target Hb (KDIGO):

A. >130 g/L

B. 100–120 g/L

C. >130 g/L

D. 110–120 g/L

E. >130 g/L

  1. Metabolic acidosis in CKD — anion gap:

A. Always high

B. Normal AG (impaired NH4+ excretion) + some HAGMA

C. Always normal

C. Respiratory compensation only

E. Pure HAGMA

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. CKD stage 4 patient, Ca 2.1, PO4 1.8, PTH 25 pmol/L (ULN 6.9). Best initial phosphate binder:

A. Sevelamer

B. Calcium acetate (low Ca, high PO4, PTH not markedly elevated)

C. Lanthanum

C. Sevelamer

E. Aluminium hydroxide

  1. CKD stage 4, Hb 95 g/L, Ferritin 150, TSAT 18%. Next step:

A. ESA only

B. IV Iron (Ferritin <200, TSAT <20) → then ESA if Hb not rising

C. Oral iron only

D. ESA only

E. B12/folate only

  1. CKD stage 5 on HD. Ca 2.0, PO4 2.2, PTH 45 (ULN 7). Mgmt:

A. Increase calcium binder

B. Cinacalcet (PTH >9 × ULN) + switch to non-calcium binder

C. Increase calcium binder

D. Parathyroidectomy

E. Increase dialysate Ca

  1. CKD stage 3b, Hb 110, Ferritin 50, TSAT 15%. Management:

A. ESA only

B. IV Iron → recheck → ESA if Hb not rising

C. Oral iron only

D. ESA + oral iron

E. Blood transfusion

  1. CKD stage 4, Ca 2.3, PO4 1.5, PTH 12. Best binder:

A. Sevelamer

B. Calcium carbonate (Ca normal, PO4 high, PTH normal-high)

C. Lanthanum

D. Aluminium hydroxide

E. No binder needed

  1. CKD patient on cinacalcet. Monitoring:

A. Ca, PO4, PTH q3mo

B. Ca, PO4, PTH q1–4wks initially, then q3mo

C. Ca only

D. PTH only

E. PO4 only

  1. CKD stage 4, Alb 28, Hb 90. Nutritional intervention:

A. High protein diet

B. Low protein (0.6–0.8 g/kg) + ketoanalogues if <30; dietary counselling

C. High protein + ESA

D. Protein restriction <0.6 g/kg

E. Protein restriction <0.4 g/kg

  1. CKD stage 5 on PD. Peritoneal equilibration test (PET) shows high transporter. Prescription:

A. Long dwells

B. Short, frequent dwells (APD)

C. Icodextrin only

E. Increase dwell volume

  1. CKD stage 4, acidosis (HCO3 16, normal AG). Treatment:

A. IV bicarbonate

B. Oral sodium bicarbonate (target HCO3 >22)

C. IV sodium bicarbonate

D. THAM

E. Dialysis

17. Flashcards

  • Q: CKD-MBD triad?

A: High PO4, Low Ca, High PTH, Low Vit D

  • Q: PTH target CKD?

A: 2–9 × ULN (KDIGO)

  • Q: Phosphate target CKD?

A: <1.13 mmol/L (KDIGO G3–5)

  • Q: Ca correction formula?

A: Ca_corr = Ca + 0.02 × (40 - Albumin)

  • Q: Anaemia CKD cause?

A: EPO deficiency

  • Q: Anaemia CKD Hb target?

A: 100–120 g/L

  • Q: Phosphate binder 1st line?

A: Calcium-based (if Ca normal/low)

  • Q: Sevelamer use?

A: Hypercalcaemia, adynamic bone, PD

  • Q: Cinacalcet indication?

A: PTH >9×ULN on max binder + Vit D

  • Q: FGF-23 in CKD?

A: Early rise, phosphate regulator

  • Q: Vit D target?

A: >50 nmol/L

  • Q: Anaemia Hb target?

A: 100–120 g/L

  • Q: Fe deficiency CKD?

A: Ferritin <100 (or <200 if inflam) + TSAT <20%

  • Q: Metabolic acidosis CKD?

A: Normal AG + HAGMA

  • Q: Alk phos in renal bone disease?

A: High turnover = high; Adynamic = low/normal

  • Q: Dialysis dementia?

A. Historical; aluminium

Blood Tests in Renal Disease is a Must Know topic. Full FCPS/MRCP content with detailed tables, CKD-MBD management algorithms, anaemia algorithms, MCQs, SBAs, flashcards, and answer keys available in the full version.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.2 18.2 2
▸ 18.2.1 Acute Kidney Injury Aki
Topic: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)]], [[Functional Anatomy and Physiology of the Kidney and Urinary Tract]], [[Investigation of Renal and Urinary Tract Disease]], [[Tubulointerstitial Diseases — Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN)]], [[Vascular Diseases of the Kidney — Thrombotic Microangiopathies (HUS, TTP)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
AKI = sudden (hours–days) decline in renal function. KDIGO definition: ↑Cr ≥26.5 µmol/L in 48h OR ↑Cr ≥1.5× baseline in 7d OR UO <0.5 mL/kg/h × 6h. Causes: Pre-renal (↓ perfusion), Intrinsic (ATN, GN, AIN, vascular), Post-renal (obstruction). Management: ABCDE, fluid resuscitation, stop nephrotoxins, treat cause, RRT if indicated.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define AKI using KDIGO criteria
  • Classify AKI aetiology (pre-renal, intrinsic, post-renal)
  • Recognise clinical presentations and diagnostic approach
  • Apply management principles (fluid resuscitation, nephrotoxin avoidance, RRT indications)
  • Manage AKI complications (hyperkalaemia, acidosis, fluid overload)

2. KDIGO Definition & Staging

StageCreatinine CriteriaUrine Output Criteria
Stage 1↑Cr ≥26.5 µmol/L in 48h OR ↑Cr 1.5–1.9× baselineUO <0.5 mL/kg/h × 6–12h
Stage 2↑Cr 2.0–2.9× baselineUO <0.5 mL/kg/h × ≥12h
Stage 3↑Cr ≥3.0× baseline OR ↑Cr ≥354 µmol/L OR RRT initiatedUO <0.3 mL/kg/h × 24h OR Anuria × 12h
[!key]
AKI = acute rise in Cr OR fall in UO. Stage by worst creatinine OR urine output criterion.

3. Aetiology Classification

CategoryExamplesKey Clues
Pre-renal (↓ renal perfusion)Hypovolaemia (haemorrhage, dehydration, burns), ↓ CO (heart failure, sepsis), renal vasoconstriction (NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, hepatorenal), renal artery stenosisBUN:Cr >20, FeNa <1%, Uosm >500, Uosm > plasma, FeUrea <35%
Intrinsic RenalATN (ischaemia, nephrotoxins: aminoglycosides, contrast, cisplatin, myoglobin, HUS/TTP)FeNa >2% (ATN), muddy brown casts, FeNa <1% (contrast, sepsis, rhabdo, glomerulonephritis)
Glomerulonephritis (RPGN, IgA, anti-GBM, lupus, post-strep)Haematuria, RBC casts, proteinuria, hypertension, oedema
Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN)Drug-induced (NSAIDs, antibiotics, PPI), fever, rash, eosinophilia, WBC casts, eosinophiluria
Vascular (renal artery thrombosis, atheroemboli, TMA/HUS/TTP, malignant HTN)Flank pain, livedo reticularis, schistocytes, hypertension
Post-renal (Obstruction)Stones, BPH, tumours, strictures, retroperitoneal fibrosis, neurogenic bladderHydronephrosis on US, palpable bladder, anuria/fluctuating output

4. Clinical Assessment

History

DomainKey Questions
OnsetAcute vs chronic progression
Urine OutputOliguria (<400 mL/d), anuria (<100 mL/d), polyuria (recovery phase)
SymptomsNausea, vomiting, confusion (uraemia), dyspnoea (fluid overload), flank pain (obstruction)
Drug HistoryNephrotoxins: NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, aminoglycosides, contrast, cisplatin, PPI, diuretics
Medical HistoryHeart failure, liver disease, sepsis, diabetes, hypertension, malignancy
ExposuresContrast (recent CT), antibiotics (aminoglycosides), chemotherapy

Examination

SystemKey Findings
Volume StatusJVP, skin turgor, mucous membranes, peripheral oedema, JVP
VitalsBP (hypotension = pre-renal; hypertension = intrinsic/GN), HR, temp
AbdomenPalpable bladder (retention), flank tenderness (pyelo, colic), renal masses
SkinRash (AIN, vasculitis), purpura (vasculitis/TTP), urticaria (AIN)
NeuroAsterixis (uraemic encephalopathy), confusion

5. Diagnostic Approach

Blood Tests

TestPurpose
U&E, Cr, eGFRBaseline, staging
VBG/ABGAcid-base, lactate, K+
FBC, CRPInfection, inflammation, eosinophilia (AIN)
Ca, PO4, Mg, UrateTumour lysis, rhabdo, AKI complications
CKRhabdomyolysis
Uric AcidTumour lysis, rhabdo
CoagulationDIC, TTP/HUS
Blood CulturesSepsis
AutoantibodiesANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, complement (if GN suspected)

Urine Tests

TestInterpretation
Urine DipstickProtein, blood, leucocytes, nitrites
MicroscopyRBC casts = GN; WBC casts = pyelo/AIN; muddy brown = ATN; eosinophiluria = AIN
FeNa / FeUreaFeNa <1% = pre-renal; FeNa >2% = ATN (unreliable on diuretics); FeUrea <35% = pre-renal
FormulaCalculation
FeNa(U_Na × P_Cr) / (P_Na × U_Cr) × 100%
FeUrea(U_Urea × P_Cr) / (P_Urea × U_Cr) × 100%

Imaging

ModalityIndication
US KUBFirst-line; hydronephrosis, kidney size, cysts, stones, bladder volume
CT KUBStones (gold standard)
CT UrogramHaematuria, masses, complex obstruction
Bladder ScanPost-void residual (retention)

6. Management Principles

1. Resuscitation (First 6 Hours)

StepAction
AAirway, high-flow O2 if hypoxic
BAssess breathing, intubate if needed
CFluid resuscitation: 500 mL crystalloid bolus, reassess; target MAP >65
DNeurological assessment (GCS, asterixis)
EExposure, monitor temperature

2. Identify & Treat Cause

CategoryAction
Pre-renalFluid resuscitation (crystalloid 500 mL boluses), stop diuretics/ACEi/ARB/NSAIDs
ATNStop nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, aminoglycosides, contrast); maintain euvolaemia
GNImmunosuppression (steroids ± cyclophosphamide/rituximab) per histology
AINStop offending drug; steroids if severe (pred 1 mg/kg)
ObstructionUrgent decompression: Foley catheter, nephrostomy, stent
VascularAnticoagulation (renal vein thrombosis), plasma exchange (TTP/HUS), BP control

3. Supportive Management

ComplicationManagement
Hyperkalaemia>6.5 or ECG changes: Ca gluconate 10 mL 10%, insulin/dextrose, salbutamol, kayexalate, dialysis
Metabolic AcidosisHCO3- <15 or pH <7.2: IV bicarbonate (target pH >7.2)
Fluid OverloadDiuretics (furosemide IV), fluid restriction, dialysis if refractory
Uraemic ComplicationsPericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding (DDAVP, platelets), neuropathy

4. Renal Replacement Therapy (RRT) Indications

IndicationAEIOU Mnemonic
AAcidosis (pH <7.2, HCO3 <12)
EElectrolytes (K+ >6.5, refractory)
IIngestion (toxic alcohols, lithium, aspirin, metformin)
OOverload (pulmonary oedema refractory to diuretics)
UUraemia (pericarditis, encephalopathy, neuropathy, bleeding)
ModalityIndication
IHDHaemodynamically stable, hyperkalaemia, overdose
CVVH/CVVHDFHaemodynamically unstable, fluid overload, sepsis
PDHaemodynamically stable, no vascular access, resource-limited

7. Special AKI Syndromes

SyndromeKey FeaturesManagement
Contrast-Induced AKI↑Cr 48–72h post-contrast; risk: CKD, DM, HF, age >70, volume depletionHydration (IV 0.9% saline 1 mL/kg/h 12h pre/post); NAC controversial; avoid NSAIDs
RhabdomyolysisCK >5000 (or >5× ULN), myoglobinuria, tea-coloured urine, hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemiaAggressive IV fluids (target UO 200–300 mL/h), alkalinisation (target urine pH >6.5), avoid myoglobin precipitation
Tumour Lysis SyndromeTLS: K+, PO4, Uric acid ↑, Ca ↓; Cairo-Bishop criteriaHydration, allopurinol/rasburicase, monitor K+/PO4/Ca, dialysis if severe
Hepatorenal Syndrome (HRS)Cirrhosis + ascites + renal failure (no other cause); HRS-1 (rapid), HRS-2 (slow)Terlipressin + albumin; TIPS; liver transplant evaluation
Post-Obstructive DiuresisPolyuria after relief of obstructionReplace urine output mL-for-mL with 0.45% saline + KCl; monitor electrolytes

8. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- KDIGO AKI: Cr ↑26.5 in 48h OR 1.5× baseline in 7d OR UO <0.5 mL/kg/h × 6h
- Staging: Stage 1 (1.5–1.9× Cr), Stage 2 (2–2.9×), Stage 3 (3×, RRT, Cr >354)
- Pre-renal: FeNa <1%, FeUrea <35%
- ATN: FeNa >2%, muddy brown casts
- AIN: Fever, rash, eosinophilia, WBC casts, eosinophiluria
- Obstruction: Hydronephrosis on US; relieve ASAP (catheter, nephrostomy, stent)
- AEIOU for RRT: Acidosis, Electrolytes, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia
- Contrast nephropathy: Hydration, stop nephrotoxins, NAC controversial
- Rhabdo: CK >5000, tea urine, aggressive fluids + alkalinisation
- TLS: K+, PO4, UA up; Ca down; rasburicase, hydration

9. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
AKI = only Cr riseAlso UO criteria (KDIGO)
FeNa <1% = always pre-renalInvalid on diuretics; use FeUrea (<35%)
FeNa >2% = always ATNAlso in GN, obstruction, sepsis, contrast; use FeUrea
Oliguria = AKIOliguria = <400 mL/d; AKI needs KDIGO criteria
AKI = always reversibleCan progress to CKD/ESRD if severe/prolonged
Contrast nephropathy = immediatePeaks at 48–72h post-contrast
Urine output = only AKI markerAKI = Cr rise OR UO decline
All AKI needs dialysisOnly AEIOU indications

10. Mnemonics

  • AKI Definition: Cr ↑26.5 in 48h OR 1.5× baseline in 7d OR UO <0.5 × 6h = C-Or-U
  • AKI Stages: 1 = 1.5–1.9×; 2 = 2–2.9×; 3 = 3× or RRT = 123
  • AEIOU RRT: Acidosis, Electrolytes (K+), Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia = AEIOU
  • Pre-renal vs ATN: FeNa <1% = Pre, >2% = ATN = FeNa 1 = Pre, 2 = ATN
  • AIN triad: Fever, Rash, Eosinophilia = FRE
  • Obstruction relief: Foley, Nephrostomy, Stent = FNS
  • AKI causes: Pre-renal, Intrinsic, Post-renal = PIP

11. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Acute Kidney Injury))
    Definition
      KDIGO[Cr, UO]
      Staging[Stage 1-3]
    Etiology
      Pre_Renal[Hypovolaemia, HF, Sepsis, Drugs]
      Intrinsic[ATN, GN, AIN, Vascular]
      Post_Renal[Obstruction]
    Diagnosis
      Labs[Cr, UO, FeNa, Microscopy]
      Imaging[US, CT]
    Management
      Resuscitation[Fluids, Stop nephrotoxins]
      Cause_Specific[Pre: fluids; Intrinsic: treat cause; Post: decompress]
      RRT[AEIOU]
    Complications
      K[HyperK], Acid[Acidosis], Fluid[Overload], Uraemia[Pericarditis, Encephalopathy]

12. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. KDIGO AKI definition (Cr + UO criteria)
  2. AKI staging (1, 2, 3 criteria)
  3. Pre-renal vs ATN (FeNa, FeUrea, casts)
  4. AEIOU indications for RRT
  5. AIN triad (fever, rash, eosinophilia)
  6. Contrast nephropathy prevention
  7. Rhabdo management (fluids, alkalinisation)
  8. Obstruction management (Foley, nephrostomy, stent)

13. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

14. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴KDIGO definition/staging, aetiology classification, FeNa/FeUrea, AEIOU, contrast nephropathy, rhabdo, obstruction management
Should Know 🟡AIN diagnosis/treatment, rhabdo details, TLS, contrast protocols, HRS, post-obstructive diuresis
Nice to Know 🟢Novel biomarkers (NGAL, KIM-1), KDIGO updates, CAR-T nephrotoxicity, SGLT2i in AKI prevention

15. MCQs (10)

  1. KDIGO AKI Stage 1 creatinine criteria:

A. ↑Cr ≥26.5 µmol/L in 48h OR ↑Cr 1.5–1.9× baseline

B. ↑Cr ≥26.5 µmol/L in 24h

C. ↑Cr 2.0–2.9× baseline

D. ↑Cr ≥3× baseline

E. ↑Cr ≥354 µmol/L

  1. FeNa <1% is characteristic of:

A. ATN

B. Pre-renal AKI

C. Glomerulonephritis

D. Obstructive uropathy

E. AIN

  1. Muddy brown granular casts are characteristic of:

A. Glomerulonephritis

B. Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN)

C. Acute Interstitial Nephritis

D. Acute Pyelonephritis

E. Minimal Change Disease

  1. Contrast nephropathy typically peaks at:

A. 6–12 hours

B. 48–72 hours

C. 24 hours

D. 5–7 days

E. Immediately

  1. AEIOU mnemonic for RRT indications:

A. Acidosis, Electrolytes, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia

B. Acidosis, Electrolytes, Intoxication, Overload, Uraemia

C. Acidosis, Electrolytes, Infection, Overload, Uraemia

D. Alkalosis, Electrolytes, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia

E. Anuria, Electrolytes, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia

  1. RRT indication - "O" in AEIOU:

A. Overhydration

B. Overload (fluid refractory to diuretics)

C. Oliguria

D. Oligoanuria

E. Obstruction

  1. Contrast nephropathy prevention - best evidence:

A. NAC alone

B. IV fluid hydration (0.9% saline 1 mL/kg/h 12h pre/post)

C. Sodium bicarbonate alone

D. NAC + bicarbonate

E. Dopamine

  1. Rhabdomyolysis - target urine output:

A. 100 mL/h

B. 200–300 mL/h

C. 500 mL/h

D. 1000 mL/h

E. 50 mL/h

  1. FeNa formula:

A. (U_Na × U_Cr) / (P_Na × P_Cr) × 100

B. (U_Na × P_Cr) / (P_Na × U_Cr) × 100

C. (P_Na × U_Cr) / (U_Na × P_Cr) × 100

D. (U_Na × P_Cr) / (P_Na × U_Cr) × 100

E. (U_Na / P_Na) × 100

  1. AEIOU "I" stands for:

A. Infection

B. Ingestion (toxins)

C. Inflammation

D. Ischemia

E. Immunocompromise

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 65-year-old man post-cardiac surgery. Day 2: Cr 180 (baseline 80), UO 15 mL/h avg. KDIGO Stage:

A. No AKI

B. Stage 1

C. Stage 2

D. Stage 3

E. No AKI (post-op expected)

  1. 50-year-old man with sepsis. Cr 250 (baseline 90). UO 20 mL/h. FeNa 0.5%. Diagnosis:

A. ATN

B. Pre-renal AKI

C. GN

D. Obstruction

E. AIN

  1. 45-year-old on piperacillin-tazobactam. Fever, rash, eosinophilia, eosinophiluria. UO 0.4 mL/kg/h. Best next step:

A. Continue antibiotic

B. Stop drug, start prednisolone 1mg/kg

C. Increase fluids

D. Renal biopsy

E. Dialysis

  1. 70-year-old post-CT coronary angiogram. Day 3 Cr 180 (baseline 100). Prevention was:

A. NAC alone

B. IV saline 1 mL/kg/h 12h pre + 12h post

C. Sodium bicarbonate only

D. NAC + bicarbonate

E. Furosemide

  1. 55-year-old man, CK 50,000, tea-coloured urine, K+ 6.8. Immediate management:

A. Dialysis

B. IV fluids 200–300 mL/h + sodium bicarbonate (urine pH >6.5)

C. Haemodialysis

D. Kayexalate

E. Calcium gluconate only

  1. Hepatorenal Syndrome Type 1 — definition:

A. Chronic kidney disease in cirrhosis

B. Rapidly progressive renal failure in cirrhosis without other cause

C. AKI from sepsis in cirrhosis

D. AKI from diuretics in cirrhosis

E. Renal vein thrombosis

  1. Tumour Lysis Syndrome lab findings:

A. Low K, Low PO4, High Ca, Low Uric Acid

B. High K, High PO4, High Uric Acid, Low Ca

C. Low K, High PO4, Low UA

D. High K, Low PO4, High UA

E. Normal electrolytes

  1. Post-obstructive diuresis management:

A. Restrict fluids

B. Replace urine output mL-for-mL with 0.45% saline + KCl

C. Furosemide infusion

D. Restrict sodium

E. Observe only

  1. Contrast nephropathy - peak creatinine rise:

A. 6–12 hours

B. 48–72 hours

C. 24 hours

D. Immediately

E. 5–7 days

  1. Tumour Lysis Syndrome - prophylaxis for intermediate risk:

A. Rasburicase

B. Allopurinol + hydration

C. Rasburicase + allopurinol

D. Hydration only

E. Febuxostat

17. Flashcards

  • Q: KDIGO AKI criteria?

A: Cr ↑26.5 µmol/L in 48h OR 1.5× baseline in 7d OR UO <0.5 mL/kg/h × 6h

  • Q: AKI staging?

A: Stage 1: 1.5–1.9×; Stage 2: 2–2.9×; Stage 3: ≥3× or RRT or Cr >354

  • Q: Pre-renal vs ATN FeNa?

A: FeNa <1% pre-renal; >2% ATN

  • Q: FeNa formula?

A: (U_Na × P_Cr) / (P_Na × U_Cr) × 100%

  • Q: FeUrea pre-renal cutoff?

A: <35%

  • Q: ATN urine casts?

A: Muddy brown granular casts

  • Q: AIN triad?

A: Fever, rash, eosinophilia

  • Q: AEIOU RRT?

A: Acidosis, Electrolytes, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia

  • Q: Contrast nephropathy peak?

A: 48–72 hours

  • Q: Contrast prevention?

A: IV saline 1 mL/kg/h 12h pre/post

  • Q: Rhabdo CK threshold?

A: >5000 (or 5× ULN)

  • Q: Rhabdo urine output target?

A: 200–300 mL/h

  • Q: Rhabdo alkalinisation target?

A: Urine pH >6.5

  • Q: TLS labs?

A: High K, PO4, UA; low Ca

  • Q: TLS prophylaxis?

A: Allopurinol + hydration (intermediate); rasburicase (high)

  • Q: HRS-1 definition?

A: Rapidly progressive renal failure in cirrhosis without other identifiable cause

  • Q: Post-obstructive diuresis replacement?

A: mL-for-mL with 0.45% saline + KCl

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — KDIGO Stage 1: Cr ↑26.5 µmol/L in 48h OR 1.5–1.9× baseline
  2. B — FeNa <1% = pre-renal
  3. B — Muddy brown granular casts = ATN
  4. B — Contrast nephropathy peaks 48–72h
  5. A — AEIOU = Acidosis, Electrolytes, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia
  6. B — "O" = Overload (pulmonary oedema refractory to diuretics)
  7. B — IV saline hydration is only evidence-based prevention
  8. B — Rhabdo target UO 200–300 mL/h
  9. B — FeNa = (U_Na × P_Cr) / (P_Na × U_Cr) × 100
  10. B — "I" = Ingestion (toxic alcohols, lithium, aspirin, metformin)

SBAs

  1. C — Cr 180/80 = 2.25× = Stage 2 (UO 15 mL/h ≈ 0.2 mL/kg/h for 70kg = Stage 1; worse of two = Stage 2)
  2. B — FeNa 0.5% = pre-renal (sepsis with renal hypoperfusion)
  3. B — Drug-induced AIN → stop drug + steroids
  4. B — IV saline 1 mL/kg/h 12h pre/post is evidence-based
  5. B — Rhabdo: fluids + alkalinisation (urine pH >6.5) first-line
  6. B — HRS-1 = rapidly progressive in cirrhosis without other cause
  7. B — TLS = high K, PO4, UA; low Ca (Cairo-Bishop)
  8. B — Post-obstructive diuresis: replace mL-for-mL 0.45% saline + KCl
  9. B — Peak 48–72h
  10. B — Intermediate risk: allopurinol + hydration (rasburicase for high risk)

19. Summary

Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: KDIGO = Cr ↑26.5/48h OR 1.5× baseline OR UO <0.5 mL/kg/h × 6h. Staging: 1.5–1.9× (1), 2–2.9× (2), ≥3×/RRT/Cr>354 (3). Pre-renal: FeNa <1%, FeUrea <35%; ATN: FeNa >2%, muddy brown casts. AIN: fever/rash/eosinophilia/WBC casts. AEIOU for RRT. Contrast nephropathy peaks 48–72h; prevent with IV fluids. Rhabdo: CK>5000, fluids + alkalinisation. Obstruction: US → decompress (Foley/nephrostomy/stent).

Exam focus: KDIGO criteria/staging, FeNa/FeUrea interpretation, cast identification, AEIOU, contrast nephropathy, rhabdo/TLS management, obstruction relief.

Clinical relevance: Early recognition, timely intervention, RRT indications, prevention strategies.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.2.2 Chronic Kidney Disease Ckd
Topic: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)]], [[Functional Anatomy and Physiology of the Kidney and Urinary Tract]], [[Glomerular Filtration Rate]], [[Blood Tests in Renal Disease]], [[Renal Replacement Therapy — Dialysis (HD, PD), Indications, Complications]], [[Kidney Transplantation (Indications, Workup, Immunosuppression, Complications)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
CKD = eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² for ≥3 months OR markers of kidney damage (ACR ≥3 mg/mmol, haematuria, structural abnormalities) for ≥3 months. KDIGO staging: G1–G5 by eGFR + A1–A3 by ACR. Management: CV risk reduction, BP <130/80, CKD-MBD, anaemia (Hb target 100–115 g/L), acidosis (HCO₃⁻ ≥22), volume, nutrition, drug dosing, RRT preparation. Avoid nephrotoxins.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define CKD and apply KDIGO staging (G1–G5 + A1–A3)
  • Apply management principles: BP control, CV risk reduction, CKD-MBD, anaemia, acidosis, nutrition
  • Manage complications: volume overload, hyperkalaemia, acidosis, anaemia, CKD-MBD
  • Prepare for renal replacement therapy (RRT) and transplantation
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP clinical scenarios

2. Definition & Diagnosis

CriterionDefinition
CKDeGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² for ≥3 months OR markers of kidney damage (ACR ≥3 mg/mmol, haematuria, structural abnormalities) for ≥3 months
Markers of Kidney DamageACR ≥3 mg/mmol (or AER ≥30 mg/24h), haematuria (non-urological), structural abnormalities (imaging/histology), transplant recipient
[!key]
CKD requires persistence ≥3 months. Single eGFR <60 or ACR ≥3 is not enough — must be confirmed on ≥2 occasions ≥90 days apart.

3. KDIGO Staging (2012)

GFR Categories (G)

CategoryeGFR (mL/min/1.73m²)Description
G1≥90Normal or high
G260–89Mildly decreased
G3a45–59Mildly to moderately decreased
G3b30–44Moderately to severely decreased
G415–29Severely decreased
G5<15Kidney failure

Albuminuria Categories (A)

CategoryACR (mg/mmol)AER (mg/24h)Description
A1<3<30Normal to mildly increased
A23–3030–300Moderately increased (microalbuminuria)
A3>30>300Severely increased (macroalbuminuria)

Prognosis by Combined G + A (Heat Map)

A1 (<3)A2 (3–30)A3 (>30)
G1 (≥90)LowModerateHigh
G2 (60–89)LowModerateHigh
G3a (45–59)ModerateHighVery High
G3b (30–44)HighVery HighVery High
G4 (15–29)Very HighVery HighVery High
G5 (<15)Very HighVery HighVery High
[!key]
G3aA1 = Moderate risk; G3bA2 = Very High risk. Low eGFR + high albuminuria = worst prognosis.

4. Aetiology (Common Causes)

Cause% in AdultsNotes
Diabetic Kidney Disease35–45%Commonest cause worldwide
Hypertensive Nephrosclerosis20–25%Often overdiagnosed; needs biopsy if atypical
Glomerulonephritis15–20%IgA, FSGS, MN, etc.
Obstructive Uropathy5–10%BPH, stones, strictures
ADPKD5%Autosomal dominant
Tubulointerstitial5%Analgesics, lithium, reflux
Unknown5–10%Late presentation, small kidneys

5. Clinical Presentation

StageTypical Features
G1–G2Asymptomatic; detected on screening (ACR, BP)
G3a–G3bFatigue, nocturia, mild anaemia, early CKD-MBD
G4Anaemia, bone disease, acidosis, volume overload, drug accumulation
G5Uraemic symptoms: pericarditis, encephalopathy, neuropathy, bleeding, severe volume overload, refractory hyperkalaemia

6. Management Principles

1. Cardiovascular Risk Reduction (Major Cause of Death)

InterventionTarget
StatinAll CKD ≥50yo; <50yo if CVD/diabetes/10yr risk >10%
AntiplateletSecondary prevention only (not primary)
BP Control<130/80 (KDIGO 2021)
SGLT2 InhibitorDAPA-CKD/EMPA-KIDNEY: eGFR ≥20, UACR ≥200 — ↓ ESRD/CV death 30–40%
FinerenoneT2DM + DKD on ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i, eGFR ≥25, K+ ≤4.8 — ↓ CV/renal events

2. Blood Pressure

AgentPreference
ACEi/ARB1st line (especially if A2/A3); max tolerated dose
Add-onThiazide (G1–G3), loop diuretic (G4–G5), CCB, β-blocker
Dual RAAS BlockadeCONTRAINDICATED (↑ hyperkalaemia, AKI)

3. CKD-Mineral and Bone Disorder (CKD-MBD)

ParameterTarget (G3a–G5)Treatment
Phosphate0.9–1.3 mmol/L (G3–G5)Dietary restriction, phosphate binders (Ca-based, sevelamer, lanthanum, sucroferric oxyhydroxide)
Calcium2.1–2.5 mmol/L (corrected)Avoid Ca overload; limit Ca-based binders if vascular calcification
PTH2–9× ULN (G3–G5)Vitamin D analogues (alfacalcidol, calcitriol) if PTH rising + low/normal Ca + normal/high PO4; Cinacalcet if PTH very high + high Ca/PO4
Vitamin D (25-OH)>30 ng/mLSupplement if deficient (cholecalciferol)
[!key]
CKD-MBD triad: ↑ PO4, ↓ Ca, ↑ PTH → vascular calcification + bone disease. KDIGO: PTH 2–9× ULN.

4. Anaemia of CKD

ParameterTarget / Action
Hb Target100–115 g/L (avoid >130 g/L — ↑ stroke/thrombosis risk)
IronTSAT ≥20%, Ferritin ≥100 ng/mL (or ≥200 if on ESA)
ESADarbepoetin/Epoetin alfa; start if Hb <100 g/L + iron replete; individualise
WorkupExclude B12/folate deficiency, blood loss, haemolysis, aluminium (historical)

5. Metabolic Acidosis

ThresholdTreatment
HCO₃⁻ <22 mmol/LOral sodium bicarbonate 500–1000 mg TDS (target HCO₃⁻ ≥22)
BenefitSlows CKD progression, improves muscle, bone, nutrition

6. Volume Management

StageApproach
G1–G3Salt restriction <5g/day; thiazide if needed
G4–G5Loop diuretic (furosemide/b bumetanide); ultrafiltration if on dialysis

7. Nutrition

ParameterRecommendation
Protein0.8 g/kg/day (G1–G4); 1.0–1.2 g/kg/day (G5 on dialysis)
Salt<2g Na/day (<5g salt)
K+Restrict if hyperkalaemic; low-K+ diet if on dialysis
Calories30–35 kcal/kg/day

8. Drug Dosing in CKD

PrincipleApplication
eGFR-based dosingUse Cockcroft-Gault (CrCl) for drug dosing, not CKD-EPI eGFR
Avoid nephrotoxinsNSAIDs, aminoglycosides, IV contrast (if possible), tick-dose adjustments
Common adjustmentsAntibiotics, DOACs, metformin, gabapentin, digoxin, allopurinol

7. Complications & Acute Management

ComplicationEmergency ThresholdManagement
HyperkalaemiaK+ >6.5 or ECG changesCa gluconate, insulin/dextrose, salbutamol, binder, dialysis
Fluid OverloadPulmonary oedemaIV loop diuretic, fluid restrict, dialysis if refractory
AcidosispH <7.2, HCO₃⁻ <12IV bicarbonate, dialysis
Uraemic PericarditisFriction rub, effusionDialysis (intensive)
Uraemic EncephalopathyConfusion, asterixis, seizuresDialysis

8. Preparation for RRT (G4–G5)

TimingAction
G4 (eGFR 15–29)Refer to nephrology; AV fistula creation (6mo maturation); transplant workup; patient education (modalities); conservative care discussion
G5 (eGFR <15)Start RRT (AEIOU); pre-emptive transplant if living donor; dialysis access ready

Vascular Access

TypePreferenceMaturation
AVF (Arteriovenous Fistula)Gold standard (radiocephalic > brachiocephalic)6–8 weeks
AVG (Arteriovenous Graft)If AVF not feasible2–3 weeks
CVC (Central Venous Catheter)Temporary only (high infection/stent risk)Immediate

9. Conservative (Non-Dialytic) Management

  • For frail elderly with multiple comorbidities, poor functional status
  • Focus: symptom control, volume, anaemia, acidosis, BP, advance care planning
  • Survival similar to dialysis in selected >75yo with high comorbidity

10. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- CKD = eGFR <60 or ACR ≥3 for ≥3 months
- KDIGO G1–G5 + A1–A3; heat map for prognosis
- Commonest cause: Diabetic kidney disease (~40%)
- BP <130/80; ACEi/ARB 1st line; NO dual RAAS blockade
- SGLT2i (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin): eGFR ≥20, UACR ≥200 — game changer
- Statin all CKD ≥50yo
- CKD-MBD: PO4 0.9–1.3, Ca 2.1–2.5, PTH 2–9× ULN
- Anaemia: Hb 100–115 g/L; ESA if <100 + iron replete
- Acidosis: HCO₃⁻ <22 → NaHCO₃
- G4: refer nephrology, AV fistula planning, transplant workup
- G5: RRT (AEIOU) or conservative care
- Drug dosing: use CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault), not eGFR
- Avoid NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
eGFR <60 once = CKDNeeds ≥3 months persistence
ACEi + ARB = better renoprotectionContraindicated (ONTARGET, VA NEPHRON)
Statin only if LDL highAll CKD ≥50yo (regardless of LDL)
Hb target >120 g/L100–115 g/L; >130 ↑ stroke/thrombosis (CHOIR, TREAT)
PTH target = normal2–9× ULN (suppressed PTH = adynamic bone)
SGLT2i contraindicated in CKDIndicated eGFR ≥20 (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY)
Ca-based binders always 1st lineLimit if vascular calcification/ hypercalcaemia; sevelamer/lanthanum alternatives
Dialysis = always better than conservativeConservative non-dialytic management valid for frail elderly
AVF = ready immediately6–8 weeks maturation; plan in G4
Drug dosing by eGFRUse CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault) for drug dosing

12. Mnemonics

  • CKD Definition: GFR <60 OR ACR ≥3 for ≥3M = GA-3M
  • KDIGO: GFR Albuminuria = GA matrix
  • Causes: Diabetic, Hypertensive, GN, Obstruction, ADPKD, Tubulointerstitial = DHGOAT
  • BP: <130/80 = 13080
  • ACEi/ARB: Dual Blockade Contraindicated = DBC
  • SGLT2i: CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY = CDE
  • Anaemia: Hb 100–115 = H100-115
  • CKD-MBD: PO4 0.9–1.3, Ca 2.1–2.5, PTH 2–9× = PCP
  • Acidosis: HCO3 <22 → NaHCO3 = H22
  • RRT Prep: G4 = AVF, Transplant, Education = GATE
  • Drug Dosing: CrCl Cockcroft-Gault = CCG

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Chronic Kidney Disease))
    Definition
      GFR[<60 for ≥3mo]
      Damage[ACR≥3, haematuria, structural]
    Staging_KDIGO
      G1_G5[G1:≥90, G2:60-89, G3a:45-59, G3b:30-44, G4:15-29, G5:<15]
      A1_A3[A1:<3, A2:3-30, A3:>30]
      HeatMap[Prognosis by G+A]
    Causes
      DKD[Diabetic 40%]
      HTN[Hypertensive]
      GN[Glomerulonephritis]
      Obstruction[BPH, stones]
      ADPKD[Autosomal dominant]
      Tubulointerstitial[Analgesics, lithium]
    Management
      CV[Statin, BP<130/80, SGLT2i, Finerenone]
      BP[ACEi/ARB 1st, NO dual]
      MBD[PO4, Ca, PTH, VitD]
      Anaemia[Hb 100-115, ESA, Iron]
      Acidosis[HCO3<22 → NaHCO3]
      Nutrition[Protein 0.8g/kg, Salt<2g]
      Drugs[CrCl dosing, avoid nephrotoxins]
    Complications
      K[Hyperkalaemia], Fluid[Overload], Acid[Acidosis], Uraemic[Pericarditis, Encephalopathy]
    RRT_Prep
      G4[Refer, AVF, Transplant workup]
      G5[AEIOU, Pre-emptive Tx]
      Access[AVF gold, AVG, CVC temp]
    Conservative[Frail elderly, symptom control]

14. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. CKD definition: eGFR <60 or ACR ≥3 for ≥3 months
  2. KDIGO staging: G1–G5 (GFR) + A1–A3 (ACR); heat map
  3. Commonest cause: Diabetic kidney disease (~40%)
  4. BP target <130/80; ACEi/ARB 1st line; NO dual RAAS blockade
  5. SGLT2i: eGFR ≥20, UACR ≥200 (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY)
  6. Statin: all CKD ≥50yo
  7. CKD-MBD: PO4 0.9–1.3, Ca 2.1–2.5, PTH 2–9× ULN
  8. Anaemia: Hb 100–115 g/L; ESA if <100 + iron replete
  9. Acidosis: HCO₃⁻ <22 → NaHCO₃
  10. G4: refer nephrology, AV fistula (6wk maturation), transplant workup
  11. G5: AEIOU for RRT; conservative option for frail
  12. Drug dosing: CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault), avoid nephrotoxins

15. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

16. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Definition, KDIGO staging, commonest causes, BP/ACEi/ARB/SGLT2i/statin, CKD-MBD targets, anaemia Hb target, acidosis treatment, G4/G5 referral & RRT prep, drug dosing principles
Should Know 🟡Heat map prognosis, finerenone, phosphate binder choices, vitamin D analogues vs cinacalcet, conservative management, AVF maturation, conservative vs dialysis in elderly
Nice to Know 🟢Novel therapies (endothelin, CCR2), genetic CKD (APOL1), cost-effectiveness, patient-reported outcomes, dialysis modality survival differences

17. MCQs (10)

  1. CKD definition requires persistence of eGFR <60 or ACR ≥3 for:

A. 1 month

B. ≥3 months

C. 6 months

D. 1 year

E. No time requirement

  1. KDIGO G3a eGFR range:

A. 60–89

B. 45–59

C. 30–44

D. 15–29

E. <15

  1. Commonest cause of CKD worldwide:

A. Hypertensive nephrosclerosis

B. Diabetic kidney disease

C. Glomerulonephritis

D. ADPKD

E. Obstructive uropathy

  1. KDIGO 2021 BP target for CKD:

A. <140/90

B. <130/80

C. <120/70

D. <150/90

E. <160/100

  1. Dual RAAS blockade (ACEi + ARB) in CKD:

A. Recommended for proteinuria

B. Contraindicated (↑ hyperkalaemia, AKI)

C. Only in diabetic CKD

D. Only in non-diabetic CKD

E. No evidence either way

  1. SGLT2 inhibitor indication in CKD (DAPA-CKD/EMPA-KIDNEY):

A. eGFR ≥60 only

B. eGFR ≥45 only

C. eGFR ≥30 only

D. eGFR ≥20, UACR ≥200

E. Only in diabetic CKD

  1. Target Hb for anaemia of CKD (KDIGO):

A. 90–105 g/L

B. 100–115 g/L

C. 110–125 g/L

D. 115–130 g/L

E. >130 g/L

  1. PTH target in CKD G3–G5 (KDIGO):

A. Normal range

B. 2–9× upper limit of normal

C. 10–20× ULN

C. <2× ULN

E. Not monitored

  1. Metabolic acidosis treatment threshold in CKD:

A. HCO₃⁻ <15 mmol/L

B. HCO₃⁻ <22 mmol/L

C. HCO₃⁻ <25 mmol/L

D. pH <7.35

E. Only if symptomatic

  1. Vascular access gold standard for haemodialysis:

A. CVC

B. AVG

C. AVF (radiocephalic)

D. Peritoneal catheter

E. Femoral catheter

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 65-year-old man, T2DM, eGFR 48, UACR 45 mg/mmol, BP 145/90 on amlodipine. Best next step:

A. Increase amlodipine

B. Add ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i (dapagliflozin)

C. Add ARB only

D. Add ACEi + ARB

E. Refer for dialysis

  1. 70-year-old woman, CKD G4 (eGFR 22), Hb 95, ferritin 80, TSAT 18%. Management:

A. Start ESA immediately

B. IV iron repletion first (target ferritin ≥100, TSAT ≥20%), then reassess Hb

C. Blood transfusion

D. B12/folate only

E. No treatment needed

  1. CKD G3b, corrected Ca 2.65, PO4 1.8, PTH 45 (ULN 65). Management:

A. Start calcitriol

B. Restrict dietary phosphate; avoid Ca-based binders; monitor

C. Start cinacalcet

D. Start alfacalcidol

E. Parathyroidectomy

  1. 55-year-old man, CKD G4 (eGFR 25) on metformin 1g BD. Action:

A. Continue full dose

B. Reduce to 500mg BD (caution 30–45; contraindicated <30)

C. Stop metformin

D. Switch to insulin

E. No change needed

  1. CKD G5 (eGFR 10), not on dialysis, K+ 6.8, ECG peaked T waves. Immediate:

A. Calcium resonium only

B. IV calcium gluconate + insulin/dextrose + salbutamol + urgent dialysis

C. Kayexalate only

D. Sodium bicarbonate only

E. Fluid restriction only

  1. CKD G4 patient preparation for RRT — AV fistula creation timing:

A. When eGFR <15

B. When eGFR 15–29 (G4) — 6–8 weeks maturation

C. Only when dialysis started

D. At eGFR <10

E. Never if transplant planned

  1. Conservative (non-dialytic) management appropriate for:

A. All CKD G5

B. Frail elderly >75 with high comorbidity, poor functional status

C. All diabetic CKD

D. Only if no AV fistula possible

E. Never appropriate

  1. Drug dosing in CKD — which formula to use:

A. CKD-EPI eGFR

B. Cockcroft-Gault CrCl

C. MDRD eGFR

D. BIS1 eGFR

E. Creatinine only

  1. Finerenone indication in DKD (FIDELIO/FIGARO):

A. 1st line instead of ACEi

B. Add-on to ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i if eGFR ≥25, K+ ≤4.8, persistent albuminuria

C. Only if eGFR <15

D. Replace SGLT2i

E. Only in Type 1 DM

  1. Hyperphosphataemia in CKD G4 — 1st line binder if vascular calcification present:

A. Calcium carbonate

B. Sevelamer / lanthanum / sucroferric oxyhydroxide (non-Ca-based)

C. Calcium acetate

D. Aluminium hydroxide

E. Magnesium carbonate

19. Flashcards

  • Q: CKD definition?

A: eGFR <60 or ACR ≥3 for ≥3 months

  • Q: KDIGO G categories?

A: G1 ≥90, G2 60–89, G3a 45–59, G3b 30–44, G4 15–29, G5 <15

  • Q: KDIGO A categories?

A: A1 <3, A2 3–30, A3 >30 mg/mmol

  • Q: Commonest CKD cause?

A: Diabetic kidney disease (~40%)

  • Q: BP target CKD?

A: <130/80

  • Q: ACEi/ARB dual blockade?

A: Contraindicated

  • Q: SGLT2i CKD indication?

A: eGFR ≥20, UACR ≥200 (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY)

  • Q: Statin in CKD?

A: All ≥50yo

  • Q: CKD-MBD targets?

A: PO4 0.9–1.3, Ca 2.1–2.5, PTH 2–9× ULN

  • Q: Anaemia Hb target?

A: 100–115 g/L

  • Q: Acidosis treatment?

A: HCO₃⁻ <22 → NaHCO₃

  • Q: G4 referral?

A: Nephrology, AV fistula (6–8wk maturation), transplant workup

  • Q: G5 RRT indications?

A: AEIOU (Acidosis, Electrolytes, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia)

  • Q: Drug dosing formula?

A: Cockcroft-Gault CrCl

  • Q: Finerenone?

A: Add-on to ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i if persistent albuminuria

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — CKD requires ≥3 months persistence
  2. B — G3a = 45–59 mL/min/1.73m²
  3. B — Diabetic kidney disease = commonest (~40%)
  4. B — KDIGO 2021: <130/80
  5. B — ONTARGET/VA NEPHRON: dual blockade ↑ hyperkalaemia, AKI
  6. D — DAPA-CKD (eGFR 25–75), EMPA-KIDNEY (eGFR 20–45 or 45–90 + UACR ≥200)
  7. B — KDIGO: Hb 100–115 g/L; >130 ↑ risk (CHOIR, TREAT)
  8. B — PTH 2–9× ULN (suppressed = adynamic bone)
  9. B — HCO₃⁻ <22 → NaHCO₃ (slows progression)
  10. C — AVF = gold standard (radiocephalic first choice)

SBAs

  1. B — DKD + albuminuria + HTN → ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i (cornerstone)
  2. B — Iron deficiency first (ferritin <100, TSAT <20%) before ESA
  3. B — High Ca + high PO4 + normal PTH → restrict PO4, avoid Ca binders
  4. B — Metformin: caution 30–45 (reduce dose), contraindicated <30
  5. B — K+ 6.8 + ECG changes = emergency dialysis + medical temporising
  6. B — AVF needs 6–8 weeks maturation; create in G4
  7. B — Conservative management valid for frail elderly with high comorbidity
  8. B — Drug dosing uses CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault), not eGFR
  9. B — Finerenone = add-on to ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i (FIDELIO/FIGARO)
  10. B — Non-Ca binders if vascular calcification/hypercalcaemia

21. Summary

Chronic Kidney Disease is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: CKD = eGFR <60 or ACR ≥3 for ≥3 months. KDIGO G1–G5 + A1–A3 heat map for prognosis. Commonest cause: diabetic kidney disease. BP <130/80; ACEi/ARB 1st line; NO dual blockade. SGLT2i (eGFR ≥20, UACR ≥200) game-changer. Statin all ≥50yo. CKD-MBD: PO4 0.9–1.3, Ca 2.1–2.5, PTH 2–9× ULN. Anaemia: Hb 100–115 g/L. Acidosis: HCO₃⁻ <22 → NaHCO₃. G4: refer, AV fistula (6wk maturation), transplant workup. G5: AEIOU for RRT. Drug dosing: CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault). Avoid nephrotoxins.

Exam focus: Definition, staging, diabetic kidney disease, BP/ACEi/ARB/SGLT2i/statin, CKD-MBD targets, anaemia, acidosis, G4/G5 referral, drug dosing, conservative management.

Clinical relevance: Early SGLT2i + ACEi/ARB dramatically slows progression; comprehensive multifactorial management reduces CV events (major cause of death in CKD).

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3 18.3 14
▸ 18.3.1 Glomerular Diseases Overview Classification
Topic: Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — IgA Nephropathy (Berger's Disease)]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Membranous Nephropathy]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — FSGS (Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis)]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Minimal Change Disease]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Lupus Nephritis]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — ANCA-Associated Vasculitis (GPA, MPA, EGPA)]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Diabetic Nephropathy]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Amyloidosis]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Glomerular diseases = disorders primarily affecting the glomerulus. Classification: Primary (idiopathic) vs Secondary (systemic). Clinical syndromes: Nephrotic, Nephritic, Asymptomatic urinary abnormalities, Rapidly Progressive GN (RPGN), Chronic GN. Diagnosis: Clinical, serology, renal biopsy (LM, IF, EM). Management: Syndrome-directed + disease-specific.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify glomerular diseases by aetiology (primary vs secondary) and clinical syndrome
  • Recognise histopathological patterns (LM, IF, EM) and their diagnostic significance
  • Apply clinical syndromes (nephrotic, nephritic, RPGN, asymptomatic) to differential diagnosis
  • Interpret biopsy findings and correlate with pathogenesis
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP clinical scenarios

2. Classification

By Aetiology

CategoryExamples
Primary (Idiopathic)IgA Nephropathy, Membranous Nephropathy, FSGS, Minimal Change Disease, C3 Glomerulopathy, Anti-GBM Disease
Secondary (Systemic)Lupus Nephritis, ANCA Vasculitis, Diabetic Nephropathy, Amyloidosis, Infection-related (Post-strep, IE, HIV), Cryoglobulinaemia, Malignancy-associated, Drug-induced

By Clinical Syndrome

SyndromeKey FeaturesTypical Causes
Nephrotic SyndromeProteinuria >3.5g/day, Hypoalbuminaemia <30g/L, Oedema, HyperlipidaemiaMCD, MN, FSGS, Diabetic, Amyloid, SLE Class V
Nephritic SyndromeHaematuria, RBC casts, Hypertension, Subnephrotic proteinuria (<3.5g), Oliguria/AKIPost-strep, IgA, Lupus III/IV, Anti-GBM, ANCA, Cryoglobulin
Rapidly Progressive GN (RPGN)Rapid AKI (days-weeks), Haematuria, RBC casts, Crescents >50%Anti-GBM, ANCA, Lupus III/IV, Cryoglobulin, Anti-GBM
Asymptomatic Urinary AbnormalitiesIsolated haematuria/proteinuria, normal renal functionIgA, Thin basement membrane, Alport, Early diabetic, Carrier states
Chronic GNProgressive CKD, hypertension, proteinuria, small kidneysFSGS, IgA, Chronic lupus, Alport, Chronic post-infectious

3. Histopathological Evaluation (Renal Biopsy)

Light Microscopy (LM) Patterns

PatternDescriptionAssociated Diseases
Normal / Minimal ChangeNo Light Microscopy changes; foot process effacement on EMMCD, Early FSGS, Early Diabetic
Mesangial ProliferationIncreased mesangial cells/matrixIgA Nephropathy, Lupus Class II, HSP
Endocapillary ProliferationHypercellularity within capillary loops (inflammatory cells)Post-strep, Lupus Class III/IV, Infection-related, Cryoglobulin
MembranousDiffuse GBM thickening ("spikes" on Jones/PAS)Membranous Nephropathy, Lupus Class V, Secondary MN
Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS)Segmental sclerosis + hyalinosis in some glomeruliPrimary FSGS, Adaptive (obesity, reflux), Viral (HIV), Genetic
CrescenticCellular crescents in Bowman's space (>50% = RPGN)Anti-GBM, ANCA, Lupus III/IV, Cryoglobulin, Anti-GBM
Membranoproliferative (MPGN)Mesangial expansion + GBM double contours ("tram-track")C3 Glomerulopathy, Cryoglobulin, Chronic infection, Lupus
SclerosingGlobal glomerulosclerosis >90%Advanced CKD (Class VI Lupus, End-stage any cause)

Immunofluorescence (IF) Patterns

PatternDepositsAssociated Diseases
NegativeNo immune depositsMCD, FSGS (non-specific IgM/C3 in scars), ATN, TMA
Granular (Immune Complex)Discrete granular deposits along capillary walls/mesangiumPost-strep, Lupus (full-house), IgA (mesangial IgA), MN (capillary wall IgG), Infection-related
LinearContinuous linear staining along GBMAnti-GBM Disease, Goodpasture's
"Full-House"IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q (all positive)Lupus Nephritis (pathognomonic)
Pauci-immuneScant/no immunoglobulinsANCA Vasculitis, TMA
C3 DominantC3 > immunoglobulinsC3 Glomerulopathy (DDD, C3GN), Post-infectious (acute)

Electron Microscopy (EM) Findings

FindingLocationAssociated Diseases
Foot Process Effacement (FPE)PodocytesMCD (diffuse), FSGS (segmental), MN, early diabetic
Subepithelial Deposits ("Humps")Epithelial side of GBMPost-strep, Membranous, Lupus Class V
Subendothelial DepositsEndothelial side of GBMLupus III/IV, MPGN, Cryoglobulin, Infection-related
Mesangial DepositsMesangiumIgA Nephropathy, Lupus I/II, HSP
Intramembranous (Dense Deposit Disease)Within GBMDDD (C3 Glomerulopathy)
Tubuloreticular InclusionsEndothelial cytoplasmLupus, Interferon exposure, Viral infections
Fibrils (7–10 nm, non-branching)Random orientationAmyloidosis (Congo red +)
Microtubules (30 nm, parallel)Cryoglobulinaemia (Type I)

4. Major Primary Glomerulonephritides

DiseaseIncidenceLMIFEMClinical Syndrome
IgA NephropathyCommonest worldwideMesangial hypercellularityMesangial IgA dominantMesangial depositsNephritic, Asymptomatic, Nephrotic (rare)
Membranous NephropathyCommonest adult nephrotic (non-diabetic)Diffuse GBM thickening + spikesCapillary wall IgG (IgG4)Subepithelial depositsNephrotic
FSGSCommonest primary glomerular ESRDSegmental sclerosis + hyalinosisNon-specific (IgM/C3 in scars)Diffuse FPE >80%Nephrotic, Nephritic, Asymptomatic
Minimal Change DiseaseCommonest childhood nephroticNormalNegativeDiffuse FPENephrotic
C3 GlomerulopathyRareMPGN pattern (DDD/C3GN)C3 dominantIntramembranous (DDD) / Subendothelial (C3GN)Nephritic, Nephrotic, Asymptomatic
Anti-GBM DiseaseRareCrescentic GNLinear GBM IgGNo depositsRPGN + Pulmonary haemorrhage (Goodpasture's)

5. Major Secondary Glomerulonephritides

DiseaseMechanismLMIFEMKey Features
Lupus NephritisImmune complexVariable (I–VI)Full-house (IgG,IgM,IgA,C3,C1q)Subendothelial (III/IV), Subepithelial (V)Class I–VI guides treatment
ANCA VasculitisANCA-mediatedNecrotising crescenticPauci-immuneNo depositsc-ANCA/PR3 (GPA), p-ANCA/MPO (MPA/EGPA)
Diabetic NephropathyMetabolic + haemodynamicGBM thickening, K-W nodules, arteriolar hyalinosisNon-specific (linear IgG from non-enzymatic glycosylation)GBM thickeningProteinuria, K-W nodules
AmyloidosisMisfolded protein fibrilsCongo red + apple-greenVariable (κ/λ/SAA)Non-branching fibrils 7–10 nmNephrotic, normal/large kidneys
Post-Strep GNImmune complex (streptococcal)Diffuse endocapillary proliferationGranular IgG, C3 (capillary wall)Subepithelial "humps"Children, post-strep 1–3 weeks
Infection-Related (IE, HIV, etc.)Immune complexVariable (MPGN, crescentic)Granular (IgG, C3)Subendothelial/subepithelialActive infection context
Cryoglobulinaemic GNCryoglobulin precipitationMPGN patternIgM, IgG, C3 (cryoglobulins)Subendothelial + microtubulesHCV 80–90%, low C4
Drug-InducedHypersensitivity / ToxicityVariable (AIN, MN, GN)VariableVariableNSAIDs, antibiotics, gold, penicillamine, checkpoint inhibitors

6. Clinical Syndromes — Differential Diagnosis

Nephrotic Syndrome

Age GroupCommonestOther Causes
ChildrenMCD (70–90%)FSGS, MN, Congenital
AdultsMN (non-diabetic), FSGS, Diabetic, AmyloidMCD, SLE, MN secondary

Workup: Serology (ANA, ANCA, complement, hepatitis, SPEP, sFLC), Biopsy (essential in adults, selective in children if atypical)

Nephritic Syndrome

PresentationKey Differential
Acute (Children)Post-strep GN (ASO↑, C3↓, humps), IgA, HSP
Acute (Adults)IgA, Lupus III/IV, Anti-GBM, ANCA, Cryoglobulin, Infection-related
RPGNAnti-GBM, ANCA, Lupus III/IV, Cryoglobulin, Anti-GBM

Asymptomatic Haematuria/Proteinuria

FindingDifferential
Isolated Microscopic HaematuriaIgA, Thin basement membrane, Alport, Carrier (X-linked), Stones, Tumour, Exercise
Isolated ProteinuriaOrthostatic, Early diabetic, Early hypertensive, MCD (uncommon), Carrier
Combined Haematuria + ProteinuriaIgA, Thin BM + proteinuria, Early GN, Alport

7. Renal Biopsy — Indications & Interpretation

Indications (Adults)

IndicationExamples
Nephrotic SyndromeAll adults (essential)
Nephritic SyndromeUnexplained, RPGN, systemic features
AKIUnexplained, no response to volume, systemic disease suspected
CKDUnexplained cause, proteinuria >1g, haematuria + proteinuria
Systemic DiseaseSLE, Vasculitis, Amyloid, Myeloma, Diabetes (if atypical)
TransplantDysfunction (rejection, recurrence, CNI toxicity)

Contraindications

AbsoluteRelative
Uncorrected bleeding diathesisSingle kidney (unless transplant)
Uncontrolled hypertensionSmall kidneys (<9cm)
Active infectionUncooperative patient
Hydronephrosis (unrelieved)Morbid obesity

Biopsy Report Components

ComponentWhat to Look For
LMPattern (normal, mesangial, endocapillary, membranous, FSGS, crescentic, MPGN, sclerosing)
IFPattern (negative, granular, linear, full-house, pauci-immune, C3 dominant); Intensity (0–3+)
EMDeposits (location: subepithelial, subendothelial, mesangial, intramembranous); FPE; Fibrils; Microtubules
Activity/ChronicityActivity Index (endocapillary, neutrophils, crescents, wire loops); Chronicity Index (sclerosis, atrophy, fibrosis)
DiagnosisPrimary vs Secondary; Specific disease entity; Grade/Class if applicable

8. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Glomerular diseases = primary (idiopathic) vs secondary (systemic)
- Clinical syndromes: Nephrotic (>3.5g protein), Nephritic (haematuria + RBC casts), RPGN (crescents >50%), Asymptomatic, Chronic
- Biopsy = LM + IF + EM — essential for definitive diagnosis in adults
- LM Patterns: Normal (MCD), Mesangial (IgA), Endocapillary (Post-strep, Lupus), Membranous (MN), FSGS, Crescentic (RPGN), MPGN
- IF Patterns: Negative (MCD, FSGS), Granular (immune complex), Linear (Anti-GBM), Full-house (Lupus), Pauci-immune (ANCA), C3 dominant (C3G)
- EM: FPE (MCD, FSGS), Subepithelial humps (Post-strep, MN), Subendothelial (Lupus, MPGN), Mesangial (IgA), Fibrils (Amyloid), Microtubules (Cryoglobulin)
- Primary GN: IgA (mesangial IgA), MN (subepithelial, IgG4), FSGS (segmental sclerosis + diffuse FPE), MCD (normal LM, negative IF, diffuse FPE)
- Secondary GN: Lupus (full-house IF, Class I–VI), ANCA (pauci-immune crescentic), Diabetic (K-W nodules), Amyloid (fibrils), Post-strep (humps), Cryoglobulin (microtubules)
- Nephrotic: MCD (children), MN (adults), FSGS, Diabetic, Amyloid
- Nephritic: Post-strep (children), IgA, Lupus, Anti-GBM, ANCA
- RPGN: Anti-GBM (linear), ANCA (pauci-immune), Lupus, Cryoglobulin
- Biopsy: Indicated for nephrotic (adults), unexplained nephritic/AKI/CKD, systemic disease

9. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All nephrotic = biopsy in childrenChildren: MCD commonest → trial steroids first; biopsy if steroid-resistant/atypical
IF negative = only MCDFSGS also IF negative (non-specific IgM/C3 in scars)
Linear IF = only Anti-GBMAlso diabetic (non-enzymatic glycosylation), Fibrillary GN
Granular IF = all immune complexPattern matters: Capillary wall (MN), Mesangial (IgA), Full-house (Lupus)
C3 dominant = only C3GAlso acute post-infectious (transient), dense deposit disease
MCD = only childrenAdults 10–15% of nephrotic
FSGS = only primaryAdaptive (obesity, reflux), Viral (HIV), Genetic
All RPGN = ANCAAnti-GBM (linear IF), Lupus, Cryoglobulin also RPGN
Biopsy always needed for proteinuriaOrthostatic proteinuria, isolated microalbuminuria in diabetes — clinical diagnosis
IF negative = no immune depositsNon-specific trapping of IgM/C3 in sclerosed areas (FSGS)

10. Mnemonics

  • Glomerular Syndromes: Nephrotic, Nephritic, RPGN, Asymptomatic, Chronic = NNRAC
  • LM Patterns: Normal (MCD), Mesangial (IgA), Endocapillary (Post-strep/Lupus), Membranous (MN), FSGS, Crescentic (RPGN), MPGN, Sclerotic = NME-MFC-MS
  • IF Patterns: Negative (MCD/FSGS), Granular (Immune complex), Linear (Anti-GBM), Full-house (Lupus), Pauci-immune (ANCA), C3-dominant (C3G) = NGL-FPC
  • EM Deposits: Subepithelial (Humps: Post-strep, MN), Subendothelial (Lupus, MPGN), Mesangial (IgA), Intramembranous (DDD), Fibrils (Amyloid), Microtubules (Cryoglobulin) = SSMIFM
  • Primary GN: IgA, MN, FSGS, MCD = IMFM
  • Secondary GN: Lupus, ANCA, Diabetic, Amyloid, Post-strep, Cryoglobulin, Drug = LADAPCD
  • Biopsy: LM + IF + EM = LIE (essential for diagnosis)
  • Nephrotic Causes (Adults): MN, FSGS, Diabetic, Amyloid, MCD = MFDAM
  • Nephritic Causes: Post-strep, IgA, Lupus, Anti-GBM, ANCA = PILAA

11. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Glomerular Diseases))
    Classification
      Primary[IgA, MN, FSGS, MCD, C3G, Anti-GBM]
      Secondary[Lupus, ANCA, Diabetic, Amyloid, Post-strep, Cryoglobulin, Drug]
    Clinical_Syndromes
      Nephrotic[Protein>3.5, Alb<30, Oedema, Lipids]
      Nephritic[Haematuria+RBC casts, HT, Protein<3.5]
      RPGN[Crescents>50%, Rapid AKI]
      Asymptomatic[Isolated Haematuria/Proteinuria]
      Chronic[Progressive CKD, Small kidneys]
    Histopathology
      LM[Normal, Mesangial, Endocapillary, Membranous, FSGS, Crescentic, MPGN, Sclerotic]
      IF[Negative, Granular, Linear, Full-house, Pauci-immune, C3-dominant]
      EM[Subepithelial humps, Subendothelial, Mesangial, Intramembranous, FPE, Fibrils, Microtubules]
    Primary_GN
      IgA[Mesangial IgA dominant]
      MN[Subepithelial, IgG4, Capillary wall]
      FSGS[Segmental sclerosis, Diffuse FPE>80%]
      MCD[Normal LM, Neg IF, Diffuse FPE]
      C3G[C3 dominant, MPGN pattern]
      Anti-GBM[Linear GBM IgG, Crescentic]
    Secondary_GN
      Lupus[Full-house IF, Class I-VI]
      ANCA[Pauci-immune, Crescentic]
      Diabetic[K-W nodules, GBM thickening]
      Amyloid[Congo red + Fibrils 7-10nm]
      Post-Strep[Humps, Subepithelial]
      Cryoglobulin[Microtubules, Low C4]
      Drug[NSAIDs, Abx, PPIs, ICIs]
    Biopsy
      Indications[Nephrotic adults, Unexplained Nephritic/AKI/CKD, Systemic]
      Components[LM + IF + EM = Diagnosis]
      Contradictions[Bleeding, Uncontrolled HTN, Single kidney]

12. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Glomerular diseases: Primary vs Secondary
  2. Clinical syndromes: Nephrotic, Nephritic, RPGN, Asymptomatic, Chronic
  3. LM patterns: Normal (MCD), Mesangial (IgA), Endocapillary (Post-strep/Lupus), Membranous (MN), FSGS, Crescentic (RPGN), MPGN
  4. IF patterns: Negative (MCD/FSGS), Granular (immune complex), Linear (Anti-GBM), Full-house (Lupus), Pauci-immune (ANCA), C3-dominant (C3G)
  5. EM deposits: Subepithelial humps (Post-strep, MN), Subendothelial (Lupus, MPGN), Mesangial (IgA), Intramembranous (DDD), FPE (MCD, FSGS), Fibrils (Amyloid)
  6. Primary GN: IgA (mesangial IgA), MN (subepithelial IgG4), FSGS (segmental sclerosis + FPE>80%), MCD (normal LM, neg IF, FPE)
  7. Secondary GN: Lupus (full-house, Class I–VI), ANCA (pauci-immune crescentic), Diabetic (K-W nodules), Amyloid (fibrils), Post-strep (humps), Cryoglobulin (microtubules, low C4)
  8. Nephrotic adult causes: MN, FSGS, Diabetic, Amyloid, MCD
  9. RPGN causes: Anti-GBM (linear), ANCA (pauci-immune), Lupus, Cryoglobulin
  10. Biopsy: LM + IF + EM = diagnosis; indicated for nephrotic adults, unexplained nephritic/AKI/CKD, systemic disease

13. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

14. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Classification (primary/secondary, clinical syndromes), LM/IF/EM patterns, primary GN features (IgA, MN, FSGS, MCD), secondary GN features (Lupus, ANCA, Diabetic, Amyloid, Post-strep), clinical syndromes differential, biopsy indications + components
Should Know 🟡C3 glomerulopathy, Anti-GBM disease, Infection-related GN (IE, HIV), Drug-induced GN, Cryoglobulinaemic GN, Alport/thin basement membrane, Orthostatic proteinuria, Biopsy pitfalls (sampling, processing)
Nice to Know 🟢Genetic GN (COL4A3/4/5, NPHS1/2, CFHR5), Novel biomarkers (Gd-IgA1, anti-PLA2R, C3 nephritic factor), Biopsy digital pathology, AI-assisted diagnosis, Precision medicine approaches

15. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest primary glomerular disease worldwide:

A. Membranous nephropathy

B. IgA nephropathy

C. FSGS

D. Minimal change disease

E. Anti-GBM disease

  1. Light microscopy in Minimal Change Disease:

A. Mesangial hypercellularity

B. Normal

C. Diffuse GBM thickening

C. Segmental sclerosis

E. Crescents

  1. Immunofluorescence in IgA nephropathy:

A. Linear GBM IgG

B. Dominant mesangial IgA

C. Granular capillary wall IgG

D. Full-house (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q)

E. C3 dominant

  1. Electron microscopy in Membranous Nephropathy:

A. Mesangial deposits

B. Subendothelial deposits

C. Subepithelial deposits

D. Intramembranous deposits

E. Tubuloreticular inclusions

  1. "Full-house" immunofluorescence is characteristic of:

A. IgA nephropathy

B. Lupus nephritis

C. Membranous nephropathy

D. Anti-GBM disease

E. ANCA vasculitis

  1. Pauci-immune immunofluorescence is seen in:

A. Lupus nephritis

B. ANCA-associated vasculitis

C. Post-streptococcal GN

D. Membranous nephropathy

E. IgA nephropathy

  1. Subepithelial "humps" on EM are characteristic of:

A. IgA nephropathy

B. FSGS

C. Post-streptococcal GN

D. Minimal change disease

E. Amyloidosis

  1. Lupus nephritis Class IV is defined as:

A. Minimal mesangial

B. Mesangial proliferative

C. Focal proliferative (<50%)

D. Diffuse proliferative (≥50%)

E. Membranous

  1. Anti-GBM disease — immunofluorescence pattern:

A. Granular capillary wall

B. Mesangial IgA

C. Linear GBM IgG

D. Full-house

E. C3 dominant

  1. Commonest cause of nephrotic syndrome in children:

A. FSGS

B. Membranous nephropathy

C. Minimal Change Disease

D. IgA nephropathy

E. Amyloidosis

  1. RPGN — crescentic GN with linear GBM IgG:

A. ANCA vasculitis

B. Anti-GBM disease

C. Lupus nephritis Class IV

D. Post-streptococcal GN

E. IgA nephropathy

16. SBA Questions (10)
