  1. 22-year-old man, macroscopic haematuria 1 day after sore throat. Biopsy: mesangial hypercellularity, dominant IgA on IF. Diagnosis:

A. Post-streptococcal GN

B. IgA nephropathy

C. Alport syndrome

D. Thin basement membrane disease

E. C3 glomerulopathy

  1. 55-year-old man, nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria 6g/day, albumin 18g/L). Biopsy: diffuse GBM thickening, spikes, subepithelial deposits, granular IgG4 on IF. Anti-PLA2R+. Management:

A. Supportive only

B. Rituximab 375mg/m² weekly × 4

C. Ponticelli regimen

D. Tacrolimus + prednisone

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 4-year-old boy, acute onset periorbital oedema, proteinuria 50mg/mmol (albumin selective), normal BP, normal Cr. Most likely diagnosis:

A. FSGS

B. Minimal Change Disease

C. Membranous nephropathy

D. Post-streptococcal GN

E. IgA nephropathy

  1. 35-year-old man, rapidly progressive GN (Cr 350, crescents 60% on biopsy), dominant mesangial IgA. Induction:

A. ACEi/ARB

B. Oral prednisolone 1mg/kg alone

C. Pulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3d + cyclophosphamide/rituximab

D. MMF + steroids

E. Plasma exchange alone

  1. 60-year-old woman, nephrotic syndrome. Biopsy: membranous pattern. Anti-PLA2R negative. Next step:

A. Diagnose primary MN

B. Screen for secondary causes (ANA, hepatitis, malignancy)

C. Start rituximab

D. Ponticelli regimen

E. Conservative only

  1. 25-year-old woman, palpable purpura on legs, abdominal pain, arthritis, microscopic haematuria. Renal biopsy: dominant mesangial IgA. Diagnosis:

A. IgA nephropathy

B. Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP) nephritis

C. Post-streptococcal GN

D. Alport syndrome

E. Lupus nephritis

  1. 60-year-old man, post-CT coronary angiogram. Day 3 Cr 180 (baseline 100). Prevention was:

A. NAC alone

B. IV saline 1 mL/kg/h 12h pre + 12h post

C. Sodium bicarbonate only

D. NAC + bicarbonate

E. Furosemide

  1. Anti-GBM disease vs ANCA vasculitis — distinguishing feature on IF:

A. Both pauci-immune

B. Anti-GBM: linear GBM IgG; ANCA: pauci-immune

C. Anti-GBM: granular IgG; ANCA: linear

D. Both: full-house

E. No difference

  1. Post-streptococcal GN distinguishing features:

A. ASO titre elevated, C3 low, subepithelial humps on EM

B. Dominant mesangial IgA

C. Normal C3

D. c-ANCA positive

E. Linear GBM staining

  1. C3 Glomerulopathy — immunofluorescence:

A. Linear GBM IgG

B. Granular capillary wall IgG

C. Mesangial IgA

D. C3 dominant (C3 > immunoglobulins)

E. Full-house

17. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest primary GN worldwide?

A: IgA Nephropathy

  • Q: MCD light microscopy?

A: Normal

  • Q: IgA nephropathy IF?

A: Dominant mesangial IgA

  • Q: MN EM?

A: Subepithelial deposits

  • Q: Lupus full-house IF?

A: IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q

  • Q: ANCA vasculitis IF?

A: Pauci-immune

  • Q: Post-strep EM?

A: Subepithelial humps

  • Q: Lupus Class IV?

A: Diffuse proliferative ≥50%

  • Q: Anti-GBM IF?

A: Linear GBM IgG

  • Q: Commonest childhood nephrotic?

A: MCD (70–90%)

  • Q: RPGN linear IF?

A: Anti-GBM disease

  • Q: RPGN pauci-immune?

A: ANCA vasculitis

  • Q: Lupus Class III vs IV?

A: III = focal <50%; IV = diffuse ≥50%

  • Q: MCD LM?

A: Normal

  • Q: IgA dominant mesangial?

A: IgA nephropathy

  • Q: MN subepithelial deposits?

A: Membranous Nephropathy

  • Q: FSGS LM?

A: Segmental sclerosis

  • Q: C3G IF?

A: C3 dominant

  • Q: Post-strep EM?

A: Subepithelial humps

  • Q: Lupus Class IV?

A: Diffuse proliferative ≥50%

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — IgA Nephropathy = commonest primary GN globally
  2. B — MCD = normal light microscopy
  3. B — Dominant mesangial IgA = pathognomonic for IgA nephropathy
  4. C — Subepithelial deposits = hallmark of Membranous Nephropathy
  5. B — Full-house = IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q = Lupus nephritis
  6. B — Pauci-immune = scant/no immunoglobulins = ANCA vasculitis
  7. C — Subepithelial "humps" = Post-streptococcal GN
  8. D — Class IV = diffuse proliferative ≥50% glomeruli
  9. C — Linear GBM IgG = Anti-GBM disease
  10. C — MCD = commonest childhood nephrotic (70–90%)

SBAs

  1. B — Young adult + post-URTI haematuria + mesangial IgA = IgA nephropathy
  2. B — Primary MN, high-risk (proteinuria 6g, albumin 18) → Rituximab first-line
  3. B — Child + selective proteinuria + normal BP/Cr = MCD
  4. C — Severe AKI + crescents 60% + IgA = RPGN IgA → pulse steroids + CYC/rituximab
  5. B — PLA2R-negative MN → screen for secondary causes (malignancy, SLE, infections)
  6. B — HSP = systemic vasculitis (palpable purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis) + IgA nephritis
  7. B — Contrast prevention: IV saline 1 mL/kg/h 12h pre/post
  8. B — Anti-GBM = linear GBM IgG; ANCA = pauci-immune
  9. A — Post-strep: ASO↑, C3↓, subepithelial humps, IgG dominant
  10. D — C3G = C3 dominant (C3 > immunoglobulins)

19. Summary

Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Primary vs Secondary. Clinical syndromes: Nephrotic (>3.5g protein), Nephritic (haematuria + RBC casts), RPGN (crescents >50%), Asymptomatic, Chronic. Biopsy = LM + IF + EM. LM: Normal (MCD), Mesangial (IgA), Endocapillary (Post-strep/Lupus), Membranous (MN), FSGS, Crescentic (RPGN), MPGN. IF: Negative (MCD/FSGS), Granular (immune complex), Linear (Anti-GBM), Full-house (Lupus), Pauci-immune (ANCA), C3-dominant (C3G). EM: Subepithelial humps (Post-strep, MN), Subendothelial (Lupus, MPGN), Mesangial (IgA), Intramembranous (DDD), FPE (MCD, FSGS), Fibrils (Amyloid), Microtubules (Cryoglobulin). Primary: IgA (mesangial IgA), MN (subepithelial IgG4), FSGS (segmental + FPE>80%), MCD (normal LM, neg IF, FPE). Secondary: Lupus (full-house, Class I–VI), ANCA (pauci-immune crescentic), Diabetic (K-W nodules), Amyloid (fibrils), Post-strep (humps), Cryoglobulin (microtubules, low C4). Nephrotic: MCD (children), MN (adults), FSGS, Diabetic, Amyloid. Nephritic: Post-strep, IgA, Lupus, Anti-GBM, ANCA. RPGN: Anti-GBM (linear), ANCA (pauci-immune), Lupus, Cryoglobulin. Biopsy: Indicated for nephrotic adults, unexplained nephritic/AKI/CKD, systemic disease.

Exam focus: Classification, LM/IF/EM patterns, primary vs secondary GN features, clinical syndromes, biopsy indications, RPGN differential.

Clinical relevance: Accurate histopathological classification directs disease-specific therapy and prognosis.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.10 Mpgn C3 Glomerulopathy
Topic: MPGN and C3 Glomerulopathy (DDD, C3GN) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Lupus Nephritis]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — ANCA-Associated Vasculitis (GPA, MPA, EGPA)]], [[Cryoglobulinaemic Glomerulonephritis]], [[Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
MPGN = Mesangiocapillary GN. "Tram-track" GBM double contours on LM. Reclassified by IF: Immune-complex MPGN (IC-MPGN) vs C3 Glomerulopathy (C3G). C3G = C3 dominant (no Ig) → DDD (intramembranous dense deposits) or C3GN (subendothelial/mesangial). Driven by alternative complement pathway dysregulation (C3NeF, CFH/CFI/C3/CFB mutations).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify MPGN by immunofluorescence (IC-MPGN vs C3G)
  • Differentiate DDD vs C3GN on EM
  • Identify underlying causes (infection, autoimmune, monoclonal gammopathy, complement mutations)
  • Apply complement studies (C3, C4, C3NeF, CFH, CFI, C3, CFB genetics)
  • Manage based on aetiology (immunosuppression, complement inhibition, plasma exchange)
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP vignettes

2. Classification (Modern — by IF)

CategoryIF PatternLight MicroscopyElectron MicroscopyKey Drivers
IC-MPGNIgG/IgM + C3 (granular)MPGN patternSubendothelial ± mesangial depositsImmune complexes (infections, autoimmune, MGUS/myeloma)
C3 Glomerulopathy (C3G)C3 dominant (≥2+ > Ig)MPGN patternDDD: Intramembranous dense deposits (osmiophilic ribbon); C3GN: Subendothelial/mesangial depositsAlternative pathway dysregulation (C3NeF, CFH/CFI/C3/CFB mutations)
Old classification (I, II, III) obsolete. Use IF-based classification (IC-MPGN vs C3G).

3. Immune-Complex MPGN (IC-MPGN)

Aetiology

CauseMechanismTypical Features
Chronic infectionsImmune complex depositionHCV (cryoglobulinaemic), HBV, Endocarditis (IE), Shunt nephritis, Malaria, Schistosomiasis
AutoimmuneIC depositionSLE (full-house IF), Cryoglobulinaemia, Sjögren's, IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein)
Monoclonal gammopathyMonoclonal ICMGUS, Myeloma, Lymphoproliferative — check serum/urine immunofixation
Drug-inducedHapten/ICInterferon-α, gemcitabine, heroin

Clinical Presentation

  • Nephritic syndrome (haematuria, RBC casts, hypertension, AKI) — most common
  • Nephrotic syndrome (20–30%)
  • Mixed nephrotic-nephritic
  • Asymptomatic proteinuria/haematuria
  • Low C3, normal C4 (alternative pathway) — except cryoglobulinaemia (low C3 + low C4)

4. C3 Glomerulopathy (C3G)

Pathogenesis

FactorRole
C3 Nephritic Factor (C3NeF)Autoantibody stabilising C3 convertase (C3bBb) → uncontrolled C3 cleavage — 80% DDD, 50% C3GN
CFH mutation/autoantibodyLoss of C3b regulation → ↑ alternative pathway
CFI mutationImpaired C3b inactivation
C3 mutationGain-of-function → resistant to inactivation
CFB mutationGain-of-function → ↑ C3 convertase formation
Thrombomodulin (THBD) mutationImpaired C3b inactivation on endothelium
C3NeF = IgG autoantibody binding C3bBb → prevents decay → persistent C3 activation.

Dense Deposit Disease (DDD)

FeatureDetail
AgeChildren/young adults (5–30yr)
PresentationNephritic (50%), Nephrotic (30%), Mixed
EMIntramembranous dense deposits (highly osmiophilic, "ribbon-like" transformation of GBM)
IFC3 dominant (linear/granular capillary wall), no significant Ig
Serum C3Low (consumed), C4 normal
C3NeF80–90% positive
AssociationsDrusen (retinal, ↑ AMD risk), Partial lipodystrophy (acquired, face/arms)
PrognosisPoor — 50% ESRD at 10yr; recurrence post-transplant >50%

C3 Glomerulonephritis (C3GN)

FeatureDetail
AgeBroader (children → elderly)
PresentationVariable (nephritic, nephrotic, asymptomatic)
EMSubendothelial &/or mesangial deposits (NOT intramembranous dense deposits)
IFC3 dominant (mesangial + capillary wall), no Ig
Serum C3Low (60–70%), C4 normal
C3NeF40–50% positive
AssociationsMonoclonal gammopathy (screen!), CFH/CFI/C3/CFB mutations
PrognosisVariable — better than DDD but still significant ESRD risk

5. Complement Workup (Essential)

TestInterpretation
C3Low in active disease (both IC-MPGN & C3G)
C4Normal (alternative pathway) — low if classical pathway (SLE, cryoglobulinaemia)
C3NeFPositive in ~80% DDD, 50% C3G
CFH, CFI, C3, CFB levelsLow if consumption; genetic testing if familial
CFH autoantibodyCauses acquired CFH deficiency
Serum/urine immunofixationMandatory — exclude monoclonal gammopathy (MGUS/myeloma)
Genetic panelCFH, CFI, CFB, C3, THBD, DGKE (if familial/early onset)

6. Management

IC-MPGN

AetiologyTreatment
HCV-associatedDAA therapy (cure HCV → GN remission) ± Rituximab if cryoglobulinaemic
HBVAntivirals (entecavir/tenofovir)
IE/EndocarditisAntibiotics (+ valve surgery if needed)
SLEStandard lupus nephritis protocol (MMF/CyC + steroids)
Monoclonal gammopathyTreat underlying clone (bortezomib/dex ± rituximab ± daratumumab)
IdiopathicMMF + steroids (KDIGO: 6mo MMF + steroids); consider CNI if refractory

C3 Glomerulopathy

ApproachDetails
SupportiveACEi/ARB (↓ proteinuria, ↓ BP), SGLT2i, BP control <130/80, statin
ImmunosuppressionMMF + steroids (some benefit); CNI (tacrolimus) — evidence limited
Eculizumab (anti-C5)Case reports/series — benefit in C3NeF+ with progressive CKD; not standard
Iptacopan (factor B inhibitor)Emerging — APPEAR-C3G trial (phase 3) shows proteinuria reduction
Pegcetacoplan (C3 inhibitor)Emerging — phase 2/3 trials ongoing
Plasma exchangeFor CFH autoantibody (removes autoantibody) + immunosuppression
Liver transplantFor CFH mutation (CFH made in liver) — curative
Transplant recurrence: High in C3G (DDD >50%, C3GN ~30%), lower in IC-MPGN if aetiology treated.

7. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- MPGN reclassified by IF: IC-MPGN (Ig+C3) vs C3G (C3 dominant).
- DDD: Intramembranous dense deposits (EM), C3NeF 80–90%, drusen, lipodystrophy, poor prognosis.
- C3GN: Subendothelial/mesangial deposits, C3NeF 40–50%, screen for monoclonal gammopathy.
- Low C3 + normal C4 = alternative pathway (C3G, IC-MPGN non-cryo). Low C3 + low C4 = classical pathway (SLE, cryoglobulinaemia).
- C3NeF = stabilises C3bBb → uncontrolled alternative pathway.
- Always check serum/urine immunofixation in C3G (exclude MGUS/myeloma).
- Genetic testing: CFH, CFI, C3, CFB, THBD if familial/early onset.
- Eculizumab considered in progressive C3G with C3NeF+.
- HCV-MPGN: Treat with DAAs (not immunosuppression).
- Transplant recurrence high in C3G (especially DDD).

8. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
MPGN types I/II/III still usedObsolete — use IF classification (IC-MPGN vs C3G)
DDD = MPGN Type IIDDD = C3G subtype (C3 dominant, intramembranous deposits)
All MPGN = immune complexC3G = no immune complexes (C3 dominant, alternative pathway)
Low C3 = always MPGNAlso post-strep GN, SLE, cryoglobulinaemia, IE, C3G
C3NeF = only in DDDAlso in C3GN (~50%), some IC-MPGN
C3GN = no monoclonal gammopathyScreen mandatory — MGUS/myeloma can mimic C3GN
Eculizumab = first-line C3GNot standard — supportive + MMF/steroids first; eculizumab case-by-case
DDD = drusen = eye onlyDrusen = retinal deposits → ↑ AMD risk (ophthalmology referral)
IC-MPGN = never recur post-TxRecurs if aetiology untreated (e.g., untreated HCV, MGUS)

9. Mnemonics

  • MPGN classification: IC-MPGN = Immune Complex (Ig+C3); C3G = C3 dominant (no Ig)
  • C3G subtypes: DDD = Dense Deposits (intramembranous); C3GN = C3 deposits (subendothelial/mesangial)
  • Complement pathway: Low C3 + Normal C4 = Alternative (C3G, IC-MPGN); Low C3 + Low C4 = Classical (SLE, cryo)
  • C3NeF: C3 Nephritic Factor = Converts C3 Forever (stabilises C3bBb)
  • DDD associations: Drusen (eyes), Lipodystrophy (face/arms) = DDL

10. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((MPGN / C3 Glomerulopathy))
    IC-MPGN[Immune-Complex MPGN]
      IF[IgG/IgM + C3 granular]
      Causes
        Infections[HCV, HBV, IE, Shunt]
        Autoimmune[SLE, Cryoglobulin, IgA vasculitis]
        Monoclonal[MGUS, Myeloma]
        Drugs[IFN-α, Gemcitabine]
      Management[Treat cause: DAA for HCV, Clone for MGUS, MMF+steroids if idiopathic]
    C3G[C3 Glomerulopathy]
      IF[C3 dominant (≥2+ > Ig)]
      Pathogenesis[Alternative pathway dysregulation: C3NeF, CFH/CFI/C3/CFB mutations]
      DDD[Dense Deposit Disease]
        EM[Intramembranous dense deposits]
        C3NeF[80-90%]
        Associations[Drusen, Lipodystrophy]
        Prognosis[Poor, 50% ESRD 10yr]
      C3GN[C3 Glomerulonephritis]
        EM[Subendothelial/mesangial]
        C3NeF[40-50%]
        Screen[Monoclonal gammopathy!]
        Prognosis[Variable]
    Workup[C3, C4, C3NeF, CFH/CFI/C3/CFB, Immunofixation, Genetics]
    Management
      Supportive[ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, BP<130/80]
      IC-MPGN[Treat aetiology]
      C3G[MMF+steroids ± CNI; Eculizumab if progressive C3NeF+; Iptacopan emerging]
      Transplant[High recurrence C3G (DDD>C3GN)]

11. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. MPGN modern classification (IC-MPGN vs C3G)
  2. DDD vs C3GN EM findings
  3. C3NeF mechanism & prevalence
  4. Complement profile: Low C3 + normal C4 vs Low C3 + low C4
  5. Mandatory test in C3G (immunofixation)
  6. DDD associations (drusen, lipodystrophy)
  7. HCV-MPGN treatment
  8. Transplant recurrence risk

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes

13. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴IC-MPGN vs C3G classification, DDD vs C3GN EM, C3NeF, complement profile (C3/C4), immunofixation screen, HCV-MPGN treatment
Should Know 🟡Genetic causes (CFH, CFI, C3, CFB), eculizumab/iptacopan role, plasma exchange for CFH autoantibody, transplant recurrence
Nice to Know 🟢Novel complement inhibitors (pegcetacoplan, danicopan), CFH liver transplant, DGKE mutations, retinal drusen screening

14. MCQs (10)

  1. Modern MPGN classification is based on:

A. Light microscopy pattern (I, II, III)

B. Immunofluorescence pattern (IC-MPGN vs C3G)

C. Electron microscopy alone

D. Clinical presentation

E. Serum complement levels

  1. C3 Glomerulopathy immunofluorescence shows:

A. IgG dominant with C3

B. C3 dominant (≥2+) with no/minimal Ig

C. IgA dominant

D. Fibrinogen only

E. IgM + C3 only

  1. Dense Deposit Disease (DDD) EM hallmark:

A. Subepithelial humps

B. Subendothelial deposits

C. Intramembranous dense deposits (ribbon-like)

D. Mesangial deposits only

E. Tubuloreticular inclusions

  1. Complement profile in C3G:

A. Low C3, Low C4

B. Low C3, Normal C4

C. Normal C3, Low C4

D. Normal C3, Normal C4

E. Low C3, Low C4, Low Factor B

  1. C3NeF prevalence in DDD:

A. 10–20%

B. 40–50%

C. 80–90%

D. 100%

E. Not tested

  1. Associations of DDD include:

A. Diabetic retinopathy

B. Retinal drusen + partial lipodystrophy

C. Pulmonary fibrosis

D. Thyroiditis

E. Inflammatory bowel disease

  1. Essential investigation in newly diagnosed C3GN:

A. Renal US

B. Serum & urine immunofixation

C. ANA

D. ANCA

E. Hepatitis B serology

  1. Treatment of HCV-associated MPGN:

A. Steroids + cyclophosphamide

B. Direct-acting antivirals (DAA)

C. Rituximab alone

D. Plasma exchange

E. Eculizumab

  1. Transplant recurrence risk highest in:

A. IC-MPGN (treated HCV)

B. DDD (C3G)

C. C3GN (C3G)

D. Post-strep GN

E. IgA nephropathy

  1. Eculizumab in C3G — target:

A. C3

B. Factor B

C. C5 (prevents MAC formation)

D. CFH

E. Properdin

15. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 25-year-old man, nephrotic syndrome, Low C3, Normal C4. Biopsy: MPGN pattern, C3 dominant IF, intramembranous dense deposits EM. Diagnosis:

A. IC-MPGN

B. DDD (C3G)

C. C3GN

D. Post-strep GN

E. Lupus nephritis

  1. 30-year-old woman, nephritic syndrome, Low C3, Normal C4. Biopsy: C3 dominant IF, subendothelial deposits EM. C3NeF negative. Next step:

A. Start eculizumab

B. Screen for monoclonal gammopathy (immunofixation)

C. Genetic testing for CFH only

C. Start MMF + steroids

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 45-year-old man, HCV+, nephrotic syndrome, cryoglobulins positive, Low C3, Low C4. Biopsy: MPGN pattern, IgM/IgG + C3. Management:

A. Rituximab + steroids

B. DAA therapy for HCV

C. Plasma exchange

D. Cyclophosphamide

E. Eculizumab

  1. 15-year-old boy, DDD diagnosed. Ophthalmology referral for:

A. Cataract screening

B. Retinal drusen (AMD risk)

C. Glaucoma screening

D. Diabetic retinopathy

E. Uveitis

  1. 50-year-old woman, C3GN, progressive CKD despite MMF+steroids, C3NeF positive. Emerging therapy option:

A. Iptacopan (Factor B inhibitor)

B. High-dose IVIG

C. Splenectomy

D. Sirolimus

E. Alemtuzumab

  1. Young adult, MPGN pattern, C3 dominant, family history of similar disease. Genetic testing priority:

A. CFH, CFI, C3, CFB, THBD

B. NPHS1, NPHS2, WT1

C. COL4A3, COL4A4, COL4A5

D. PKD1, PKD2

E. UMOD, MUC1

  1. CFH mutation — curative transplant option:

A. Kidney alone

B. Liver-kidney (CFH produced in liver)

C. Heart-kidney

C. Lung-kidney

E. Pancreas-kidney

  1. DDD vs C3GN — key EM difference:

A. DDD = subepithelial; C3GN = mesangial

B. DDD = intramembranous dense deposits; C3GN = subendothelial/mesangial

C. DDD = no deposits; C3GN = deposits

D. DDD = humps; C3GN = tram-tracks

E. No difference

  1. Post-strep GN vs C3G — distinguishing feature:

A. Both have Low C3

B. Post-strep: C3NeF negative, resolves; C3G: C3NeF often positive, chronic

C. Post-strep: C3 dominant

D. Both have subepithelial humps

E. No distinction possible

  1. Monitoring on eculizumab for C3G:

A. C3 levels only

B. C3, C5, CH50, meningococcal vaccination status

C. LFTs only

D. CBC only

E. Urine protein only

16. Flashcards

  • Q: MPGN modern classification?

A: IC-MPGN (Ig+C3) vs C3G (C3 dominant)

  • Q: DDD EM finding?

A: Intramembranous dense deposits (osmiophilic ribbon)

  • Q: C3GN EM finding?

A: Subendothelial/mesangial deposits

  • Q: C3G complement profile?

A: Low C3, Normal C4 (alternative pathway)

  • Q: C3NeF in DDD?

A: 80–90% positive

  • Q: C3NeF mechanism?

A: Stabilises C3bBb → uncontrolled C3 cleavage

  • Q: DDD associations?

A: Retinal drusen, partial lipodystrophy

  • Q: Mandatory test in C3G?

A: Serum/urine immunofixation (exclude MGUS/myeloma)

  • Q: HCV-MPGN treatment?

A: DAAs (not immunosuppression)

  • Q: Transplant recurrence C3G?

A: High (DDD >50%, C3GN ~30%)

  • Q: Genetic causes C3G?

A: CFH, CFI, C3, CFB, THBD

  • Q: Eculizumab target?

A: C5 (blocks terminal complement)

  • Q: Iptacopan target?

A: Factor B (alternative pathway)

  • Q: CFH mutation transplant?

A: Liver-kidney (CFH from liver)

  • Q: Low C3 + Low C4 pathway?

A: Classical (SLE, cryoglobulinaemia)

  • Q: Low C3 + Normal C4 pathway?

A: Alternative (C3G, IC-MPGN non-cryo)

17. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Modern classification by IF: IC-MPGN (Ig+C3) vs C3G (C3 dominant). Old types I/II/III obsolete.
  2. B — C3G definition: C3 ≥2+ intensity > any Ig.
  3. C — DDD = intramembranous highly osmiophilic dense deposits transforming GBM.
  4. B — Alternative pathway activation → C3 consumed, C4 normal.
  5. C — C3NeF present in 80–90% DDD.
  6. B — Drusen (retinal) + acquired partial lipodystrophy (face/arms).
  7. B — Monoclonal gammopathy (MGUS/myeloma) mimics C3GN — must exclude.
  8. B — HCV-MPGN: treat virus with DAAs; GN often remits.
  9. B — DDD recurrence >50% post-transplant; C3GN ~30%; IC-MPGN lower if aetiology treated.
  10. C — Eculizumab = anti-C5 monoclonal antibody → blocks MAC formation.

SBAs

  1. B — C3 dominant IF + intramembranous dense deposits = DDD (C3G subtype).
  2. B — C3GN with negative C3NeF: screen for monoclonal gammopathy (MGUS/myeloma).
  3. B — HCV cryoglobulinaemic MPGN: DAAs first-line; rituximab if severe vasculitis.
  4. B — DDD associated with retinal drusen → ↑ AMD risk (ophthalmology monitoring).
  5. A — Iptacopan (Factor B inhibitor) phase 3 APPEAR-C3G trial; emerging for C3G.
  6. A — C3G genetic panel: CFH, CFI, C3, CFB, THBD (alternative pathway regulators).
  7. B — CFH synthesised in liver → liver-kidney transplant curative for CFH mutations.
  8. B — DDD = intramembranous dense deposits; C3GN = subendothelial/mesangial.
  9. B — Post-strep: self-limited, C3NeF negative; C3G: chronic, C3NeF often positive.
  10. B — Eculizumab: monitor CH50 (functional complement), meningococcal vaccine mandatory.

18. Summary

MPGN & C3 Glomerulopathy is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Classification: IC-MPGN (immune complexes: Ig+C3) vs C3G (C3 dominant, no Ig).

DDD: Intramembranous dense deposits, C3NeF 80–90%, drusen/lipodystrophy, poor prognosis.

C3GN: Subendothelial/mesangial deposits, C3NeF 40–50%, screen for monoclonal gammopathy.

Complement: Low C3 + Normal C4 = alternative pathway. C3NeF = stabilises C3bBb.

Management: Treat cause (DAAs for HCV, clone-directed for MGUS). Supportive: ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i. C3G: MMF+steroids ± CNI; eculizumab/iptacopan emerging for progressive C3NeF+.

Transplant: High recurrence in C3G (DDD > C3GN). CFH mutation → liver-kidney transplant.

Exam focus: IF classification, EM distinction DDD vs C3GN, complement profile, immunofixation mandate, C3NeF, HCV treatment, transplant recurrence.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.11 Anti Gbm Goodpasture
Topic: Anti-GBM Disease (Goodpasture Syndrome) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[MPGN and C3 Glomerulopathy (DDD, C3GN)]], [[Cryoglobulinaemic Glomerulonephritis]], [[Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis (RPGN)]], [[Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Anti-GBM disease = antibodies against α3 chain of type IV collagen (NC1 domain) in GBM & alveolar basement membrane. 40–60% have pulmonary haemorrhage (Goodpasture's syndrome). Linear IgG on IF (pathognomonic). RPGN with crescents >50%. Peak incidence: young men (20–30) & older adults (60–70). HLA-DR15 association.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise clinical presentation (RPGN ± pulmonary haemorrhage)
  • Interpret anti-GBM antibody testing and renal biopsy (linear IgG)
  • Apply emergency management (plasma exchange + cyclophosphamide + steroids)
  • Understand prognosis factors (dialysis dependence, pulmonary haemorrhage)
  • Differentiate from ANCA vasculitis (double-positive overlap)
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP emergency vignettes

2. Pathogenesis

ComponentDetail
Target antigenα3(IV)NC1 collagen (non-collagenous domain of α3 chain of type IV collagen)
AntibodyIgG (mostly IgG1, IgG3) — anti-GBM
LocationGBM (renal) + Alveolar basement membrane (pulmonary)
HLA associationHLA-DR15 (DRB1*1501) — strong (88% vs 25% controls)
TriggersHydrocarbon exposure, smoking, viral infections (influenza), cocaine, HLA-DR15
Mechanism: Anti-GBM IgG binds GBM → complement activation (C5b-9) + neutrophil recruitment → crescent formation. Same antigen in alveoli → pulmonary capillaritis → haemorrhage.

3. Clinical Presentation

Renal (100%)

FeatureFrequency
RPGN (AKI, haematuria, RBC casts, proteinuria)100%
Hypertension60–80%
Oliguria/anuriaIf advanced
Subnephrotic proteinuria<3.5g/day typical

Pulmonary (40–60% = Goodpasture's Syndrome)

FeatureFrequency
Haemoptysis70–90% of pulmonary involvement
DyspnoeaCommon
Iron deficiency anaemiaChronic occult blood loss
CXR/CTBilateral diffuse alveolar infiltrates (flitting)
Smoking/hydrocarbon exposure ↑ pulmonary involvement risk.

4. Diagnosis

Serology

TestSensitivitySpecificityRole
Anti-GBM ELISA90–95%95–100%Diagnostic (quantitative — monitors disease)
Anti-GBM IIFLowerHighConfirmatory
ANCA (p-ANCA/MPO, c-ANCA/PR3)——Check for double-positive (20–30%)
Anti-GBM titre correlates with disease activity. Falling titre = response to treatment.

Renal Biopsy (If needed — often diagnosed on serology + clinical)

FindingDescription
LMCrescentic GN — cellular crescents >50% (RPGN)
IFLinear IgG along GBM (pathognomonic) — ± C3 linear
EMNo immune deposits (pauci-immune by EM) — GBM disruption
Differential on IF: Linear IgG also in diabetic nephropathy (non-specific, weak), fibrillary GN (smudgy), amyloid (kappa/lambda).

5. Double-Positive Anti-GBM + ANCA (20–30%)

FeatureAnti-GBM aloneDouble-positive (Anti-GBM + ANCA)
AgeBimodal (20–30, 60–70)Older (mean 60+)
ANCA type—p-ANCA/MPO (90%)
Pulmonary haemorrhage40–60%↑ (60–80%)
Renal survivalBetter if treated earlyWorse
RelapseRare↑ (ANCA component)
TreatmentPLEX + CyC + SteroidsSame + consider maintenance (azathioprine/rituximab)
Always check ANCA in anti-GBM disease. Double-positive = vasculitis overlap.

6. Management (EMERGENCY)

Indications for Urgent Treatment

  • Anti-GBM positive + RPGN (creatinine rising, crescents on biopsy)
  • Pulmonary haemorrhage (life-threatening)

Standard Regimen (KDIGO/ERA-EDTA)

ComponentDose/DurationRole
Plasma Exchange (PLEX)1–1.5 plasma vol daily × 14 days (or until anti-GBM negative)Removes circulating anti-GBM antibody
Cyclophosphamide (CyC)IV 500mg/m² q2wk × 3mo (or oral 2mg/kg/day × 3mo)Stops new antibody production
CorticosteroidsMethylprednisolone 500–1000mg IV × 3 days → Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day (taper 3–6mo)Anti-inflammatory, ↓ cytokine storm
PLEX: Start immediately — do not wait for biopsy if clinical picture clear. Use 5% albumin or FFP replacement.

Pulmonary Haemorrhage Additions

InterventionIndication
Intubation/ventilationMassive haemorrhage, hypoxia
Recombinant Factor VIIaRefractory bleeding (case reports)
Tranexamic acidAdjunct (avoid if thrombotic risk)

Monitoring

ParameterFrequency
Anti-GBM titreWeekly during PLEX, then monthly × 6mo, then q3mo
Renal functionDaily during acute, then weekly
Hb/iron studiesWeekly (pulmonary bleed)
ANCAIf double-positive, monitor q3mo

7. Prognosis

FactorGood PrognosisPoor Prognosis
Serum Creatinine at presentation<300 μmol/L>500–600 μmol/L / dialysis-dependent
% Crescents on biopsy<50%>75–80%
Pulmonary haemorrhageAbsentPresent (↑ early mortality)
Anti-GBM titreLowHigh
Double-positive ANCA—Worse renal survival, ↑ relapse
Dialysis dependence at presentation → <10% renal recovery. Early treatment (Cr <300) → 70–80% renal survival at 1yr.

Relapse

  • Anti-GBM alone: Very rare (<5%) once antibody cleared.
  • Double-positive: Relapse rate ~20–30% (ANCA component) — consider maintenance azathioprine/rituximab.

8. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Anti-GBM disease: Anti-α3(IV)NC1 collagen IgG → linear IgG on IF.
- Goodpasture's syndrome = Anti-GBM + pulmonary haemorrhage (40–60%).
- HLA-DR15 strong association.
- Emergency: PLEX (daily × 14 days) + IV CyC + IV methylprednisolone → START IMMEDIATELY.
- Dialysis-dependent at presentation → poor renal recovery (<10%).
- Check ANCA — 20–30% double-positive (p-ANCA/MPO) → worse prognosis, ↑ relapse.
- Anti-GBM titre monitors disease activity.
- Relapse rare in pure anti-GBM; common in double-positive.
- Differential linear IgG: Diabetic nephropathy (weak, non-specific), fibrillary GN, amyloid.

9. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Linear IgG = always anti-GBMAlso in diabetic nephropathy (weak, non-specific), fibrillary GN, amyloid
Anti-GBM = no ANCA20–30% double-positive (p-ANCA/MPO) — worse prognosis
Wait for biopsy before treatmentNO — treat immediately if clinical + serology fits (PLEX life-saving)
PLEX = only for pulmonary bleedPLEX for ALL anti-GBM RPGN (removes antibody)
Good prognosis if creatinine normalCreatinine at presentation is key — >500 = dialysis likely permanent
Maintenance immunosuppression always neededPure anti-GBM: no maintenance (relapse rare); Double-positive: consider maintenance
Anti-GBM IFA = ELISAELISA = quantitative, diagnostic standard; IFA = confirmatory
Children never get anti-GBMRare but occurs (paediatric RPGN differential)
Pulmonary haemorrhage = always Goodpasture'sAlso in ANCA vasculitis, SLE, cryoglobulinaemia, anticoagulation

10. Mnemonics

  • Anti-GBM target: α3(IV)NC1 = α3 chain, IV collagen, Non-Collagenous 1 domain
  • Goodpasture's triad: RPGN + Pulmonary haemorrhage + Anti-GBM = RPA
  • HLA: DR15 = DRives 15% of controls vs 88% patients
  • Treatment: PLEX + CyC + Steroids = PCS (start IMMEDIATELY)
  • Prognosis: Creatinine >500 = Chronic Dialysis
  • Double-positive: Anti-GBM + ANCA = Awful prognosis (worse renal survival)

11. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Anti-GBM Disease))
    Pathogenesis
      Target[α3(IV)NC1 collagen]
      Antibody[IgG1/IgG3]
      HLA[HLA-DR15]
      Triggers[Smoking, Hydrocarbons, Viral]
    Clinical
      Renal[RPGN 100%, Haematuria, RBC casts, Proteinuria]
      Pulmonary[Haemoptysis 40-60% = Goodpasture's]
    Diagnosis
      Serology[Anti-GBM ELISA (quantitative), ANCA (check double-positive!)]
      Biopsy[LM: Crescents >50%; IF: Linear IgG (pathognomonic); EM: No deposits]
    Double_Positive[Anti-GBM + ANCA (20-30%)]
      Features[Older, MPO-ANCA, More pulmonary, Worse prognosis, Relapse risk]
    Management
      Emergency[PLEX daily x14d + IV CyC + IV Methylpred → oral taper]
      Monitoring[Anti-GBM titre weekly, Renal function, Hb]
      Double_Positive_Maint[Azathioprine/Rituximab for ANCA component]
    Prognosis
      Good[Cr<300, <50% crescents, No pulmonary]
      Poor[Dialysis-dependent, >75% crescents, Pulmonary haemorrhage]
      Relapse[Rare pure; 20-30% if double-positive]

12. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Anti-GBM target antigen (α3 chain type IV collagen NC1)
  2. Goodpasture's syndrome definition
  3. Renal biopsy IF hallmark (linear IgG)
  4. Emergency treatment (PLEX + CyC + Steroids)
  5. Prognostic factors (Cr, crescents, pulmonary)
  6. Double-positive prevalence & significance
  7. Anti-GBM titre monitoring
  8. Differential for linear IgG on IF

13. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

14. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Clinical presentation, linear IgG IF, anti-GBM ELISA, emergency PLEX+CyC+steroids, prognosis (Cr, crescents), double-positive ANCA
Should Know 🟡HLA-DR15, pulmonary haemorrhage management, relapse patterns, differential linear IgG, titration monitoring
Nice to Know 🟢Recombinant Factor VIIa, rFVIIa for refractory bleed, genetic HLA typing, novel B-cell depletion strategies

15. MCQs (10)

  1. Target antigen in anti-GBM disease:

A. Type III collagen

B. α3 chain of type IV collagen (NC1 domain)

C. Type I collagen

D. Laminin

E. Fibronectin

  1. Pathognomonic immunofluorescence finding in anti-GBM disease:

A. Granular IgG + C3

B. Linear IgG along GBM

C. Mesangial IgA

D. Full-house (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q)

E. C3 dominant, no Ig

  1. Goodpasture's syndrome definition:

A. Anti-GBM + nephrotic syndrome

B. Anti-GBM disease + pulmonary haemorrhage

C. ANCA + pulmonary haemorrhage

D. SLE + diffuse alveolar haemorrhage

E. Cryoglobulinaemia + RPGN

  1. HLA association with anti-GBM disease:

A. HLA-B27

B. HLA-DR3

C. HLA-DR15 (DRB1*1501)

D. HLA-DQ2

E. HLA-B51

  1. Standard emergency treatment for anti-GBM RPGN:

A. Steroids alone

B. Cyclophosphamide alone

C. Plasma exchange + cyclophosphamide + steroids

D. Rituximab + steroids

E. Plasma exchange alone

  1. Duration of plasma exchange in anti-GBM disease:

A. 3 days

B. 7 days

C. 14 days (or until anti-GBM negative)

D. 21 days

E. 28 days

  1. Prognosis — dialysis dependence at presentation:

A. 50% renal recovery

B. 30% renal recovery

C. <10% renal recovery

D. 80% renal recovery

E. 100% renal recovery

  1. Double-positive anti-GBM + ANCA prevalence:

A. <5%

B. 20–30%

C. 50%

D. 70%

E. 90%

  1. ANCA type in double-positive anti-GBM disease:

A. c-ANCA/PR3

B. p-ANCA/MPO

C. Both equally

C. Atypical ANCA

E. Anti-GBM only

  1. Relapse in pure anti-GBM disease after treatment:

A. Common (50%)

B. Very rare (<5%)

C. 20–30%

D. 40%

E. 100%

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 25-year-old man, sudden haemoptysis, AKI (Cr 450), haematuria, RBC casts. Anti-GBM positive, ANCA negative. CXR: bilateral alveolar infiltrates. Immediate management:

A. IV methylprednisolone 1g × 3 days only

B. Plasma exchange + IV cyclophosphamide + IV methylprednisolone

C. Rituximab 375mg/m² weekly × 4

D. Oral prednisolone 1mg/kg + oral cyclophosphamide

E. Haemodialysis only

  1. 60-year-old woman, RPGN, anti-GBM positive, p-ANCA/MPO positive. Creatinine 380. Treatment differs from pure anti-GBM by:

A. No plasma exchange needed

B. Add maintenance immunosuppression (azathioprine/rituximab) after induction

C. Shorter PLEX course (7 days)

D. No cyclophosphamide

E. Higher steroid dose

  1. 30-year-old man, anti-GBM positive, Cr 180, 30% crescents on biopsy. Best predictor of renal recovery:

A. Anti-GBM titre

B. Serum creatinine at presentation (<300)

C. Age

D. HLA-DR15 status

E. Number of PLEX sessions

  1. 28-year-old man, RPGN, linear IgG on IF. Anti-GBM ELISA negative. Next step:

A. Diagnose anti-GBM disease anyway

B. Check for diabetic nephropathy (weak linear IgG), fibrillary GN, amyloid

C. Repeat ELISA in 1 week

C. Treat as anti-GBM

E. Diagnose ANCA vasculitis

  1. 65-year-old man, anti-GBM + p-ANCA positive, on maintenance azathioprine after induction. 1 year later: rising Cr, positive ANCA, negative anti-GBM. Management:

A. Restart plasma exchange

B. Treat as ANCA vasculitis relapse (rituximab or increase immunosuppression)

C. Increase azathioprine only

D. Stop immunosuppression

E. Repeat kidney biopsy only

  1. Patient on PLEX for anti-GBM. Replacement fluid of choice:

A. Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) only

B. 5% Albumin (or FFP if bleeding/coagulopathy)

C. Saline

C. Cryoprecipitate

E. IVIG

  1. Monitoring anti-GBM titre during treatment:

A. Daily

B. Weekly during PLEX, then monthly × 6mo, then q3mo

C. Monthly only

D. Only at diagnosis

E. Only if symptomatic

  1. 25-year-old woman, anti-GBM disease treated successfully, anti-GBM negative × 6mo. Now pregnant. Risk of relapse:

A. High (need prophylaxis)

B. Very low (pure anti-GBM relapse rare)

C. 50%

D. 25%

E. Unknown

  1. Pulmonary haemorrhage in anti-GBM — adjunctive therapy for refractory bleeding:

A. High-dose IVIG

B. Recombinant Factor VIIa (case reports)

C. Splenectomy

D. Rituximab

E. Eculizumab

  1. Biopsy in anti-GBM — EM finding:

A. Subepithelial humps

B. Intramembranous dense deposits

C. No immune deposits (pauci-immune by EM)

D. Subendothelial deposits

E. Mesangial deposits

17. Flashcards

  • Q: Anti-GBM target?

A: α3(IV)NC1 collagen

  • Q: Goodpasture's = ?

A: Anti-GBM + pulmonary haemorrhage

  • Q: IF hallmark?

A: Linear IgG along GBM

  • Q: HLA association?

A: HLA-DR15

  • Q: Emergency Rx?

A: PLEX + CyC + Steroids (PCS)

  • Q: PLEX duration?

A: 14 days or until anti-GBM negative

  • Q: Prognosis if dialysis-dependent?

A: <10% renal recovery

  • Q: Double-positive prevalence?

A: 20–30% (p-ANCA/MPO)

  • Q: Double-positive prognosis?

A: Worse renal survival, ↑ relapse

  • Q: Pure anti-GBM relapse?

A: Very rare (<5%)

  • Q: Anti-GBM titre use?

A: Monitors disease activity

  • Q: Linear IgG differential?

A: Diabetes (weak), fibrillary GN, amyloid

  • Q: Pulmonary bleed Rx adjunct?

A: rFVIIa (refractory)

  • Q: PLEX replacement?

A: 5% Albumin (FFP if coagulopathy)

  • Q: ANCA in anti-GBM?

A: Always check — 20-30% double-positive

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — α3 chain of type IV collagen, non-collagenous domain 1 (NC1).
  2. B — Linear IgG along GBM = pathognomonic for anti-GBM disease.
  3. B — Goodpasture's syndrome = anti-GBM disease with pulmonary haemorrhage.
  4. C — HLA-DR15 (DRB1*1501) — 88% patients vs 25% controls.
  5. C — KDIGO: PLEX (14 days) + IV Cyclophosphamide + IV Methylprednisolone.
  6. C — Daily PLEX × 14 days or until anti-GBM antibody negative.
  7. C — Dialysis-dependent at presentation → <10% renal recovery.
  8. B — 20–30% have concurrent ANCA (usually p-ANCA/MPO).
  9. B — p-ANCA/MPO is the typical ANCA in double-positive disease.
  10. B — Pure anti-GBM relapse very rare (<5%) once antibody cleared.

SBAs

  1. B — Emergency: PLEX + CyC + IV Methylprednisolone simultaneously. Do not delay PLEX.
  2. B — Double-positive: ANCA component relapses → maintenance immunosuppression (azathioprine/rituximab) after induction.
  3. B — Serum creatinine at presentation is strongest predictor: <300 = good recovery; >500 = dialysis likely permanent.
  4. B — Linear IgG with negative ELISA: consider diabetic nephropathy (weak linear), fibrillary GN (smudgy), amyloid (congo red+).
  5. B — Rising Cr + positive ANCA + negative anti-GBM = ANCA vasculitis relapse → treat with rituximab or escalate immunosuppression.
  6. B — 5% Albumin standard; FFP if active bleeding/coagulopathy (replaces coagulation factors).
  7. B — Weekly during PLEX, monthly × 6mo, then q3mo. Rising titre = relapse (especially double-positive).
  8. B — Pure anti-GBM relapse extremely rare once antibody negative.
  9. B — rFVIIa case reports for refractory alveolar haemorrhage.
  10. C — EM shows no immune deposits (pauci-immune); GBM disruption from antibody-mediated injury.

19. Summary

Anti-GBM Disease is a Must Know 🔴 emergency topic.

Pathogenesis: IgG against α3(IV)NC1 collagen → linear IgG on IF. HLA-DR15.

Clinical: RPGN (100%) ± pulmonary haemorrhage (40–60% = Goodpasture's).

Diagnosis: Anti-GBM ELISA (quantitative) + always check ANCA (20–30% double-positive). Biopsy: crescents >50%, linear IgG IF, no deposits EM.

Emergency Rx: PLEX (daily × 14d) + IV CyC + IV Methylpred → oral taper. Start immediately — do not wait for biopsy.

Prognosis: Dialysis-dependent at presentation → <10% renal recovery. Cr <300 = good recovery.

Double-positive (20–30%): p-ANCA/MPO, worse prognosis, ↑ relapse → maintenance immunosuppression.

Monitoring: Anti-GBM titre weekly → monthly → q3mo.

Exam focus: Linear IgG, emergency PLEX+CyC+steroids, creatinine prognosis, double-positive ANCA, Goodpasture's definition, relapse patterns.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.12 Cryoglobulinaemic Gn
Topic: Cryoglobulinaemic Glomerulonephritis — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[MPGN and C3 Glomerulopathy (DDD, C3GN)]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Lupus Nephritis]], [[Anti-GBM Disease (Goodpasture Syndrome)]], [[Hepatitis C]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Cryoglobulinaemic GN = immune-complex MPGN from cryoglobulin precipitation in glomeruli. Types: Type I (monoclonal, malignancy), Type II (mixed monoclonal + polyclonal, HCV), Type III (mixed polyclonal, SLE/RA). HCV = 80–90% of mixed cryoglobulinaemia. Low C4 is hallmark. Renal: MPGN pattern, nephritic/nephrotic. Treat HCV (DAAs) ± rituximab; severe: plasma exchange + rituximab + steroids.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify cryoglobulinaemia (Types I, II, III)
  • Recognise clinical triad (purpura, arthralgia, weakness) + renal involvement
  • Interpret complement profile (Low C4, Low/Normal C3, Cryoglobulins+, RF+)
  • Manage based on type and severity (DAAs for HCV, rituximab, plasma exchange)
  • Differentiate from other MPGN causes
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP immune-complex GN vignettes

2. Classification (Brouet)

TypeCompositionAssociationPrevalence
Type IMonoclonal (IgM/IgG/IgA κ/λ)Lymphoproliferative (Waldenström, myeloma, CLL, lymphoma)10–15%
Type IIMixed: Monoclonal IgM (κ) + Polyclonal IgGHCV (80–90%), also lymphoproliferative50–60%
Type IIIMixed: Polyclonal IgM + Polyclonal IgGSLE, RA, Sjögren's, other autoimmune25–30%
Type II (HCV) = most common mixed cryoglobulinaemia. Type I = malignancy. Type III = autoimmune.

3. Pathogenesis

  • Cryoglobulins = immunoglobulins that precipitate at <37°C and dissolve on rewarming
  • Type II/III: IgM (with rheumatoid factor activity) binds polyclonal IgG → immune complexes
  • Cold exposure → precipitation in capillaries (skin, kidneys, nerves) → vasculitis
  • Renal: Subendothelial deposits → MPGN pattern (mesangial expansion, GBM duplication)

4. Clinical Presentation

Classic Triad (Meltzer's Triad)

FeatureFrequency
Palpable purpura (lower limbs)80–90%
Arthralgia (symmetrical, non-erosive)60–80%
Weakness / Fatigue70–90%

Renal Involvement (20–60%)

PresentationFrequency
Nephritic syndrome (haematuria, RBC casts, hypertension)Common
Nephrotic syndromeLess common
Mixed nephritic-nephroticCommon
AKI / RPGNSevere cases
Asymptomatic proteinuria/haematuriaEarly

Extrarenal

SystemManifestation
SkinPurpura, ulcers, livedo reticularis, Raynaud's
JointsSymmetrical arthralgia (knees, ankles, wrists)
NervesPeripheral neuropathy (sensory > motor, mononeuritis multiplex)
LiverHCV chronic hepatitis, cirrhosis
LungsInterstitial pneumonitis (rare)

5. Laboratory Findings

TestTypical FindingSignificance
CryoglobulinsPositive (send warm tube!)Diagnostic
C4Low (hallmark)Classical pathway consumption
C3Low or NormalVariable consumption
Rheumatoid Factor (RF)High (IgM RF)Monoclonal IgM has RF activity
Serum ImmunofixationMonoclonal IgM κ (Type II)Confirms type
HCV RNAPositive (80–90% Type II)Aetiology
ANA / ENAPositive (Type III/SLE)Autoimmune
ANCAUsually negativeDifferentiate from ANCA vasculitis
UrineHaematuria, Proteinuria, RBC castsRenal involvement
Key: Low C4 + High RF + Cryoglobulins+ = cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis.

6. Renal Histology

FindingDescription
LMMPGN pattern (mesangial expansion, GBM double contours "tram-track")
IFGranular IgM, IgG, C3 (capillary wall + mesangium)
EMSubendothelial deposits + microtubular/fibrillary structures (cryoglobulin precipitates)
Microtubules30–50nm hollow microtubules (pathognomonic for cryoglobulins)

7. Management

Type I (Monoclonal) — Treat Underlying Malignancy

ApproachDetail
PrimaryTreat lymphoproliferative disorder (rituximab-based chemo, BTKi, etc.)
RenalPlasmapheresis if severe RPGN/acute
RituximabOften effective (targets B-cell clone)

Type II (HCV-Associated) — Treat HCV First

SeverityRegimen
Mild (proteinuria <1g, preserved eGFR)DAAs alone (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir × 12wk) — cryoglobulins clear with HCV cure
Moderate (proteinuria 1–3.5g, eGFR >30)DAAs + Rituximab (375mg/m² weekly × 4)
Severe (RPGN, nephrotic, AKI, eGFR <30)Plasma Exchange + Rituximab + Steroids → then DAAs
DAAs cure HCV → cryoglobulins disappear in 90%. Rituximab for persistent/severe vasculitis.

Type III (Autoimmune) — Treat Underlying Disease

DiseaseManagement
SLEStandard lupus nephritis protocol (MMF/CyC + steroids)
RA / Sjögren'sRituximab + steroids ± immunosuppressants
SeverePlasma exchange + rituximab + steroids

8. Plasma Exchange (PLEX) Indications

IndicationProtocol
RPGN (rapidly rising Cr)1–1.5 plasma vol daily × 7–14 days
Severe neuropathy (mononeuritis multiplex)Same
Severe skin ulcers/gangreneSame
Hyperviscosity (Type I, high monoclonal)Same
PLEX removes cryoglobulins acutely; bridge to immunosuppression/DAAs.

9. Monitoring

ParameterFrequency
Cryoglobulin levelMonthly × 3mo, then q3mo
C3, C4Monthly × 3mo, then q3mo
RFq3mo
HCV RNAAt 12wk post-DAAs (SVR12)
Renal functionMonthly during active disease
Urine proteinq3mo

10. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Cryoglobulinaemic GN = MPGN from cryoglobulin precipitation.
- Types: I (monoclonal, malignancy), II (mixed monoclonal+polyclonal, HCV 80–90%), III (polyclonal, SLE/RA).
- Hallmark labs: Low C4, High RF, Cryoglobulins+, HCV RNA+ (Type II).
- Renal: MPGN pattern, subendothelial deposits + microtubules (30–50nm) on EM.
- Clinical: Purpura, arthralgia, weakness + neuropathy.
- Type II (HCV): DAAs first-line (cure HCV → cryoglobulins clear); add rituximab if moderate/severe.
- Type I: Treat malignancy (rituximab-chemo).
- Type III: Treat autoimmune (SLE/RA protocol).
- Severe: Plasma exchange + rituximab + steroids.
- Differential: MPGN (C3G, IC-MPGN), SLE, ANCA, anti-GBM, IE.

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All cryoglobulinaemia = HCVType I = malignancy; Type III = SLE/RA
Low C3 + Low C4 = always SLECryoglobulinaemia also Low C3 + Low C4 (classical pathway)
RF = only RAHigh RF in cryoglobulinaemia (monoclonal IgM has RF activity)
DAAs alone for all HCV cryoSevere (RPGN/nephrotic) = PLEX + rituximab + steroids first
Cryoglobulins = send cold tubeSend WARM tube (37°C) — cold precipitates in tube, false negative
Type I = MPGNType I = usually no renal or minimal; Type II/III = MPGN
Cryoglobulins = only renalSkin, joints, nerves, liver all involved
ANCA positive in cryoUsually ANCA negative — differentiates from ANCA vasculitis

12. Mnemonics

  • Types: I = Immune malignancy (monoclonal); II = II = HCV (mixed); III = III = Autoimmune (polyclonal)
  • Type II: HCV + Mixed = HM (80–90%)
  • Lab triad: Low C4, High RF, Cryoglobulins+ = LHC
  • EM: Microtubules 30–50nm = M3
  • Treatment: HCV = DAAs; Malignancy = Rituximab-chemo; Autoimmune = Rituximab/immunosuppression

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Cryoglobulinaemic GN))
    Types
      I[Monoclonal - Malignancy (Waldenstrom, Myeloma)]
      II[Mixed Monoclonal+Polyclonal - HCV 80-90%]
      III[Mixed Polyclonal - SLE, RA, Sjogren's]
    Lab
      C4[LOW - hallmark]
      C3[Low/Normal]
      RF[HIGH - IgM RF activity]
      Cryo[Positive - WARM tube!]
      HCV[RNA+ in Type II]
      IFX[Monoclonal IgM kappa (Type II)]
    Renal
      LM[MPGN pattern]
      IF[Granular IgM, IgG, C3]
      EM[Subendothelial + MICROTUBULES 30-50nm]
    Clinical
      Triad[Purpura, Arthralgia, Weakness]
      Nerves[Peripheral neuropathy, mononeuritis multiplex]
      Skin[Purpura, ulcers, Raynaud's]
    Management
      Type_I[Treat malignancy (Rituximab-chemo)]
      Type_II[DAAs first; +Rituximab if moderate/severe; PLEX+Rituximab+Steroids if RPGN]
      Type_III[Treat autoimmune (SLE/RA protocol)]
      Severe[PLEX + Rituximab + Steroids]

14. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Three types of cryoglobulinaemia (composition, association)
  2. Type II = HCV (80–90%)
  3. Hallmark labs: Low C4, High RF, Cryoglobulins+
  4. EM finding: microtubules 30–50nm
  5. Meltzer's triad (purpura, arthralgia, weakness)
  6. Treatment by type (DAAs for HCV, chemo for Type I, immunosuppression for Type III)
  7. PLEX indications (RPGN, severe neuropathy, hyperviscosity)
  8. Send cryoglobulins in WARM tube

15. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

16. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Three types, HCV-Type II link, Low C4/High RF/Cryo+, microtubules EM, DAAs for HCV cryo, PLEX+Rituximab for severe
Should Know 🟡Type I malignancy link, Type III autoimmune, clinical triad, neuropathy, monitoring (C3/C4/RF/HCV RNA), PLEX protocol
Nice to Know 🟢Novel B-cell therapies (BTKi, daratumumab), cryoglobulin kinetics, cost-effectiveness of DAAs vs rituximab, pregnancy management

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Most common cause of mixed cryoglobulinaemia (Type II):

A. SLE

B. HCV (80–90%)

C. Myeloma

D. RA

E. CLL

  1. Hallmark complement finding in cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis:

A. Low C3, Normal C4

B. Low C4 (with Low/Normal C3)

C. Normal C3, Normal C4

D. High C3, High C4

E. Low C3 only

  1. Type I cryoglobulinaemia is associated with:

A. HCV

B. Lymphoproliferative malignancy (Waldenström, Myeloma)

C. SLE

C. RA

E. Sjögren's

  1. EM finding pathognomonic for cryoglobulinaemic GN:

A. Subepithelial humps

B. Intramembranous dense deposits

C. Microtubules 30–50nm (hollow)

D. Tubuloreticular inclusions

E. Fibrillary deposits

  1. Correct sample handling for cryoglobulin testing:

A. Send on ice

B. Send in WARM tube (maintained at 37°C)

C. Freeze immediately

D. Add EDTA

E. Centrifuge cold

  1. Meltzer's triad of cryoglobulinaemia:

A. Fever, rash, arthralgia

B. Purpura, arthralgia, weakness

C. Haematuria, proteinuria, hypertension

D. Neuropathy, livedo, purpura

E. Raynaud's, ulcers, gangrene

  1. Type II (HCV) cryoglobulinaemia — mild renal disease management:

A. Plasma exchange

B. Rituximab alone

C. DAAs alone (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir × 12wk)

D. Steroids alone

E. Cyclophosphamide

  1. Severe cryoglobulinaemic GN (RPGN, nephrotic) — acute management:

A. DAAs alone

B. Plasma exchange + Rituximab + Steroids

C. Rituximab alone

D. Cyclophosphamide alone

E. Supportive only

  1. Rheumatoid factor in cryoglobulinaemia:

A. Absent

B. High (IgM monoclonal has RF activity)

C. Low

C. Only in Type III

E. Only in SLE

  1. Cryoglobulinaemia vs ANCA vasculitis — distinguishing serology:

A. ANCA positive in cryo

B. ANCA usually negative in cryo

C. Both have low C4

D. Both have microtubules

E. No distinction

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 50-year-old man, HCV+, palpable purpura, arthralgia, proteinuria 2g/day, haematuria. C4 low, RF high, cryoglobulins+, HCV RNA+. Renal biopsy: MPGN, subendothelial deposits, microtubules. Management:

A. Plasma exchange

B. Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir × 12wk + Rituximab 375mg/m² weekly × 4

C. Cyclophosphamide + steroids

D. Rituximab alone

E. Interferon-α

  1. 60-year-old woman, Waldenström macroglobulinaemia, new-onset nephrotic syndrome, cryoglobulins+. Type I cryoglobulinaemia. Renal management:

A. Plasma exchange

B. Treat underlying malignancy (rituximab-based chemo)

C. DAAs

D. Rituximab alone

E. Steroids alone

  1. 35-year-old woman, SLE, cryoglobulins+, Low C3/C4, RF+, proteinuria 4g/day. Biopsy: MPGN, microtubules. Management:

A. DAAs

B. MMF + Steroids (standard lupus nephritis protocol)

C. Rituximab alone

D. Plasma exchange

E. Cyclophosphamide alone

  1. HCV+ patient with cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis, severe RPGN (Cr 350→550 in 1 week), diffuse alveolar haemorrhage. Immediate management:

A. Start DAAs

B. Plasma exchange + Rituximab + IV Methylprednisolone → then DAAs

C. Rituximab alone

D. Steroids alone

C. Cyclophosphamide + plasma exchange

  1. Patient with cryoglobulinaemia, sample sent on ice. Lab reports "cryoglobulins negative." Correct action:

A. Accept negative result

B. Repeat sample in WARM tube (37°C) — cold precipitates in tube

C. Diagnose cryo-negative vasculitis

D. Send for PCR

E. Start treatment empirically

  1. Type II cryoglobulinaemia — after HCV cure with DAAs, cryoglobulins persist. Next step:

A. Stop all treatment

B. Add Rituximab (targets B-cell clone producing RF)

C. Increase DAA duration

D. Plasma exchange

E. Steroids

  1. Cryoglobulinaemic GN biopsy — EM shows microtubules. Size:

A. 10–20nm

B. 30–50nm (hollow)

C. 100nm

D. 200nm

E. Variable

  1. Monoclonal gammopathy with cryoglobulinaemia — screen for:

A. HCV only

B. Lymphoproliferative disorder (skeletal survey, bone marrow, CT)

C. SLE only

D. RA only

E. No screening needed

  1. Cryoglobulinaemia patient develops mononeuritis multiplex. Management:

A. DAAs only

B. Plasma exchange + Rituximab + Steroids (severe vasculitis)

C. Gabapentin only

D. NSAIDs

E. Physiotherapy only

  1. Monitoring post-DAA cure in HCV cryoglobulinaemia:

A. HCV RNA only

B. HCV RNA (SVR12), Cryoglobulins, C4, RF, Renal function, Proteinuria q3mo

C. C4 only

D. Cryoglobulins only

E. No monitoring needed

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Cryoglobulinaemia Type II cause?

A: HCV (80–90%)

  • Q: Cryoglobulinaemia Type I association?

A: Lymphoproliferative malignancy

  • Q: Cryoglobulinaemia Type III association?

A: SLE, RA, Sjögren's

  • Q: Hallmark complement?

A: Low C4 (classical pathway)

  • Q: RF in cryoglobulinaemia?

A: High (IgM monoclonal has RF activity)

  • Q: EM finding?

A: Microtubules 30–50nm (hollow)

  • Q: Sample handling?

A: WARM tube (37°C)

  • Q: Meltzer's triad?

A: Purpura, arthralgia, weakness

  • Q: Type II mild Rx?

A: DAAs

  • Q: Severe Rx?

A: PLEX + Rituximab + Steroids

  • Q: Monoclonal IgM κ = ?

A: Type II

  • Q: Microtubules = ?

A: Cryoglobulin precipitates (pathognomonic)

  • Q: ANCA in cryo?

A: Usually negative

  • Q: Cryoglobulins tube?

A: WARM (37°C)

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — HCV accounts for 80–90% of Type II (mixed) cryoglobulinaemia.
  2. B — Classical pathway consumption → Low C4 (hallmark); C3 low/normal.
  3. B — Type I = monoclonal = lymphoproliferative (Waldenström, myeloma, CLL, lymphoma).
  4. C — 30–50nm hollow microtubules (cryoglobulin precipitates) = pathognomonic.
  5. B — Cryoglobulins precipitate at <37°C; if tube cools, they precipitate in tube → false negative. Must send warm (37°C).
  6. B — Meltzer's triad: palpable purpura, arthralgia, weakness/fatigue.
  7. C — Mild HCV cryo: DAAs alone (cure HCV → cryoglobulins clear in 90%).
  8. B — Severe (RPGN, nephrotic, alveolar haemorrhage): PLEX + Rituximab + Steroids acutely, then DAAs.
  9. B — Monoclonal IgM has rheumatoid factor activity → High RF.
  10. B — ANCA usually negative in cryoglobulinaemia; positive in ANCA vasculitis.

SBAs

  1. B — Moderate-severe HCV cryo (proteinuria 2g): DAAs + Rituximab. DAAs alone for <1g proteinuria.
  2. B — Type I = malignancy-driven → treat underlying clone (rituximab-chemo).
  3. B — SLE cryoglobulinaemia: standard lupus nephritis protocol (MMF + steroids).
  4. B — RPGN + alveolar haemorrhage = life-threatening vasculitis → PLEX + Rituximab + IV steroids.
  5. B — Cold sample → cryoglobulins precipitate in tube → false negative. Repeat in warm tube.
  6. B — Persistent cryoglobulins post-HCV cure = persistent B-cell clone → Rituximab targets RF-producing clone.
  7. B — Microtubules 30–50nm hollow = cryoglobulin EM signature.
  8. B — Monoclonal cryoglobulin = screen for lymphoproliferative (myeloma, Waldenström, lymphoma).
  9. B — Mononeuritis multiplex = severe vasculitis → PLEX + Rituximab + Steroids.
  10. B — Comprehensive monitoring: HCV SVR12, cryoglobulin levels, C4, RF, renal function, proteinuria.

21. Summary

Cryoglobulinaemic Glomerulonephritis is a Must Know 🔴 immune-complex GN topic.

Classification: Type I (monoclonal, malignancy), Type II (mixed monoclonal+polyclonal, HCV 80–90%), Type III (polyclonal, SLE/RA).

Labs: Low C4 (hallmark), High RF, Cryoglobulins+ (send warm tube), HCV RNA+ (Type II).

Renal: MPGN pattern, subendothelial microtubules (30–50nm) on EM.

Clinical: Meltzer's triad (purpura, arthralgia, weakness) + neuropathy, skin ulcers.

Management: Type II (HCV) — DAAs first; add rituximab if moderate/severe; PLEX + Rituximab + Steroids for RPGN/severe. Type I — treat malignancy. Type III — treat autoimmune.

PLEX indications: RPGN, severe neuropathy, hyperviscosity, alveolar haemorrhage.

Exam focus: Three types, Low C4/High RF, microtubules EM, warm tube, DAAs for HCV, PLEX+Rituximab for severe, Meltzer's triad.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.13 Post Streptococcal Gn Psgn
Topic: Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (PSGN) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)]], [[MPGN and C3 Glomerulopathy (DDD, C3GN)]], [[Anti-GBM Disease (Goodpasture Syndrome)]], [[Infective Endocarditis]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
PSGN = immune-complex GN following group A β-haemolytic Streptococcus (GAS) infection (pharyngitis/impetigo). Peak 1–3 weeks post-infection. Children 5–15yr. Nephritic syndrome: haematuria (cola-coloured urine), RBC casts, hypertension, oliguria, periorbital oedema. Low C3, normal C4. ASO titre ↑. Subepithelial "humps" on EM. Self-limited (95% childhood recovery). Adults: worse prognosis, ↑ CKD risk.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise classic post-strep presentation (nephritic syndrome 1–3 wks post-GAS)
  • Interpret serology (ASO, anti-DNase B, C3, C4)
  • Differentiate from other nephritic causes (IgA, anti-GBM, ANCA, lupus, C3G)
  • Manage supportively (BP control, volume, antibiotics for residual infection)
  • Identify indications for biopsy (atypical features, adult, persistent)
  • Counsel on prognosis (childhood vs adult)

2. Epidemiology & Aetiology

ParameterDetail
OrganismGroup A β-haemolytic Streptococcus (GAS) — M-types 1, 2, 4, 12, 25, 49 (nephritogenic)
Infection sitePharyngitis (most common) or Impetigo (skin) — M-types 49, 55, 57, 59, 60
Latency1–3 weeks post-pharyngitis; 3–6 weeks post-impetigo
AgeChildren 5–15 years (peak); rare <3yr, >40yr
SexMale = Female
GeographyWorldwide; ↓ in developed (antibiotics), still common in developing

3. Pathogenesis

  • Immune complexes (streptococcal antigen + antibody) deposit in subepithelial space of GBM
  • Antigens: Streptococcal pyrogenic exotoxin B (SpeB), nephritis-associated plasmin receptor (NAPlr), streptococcal DNAase B
  • Complement: Alternative pathway activation → Low C3, Normal C4
  • EM: Subepithelial "humps" (large, dome-shaped deposits on epithelial side of GBM)

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureTypical (Child)Atypical / Adult
Gross haematuriaCoca-cola / tea-coloured urine (90%)Microscopic may predominate
Periorbital oedemaMorning, improves by eveningMay be anasarca
Hypertension60–80% (volume + RAAS)Often severe
Oliguria/AKIMild-moderate (20–30%)More severe, dialysis may be needed
Flank painRarePossible
FeverLow-grade (if recent infection)Uncommon
ASO titre↑ (peaks 3–5 wks)May be negative (impetigo)

5. Laboratory Findings

TestPSGN TypicalDifferential Clues
UrineHaematuria (RBC >50/HPF), RBC casts, proteinuria (subnephrotic 0.5–3g/day)RBC casts = glomerular
Serum C3Low (85–90%) — normalises 6–8 wksPersistently low → C3G, MPGN, SLE, endocarditis
Serum C4NormalLow C4 = classical pathway (SLE, cryo, MPGN)
ASO titre↑ >200 IU/mL (or rising) — peaks 3–5 wksImpetigo: ASO may be low → Anti-DNase B better
Anti-DNase B↑ (more sensitive for skin infection)Use if ASO negative, impetigo history
Anti-streptolysin O (ASO)Not streptolysin O specific—
Throat/skin cultureMay be negative (latent phase)Confirms recent GAS if positive
CBCNormal or leukocytosis—
C3 normalisation by 8 weeks = typical PSGN. Persistent low C3 >12 weeks → consider C3G, MPGN, SLE, infective endocarditis.

6. Differential Diagnosis (Nephritic Syndrome)

ConditionC3C4Key Differentiator
PSGNLowNormalRecent GAS, ASO↑, humps EM, self-limited
IgA NephropathyNormalNormalCoincident with URTI (not 1–3 wks after), no ASO rise
Anti-GBMNormal/LowNormalLinear IgG, anti-GBM+, pulmonary bleed
ANCA VasculitisNormalNormalp-ANCA/c-ANCA, pauci-immune, no ASO
Lupus NephritisLowLowFull-house IF, ANA/dsDNA+, systemic features
C3G (DDD/C3GN)LowNormalC3 dominant IF, C3NeF+, persistent low C3 >12 wks
Infective EndocarditisLowLow/NormalBlood cultures +, murmur, embolic phenomena
CryoglobulinaemiaLowLowCryoglobulins+, HCV+, low C4

7. Indications for Renal Biopsy

IndicationReason
Adult >40 yearsHigher malignancy/vasculitis risk; PSGN rare
Atypical featuresNephrotic-range proteinuria, severe AKI, persistent low C3 >12 wks
Systemic symptomsFever, weight loss, rash, arthralgia (suggest SLE, vasculitis, IE)
Persistent proteinuria/haematuria>6 months (risk of chronic GN)
Uncertain diagnosisDifferentiate from IgA, C3G, lupus, anti-GBM
Children with classic presentation: Biopsy NOT routinely needed.

8. Management

Supportive (Mainstay)

IssueManagement
HypertensionACEi/ARB (if no hyperkalaemia/AKI contraindication), CCB, diuretics; target <130/80
Volume overloadLoop diuretic (furosemide) ± fluid restriction
AKIConservative — avoid nephrotoxins, monitor electrolytes, dialysis if severe (rare in kids)
Residual GAS infectionPenicillin V 500mg QID × 10 days (or Amoxicillin) — eradicates carriage, prevents spread
Contact prophylaxisHousehold contacts: throat culture → treat if GAS+

Immunosuppression

SituationApproach
Typical childhood PSGNNOT INDICATED — self-limited
Adult with severe diseaseConsider steroids ± cyclophosphamide if crescentic >50% / rapidly progressive
Persistent low C3 + proteinuria >3.5gEvaluate for C3G/MPGN — treat accordingly

9. Prognosis

Children (95% complete recovery)

OutcomeFrequency
Complete resolution95% (proteinuria/haematuria resolve by 1–2 yrs)
Persistent microscopic haematuria5–10% (benign)
Proteinuria >1yr2–5%
CKD/ESRD<1%

Adults (Worse)

OutcomeFrequency
Complete resolution50–60%
Persistent proteinuria30–40%
CKD/ESRD10–20%
RecurrenceRare (but may indicate C3G/mutation)
Risk factors for poor outcome: Age >40, nephrotic syndrome, severe AKI (dialysis), crescents >50%, persistent low C3 >12 wks.

10. Complications

ComplicationManagement
Hypertensive encephalopathyIV labetalol/nicardipine, ICU
Acute pulmonary oedemaIV furosemide, nitrates, NIPPV
AKI requiring dialysisTemporary (usually) — supportive
Post-strep reactive arthritisNSAIDs, self-limited
Rheumatic feverSeparate entity (Jones criteria) — not caused by PSGN

11. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- PSGN: Nephritic syndrome 1–3 wks post-GAS pharyngitis/impetigo. Children 5–15yr.
- Classic: Cola-coloured urine, periorbital oedema, hypertension, RBC casts.
- Labs: Low C3, Normal C4, ASO titre ↑ (peaks 3–5 wks), Anti-DNase B for impetigo.
- EM: Subepithelial "humps" (pathognomonic).
- C3 normalises by 8 wks — persistent low C3 >12 wks = NOT PSGN (think C3G, MPGN, SLE, IE).
- Management: Supportive (BP, volume, antibiotics for carriage). No steroids in typical childhood PSGN.
- Prognosis: Children 95% recovery; Adults 10–20% ESRD.
- Biopsy NOT needed in classic childhood presentation.
- Differential: IgA (synpharyngitic), anti-GBM, ANCA, lupus, C3G, IE.

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
PSGN = immediate post-infectionLatency 1–3 wks (pharyngitis), 3–6 wks (impetigo)
ASO always positiveImpetigo: ASO often negative → use Anti-DNase B
Low C3 + Low C4 = PSGNPSGN = Low C3 + Normal C4. Low C4 = SLE, cryo, IE
Steroids help childhood PSGNNo steroids — self-limited, supportive only
C3 stays low forever in PSGNC3 normalises by 6–8 wks — persistent low C3 = C3G/MPGN/SLE/IE
Adults recover like childrenAdults: 10–20% ESRD, higher persistent proteinuria
Biopsy all nephritic syndromeChildhood classic PSGN: NO biopsy needed
PSGN causes rheumatic feverSeparate entity — PSGN ≠ rheumatic fever (different M-types, different immune response)
Impetigo PSGN = throat PSGNDifferent M-types (49, 55–60), longer latency, ASO may be negative
ASO titre = single value diagnosticRising titre (convalescent > acute) more diagnostic than single value

13. Mnemonics

  • PSGN triad: Haematuria + Hypertension + Oedema = HHO
  • PSGN complement: C3 Low, C4 Normal = C3L C4N
  • PSGN latency: Pharyngitis 1–3 wks; Impetigo 3–6 wks = PHARYNGITIS SHORT, IMPETIGO LONG
  • PSGN EM: Humps = Huge Under Membrane Subepithelial
  • ASO alternative: Anti-DNase B for Body (skin/impetigo)
  • Prognosis: Kids = 95% OK; Adults = 20% ESRD = K95 A20

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Post-Strep GN))
    Aetiology
      Organism[Group A Strep (GAS)]
      Types[M1,2,4,12,25,49 pharyngitis; M49,55-60 impetigo]
      Latency[1-3 wks pharyngitis; 3-6 wks impetigo]
    Clinical
      Age[Children 5-15yr peak]
      Presentation[Cola urine, Periorbital oedema, HTN, RBC casts]
      AKI[20-30% mild-moderate]
    Labs
      Urine[Haematuria, RBC casts, Proteinuria <3.5g]
      Complement[Low C3, Normal C4 → normalises 8 wks]
      Serology[ASO↑ (peaks 3-5 wks), Anti-DNase B (impetigo)]
      EM[Subepithelial HUMPS]
    Differential
      IgA[Synpharyngitic, no ASO rise]
      Anti-GBM[Linear IgG, pulmonary]
      ANCA[Pauci-immune, ANCA+]
      SLE[Low C3+C4, Full-house]
      C3G[Persistent low C3, C3 dominant]
      IE[Blood cultures+, murmur]
    Management
      Supportive[BP control, Diuretics, Antibiotics for carriage]
      No_Steroids[Childhood typical]
      Adult_Severe[Consider if crescents>50%/RPGN]
    Prognosis
      Children[95% recovery]
      Adults[10-20% ESRD]
      C3_Course[Normalises 8 wks → if not = C3G/MPGN/SLE/IE]

15. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Latency period (pharyngitis vs impetigo)
  2. Classic triad (haematuria, HTN, oedema)
  3. Complement profile (Low C3, Normal C4)
  4. ASO vs Anti-DNase B indication
  5. EM finding (subepithelial humps)
  6. C3 normalisation timeline
  7. When to biopsy
  8. Childhood vs adult prognosis

16. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

17. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Classic presentation, latency, Low C3/Normal C4, ASO/anti-DNase B, humps EM, supportive Rx, no steroids in kids, C3 normalises 8 wks
Should Know 🟡Impetigo vs pharyngitis differences, biopsy indications, adult prognosis, differential diagnosis table, contact prophylaxis
Nice to Know 🟢Nephritogenic M-types, SpeB/NAPlr antigens, genetic susceptibility, long-term follow-up studies

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Typical latency between streptococcal pharyngitis and PSGN:

A. 1–3 days

B. 1–3 weeks

C. 1–3 months

D. 3–6 weeks

E. Immediate

  1. Characteristic complement profile in PSGN:

A. Low C3, Low C4

B. Low C3, Normal C4

C. Normal C3, Low C4

D. Normal C3, Normal C4

E. Low C3, Low C4, Low CH50

  1. EM hallmark of PSGN:

A. Subendothelial deposits

B. Mesangial deposits

C. Subepithelial "humps"

D. Intramembranous dense deposits

E. Tubuloreticular inclusions

  1. ASO titre interpretation — most diagnostic:

A. Single value >200 IU/mL

B. Rising titre (convalescent > acute)

C. Falling titre

D. Any positive value

E. ASO not useful

  1. Best serology for post-impetigo PSGN:

A. ASO titre

B. Anti-DNase B

C. Anti-hyaluronidase

C. Anti-streptokinase

E. Anti-NADase

  1. C3 normalisation in typical childhood PSGN:

A. 1–2 weeks

B. 6–8 weeks

C. 3–4 months

C. 6 months

E. Never (persistently low)

  1. Management of typical childhood PSGN:

A. High-dose steroids

B. Supportive (BP, volume, antibiotics for carriage) — NO steroids

C. Cyclophosphamide

D. Plasma exchange

E. Rituximab

  1. Prognosis in children with PSGN:

A. 50% recovery

B. 95% complete recovery

C. 70% recovery

D. 80% recovery

E. 60% recovery

  1. Indication for renal biopsy in PSGN:

A. All children

B. Adult >40, atypical features, persistent low C3 >12 wks, nephrotic syndrome

C. All adults

D. Only if ASO negative

E. Never indicated

  1. Persistent low C3 >12 weeks after PSGN suggests:

A. Normal recovery

B. C3G, MPGN, SLE, or Infective Endocarditis

C. Treatment failure

D. Steroid resistance

E. Reinfection

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 8-year-old boy, 2 weeks post-strep throat, cola-coloured urine, periorbital oedema, BP 130/85. Urine: RBC casts, proteinuria 1.2g/day. C3 low, C4 normal, ASO 400. Management:

A. Prednisolone 1mg/kg

B. Supportive: ACEi/ARB for BP, furosemide for oedema, penicillin V × 10 days

C. IV methylprednisolone 500mg × 3 days

D. Cyclophosphamide 500mg/m²

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 10-year-old girl, post-impetigo PSGN. ASO negative. Best confirmatory serology:

A. Repeat ASO in 2 weeks

B. Anti-DNase B titre

C. Anti-hyaluronidase

D. Anti-streptokinase

E. Throat culture

  1. 35-year-old man, PSGN presentation (cola urine, HTN, RBC casts), C3 low, C4 normal, ASO 800. Biopsy: 60% crescents. Management:

A. Supportive only

B. Steroids ± cyclophosphamide (RPGN protocol)

C. No treatment needed

D. Plasma exchange

E. Rituximab

  1. Child with PSGN, C3 still low at 14 weeks. Next step:

A. Repeat ASO

B. Renal biopsy (exclude C3G, MPGN, SLE, IE)

C. Start steroids

C. Increase penicillin dose

E. Observe — will normalise eventually

  1. Adult with PSGN — risk factor for poor prognosis:

A. Age <30

B. Age >40, nephrotic proteinuria, severe AKI, crescents >50%

C. Male sex

D. High ASO titre

E. Short latency

  1. Prophylaxis for household contacts of PSGN patient:

A. All get penicillin × 10 days

B. Throat culture → treat if GAS positive

C. Azithromycin × 5 days for all

D. No prophylaxis needed

E. Benzathine penicillin IM for all

  1. Differentiating PSGN from IgA nephropathy:

A. PSGN: synpharyngitic haematuria

B. PSGN: 1–3 wks POST-infection; IgA: DURING/COINCIDENT with URTI

C. IgA: Low C3

D. PSGN: No RBC casts

E. IgA: ASO positive

  1. Impetigo-associated PSGN vs Pharyngitis-associated:

A. Shorter latency

B. Longer latency (3–6 wks), different M-types (49,55-60), ASO often negative

C. Higher C3

D. No hypertension

E. No RBC casts

  1. 95% recovery statistic in PSGN refers to:

A. Adults

B. Children (5–15yr)

C. All ages

D. Impetigo-only

E. Pharyngitis-only

  1. Monitoring post-PSGN — persistent proteinuria at 1 year:

A. Normal

B. Investigate for C3G, MPGN, IgA, secondary causes

C. Start steroids

D. Repeat ASO

E. Increase ACEi dose only

20. Flashcards

  • Q: PSGN latency pharyngitis?

A: 1–3 weeks

  • Q: PSGN latency impetigo?

A: 3–6 weeks

  • Q: PSGN classic triad?

A: Haematuria, HTN, Periorbital oedema

  • Q: PSGN complement?

A: Low C3, Normal C4 (normalises 8 wks)

  • Q: PSGN EM?

A: Subepithelial humps

  • Q: ASO peak?

A: 3–5 weeks post-infection

  • Q: Impetigo PSGN serology?

A: Anti-DNase B (ASO often negative)

  • Q: PSGN management kids?

A: Supportive only — NO steroids

  • Q: PSGN prognosis kids?

A: 95% complete recovery

  • Q: PSGN prognosis adults?

A: 10–20% ESRD

  • Q: C3 normalisation?

A: 6–8 weeks

  • Q: Persistent low C3 >12 wks?

A: Think C3G, MPGN, SLE, IE

  • Q: Biopsy indication?

A: Adult >40, atypical, persistent low C3, nephrotic

  • Q: PSGN vs IgA timing?

A: PSGN = POST-infection (1–3 wks); IgA = DURING URTI

  • Q: Contact prophylaxis?

A: Throat culture → treat if GAS+

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Latency 1–3 weeks after pharyngitis; 3–6 weeks after impetigo.
  2. B — Classic PSGN: Alternative pathway activation → Low C3, Normal C4.
  3. C — Subepithelial "humps" = large dome-shaped deposits on epithelial side of GBM.
  4. B — Rising titre (convalescent > acute) more diagnostic than single value.
  5. B — Anti-DNase B more sensitive for skin/impetigo strains (ASO often negative).
  6. B — C3 normalises by 6–8 weeks in typical PSGN.
  7. B — Childhood PSGN: supportive only (BP, volume, abx for carriage). No immunosuppression.
  8. B — 95% of children recover completely.
  9. B — Biopsy if adult >40, atypical, persistent low C3 >12 wks, nephrotic syndrome, systemic symptoms.
  10. B — Persistent low C3 >12 wks = NOT typical PSGN; consider C3G, MPGN, SLE, infective endocarditis.

SBAs

  1. B — Typical childhood PSGN: supportive management only. Penicillin V eradicates carriage.
  2. B — Post-impetigo PSGN: ASO often negative; Anti-DNase B is the sensitive test.
  3. B — Adult with crescentic RPGN (60% crescents): consider immunosuppression (steroids ± CyC).
  4. B — Persistent low C3 at 14 weeks = atypical → biopsy to exclude C3G, MPGN, SLE, IE.
  5. B — Poor prognosis: age >40, nephrotic proteinuria, severe AKI, crescents >50%.
  6. B — Household contacts: culture first, treat only if GAS positive (avoid unnecessary antibiotics).
  7. B — Key timing difference: PSGN = 1–3 wks POST-infection; IgA = DURING/COINCIDENT with URTI.
  8. B — Impetigo PSGN: longer latency, M-types 49/55–60, ASO often negative.
  9. B — 95% recovery refers specifically to children (5–15yr). Adults: 10–20% ESRD.
  10. B — Persistent proteinuria >1yr = investigate for underlying chronic GN (C3G, MPGN, IgA).

22. Summary

Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Aetiology: GAS infection (pharyngitis/impetigo) → immune complexes (subepithelial) → nephritic syndrome after 1–3 wks (pharyngitis) or 3–6 wks (impetigo).

Clinical: Cola-coloured urine, periorbital oedema, hypertension, RBC casts. Children 5–15yr.

Labs: Low C3, Normal C4 (alternative pathway), ASO ↑ (peaks 3–5 wks), Anti-DNase B for impetigo. EM: Subepithelial humps.

C3 normalises by 8 weeks — persistent low C3 >12 wks = C3G/MPGN/SLE/IE.

Management: Supportive (BP control, diuretics, Penicillin V × 10 days for carriage). NO steroids in typical childhood PSGN.

Prognosis: Children 95% recovery; Adults 10–20% ESRD (worse if crescents >50%, nephrotic, severe AKI).

Biopsy: Not routine in classic childhood; indicated if adult >40, atypical, persistent low C3, nephrotic.

Exam focus: Latency, complement profile, ASO/anti-DNase B, humps EM, supportive Rx, C3 timeline, child vs adult prognosis, biopsy indications.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.14 Rapidly Progressive Gn Rpgn
Topic: Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis (RPGN) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Anti-GBM Disease (Goodpasture Syndrome)]], [[MPGN and C3 Glomerulopathy (DDD, C3GN)]], [[Cryoglobulinaemic Glomerulonephritis]], [[ANCA-Associated Vasculitis (GPA, MPA, EGPA)]], [[Lupus Nephritis]], [[Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
RPGN = clinical syndrome of rapid loss of renal function (days–weeks) with glomerular disease. Pathology: Crescentic GN (>50% crescents). Three aetiological categories: Type I (Anti-GBM), Type II (Immune-complex), Type III (Pauci-immune/ANCA). Emergency: treat based on serology (Anti-GBM, ANCA, ANA, Cryo, Complement). Pulse steroids + cyclophosphamide/rituximab ± plasma exchange.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define RPGN (rapid AKI + active sediment + crescents)
  • Classify by aetiology (Anti-GBM, Immune-complex, Pauci-immune)
  • Recognise clinical presentations and serological patterns
  • Apply urgent diagnostic workup (serology, biopsy)
  • Manage emergently based on category (pulse steroids + CyC/rituximab ± PLEX)
  • Differentiate from other causes of rapid AKI
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP emergency nephrology vignettes

2. Definition & Clinical Presentation

FeatureDetail
DefinitionRapid loss of GFR (50% decline in days–weeks) + active urinary sediment (haematuria, RBC casts, proteinuria)
PathologyCrescentic GN — cellular crescents in >50% glomeruli
TimecourseDays to weeks (vs CKD = months–years)
PresentationAKI, haematuria (microscopic/macroscopic), proteinuria (subnephrotic), hypertension, oedema, ± pulmonary haemorrhage

3. Aetiological Classification (Crucial for Management)

CategoryMechanismSerologyExamples
Type I: Anti-GBMAnti-α3(IV)NC1 collagen IgGAnti-GBM+, ANCA−Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture's if pulmonary)
Type II: Immune-ComplexImmune complex depositionLow C3/C4, ANA/dsDNA+, Cryo+, ANCA−Lupus nephritis (III/IV), Post-strep, Cryoglobulinaemia, IE, IgA (rare), MPGN
Type III: Pauci-immuneANCA-mediated (neutrophil activation)ANCA+ (p-ANCA/MPO or c-ANCA/PR3), C3/C4 normalGPA, MPA, EGPA, Renal-limited vasculitis
Double-positive (Anti-GBM + ANCA) = 20–30% of Type I — treat as both.

4. Serological Workup (Order STAT)

TestType IType IIType III
Anti-GBMPositiveNegativeNegative
ANCA (MPO/PR3)Negative (± double-positive)NegativePositive
C3, C4Normal/Low C3Low C3 ± Low C4Normal
ANA, dsDNANegativePositive (SLE)Negative
CryoglobulinsNegativePositive (Type II/III)Negative
Hepatitis B/C, HIVNegativePositive (HCV, HBV)Negative

5. Clinical Differentiation

FeatureAnti-GBM (Type I)Immune-Complex (Type II)Pauci-immune/ANCA (Type III)
AgeBimodal (20–30, 60–70)Variable (SLE: young ♀; Post-strep: children)Older (>50)
Pulmonary haemorrhageCommon (40–60%)Rare (SLE, cryo)Common in GPA/MPA
Anti-GBMPositiveNegativeNegative
ANCANegative*NegativePositive
ComplementNormalLow C3/C4Normal
Biopsy IFLinear IgGGranular ("full-house" SLE)Pauci-immune (scant)
Double-positive20–30% ANCA+—20–30% Anti-GBM+
Always check both Anti-GBM and ANCA in RPGN — double-positive changes management.

6. Emergency Management (Start IMMEDIATELY)

General Principles

  • Do NOT wait for biopsy if clinical + serology clear
  • Pulse IV Methylprednisolone 500–1000mg × 3 days → Oral Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day
  • Cyclophosphamide (IV 500mg/m² q2wk × 3mo OR oral 2mg/kg/day × 3mo)
  • Plasma Exchange (PLEX) based on category

Category-Specific Regimens

Type I: Anti-GBM

ComponentRegimen
PLEXDaily × 14 days (or until anti-GBM negative) — MANDATORY
CyclophosphamideIV 500mg/m² q2wk × 3mo
SteroidsPulse → Oral taper 3–6mo
RituximabAlternative to CyC (especially young women — fertility)
Anti-GBM: PLEX = life-saving (removes antibody). Dialysis-dependent at presentation → <10% renal recovery.

Type II: Immune-Complex (SLE, Cryo, Post-strep, IE)

AetiologyRegimen
SLE (Class III/IV)Pulse steroids → MMF 2–3g/day OR IV CyC 500mg/m² q2wk + steroids
CryoglobulinaemiaPLEX + Rituximab + Steroids (if severe/RPGN)
Post-strepUsually self-limited; supportive; steroids if crescentic >50%
Infective EndocarditisAntibiotics (surgery if needed); immunosuppression contraindicated
IgA NephropathyRarely RPGN; if so: steroids ± CyC
Identify and treat infection first (IE, post-strep) — immunosuppression can worsen.

Type III: Pauci-immune (ANCA Vasculitis — GPA, MPA, EGPA)

ComponentRegimen
PLEXIf Cr >500 μmol/L or pulmonary haemorrhage (PEXIVAS trial: PLEX not routine for all)
InductionRituximab 375mg/m² weekly × 4 (preferred) OR IV CyC 500mg/m² q2wk × 3mo
SteroidsPulse → Oral taper (RAVE/PEXIVAS: lower cumulative steroid better)
MaintenanceRituximab q6mo OR Azathioprine/MMf (MAINRITSAN: rituximab superior)
ANCA RPGN: Rituximab non-inferior to CyC (RAVE); preferred for relapsing/young women.

7. PLEX Indications Summary

CategoryPLEX Indication
Anti-GBMALWAYS (daily × 14d)
ANCA (Type III)Cr >500 (5.7 mg/dL) OR Pulmonary haemorrhage
CryoglobulinaemiaRPGN, alveolar haemorrhage, severe neuropathy
SLENot routine (consider if TMA, catastrophic APS)
TMA/HUSDaily (see TMA topic)

8. Prognosis by Category

CategoryRenal Recovery (if treated early)Relapse Risk
Anti-GBMCr <300: 70–80%; Dialysis-dependent: <10%Rare (<5%) once Ab cleared
SLEGood with early tx (MMF/CyC)Moderate (30–40%)
ANCACr <300: 70–80%High (30–50%) — maintenance needed
CryoglobulinaemiaVariable (HCV cure improves)High if HCV persists

9. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- RPGN = rapid AKI + active sediment + crescents >50%.
- Three categories: Anti-GBM (Type I), Immune-complex (Type II), Pauci-immune/ANCA (Type III).
