- Emergency workup: Anti-GBM, ANCA (MPO/PR3), ANA/dsDNA, C3/C4, Cryoglobulins, Hepatitis.
- Anti-GBM: Linear IgG, pulmonary haemorrhage common, PLEX mandatory × 14d.
- ANCA vasculitis: Rituximab preferred induction (non-inferior to CyC, fertility-sparing).
- Double-positive (Anti-GBM + ANCA): 20–30% — treat as both.
- PLEX: Anti-GBM = always; ANCA = if Cr >500 or pulmonary bleed.
- SLE RPGN: MMF or CyC + steroids (not PLEX routine).
- Prognosis: Dialysis-dependent Anti-GBM → <10% recovery; ANCA high relapse → maintenance.

10. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
RPGN = always ANCAOnly Type III = ANCA; Type I = Anti-GBM; Type II = Immune-complex
PLEX for all RPGNAnti-GBM = always; ANCA = only if Cr >500 or pulmonary bleed
Rituximab for allAnti-GBM: CyC standard (rituximab alternative); ANCA: rituximab preferred; SLE: MMF/CyC
Crescents = RPGN>50% crescents = RPGN; <50% = crescentic GN but not RPGN clinically
Anti-GBM = no ANCA20–30% double-positive — worse prognosis, ↑ relapse
Wait for biopsy before treatmentNO — treat emergently on serology; biopsy confirms
SLE RPGN = PLEXPLEX not routine for SLE; MMF/CyC + steroids first
All pulmonary haemorrhage = Goodpasture'sAlso ANCA vasculitis, SLE, cryoglobulinaemia, catastrophic APS
ANCA typesp-ANCA/MPO = MPA/EGPA/renal-limited; c-ANCA/PR3 = GPA
Post-strep = RPGN rarePost-strep usually self-limited; RPGN rare, steroids if crescents >50%

11. Mnemonics

  • RPGN Types: I = Immune Anti-GBM; II = Immune Complex; III = Pauci-immune ANCA = ICAP
  • Anti-GBM: Linear IgG + Pulmonary = LIP
  • ANCA: PR3 = PR3 GPA; MPO = MPA/EGPA
  • PLEX: Anti-GBM = Always; ANCA = Always if Cr>500/Pulmonary
  • Double-positive: Anti-GBM + ANCA = Awful (worse prognosis)

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((RPGN))
    Definition[Rapid AKI + Active sediment + Crescents >50%]
    Type_I[Anti-GBM (Type I)]
      Serology[Anti-GBM+]
      IF[Linear IgG]
      Pulmonary[Common 40-60%]
      Rx[PLEX x14d + CyC + Steroids]
      Prognosis[Dialysis-dep <10% recovery]
    Type_II[Immune-Complex (Type II)]
      Causes[SLE, Cryo, Post-strep, IE, IgA]
      Serology[Low C3/C4, ANA+, Cryo+]
      IF[Granular (Full-house SLE)]
      Rx[Treat cause: SLE=MMF/CyC; Cryo=PLEX+Rituximab; IE=Abx]
    Type_III[Pauci-immune/ANCA (Type III)]
      Causes[GPA, MPA, EGPA, Renal-limited]
      Serology[ANCA+ (PR3/MPO)]
      IF[Pauci-immune]
      Rx[Rituximab preferred + Steroids; PLEX if Cr>500/Pulm bleed]
      Relapse[High - maintenance Rituximab/AZA]
    Double_Positive[Anti-GBM + ANCA (20-30%)]
      Rx[Both protocols]
      Prognosis[Worse, high relapse]

13. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. RPGN definition (rapid AKI + crescents >50%)
  2. Three aetiological types (Anti-GBM, Immune-complex, Pauci-immune)
  3. Key serology for each (Anti-GBM, ANCA, C3/C4/ANA/Cryo)
  4. Anti-GBM: Linear IgG, PLEX mandatory, pulmonary bleed
  5. ANCA: Rituximab preferred, PLEX if Cr>500/pulmonary
  6. Double-positive (20–30%)
  7. SLE RPGN: MMF/CyC (not PLEX routine)
  8. Prognosis by category (dialysis-dep Anti-GBM <10%, ANCA high relapse)

14. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴RPGN definition, 3 types, serology workup, Anti-GBM PLEX mandatory, ANCA rituximab preferred, PLEX indications, double-positive
Should Know 🟡SLE RPGN management (MMF/CyC), cryoglobulinaemia RPGN, post-strep RPGN, PEXIVAS/RAVE/MAINRITSAN trials, prognosis by category
Nice to Know 🟢Novel ANCA therapies (avacopan, narsoplimab), anti-GBM antigen-specific therapy, complement inhibition in IC-RPGN, biomarkers for relapse prediction

16. MCQs (10)

  1. RPGN histopathological hallmark:

A. Crescents in >50% glomeruli

B. Wire-loop lesions

C. Spike and dome

D. Tram-tracking

E. Foot process effacement

  1. Anti-GBM disease — immunofluorescence pattern:

A. Granular IgG + C3

B. Linear IgG along GBM

C. Mesangial IgA

D. Full-house

E. C3 dominant

  1. ANCA vasculitis — preferred induction agent (RAVE trial):

A. Cyclophosphamide

B. Rituximab

C. Mycophenolate

C. Azathioprine

E. Plasma exchange

  1. Plasma exchange in ANCA RPGN — indication (PEXIVAS):

A. All cases

B. Serum creatinine >500 μmol/L OR pulmonary haemorrhage

C. Only pulmonary haemorrhage

D. Creatinine >300

E. Never indicated

  1. Double-positive Anti-GBM + ANCA prevalence:

A. <5%

B. 20–30%

C. 50%

D. 70%

E. 90%

  1. Anti-GBM RPGN — dialysis-dependent at presentation, renal recovery:

A. 50%

B. 30%

C. <10%

D. 80%

E. 100%

  1. SLE Class III/IV RPGN — first-line induction:

A. Plasma exchange

B. MMF 2–3g/day OR IV Cyclophosphamide + Steroids

C. Rituximab alone

D. Plasma exchange + Rituximab

E. Azathioprine

  1. ANCA type in Microscopic Polyangiitis (MPA):

A. c-ANCA/PR3

B. p-ANCA/MPO

C. Both equally

D. Atypical ANCA

E. Anti-GBM

  1. Maintenance therapy post-ANCA induction (MAINRITSAN):

A. Azathioprine

B. Rituximab q6mo (superior to AZA)

C. MMF

D. Low-dose cyclophosphamide

E. Steroids alone

  1. Anti-GBM disease — PLEX duration:

A. 7 days

B. 14 days (or until anti-GBM negative)

C. 21 days

D. 28 days

E. 3 days

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 55-year-old man, AKI (Cr 450), haematuria, RBC casts, pulmonary haemorrhage. Anti-GBM+, ANCA−. Immediate management:

A. IV Methylprednisolone only

B. PLEX daily × 14d + IV Cyclophosphamide + IV Methylprednisolone

C. Rituximab + steroids

D. Plasma exchange × 7 days

E. Supportive only

  1. 65-year-old woman, AKI (Cr 620), haematuria, proteinuria, no pulmonary symptoms. p-ANCA/MPO+, Anti-GBM−, C3/C4 normal. PLEX indicated?

A. Yes (Cr >500)

B. Yes (Cr >500 — PEXIVAS criterion)

C. No (only if pulmonary bleed)

D. No (PLEX never in ANCA)

E. Only if dialysis-dependent

  1. 25-year-old woman, SLE, AKI (Cr 380), active sediment, biopsy: Class IV lupus nephritis with crescents. Management:

A. PLEX + Rituximab

B. MMF 3g/day + Steroids (or IV CyC + steroids)

C. Rituximab alone

D. Plasma exchange

E. Cyclophosphamide alone

  1. 30-year-old man, GPA (c-ANCA/PR3+), RPGN (Cr 550), no pulmonary bleed. Biopsy: pauci-immune crescentic GN. Induction:

A. Cyclophosphamide only

B. Rituximab 375mg/m² weekly × 4 + Steroids

C. MMF + steroids

D. PLEX + Cyclophosphamide

E. Azathioprine + steroids

  1. Anti-GBM + p-ANCA double-positive patient. Compared to pure Anti-GBM:

A. Better prognosis

B. Worse renal survival, higher relapse risk

C. No difference

D. Lower PLEX requirement

E. No maintenance needed

  1. ANCA vasculitis patient, post-induction (rituximab), remission. Maintenance per MAINRITSAN:

A. Azathioprine 2mg/kg

B. Rituximab 500mg q6mo × 2 years

C. MMF 1g BD

D. Low-dose prednisolone only

E. No maintenance

  1. Patient with RPGN, both Anti-GBM and p-ANCA positive. PLEX duration:

A. 7 days

B. 14 days (Anti-GBM protocol) + ANCA immunosuppression

C. 21 days

D. Until ANCA negative

E. 3 days

  1. ANCA vasculitis with pulmonary haemorrhage. PLEX protocol:

A. Alternate days × 7

B. Daily × 7–14 days (as per Anti-GBM protocol for pulmonary bleed)

C. 3 sessions only

E. Not indicated

  1. RPGN differential — pauci-immune crescentic GN with NO extrarenal features. Most likely:

A. GPA

B. Renal-limited ANCA vasculitis

C. MPA

D. EGPA

E. Anti-GBM

  1. Post-strep GN with crescents >50% (RPGN). Management:

A. Supportive only

B. Steroids (if crescents >50% and progressive)

C. PLEX

D. Cyclophosphamide

E. Rituximab

18. Flashcards

  • Q: RPGN crescent threshold?

A: >50% glomeruli

  • Q: RPGN Type I = ?

A: Anti-GBM (Linear IgG)

  • Q: RPGN Type II = ?

A: Immune-complex (SLE, Cryo, Post-strep, IE)

  • Q: RPGN Type III = ?

A: Pauci-immune / ANCA (GPA, MPA, EGPA)

  • Q: Anti-GBM PLEX?

A: Daily × 14d (mandatory)

  • Q: ANCA PLEX indication?

A: Cr >500 OR pulmonary haemorrhage

  • Q: ANCA induction preferred?

A: Rituximab

  • Q: ANCA maintenance?

A: Rituximab q6mo (superior to AZA)

  • Q: Double-positive prevalence?

A: 20–30%

  • Q: Anti-GBM dialysis recovery?

A: <10%

  • Q: SLE RPGN Rx?

A: MMF or CyC + steroids (NO routine PLEX)

  • Q: c-ANCA = ?

A: PR3 = GPA

  • Q: p-ANCA = ?

A: MPO = MPA/EGPA

  • Q: Post-strep RPGN Rx?

A: Steroids if progressive crescentic

  • Q: PEXIVAS trial?

A: PLEX not routine for all ANCA; only Cr>500/pulm bleed

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Crescentic GN = crescents >50% glomeruli = RPGN histology.
  2. B — Anti-GBM = linear IgG along GBM (pathognomonic).
  3. B — Rituximab non-inferior to CyC (RAVE), preferred for relapsing/fertility.
  4. B — PEXIVAS: PLEX only if Cr >500 μmol/L (5.7 mg/dL) OR pulmonary haemorrhage.
  5. B — 20–30% of Anti-GBM have concurrent ANCA (usually p-ANCA/MPO).
  6. C — Dialysis-dependent Anti-GBM at presentation → <10% renal recovery.
  7. B — SLE RPGN: MMF (preferred) or IV CyC + steroids; PLEX not routine.
  8. B — MPA/EGPA/renal-limited = p-ANCA/MPO; GPA = c-ANCA/PR3.
  9. B — MAINRITSAN: Rituximab 500mg q6mo × 2yr superior to AZA for relapse prevention.
  10. B — Anti-GBM PLEX: daily × 14 days or until antibody negative.

SBAs

  1. B — Anti-GBM + pulmonary bleed = emergency PLEX + CyC + steroids.
  2. B — Cr 620 (>500) = PLEX indicated per PEXIVAS even without pulmonary bleed.
  3. B — SLE Class IV RPGN: MMF 3g/day or IV CyC + steroids; PLEX not standard.
  4. B — Rituximab preferred induction for ANCA vasculitis (RAVE).
  5. B — Double-positive: worse renal survival, higher relapse (ANCA component).
  6. B — MAINRITSAN: Rituximab 500mg q6mo × 2yr = superior maintenance.
  7. B — Double-positive: PLEX per Anti-GBM protocol (14d) + full ANCA immunosuppression.
  8. B — Pulmonary haemorrhage in ANCA = PLEX daily × 7–14d (same as Anti-GBM protocol).
  9. B — Pauci-immune crescentic GN without extrarenal = renal-limited ANCA vasculitis.
  10. B — Post-strep RPGN rare; if progressive crescentic >50% → steroids (not PLEX/CyC).

20. Summary

Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis (RPGN) is a Must Know 🔴 emergency nephrology topic.

Definition: Rapid AKI (days–weeks) + active sediment + crescents >50%.

Three Categories: Type I = Anti-GBM (linear IgG); Type II = Immune-complex (SLE, Cryo, Post-strep, IE); Type III = Pauci-immune/ANCA (GPA, MPA, EGPA).

Emergency Workup: Anti-GBM, ANCA (MPO/PR3), ANA/dsDNA, C3/C4, Cryoglobulins, Hepatitis.

Management: Anti-GBM: PLEX daily × 14d + CyC + Steroids (mandatory). ANCA: Rituximab preferred + Steroids; PLEX if Cr >500 or pulmonary bleed (PEXIVAS). SLE: MMF/CyC + steroids (PLEX not routine). Cryo: PLEX + Rituximab + Steroids. Double-positive (20–30%): Treat as both.

Prognosis: Dialysis-dependent Anti-GBM <10% recovery; ANCA high relapse → maintenance rituximab.

Exam focus: 3 types, serology, PLEX indications, rituximab vs CyC, double-positive, SLE vs ANCA management, crescent threshold.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.2 Iga Nephropathy Bergers Disease
Topic: Primary Glomerulonephritides — IgA Nephropathy (Berger's Disease) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Membranous Nephropathy]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — FSGS]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Minimal Change Disease]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
IgA Nephropathy = commonest primary GN worldwide. Aberrant IgA1 glycosylation → immune complexes → mesangial deposition. Classic: macroscopic haematuria 1–2 days post-URTI. Diagnosis: renal biopsy (dominant mesangial IgA on IF). Management: supportive (ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i) ± immunosuppression if high-risk.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise clinical presentations (synpharyngitic haematuria, asymptomatic, nephrotic, RPGN)
  • Interpret biopsy findings (LM, IF, EM) and Oxford MEST-C classification
  • Apply risk stratification and treatment algorithm
  • Differentiate from HSP nephritis, post-strep GN, IgA-dominant infection-related GN

2. Epidemiology & Pathogenesis

FeatureDetail
EpidemiologyCommonest primary GN worldwide; young adults (20–30s); M>F; high prevalence in East Asia
PathogenesisAberrant IgA1 O-glycosylation → galactose-deficient IgA1 → anti-glycan IgG/IgA autoantibodies → immune complexes → mesangial deposition → complement activation (alternative pathway) → inflammation
GeneticsGWAS: HLA region, complement regulators (CFH, C3), mucosal immunity loci; familial clustering
AssociationsCeliac disease, liver disease, IBD, HIV, Hank's disease

3. Clinical Presentation

TypeFeatures
Classic (Synpharyngitic)Episodic macroscopic haematuria 1–2 days post-URTI (often with loin pain); self-limiting, recurs
AsymptomaticMicroscopic haematuria ± proteinuria (incidental on screening); most common presentation
Nephrotic Syndrome<5%; heavy proteinuria, oedema, hypoalbuminaemia, hyperlipidaemia
Rapidly Progressive GN (RPGN)Rare; crescentic transformation (>50% crescents); acute kidney injury
Chronic ProgressiveGradual eGFR decline, hypertension, proteinuria

4. Histopathology

ModalityFindings
Light MicroscopyMesangial hypercellularity, matrix expansion; segmental/global sclerosis in chronic; crescents if RPGN
ImmunofluorescenceDominant mesangial IgA (+ C3, IgG, IgM); no IgG dominance (distinguishes from infection-related)
Electron MicroscopyMesangial electron-dense deposits; no subepithelial/subendothelial deposits (if present = overlap)

5. Oxford MEST-C Classification (Prognostic)

LesionScoreDefinitionPrognostic Significance
M (Mesangial hypercellularity)M0/M1M0: <50% glomeruli; M1: ≥50%M1 → worse renal survival
E (Endocapillary hypercellularity)E0/E1E1: hypercellularity in any glomerulusE1 → worse prognosis; responds to immunosuppression
S (Segmental glomerulosclerosis)S0/S1S1: adhesion/sclerosis in any glomerulusS1 → worse renal survival
T (Tubular atrophy/Interstitial fibrosis)T0/T1/T2T0: <25%; T1: 25–50%; T2: >50%Strongest predictor of progression
C (Crescents)C0/C1/C2C0: none; C1: <25%; C2: ≥25%C1/C2 → worse outcome; indicates immunosuppression
[!key]
Oxford MEST-C: M1, E1, S1, T1/2, C1/2 = adverse features. T score is strongest predictor of progression.

6. Risk Stratification & Management Algorithm

Risk CategoryCriteriaManagement
Low RiskProteinuria <0.5g/day, eGFR >60, normal BP, no MEST-C adverse featuresSupportive only: ACEi/ARB (max tolerated), SGLT2i, BP <130/80, statin, weight loss, smoking cessation
Intermediate RiskProteinuria 0.5–1g/day, or eGFR 45–60, or isolated MEST-C E1/S1Supportive + consider 6mo steroids if persistent proteinuria >0.75g despite 3–6mo ACEi/ARB (TESTING trial)
High RiskProteinuria >1g/day, or eGFR <45, or crescents (C1/C2), or T1/T2Immunosuppression: 6mo steroids ± cyclophosphamide (if crescentic); consider MMF/rituximab
RPGN>50% crescents, rapidly rising CrPulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3d → oral pred 1mg/kg + cyclophosphamide 0.5g/m² IV monthly × 6mo (or rituximab)

7. Supportive Care (All Patients)

InterventionTarget / Dose
ACEi/ARBMax tolerated dose; target BP <130/80; monitor K+/Cr
SGLT2 InhibitorDapagliflozin/empagliflozin (if eGFR ≥20); reduces progression by 35–40%
Blood Pressure<130/80 (KDIGO 2021)
LipidsStatin if >50yo or CVD risk
DietLow salt (<2g Na/day), moderate protein (0.8g/kg)
Weight/SmokingBMI <25, smoking cessation
MonitoringUPCR, eGFR, BP every 3–6mo

8. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Feature
Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP) NephritisSystemic vasculitis (palpable purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis); same pathology (IgA) but systemic
Post-Streptococcal GNASO titre ↑, C3 low, subepithelial "humps" on EM; follows strep by 1–3 weeks
IgA-Dominant Infection-Related GNStaph infection, IgG co-dominance on IF, older patients, systemic infection
C3 GlomerulopathyC3 dominant on IF, IgA absent/minimal; alternative pathway dysregulation
Lupus Nephritis Class IIFull-house IF (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q), positive ANA/anti-dsDNA

9. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Commonest primary GN worldwide → IgA Nephropathy
- Classic: Macroscopic haematuria 1–2 days post-URTI (synpharyngitic)
- Biopsy: Mesangial IgA dominant on IF; mesangial deposits on EM
- Oxford MEST-C: T score = strongest predictor of progression
- Supportive: ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + BP control (KDIGO 2021)
- Immunosuppression: Persistent proteinuria >1g despite 3–6mo ACEi/ARB → 6mo steroids (STOP-IgAN, TESTING trials)
- RPGN with crescents: Pulse steroids + cyclophosphamide/rituximab
- HSP vs IgA: HSP = systemic (purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis); same renal pathology

10. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
IgA = always benign30–40% progress to ESRD at 20 years
All need immunosuppressionOnly high-risk (>1g proteinuria, crescents, declining eGFR); low-risk = supportive
Post-strep = IgA dominantPost-strep = IgG dominant, C3 low, subepithelial humps
HSP = different diseaseHSP nephritis = systemic IgA vasculitis; same renal pathology
Steroids always harmful6mo steroids reduce ESRD risk in high-risk (TESTING trial; lower dose safer)
MEST-C all equalT score (tubular atrophy/fibrosis) is strongest predictor

11. Mnemonics

  • IgA classic: Synpharyngitic Haematuria post-URTI = SHU
  • Oxford MEST-C: Mesangial, Endocapillary, Segmental sclerosis, Tubular atrophy, Crescents = MEST-C
  • Biopsy: Mesangial IgA Deposits = MID
  • Risk: Proteinuria >1g, EGFR <45, Crescents, Tubular atrophy = PECT

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((IgA Nephropathy))
    Pathogenesis
      Aberrant_IgA1[Galactose-deficient IgA1]
      Immune_Complexes[Anti-glycan Abs]
      Mesangial_Deposition[Mesangial immune complexes]
      Complement[Alternative pathway]
    Clinical
      Synpharyngitic[Post-URTI haematuria]
      Asymptomatic[Microscopic haematuria]
      Nephrotic[Rare]
      RPGN[Crescents]
    Biopsy
      LM[Mesangial hypercellularity]
      IF[Dominant mesangial IgA]
      EM[Mesangial dense deposits]
      Oxford[MEST-C classification]
    Management
      Supportive[ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, BP]
      Immunosuppression[Steroids ± CYC if high risk]
      RPGN[Pulse steroids + CYC/Rituximab]

13. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. IgA classic presentation (synpharyngitic haematuria timeline)
  2. Biopsy: IF finding (dominant mesangial IgA)
  3. Oxford MEST-C components and which is strongest predictor
  4. When to immunosuppress (proteinuria threshold, crescents)
  5. HSP nephritis vs IgA (systemic vs renal-only)
  6. Supportive care: ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, BP target
  7. TESTING/STOP-IgAN trial significance

14. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Clinical presentation, biopsy (IF/EM), Oxford MEST-C (esp T score), supportive care (ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, BP), immunosuppression threshold (>1g proteinuria)
Should Know 🟡Pathogenesis (aberrant IgA1), TESTING/STOP-IgAN trials, RPGN management, differential (HSP, post-strep, infection-related)
Nice to Know 🟢Genetic associations, novel biomarkers (Gd-IgA1), clinical trials pipeline, complement inhibition therapy

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest primary glomerulonephropathy worldwide:

A. Membranous nephropathy

B. IgA nephropathy

C. FSGS

D. Minimal change disease

E. Post-streptococcal GN

  1. Classic presentation of IgA nephropathy:

A. Nephrotic syndrome

B. Macroscopic haematuria 1–2 days post-URTI

C. Rapidly progressive GN

D. Asymptomatic proteinuria

E. Hypertensive emergency

  1. Immunofluorescence hallmark of IgA nephropathy:

A. Granular capillary wall IgG

B. Dominant mesangial IgA

C. Linear GBM IgG

D. Mesangial C3 only

E. Subepithelial IgG

  1. Oxford MEST-C score - strongest predictor of progression:

A. M (Mesangial hypercellularity)

B. E (Endocapillary hypercellularity)

C. S (Segmental sclerosis)

D. T (Tubular atrophy/Interstitial fibrosis)

E. C (Crescents)

  1. First-line supportive management for all IgA nephropathy patients:

A. High-dose steroids

B. ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + BP <130/80

C. Cyclophosphamide

D. Rituximab

E. Mycophenolate mofetil

  1. Immunosuppression indicated when:

A. Proteinuria >0.5g/day

B. Proteinuria >1g/day despite 3–6mo ACEi/ARB

C. Any microscopic haematuria

D. eGFR >60

E. Oxford M0

  1. TESTING trial investigated:

A. Rituximab in IgA

B. Steroids in IgA (6mo reduced ESRD but increased infections)

C. SGLT2i in IgA

D. CYC in IgA

E. Complement inhibition

  1. Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP) nephritis vs IgA nephropathy:

A. Different IF findings

B. HSP = systemic vasculitis (purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis)

C. HSP has no haematuria

D. HSP only in adults

E. HSP = subepithelial deposits

  1. Post-streptococcal GN distinguishing features:

A. ASO titre elevated, C3 low, subepithelial humps on EM

B. Dominant mesangial IgA

C. Normal C3

D. c-ANCA positive

E. Linear GBM staining

  1. Oxford 'E' lesion indicates:

A. Mesangial hypercellularity

B. Endocapillary hypercellularity

C. Segmental sclerosis

D. Tubular atrophy

E. Crescents

  1. RPGN in IgA (crescentic >50%):

A. ACEi/ARB only

B. Pulse methylprednisolone + cyclophosphamide/rituximab

C. SGLT2i alone

D. Observation

E. Plasma exchange alone

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 22-year-old man, macroscopic haematuria 1 day after sore throat. Biopsy: mesangial hypercellularity, dominant IgA on IF. Diagnosis:

A. Post-streptococcal GN

B. IgA nephropathy

C. Alport syndrome

D. Thin basement membrane disease

E. C3 glomerulopathy

  1. 30-year-old woman, incidental microscopic haematuria + proteinuria 0.8g/day, eGFR 75. Biopsy: M1E0S0T0C0. Management:

A. Pulse steroids + CYC

B. Rituximab

C. ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + BP control, monitor

D. MMF

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 40-year-old man, proteinuria 2.5g/day, eGFR 45, biopsy: M1E1S1T1C1. Best next step:

A. Supportive only

B. 6-month steroids ± cyclophosphamide

C. Rituximab alone

E. Transplant referral

D. ACEi/ARB only

  1. 15-year-old boy, palpable purpura on legs, abdominal pain, arthritis, microscopic haematuria. Renal biopsy: dominant mesangial IgA. Diagnosis:

A. IgA nephropathy

B. Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP) nephritis

C. Post-streptococcal GN

D. Alport syndrome

E. Lupus nephritis

  1. 60-year-old man, Staph aureus bacteraemia, AKI, nephrotic range proteinuria. Biopsy: IgA-dominant, IgG co-dominant, subepithelial deposits. Diagnosis:

A. IgA nephropathy

B. IgA-dominant infection-related GN

C. Membranous nephropathy

D. Post-streptococcal GN

E. C3 glomerulopathy

  1. Oxford MEST-C 'T2' score means:

A. No tubular atrophy

B. <25% tubular atrophy/fibrosis

C. 25–50% tubular atrophy/fibrosis

D. >50% tubular atrophy/interstitial fibrosis

E. Crescents in >50% glomeruli

  1. In IgA nephropathy, which complement pathway is primarily activated?

A. Classical

B. Lectin

C. Alternative

D. Terminal only

E. None

  1. KDIGO 2024 BP target for IgA nephropathy with proteinuria:

A. <140/90

B. <130/80

C. <120/70

D. <150/90

E. No specific target

  1. SGLT2 inhibitor benefit in IgA nephropathy (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY):

A. Reduces proteinuria only

B. Reduces kidney failure progression by ~35–40% regardless of diabetes

C. Only works in diabetic patients

D. Increases eGFR acutely

E. Replaces ACEi/ARB

  1. 25-year-old woman, RPGN (Cr 350, crescents 60% on biopsy), dominant mesangial IgA. Induction:

A. ACEi/ARB

B. Oral prednisolone 1mg/kg alone

C. Pulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3d + cyclophosphamide/rituximab

D. MMF + steroids

E. Plasma exchange alone

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest primary GN worldwide?

A: IgA Nephropathy

  • Q: IgA classic presentation?

A: Macroscopic haematuria 1–2 days post-URTI (synpharyngitic)

  • Q: IF hallmark of IgA nephropathy?

A: Dominant mesangial IgA (+C3), no IgG dominance

  • Q: EM finding in IgA?

A: Mesangial electron-dense deposits

  • Q: Oxford MEST-C components?

A: Mesangial, Endocapillary, Segmental sclerosis, Tubular atrophy/fibrosis, Crescents

  • Q: Oxford MEST-C strongest predictor?

A: T score (Tubular atrophy/Interstitial fibrosis)

  • Q: Supportive Rx for all IgA?

A: ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + BP <130/80 + statin + lifestyle

  • Q: When to immunosupress?

A: Proteinuria >1g/day despite 3–6mo ACEi/ARB, or crescents, or eGFR <45

  • Q: RPGN IgA management?

A: Pulse methylpred + CYC/rituximab

  • Q: HSP vs IgA?

A: HSP = systemic (purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis); same renal pathology

  • Q: Post-strep vs IgA?

A: Post-strep = ASO↑, C3 low, subepithelial humps, IgG dominant

  • Q: IgA-dominant infection-related GN?

A: Staph infection, older, IgG co-dominance, systemic infection

  • Q: Complement pathway in IgA?

A: Alternative pathway

  • Q: Oxford E1 significance?

A: Endocapillary hypercellularity; responds to immunosuppression

  • Q: TESTING trial result?

A: 6mo steroids reduce ESRD but increase infections; lower dose safer

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — IgA nephropathy is commonest primary GN globally
  2. B — Classic = synpharyngitic macroscopic haematuria 1–2d post-URTI
  3. B — Dominant mesangial IgA on IF (pathognomonic)
  4. D — T score (tubular atrophy/interstitial fibrosis) = strongest predictor
  5. B — Supportive: ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + BP control is first-line for all
  6. B — KDIGO: immunosuppression if proteinuria >1g despite 3–6mo ACEi/ARB
  7. B — TESTING: 6mo methylpred reduced ESRD but increased adverse events (infections)
  8. B — HSP = systemic IgA vasculitis (purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis)
  9. A — Post-strep: ASO↑, C3↓, subepithelial "humps", IgG dominant
  10. B — E = endocapillary hypercellularity
  11. B — Crescentic RPGN: pulse steroids + CYC/rituximab

SBAs

  1. B — Young adult + post-URTI haematuria + mesangial IgA = IgA nephropathy
  2. C — Proteinuria 0.8g, eGFR 75, M1 only → low/intermediate risk → supportive
  3. B — Proteinuria >1g, eGFR 45, adverse MEST-C (E1, S1, T1, C1) → immunosuppression
  4. B — HSP = systemic vasculitis (palpable purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis) + IgA nephritis
  5. B — Infection-related GN: IgA/IgG co-dominance, subepithelial deposits, active infection
  6. D — T2 = >50% tubular atrophy/interstitial fibrosis (worst prognosis)
  7. C — Alternative pathway (C3 deposition, no C1q/C4)
  8. B — KDIGO 2024: BP <130/80 for proteinuric CKD
  9. B — DAPA-CKD/EMPA-KIDNEY: SGLT2i reduces progression ~35–40% in CKD incl. IgA (non-diabetic)
  10. C — RPGN with >50% crescents: pulse steroids + CYC/rituximab

20. Summary

IgA Nephropathy is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Commonest primary GN. Classic = synpharyngitic macroscopic haematuria. Biopsy = dominant mesangial IgA on IF, mesangial deposits on EM. Oxford MEST-C for prognosis (T score strongest). Supportive = ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + BP <130/80. Immunosuppression = proteinuria >1g despite 3–6mo ACEi/ARB → 6mo steroids (TESTING). RPGN = pulse steroids + CYC/rituximab. HSP = systemic vasculitis + same renal pathology.

Exam focus: Clinical presentations, biopsy interpretation, Oxford MEST-C, supportive vs immunosuppressive thresholds, HSP distinction, TESTING trial.

Clinical relevance: Most common GN; early recognition + ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i prevents progression.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.3 Membranous Nephropathy
Topic: Primary Glomerulonephritides — Membranous Nephropathy — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — IgA Nephropathy (Berger's Disease)]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — FSGS]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Minimal Change Disease]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Lupus Nephritis]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Membranous Nephropathy = commonest cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults (non-diabetic). Subepithelial immune complexes → complement-mediated podocyte injury. Primary (anti-PLA2R/THSD7A) vs Secondary (SLE, malignancy, infections, drugs). Spontaneous remission ~30%. Management: supportive (ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i) + immunosuppression if high-risk.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Distinguish primary vs secondary membranous nephropathy
  • Interpret anti-PLA2R/THSD7A serology and biopsy stages
  • Apply KDIGO risk stratification (low/medium/high) for immunosuppression
  • Manage nephrotic complications (thrombosis, infection, lipids)

2. Epidemiology & Classification

FeatureDetail
EpidemiologyCommonest cause of adult nephrotic syndrome (non-diabetic); peak 50–60yo; M>F
Primary (Idiopathic)~70–80%; anti-PLA2R+ (70–80%), anti-THSD7A+ (2–5%), double-negative (15–20%)
Secondary~20–30%; SLE (Class V), malignancy (solid tumours), infections (HBV, HCV, syphilis), drugs (NSAIDs, gold, penicillamine), sarcoidosis

3. Pathogenesis

MechanismDetail
PrimaryPodocyte antigens: PLA2R (phospholipase A2 receptor), THSD7A (thrombospondin type-1 domain-containing 7A) → autoantibodies (IgG4 dominant) → in situ immune complex formation in subepithelial space
SecondaryExogenous antigens (malignancy, drugs, infections) or immune dysregulation (SLE)
ComplementMembrane attack complex (C5b-9) → podocyte injury → proteinuria
IgG SubclassIgG4 dominant in primary MN (vs IgG1/3 in secondary)

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureDetail
Nephrotic Syndrome~80%: heavy proteinuria (>3.5g/day), hypoalbuminaemia, oedema, hyperlipidaemia
Asymptomatic ProteinuriaIncidental finding; subnephrotic range
Renal Impairment~20–30% at presentation (usually chronic)
Thrombotic ComplicationsRenal vein thrombosis (20–40%), PE, DVT — hypercoagulable state
Extrarenal (Secondary)SLE features, malignancy symptoms, infection signs

5. Histopathology (Biopsy)

ModalityFindings
Light MicroscopyDiffuse GBM thickening (spikes on Jones/PAS); no hypercellularity; advanced = sclerosis
ImmunofluorescenceGranular capillary wall deposits: IgG (dominant), C3; IgG4 predominant in primary
Electron MicroscopySubepithelial electron-dense deposits (subepithelial "humps"); corresponding GBM spikes; podocyte foot process effacement

6. Staging (Ehrenreich-Churg)

StageEM FindingsPrognosis
Stage ISmall, sparse subepithelial deposits; no spikesBest
Stage IILarger, confluent deposits; early spikesGood
Stage IIIDeposits incorporated into GBM; prominent spikesIntermediate
Stage IVAdvanced GBM thickening, sclerosis, few depositsWorst (chronic)
[!key]
Stage I–II better response to immunosuppression; Stage III–IV more chronic, less responsive.

7. Serology & Biomarkers

MarkerSignificance
Anti-PLA2R AbHighly specific (90–95%) for primary MN; titre correlates with disease activity; serial monitoring guides therapy
Anti-THSD7A Ab~2–5% primary MN; associated with malignancy
Complement (C3, C4)Usually normal (vs low in SLE, post-infectious, C3G)
ANA, anti-dsDNARule out SLE (Class V lupus)
Malignancy screenCT chest/abdomen/pelvis if >60yo, anti-THSD7A+, refractory

8. KDIGO Risk Stratification & Management

RiskCriteria5-yr ESRD RiskManagement
LowPersistent proteinuria <4g/day, normal eGFR<5%Supportive only: ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, statin, anticoagulation if indicated, monitor q3–6mo
MediumProteinuria 4–8g/day OR eGFR <605–15%Consider immunosuppression if persistent >6mo supportive; shared decision-making
HighProteinuria >8g/day OR eGFR <40>15%Immunosuppression recommended (see below)

9. Immunosuppression Regimens (Primary MN)

RegimenInductionMaintenanceNotes
Rituximab (Preferred)375mg/m² weekly × 4 (or 1g × 2 doses 2wks apart)Repeat if relapse (B-cell driven); monitor CD19+MENTOR trial: non-inferior to CYC, safer; first-line per KDIGO 2024
Cyclophosphamide + Steroids (Ponticelli)CYC 0.5–1g/m² IV monthly × 6mo (alt monthly oral) + pred 0.5mg/kg alt daysNone (6mo total)Classic regimen; effective but higher toxicity (gonadal, bladder, infection)
CNIs (Tacrolimus/Cyclosporine) + SteroidsTac 0.05–0.1mg/kg/day + predTaper to lowest effective doseHigh relapse rate after stopping; nephrotoxicity risk
Anti-PLA2R-guidedTreat to anti-PLA2R negativityMonitor titres q3mo; retreat if risingPersonalised approach; correlate with proteinuria

10. Supportive Care (All Patients)

InterventionTarget
ACEi/ARBMax tolerated; target proteinuria reduction >50%
SGLT2 InhibitorDapagliflozin/empagliflozin (eGFR ≥20); reduces progression
BP Control<130/80
StatinsAll nephrotic adults (CVD risk)
AnticoagulationProphylactic if serum albumin <20g/L + additional risk factors (immobility, surgery, prior VTE); Therapeutic if VTE/renal vein thrombosis
Infection ProphylaxisPneumococcal, influenza, COVID vaccines; consider varicella if immunosuppressed
DietLow salt, moderate protein

11. Secondary MN — Key Associations

CauseKey Features
SLE (Class V)High ANA/dsDNA, low C3/C4, full-house IF (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q); often mixed Class III/IV + V
MalignancySolid tumours (lung, colon, breast, prostate); older age; anti-THSD7A+; remission with cancer treatment
InfectionsHBV (children, HBeAg+), HCV, syphilis, malaria; treat infection → remission
DrugsNSAIDs, gold, penicillamine, captopril, tiopronin; stop drug → remission
SarcoidosisGranulomatous disease; ACE elevated

12. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Commonest cause adult nephrotic syndrome (non-diabetic) → Membranous Nephropathy
- Primary: anti-PLA2R+ (70–80%), anti-THSD7A+ (2–5%)
- Biopsy: Diffuse GBM thickening + spikes (Jones); subepithelial deposits on EM; IgG4 dominant on IF
- Staging: Ehrenreich-Churg I–IV; early stages = better immunosuppression response
- KDIGO Risk: Low (<4g protein, normal eGFR) → supportive; High (>8g or eGFR <40) → immunosuppress
- First-line immunosuppression: Rituximab (MENTOR trial) — safer than CYC
- Ponticelli: CYC + steroids (alternating monthly × 6mo) — classic but toxic
- Secondary MN screen: ANA, dsDNA, C3/C4, hepatitis serology, malignancy screen (if >60)
- Renal vein thrombosis: 20–40% risk; prophylactic anticoagulation if albumin <20g/L + risk factors
- Spontaneous remission: ~30% (esp. low-risk)

13. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All MN = primary20–30% secondary; always screen (ANA, hepatitis, malignancy if >60)
Anti-PLA2R = 100% specific90–95% specific; false + in secondary (rare)
Subepithelial deposits = only MNAlso post-strep (humps), lupus Class V, infection-related GN
IgG4 dominant = primaryIgG4 dominant in primary; IgG1/3 in secondary (e.g., SLE)
Rituximab = only for relapseNow first-line induction per KDIGO 2024 (MENTOR trial)
All need immunosuppressionLow-risk: supportive only; spontaneous remission 30%
Anticoagulation for everyoneOnly if albumin <20g/L + risk factors (or therapeutic if VTE)
SLE Class V = primary MNSLE = full-house IF, low complement, positive ANA/dsDNA

14. Mnemonics

  • MN = adult nephrotic: Membranous = Most common Adult Nephrotic
  • PLA2R: Phospholipase A2 Receptor = primary MN marker
  • Biopsy: Subepithelial Deposits, Spikes, Start EM = SDS
  • Staging: I = best, IV = worst = 1-2-3-4
  • Risk: Low <4g, Medium 4–8g, High >8g = LMH
  • Rituximab: Rituximab First-line (MENTOR) = RF
  • Secondary screen: ANA, Hepatitis, Malignancy (if >60), Drugs = AHMD

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Membranous Nephropathy))
    Classification
      Primary[Anti-PLA2R/THSD7A]
      Secondary[SLE, Cancer, HBV/HCV, Drugs]
    Pathogenesis
      Podocyte_Antigens[PLA2R, THSD7A]
      IgG4_Autoantibodies[In situ immune complexes]
      Subepithelial_Deposits[Complement C5b-9]
      Podocyte_Injury[Proteinuria]
    Biopsy
      LM[Diffuse GBM thickening, spikes]
      IF[Granular capillary wall, IgG4 dominant]
      EM[Subepithelial deposits]
      Staging[Ehrenreich-Churg I-IV]
    Serology
      Anti_PLA2R[Specific, monitors activity]
      Anti_THSD7A[Malignancy association]
    Management
      Supportive[ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, Anticoagulation]
      Low_Risk[Conservative]
      Immunosuppression[Rituximab (1st line), CYC+steroids, CNI]

16. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Commonest cause adult nephrotic syndrome (non-diabetic)
  2. Primary MN autoantibodies (PLA2R, THSD7A)
  3. Biopsy: LM (spikes), IF (IgG4), EM (subepithelial)
  4. KDIGO risk stratification (Low/Medium/High thresholds)
  5. First-line immunosuppression (Rituximab per MENTOR)
  6. Secondary MN screen (ANA, hepatitis, malignancy)
  7. Renal vein thrombosis prophylaxis (albumin <20)
  8. Spontaneous remission rate (~30%)

17. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15

18. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Clinical presentation, primary vs secondary, anti-PLA2R, biopsy findings, KDIGO risk stratification, Rituximab first-line, renal vein thrombosis prophylaxis
Should Know 🟡Ehrenreich-Churg staging, Ponticelli regimen, CNI regimen, anti-PLA2R-guided therapy, spontaneous remission rate
Nice to Know 🟢THSD7A-malignancy link, IgG subclass significance, complement inhibition trials, novel biomarkers

19. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest cause of nephrotic syndrome in non-diabetic adults:

A. Minimal change disease

B. FSGS

C. Membranous nephropathy

D. IgA nephropathy

E. Amyloidosis

  1. Autoantibody most specific for primary membranous nephropathy:

A. Anti-GBM

B. ANCA

C. Anti-PLA2R

D. Anti-dsDNA

E. Cryoglobulins

  1. Immunofluorescence pattern in primary membranous nephropathy:

A. Linear GBM IgG

B. Granular capillary wall IgG (IgG4 dominant)

C. Mesangial IgA

D. Full-house (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q)

E. C3 dominant

  1. Electron microscopy hallmark of membranous nephropathy:

A. Subendothelial deposits

B. Mesangial deposits

C. Subepithelial deposits

D. Intramembranous deposits

E. Tubuloreticular inclusions

  1. Ehrenreich-Churg Stage I vs Stage IV:

A. Stage I: diffuse GBM thickening; Stage IV: normal GBM

B. Stage I: small deposits, no spikes; Stage IV: advanced sclerosis, few deposits

C. Stage I: subendothelial; Stage IV: subepithelial

D. Stage I: worst prognosis; Stage IV: best

E. No difference

  1. KDIGO 2024 "High Risk" membranous nephropathy criteria:

A. Proteinuria <4g/day, eGFR >60

B. Proteinuria 4–8g/day OR eGFR <60

C. Proteinuria >8g/day OR eGFR <40

D. Any proteinuria with normal eGFR

E. Only anti-PLA2R titre >100

  1. First-line immunosuppression for primary MN per KDIGO 2024 (MENTOR trial):

A. Cyclophosphamide + steroids

B. Rituximab

C. Tacrolimus + steroids

D. Mycophenolate mofetil

E. Plasma exchange

  1. Ponticelli regimen for MN:

A. Rituximab 375mg/m² × 4 weekly

B. Cyclophosphamide + prednisone alternating monthly × 6mo

C. Tacrolimus 0.1mg/kg/day

D. Plasma exchange weekly × 5

E. Mycophenolate 2g/day × 12mo

  1. Prophylactic anticoagulation in MN indicated when:

A. All nephrotic patients

B. Serum albumin <20g/L + additional risk factors

C. Proteinuria >8g/day only

D. Anti-PLA2R positive only

E. Never indicated

  1. Secondary MN screen should include:

A. Anti-PLA2R only

B. ANA, dsDNA, C3/C4, hepatitis B/C, malignancy screen (if >60)

C. ANCA only

D. Cryoglobulins only

E. No screen needed

20. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 55-year-old man, nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria 6g/day, albumin 18g/L). Biopsy: diffuse GBM thickening, spikes, subepithelial deposits, granular IgG4 on IF. Anti-PLA2R+. Management:

A. Supportive only

B. Rituximab 375mg/m² weekly × 4

C. Ponticelli regimen

D. Tacrolimus + prednisone

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 62-year-old woman, nephrotic syndrome. Biopsy: membranous pattern. Anti-PLA2R negative. Next step:

A. Diagnose primary MN

B. Screen for secondary causes (ANA, hepatitis, malignancy)

C. Start rituximab

D. Ponticelli regimen

E. Conservative only

  1. 45-year-old man, MN, proteinuria 3g/day, eGFR 70, albumin 30g/L. KDIGO risk category:

A. High

B. Medium

C. Low

D. Not classifiable

E. Requires biopsy restaging

  1. Membranous nephropathy with mixed Class III/IV + V on biopsy. Serology: ANA+, anti-dsDNA+, low C3/C4. Diagnosis:

A. Primary MN

B. Lupus nephritis Class V (mixed)

C. Anti-PLA2R negative primary MN

D. Drug-induced MN

E. HBV-associated MN

  1. MN patient develops flank pain, haematuria, acute kidney injury. Ultrasound: absent renal venous flow. Diagnosis:

A. Acute tubular necrosis

B. Renal vein thrombosis

C. Acute interstitial nephritis

D. Pyelonephritis

E. Renal artery thrombosis

  1. MN, albumin 15g/L, history of DVT. Anticoagulation:

A. Prophylactic only

B. Therapeutic (treatment dose)

C. No anticoagulation

D. Aspirin only

E. IVC filter only

  1. Anti-PLA2R titre monitoring in MN:

A. Not useful

B. Correlates with disease activity; guides immunosuppression tapering

C. Only for diagnosis

D. Replaces proteinuria monitoring

E. Only in secondary MN

  1. Spontaneous remission rate in primary MN:

A. <5%

B. 10–15%

C. ~30% (higher in low-risk)

D. 50%

E. >70%

  1. MN with anti-THSD7A positivity. Association:

A. Better prognosis

B. Malignancy (screen required)

C. SLE

D. HBV

E. Drug-induced

  1. Ponticelli regimen — cyclophosphamide dosing:

A. Daily oral 2mg/kg

B. IV 0.5–1g/m² monthly alternating with prednisone × 6mo

C. IV 1g/m² weekly × 6

D. Oral daily × 12mo

E. IV pulse 500mg × 3 days

21. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest adult nephrotic cause (non-diabetic)?

A: Membranous Nephropathy

  • Q: Primary MN main autoantibody?

A: Anti-PLA2R (70–80%)

  • Q: Secondary MN main autoantibody?

A: Anti-THSD7A (malignancy association)

  • Q: IF in primary MN?

A: Granular capillary wall IgG (IgG4 dominant)

  • Q: EM in MN?

A: Subepithelial deposits (spikes on LM)

  • Q: Ehrenreich-Churg I vs IV?

A: I = small deposits no spikes (best); IV = advanced sclerosis (worst)

  • Q: KDIGO Low Risk MN?

A: Proteinuria <4g/day, normal eGFR → supportive only

  • Q: KDIGO High Risk MN?

A: Proteinuria >8g/day or eGFR <40 → immunosuppression

  • Q: First-line immunosuppression MN (KDIGO 2024)?

A: Rituximab (MENTOR trial)

  • Q: Ponticelli regimen?

A: CYC + pred alternating monthly × 6mo

  • Q: Renal vein thrombosis prophylaxis?

A: Albumin <20g/L + risk factors

  • Q: Therapeutic anticoagulation in MN?

A: Confirmed VTE or renal vein thrombosis

  • Q: Spontaneous remission MN?

A: ~30% (higher in low-risk)

  • Q: Secondary MN screen?

A: ANA, dsDNA, C3/C4, Hep B/C, malignancy (if >60), drugs

  • Q: SLE Class V vs Primary MN?

A: SLE = full-house IF, low C3/C4, ANA/dsDNA+

  • Q: Anti-PLA2R monitoring?

A: Correlates with activity; guides taper/retreatment

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — Membranous nephropathy = commonest cause adult nephrotic syndrome (non-diabetic)
  2. C — Anti-PLA2R highly specific (90–95%) for primary MN
  3. B — Granular capillary wall IgG, IgG4 dominant in primary
  4. C — Subepithelial deposits (with corresponding GBM spikes)
  5. B — Stage I: small sparse deposits, no spikes; Stage IV: advanced sclerosis
  6. C — High risk: proteinuria >8g/day OR eGFR <40
  7. B — MENTOR trial: Rituximab non-inferior to CYC, safer; KDIGO 2024 first-line
  8. B — Ponticelli: CYC IV monthly alternating with prednisone × 6mo
  9. B — Prophylactic anticoagulation if albumin <20g/L + risk factors
  10. B — Screen for secondary: ANA, dsDNA, C3/C4, hepatitis, malignancy (if >60)

SBAs

  1. B — Primary MN, high-risk (proteinuria 6g, albumin 18) → Rituximab first-line
  2. B — PLA2R-negative MN → screen for secondary causes (malignancy, SLE, infections)
  3. C — Proteinuria 3g (<4g), eGFR 70, albumin 30 → Low risk (supportive)
  4. B — Mixed Class III/IV+V + ANA/dsDNA+/low complement = Lupus nephritis
  5. B — Flank pain + haematuria + AKI + absent renal venous flow = Renal vein thrombosis
  6. B — Prior DVT = therapeutic anticoagulation indicated
  7. B — Anti-PLA2R titre correlates with disease activity; guides therapy
  8. C — ~30% spontaneous remission (higher in low-risk)
  9. B — Anti-THSD7A associated with malignancy; screen required
  10. B — Ponticelli: CYC IV 0.5–1g/m² monthly alternating with prednisone × 6mo

23. Summary

Membranous Nephropathy is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Commonest adult nephrotic (non-diabetic). Primary = anti-PLA2R+ (70–80%), anti-THSD7A+ (malignancy). Biopsy: GBM thickening + spikes (LM), granular capillary wall IgG4 (IF), subepithelial deposits (EM). KDIGO risk: Low (<4g, normal eGFR) → supportive; High (>8g or eGFR<40) → immunosuppress. Rituximab first-line (MENTOR). Secondary screen: ANA, hepatitis, malignancy (>60). Renal vein thrombosis: 20–40%; prophylactic anticoagulation if albumin <20 + risk factors.

Exam focus: Primary vs secondary, anti-PLA2R, biopsy findings, KDIGO risk, Rituximab vs Ponticelli, complications.

Clinical relevance: Early rituximab improves remission rates; screening avoids missing secondary causes.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.4 Fsgs Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis
Topic: Primary Glomerulonephritides — FSGS (Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — IgA Nephropathy (Berger's Disease)]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Membranous Nephropathy]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Minimal Change Disease]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
FSGS = commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults. Segmental sclerosis of some glomeruli. Primary (genetic, circulating permeability factor) vs Secondary (adaptive, viral, drug). Primary: steroid-resistant ~60%. Management: high-dose steroids induction, then CNI/MMF/rituximab. Recurrence post-transplant ~30–50% (circulating factor).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify FSGS variants (Columbia classification) and their prognostic significance
  • Distinguish primary vs secondary FSGS
  • Apply treatment algorithms (steroids, CNI, MMF, rituximab)
  • Recognise post-transplant recurrence risk

2. Classification (Columbia Histological Variants)

VariantHistologyPrognosisNotes
CollapsingSegmental/global collapse + podocyte hyperplasiaWorst (rapid progression to ESRD)HIV-associated, pamidronate, interferon
Tip LesionSclerosis at tubular pole (tip)Best (high steroid response)Often nephrotic
CellularEndocapillary hypercellularity + foam cellsIntermediateMay represent early collapsing
PerihilarSclerosis at vascular pole (hilar)IntermediateOften secondary/adaptive (hyperfiltration)
NOS (Not Otherwise Specified)Segmental sclerosis not fitting aboveIntermediateMost common variant

3. Aetiology

CategoryMechanismExamples
Primary (Idiopathic)Circulating permeability factor (unknown), podocyte injuryGenetic (NPHS2, ACTN4, TRPC6, INF2), idiopathic
Secondary — AdaptiveHyperfiltration, reduced nephron massObesity, reduced renal mass, reflux nephropathy, prematurity
Secondary — ViralDirect podocyte infectionHIV (collapsing variant), Parvovirus B19
Secondary — Drug/ToxinPodocyte toxicityPamidronate, interferon, lithium, sirolimus
Secondary — Familial/GeneticPodocyte structural protein mutationsNPHS2 (podocin, AR, childhood), ACTN4 (α-actinin-4, AD, adult), TRPC6, INF2
[!key]
Primary FSGS: Permeability factor, steroid-sensitive ~40%, recurrence post-Tx 30–50%.
Adaptive FSGS: Subnephrotic proteinuria, non-progressive if underlying cause treated.
Collapsing variant: Worst prognosis; HIV, pamidronate, interferon.

4. Clinical Presentation

FeaturePrimary FSGSAdaptive/Secondary FSGS
ProteinuriaNephrotic range (>3.5g/day) commonSubnephrotic (<3.5g) common
Nephrotic Syndrome~70%Rare
Renal ImpairmentCommon at presentationVariable
HypertensionCommonCommon
HaematuriaMicroscopic ~50%Less common
ExtrarenalNoneObesity, HIV, drug history

5. Histopathology

ModalityFindings
Light MicroscopySegmental glomerulosclerosis (adhesion to Bowman's capsule); foam cells; variant-specific features
ImmunofluorescenceNon-specific: IgM, C3 in sclerotic areas ("trapped"); no immune complex pattern
Electron MicroscopyDiffuse foot process effacement (>80%); no electron-dense deposits

6. Genetic Testing Indications

IndicationGenes
Childhood-onset SRNSNPHS2 (podocin, AR), WT1, PLCE1
Adult-onset SRNS + family historyACTN4 (AD), TRPC6 (AD), INF2 (AD)
Pre-transplant workupExclude genetic (no recurrence if genetic)
Syndromic featuresWT1 (Denys-Drash, Frasier), LMX1B (nail-patella)
[!key]
Genetic FSGS = no recurrence post-transplant (no circulating factor). NPHS2 = childhood, AR. ACTN4/INF2/TRPC6 = adult, AD.

7. Management

Primary FSGS — Nephrotic Syndrome

PhaseRegimen
InductionPrednisolone 1 mg/kg/day (max 80mg) × 16 weeks → if remission, taper over 6mo
Steroid-Resistant (no remission at 16 weeks)Cyclosporine/Tacrolimus (CNIs) — target trough CsA 150–250, Tac 5–10 ng/mL + low-dose steroids
CNI-Intolerant/ResistantMycophenolate mofetil 2–3g/day + steroids; Rituximab 375mg/m² × 4 weekly (evidence evolving)
RelapseRestart steroids; CNI/rituximab

Adaptive/Secondary FSGS

ApproachDetail
Treat underlying causeWeight loss (obesity), stop offending drug, treat HIV, control BP
ACEi/ARB + SGLT2iReduce proteinuria, slow progression
ImmunosuppressionNot indicated (no permeability factor)

8. Post-Transplant Recurrence

FeatureDetail
Risk30–50% (primary FSGS); collapsing variant highest
TimingImmediate to days-weeks post-Tx (circulating factor)
PresentationSudden nephrotic proteinuria, allograft dysfunction
ProphylaxisPlasmapheresis preemptive (controversial, not standard)
TreatmentPlasmapheresis (removes factor) + rituximab + CNI optimisation
Genetic FSGSNo recurrence (no circulating factor)

9. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- FSGS = commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults
- Columbia variants: Collapsing (worst), Tip (best steroid response), Perihilar (adaptive/secondary)
- Primary vs Secondary: Primary = nephrotic, permeability factor; Secondary = adaptive (hyperfiltration), viral, drug
- EM: Diffuse foot process effacement >80% (vs segmental in MCD)
- Induction: Pred 1mg/kg × 16 weeks (primary)
- Steroid-resistant: CNI (CsA/Tac) + low-dose steroids
- Post-Tx recurrence: 30–50% (primary); no recurrence if genetic
- Collapsing variant: HIV, pamidronate, interferon; worst prognosis
- Genetic: NPHS2 (childhood, AR), ACTN4/INF2/TRPC6 (adult, AD)

10. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All FSGS = primaryAdaptive (obesity, reduced renal mass) = no immunosuppression
Foot process effacement = MCDMCD = diffuse but selective proteinuria; FSGS = diffuse + non-selective
Steroids work for allPrimary: ~40% respond; Adaptive: NO; Genetic: NO
Recurrence = all FSGS post-TxOnly primary (circulating factor); genetic = no recurrence
Collapsing = only HIVAlso pamidronate, interferon, idiopathic
Perihilar = primaryPerihilar = adaptive/secondary (hyperfiltration)
FSGS = always nephroticAdaptive FSGS often subnephrotic

11. Mnemonics

  • Columbia variants: Collapsing, Tip, Cellular, Perihilar, NOS = CTCPN
  • Prognosis: Collapsing Worst, Tip Best = CW/TB
  • Primary FSGS: Permeability Factor, Steroid Resistant ~60% = PFSR
  • Genetic: NPHS2 = Neonatal/Nephrotic Childhood, AR; ACTN4 = Adult AD
  • Post-Tx: Primary FSGS Recurs (30–50%), Genetic No = PR/GN

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((FSGS))
    Variants
      Collapsing[Worst, HIV, pamidronate]
      Tip[Best steroid response]
      Cellular[Intermediate]
      Perihilar[Adaptive/secondary]
      NOS[Most common]
    Aetiology
      Primary[Permeability factor]
      Adaptive[Hyperfiltration]
      Viral[HIV, Parvovirus]
      Drug[Pamidronate, IFN]
      Genetic[NPHS2, ACTN4, TRPC6, INF2]
    Histology
      LM[Segmental sclerosis, foam cells]
      IF[Non-specific IgM, C3 in scars]
      EM[Diffuse FPE >80%]
    Management
      Induction[Pred 1mg/kg 16wk]
      Steroid_Resistant[CNI + steroids]
      Refractory[MMF, Rituximab]
      Adaptive[ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, NO immunosuppression]
    Transplant
      Recurrence[30-50% primary]
      Treatment[PLEX + Rituximab]
      Genetic[No recurrence]

13. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults
  2. Columbia variants: Collapsing (worst), Tip (best), Perihilar (adaptive)
  3. Primary vs Adaptive FSGS features
  4. EM: Diffuse foot process effacement >80%
  5. Induction: Pred 1mg/kg × 16 weeks
  6. Steroid-resistant: CNI + low-dose steroids
  7. Post-Tx recurrence: 30–50% primary; no recurrence if genetic
  8. Genetic: NPHS2 (childhood AR), ACTN4/INF2/TRPC6 (adult AD)

14. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Columbia variants, primary vs secondary/adaptive, EM findings, steroid induction, CNI for resistance, post-Tx recurrence, genetic implications
Should Know 🟡Specific genetic genes, collapsing variant associations, rituximab role, plasmapheresis for recurrence
Nice to Know 🟢Circulating permeability factor research, suPAR, novel therapies (sparsentan), APOL1 risk variants

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults:

A. IgA nephropathy

B. Membranous nephropathy

C. FSGS

D. Minimal change disease

E. Anti-GBM disease

  1. Columbia variant with BEST steroid response:

A. Collapsing

B. Tip lesion

C. Cellular

D. Perihilar

E. NOS

  1. Columbia variant associated with HIV and pamidronate:

A. Tip lesion

B. Collapsing variant

C. Cellular

D. Perihilar

E. NOS

  1. Adaptive FSGS (secondary) — typical proteinuria:

A. Nephrotic range (>3.5g/day)

B. Subnephrotic (<3.5g/day)

C. Always nephrotic

D. No proteinuria

E. Only microscopic haematuria

  1. EM finding in FSGS:

A. Subepithelial deposits

B. Subendothelial deposits

C. Mesangial deposits

D. Diffuse foot process effacement >80%

E. Tubuloreticular inclusions

  1. Primary FSGS induction therapy (nephrotic):

A. CNI first-line

B. Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day × 16 weeks

C. Rituximab first-line

D. MMF + steroids

E. Plasma exchange

  1. Steroid-resistant FSGS — next line:

A. Increase steroids

B. Cyclosporine/Tacrolimus + low-dose steroids

C. Plasma exchange

D. Rituximab alone

E. Transplant referral

  1. Post-transplant FSGS recurrence risk (primary):

A. <5%

B. 10–15%

C. 30–50%

D. 70–80%

E. 100%

  1. Genetic FSGS post-transplant recurrence:

A. Same as primary (30–50%)

B. Higher (50–70%)

C. No recurrence

D. Only if NPHS2

E. Only if ACTN4

  1. Perihilar variant FSGS suggests:

A. Primary idiopathic

B. Adaptive/secondary (hyperfiltration)

C. Drug-induced

D. Genetic

E. Viral

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 35-year-old man, nephrotic syndrome, proteinuria 5g/day. Biopsy: segmental sclerosis at tubular pole, foot process effacement >80%. Variant and prognosis:

A. Collapsing — worst

B. Tip lesion — best steroid response

C. Cellular — intermediate

D. Perihilar — adaptive

E. NOS — intermediate

  1. 45-year-old obese man (BMI 42), proteinuria 1.8g/day, eGFR 65. Biopsy: perihilar sclerosis. Management:

A. High-dose steroids

B. Weight loss, ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i, BP control

C. Cyclosporine

D. Rituximab

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 25-year-old HIV+ man, nephrotic syndrome, AKI. Biopsy: collapsing glomerulopathy. Management:

A. Steroids alone

B. ART optimisation + ACEi/ARB; steroids if severe (controversial)

C. CNI

D. Rituximab

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 12-year-old boy, steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome. Genetic testing before transplant. Gene most likely:

A. ACTN4

B. NPHS2 (podocin)

C. TRPC6

D. INF2

E. APOL1

  1. Adult FSGS, family history, AD inheritance. Gene:

A. NPHS2

B. ACTN4 or INF2 or TRPC6

C. WT1

D. PLCE1

E. APOL1

  1. FSGS post-transplant day 3: sudden proteinuria 8g/day. Native disease: primary FSGS. Immediate management:

A. Increase CNI

B. Plasmapheresis + rituximab

C. Pulse steroids only

D. Biopsy only

E. Switch to mTOR inhibitor

  1. Adaptive FSGS vs Primary FSGS — key distinguishing feature:

A. EM foot process effacement

B. Proteinuria: subnephrotic (adaptive) vs nephrotic (primary)

C. LM segmental sclerosis

D. IF IgM/C3 trapping

E. All above

  1. Pamidronate-associated FSGS variant:

A. Tip

B. Collapsing

C. Cellular

D. Perihilar

E. NOS

  1. Genetic FSGS — no recurrence post-transplant because:

A. CNI prevents it

B. No circulating permeability factor (structural podocyte defect)

C. Rituximab prophylaxis

D. Plasmapheresis prophylaxis

E. Different histology

  1. NPHS2 mutation — inheritance and onset:

A. AD, adult

B. AR, childhood

C. AD, childhood

A. AR, adult

E. X-linked

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults?

A: FSGS

  • Q: Columbia variants?

A: Collapsing, Tip, Cellular, Perihilar, NOS

  • Q: Worst prognosis variant?

A: Collapsing

  • Q: Best steroid response variant?

A: Tip lesion

  • Q: Perihilar variant suggests?

A: Adaptive/secondary (hyperfiltration)

  • Q: EM in FSGS?

A: Diffuse foot process effacement >80%

  • Q: Primary FSGS induction?

A: Pred 1mg/kg × 16 weeks

  • Q: Steroid-resistant FSGS?

A: CNI (CsA/Tac) + low-dose steroids

  • Q: Adaptive FSGS proteinuria?

A: Subnephrotic (<3.5g/day)

  • Q: Post-Tx recurrence primary FSGS?

A: 30–50%

  • Q: Genetic FSGS post-Tx recurrence?

A: None (no circulating factor)

  • Q: NPHS2 — inheritance, onset?

A: AR, childhood

  • Q: ACTN4/INF2/TRPC6 — inheritance, onset?

A: AD, adult

  • Q: Collapsing variant associations?

A: HIV, pamidronate, interferon

  • Q: Post-Tx recurrence treatment?

A: Plasmapheresis + rituximab + CNI optimisation

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — FSGS = commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults
  2. B — Tip lesion = best steroid response, best prognosis
  3. B — Collapsing variant = HIV, pamidronate, interferon; worst prognosis
  4. B — Adaptive FSGS = subnephrotic proteinuria (hyperfiltration mechanism)
  5. D — EM: diffuse foot process effacement >80% (vs MCD also diffuse but clinical difference)
  6. B — Primary FSGS nephrotic: pred 1mg/kg × 16 weeks induction
  7. B — Steroid-resistant: CNI + low-dose steroids next line
  8. C — Post-Tx recurrence 30–50% for primary FSGS
  9. C — Genetic FSGS = no circulating factor = no recurrence
  10. B — Perihilar = adaptive/secondary (obesity, reduced renal mass)

SBAs

  1. B — Tip lesion (tubular pole) = best steroid response
  2. B — Obesity + subnephrotic + perihilar = adaptive FSGS → supportive only
  3. B — HIV collapsing: ART optimisation primary; steroids controversial
  4. B — Childhood SRNS = NPHS2 (podocin) most common (AR)
  5. B — Adult AD FSGS = ACTN4, INF2, TRPC6
  6. B — Post-Tx recurrence: plasmapheresis (removes factor) + rituximab (B-cell depletion)
  7. B — Adaptive = subnephrotic; Primary = nephrotic (key clinical distinction)
  8. B — Pamidronate = collapsing variant
  9. B — Genetic = structural podocyte defect, no circulating factor
  10. B — NPHS2 = AR, childhood onset

20. Summary

FSGS is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD. Columbia variants: Collapsing (worst, HIV/pamidronate), Tip (best steroid response), Perihilar (adaptive). Primary = nephrotic, permeability factor; Adaptive = subnephrotic, hyperfiltration. EM = diffuse FPE >80%. Induction = pred 1mg/kg × 16wk. Steroid-resistant = CNI + steroids. Post-Tx recurrence 30–50% (primary only); genetic = no recurrence. Genetic: NPHS2 (AR, childhood), ACTN4/INF2/TRPC6 (AD, adult).

Exam focus: Variants prognosis, primary vs adaptive distinction, EM findings, treatment algorithm, post-Tx recurrence, genetics.

Clinical relevance: Early aggressive treatment improves outcomes; genetic testing avoids unnecessary immunosuppression and predicts transplant recurrence.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.5 Minimal Change Disease
Topic: Primary Glomerulonephritides — Minimal Change Disease — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — IgA Nephropathy (Berger's Disease)]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Membranous Nephropathy]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — FSGS (Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
MCD = commonest cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (70–90%). "Minimal change" on LM; diffuse foot process effacement on EM; negative IF. Highly steroid-responsive (>90% children, ~80% adults). Relapses common; frequent relapsers need steroid-sparing agents.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise typical presentation (selective proteinuria, nephrotic syndrome)
  • Differentiate MCD from FSGS (clinical, histological)
  • Apply steroid treatment protocols (induction, taper, relapse management)
  • Manage frequent relapsers and steroid-dependent cases
  • Recognise complications (infection, thrombosis, steroid toxicity)

2. Epidemiology & Classification

FeatureDetail
ChildrenCommonest cause of nephrotic syndrome (70–90%); peak 2–6 years; M>F
Adults~10–15% of adult nephrotic syndrome; older than children
Primary (Idiopathic)~90%; T-cell dysregulation → permeability factor
Secondary~10%; drugs (NSAIDs, lithium, gold), infections (syphilis, HBV), malignancy (Hodgkin's), allergies, bee stings, vaccines

3. Pathogenesis

MechanismDetail
T-cell DysregulationCirculating permeability factor (unknown cytokine) → podocyte injury
Key MoleculesReduced nephrin, podocin expression; altered glomerular charge barrier
SelectivitySelective proteinuria (albumin lost, larger proteins retained) — charge/size selectivity preserved
GeneticsMostly sporadic; rare familial (NPHS1, NPHS2, WT1 — but these usually present as congenital/SRNS)

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureChildrenAdults
Age2–6 years (peak)Any age
OnsetAcute (days)Acute/subacute
Nephrotic Syndrome~90% (heavy proteinuria, oedema, hypoalbuminaemia, hyperlipidaemia)~70%
ProteinuriaSelective (albumin selective index <0.1)Selective/non-selective
HaematuriaMicroscopic ~20%Microscopic ~30%
HypertensionRare~30%
Renal FunctionNormalNormal/mildly impaired
ExtrarenalNone (unless secondary)May have secondary cause clues

5. Histopathology

ModalityFindings
Light MicroscopyNormal / minimal change (no proliferation, no sclerosis, no crescents)
ImmunofluorescenceNegative (no immune deposits); occasional IgM/C3 in hyalinosis (non-specific)
Electron MicroscopyDiffuse foot process effacement (FPE) of podocytes (≥80%); no electron-dense deposits
[!key]
Triad of MCD: Normal LM + Negative IF + Diffuse FPE on EM = diagnostic.

6. Differential Diagnosis (MCD vs FSGS)

FeatureMCDFSGS
LMNormalSegmental sclerosis
IFNegativeNon-specific (IgM, C3 in scars)
EMDiffuse FPEDiffuse FPE
ProteinuriaSelectiveNon-selective
Steroid Response>90% (children)~40% (primary)
Relapse RateHigh (50–75%)Lower
Progression to ESRDVery rareCommon
AgeChildren predominantAdults predominant

7. Steroid Treatment Protocols

Children (ISKDC / KDIGO)

PhaseRegimen
InductionPrednisolone 60 mg/m²/day (max 60mg) × 4–6 weeks until remission (proteinuria negative × 3 days)
Taper40 mg/m² alternate days × 4–6 weeks
StopThen stop (total ~12–16 weeks)
RelapsePrednisolone 60 mg/m²/day until remission × 3 days → 40 mg/m² alternate days × 4 weeks → taper

Adults (KDIGO)

PhaseRegimen
InductionPrednisolone 1 mg/kg/day (max 80mg) × 8–16 weeks until remission
TaperReduce by 10mg every 1–2 weeks over 4–6 months
Total Duration~6 months minimum

8. Relapse Classification & Management

CategoryDefinitionManagement
Infrequent Relapser<2 relapses in 6mo or <4 in 12moStandard steroid course each relapse
Frequent Relapser≥2 relapses in 6mo or ≥4 in 12moSteroid-sparing agent: Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 1200–1500 mg/m²/day (1st line); Levamisole (2nd line); Cyclophosphamide (8–12 weeks); Tacrolimus/Cyclosporine; Rituximab
Steroid-DependentRelapse within 2 weeks of stopping steroids / during taperSame as frequent relapser
Steroid-ResistantNo remission after 4 weeks (children) or 8–16 weeks (adults) of daily steroidsRe-evaluate diagnosis (FSGS?); renal biopsy; consider CNI, MMF, rituximab

9. Steroid-Sparing Agents (Evidence-Based)

AgentDoseNotes
MMF600–1000 mg/m² BD (max 3g/day)1st line per KDIGO; good safety; monitor FBC, LFT
Levamisole2.5 mg/kg alternate days2nd line; only in children; immunomodulatory; monitor WBC
Cyclophosphamide2–3 mg/kg/day × 8–12 weeksAlkylator; gonadal toxicity; bladder toxicity; single course
CNI (Tac/CsA)Tac 0.05–0.1 mg/kg/day; CsA 3–5 mg/kg/dayNephrotoxicity; relapse on withdrawal common; monitor levels
Rituximab375 mg/m² weekly × 4 (or 1g × 2 doses)B-cell depletion; sustained remission in ~60–70%; infection risk

10. Complications & Prevention

ComplicationRiskPrevention / Management
Infections (pneumococcal, varicella, TB)High (steroids, immunosuppression, proteinuria → IgG loss)Vaccines: pneumococcal, Hib, MenACWY, varicella (pre-steroids); IVIG if severe/refractory; Penicillin prophylaxis if asplenic/hypoglobulinaemia
Thrombosis (renal vein, DVT, PE)Hypercoagulable (antithrombin III loss, ↑ fibrinogen)Anticoagulation if albumin <20g/L + risk factors; treat VTE
Steroid ToxicityCushingoid, growth failure, osteoporosis, cataracts, glucose intoleranceCalcium/Vit D, height monitoring, BP/glucose monitoring, DEXA
Acute Kidney InjuryHypovolaemia (diuresis), sepsis, nephrotoxinsAvoid over-diuresis; monitor volume
HyperlipidaemiaNephrotic syndromeStatin if persistent; diet

11. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- MCD = commonest childhood nephrotic syndrome (70–90%)
- Triad: Normal LM + Negative IF + Diffuse FPE on EM
- Selective proteinuria (albumin selective index <0.1)
- Steroid response: >90% children, ~80% adults
- Induction: Children 60 mg/m² × 4–6wk; Adults 1mg/kg × 8–16wk
- Relapse: 50–75% relapse; Frequent relapser = ≥2/6mo or ≥4/12mo
- Steroid-sparing: MMF 1st line (KDIGO); Levamisole (children); CNI; Cyclophosphamide (single course); Rituximab
- Steroid-resistant = NOT MCD → re-biopsy (likely FSGS)
- Complications: Infection (vaccines!), Thrombosis (albumin <20), Steroid toxicity
- Secondary MCD: NSAIDs, lithium, Hodgkin's, infections, allergies

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
MCD = normal EMEM = diffuse foot process effacement (hallmark)
MCD = always steroid-responsive10–15% adults steroid-resistant → re-biopsy for FSGS
Selective proteinuria = only MCDAlso early membranous, early diabetic nephropathy
All relapses need CNIInfrequent relapsers = standard steroids only
Levamisole for adultsLevamisole = children only (2nd line)
CNI = cureCNI = relapse on withdrawal common; not curative
Rituximab = only for refractoryNow used earlier for frequent relapsers (sustained remission)
MCD → ESRDVery rare; progression = likely FSGS misdiagnosis

13. Mnemonics

  • MCD Triad: Normal LM, Negative IF, Diffuse FPE = NL-NIF-DF (or MINimal = Minimal LM, Immuno -, Normal EM... no, EM shows FPE)
  • MCD Kids: MCD = Most Childhood Nephrotic
  • Selective proteinuria: Small Albumin Lost = SALective
  • Treatment: Induction 60→40→stop (kids); Relapse = MMF 1st line
  • Frequent Relapser: FR = Four in 12 / Two in 6 = F4/T2
  • Complications: Infection, Thrombosis, Steroid toxicity = ITS

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Minimal Change Disease))
    Epidemiology
      Children[70-90% childhood nephrotic]
      Adults[10-15% adult nephrotic]
    Pathogenesis
      Tcell[T-cell dysregulation]
      Permeability[Circulating factor]
      Selective[Selective proteinuria]
    Histology
      LM[Normal]
      IF[Negative]
      EM[Diffuse FPE ≥80%]
    Differential
      vs_FSGS[LM: normal vs sclerosis]
      vs_MN[IF: neg vs granular IgG]
    Treatment
      Induction[Kids: 60mg/m2 4-6wk; Adults: 1mg/kg 8-16wk]
      Relapse[Standard steroids]
      Frequent[MMF 1st line; CYC; CNI; Rituximab]
      Resistant[Re-biopsy → FSGS]
    Complications
      Infection[Vaccines, IVIG]
      Thrombosis[Albumin <20]
      Steroid[Cushingoid, growth, bone]

15. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Commonest childhood nephrotic cause (70–90%)
  2. Histology triad: Normal LM, Negative IF, Diffuse FPE on EM
  3. Selective proteinuria definition
  4. Steroid induction: kids 60mg/m² × 4–6wk; adults 1mg/kg × 8–16wk
  5. Frequent relapser definition (≥2/6mo or ≥4/12mo)
  6. Steroid-sparing 1st line: MMF (KDIGO)
  7. Steroid-resistant = re-biopsy (FSGS)
  8. Complications: Infection, Thrombosis, Steroid toxicity

16. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

17. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Triad (LM/IF/EM), selective proteinuria, steroid induction/taper, frequent relapser definition, MMF 1st line, steroid-resistant = FSGS
Should Know 🟡Secondary causes, levamisole (children), CNI relapse on withdrawal, cyclophosphamide single course, rituximab role, vaccines
Nice to Know 🟢Permeability factor research, genetic associations, APOL1, novel therapies, long-term outcomes

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (2–6 years):

A. FSGS

B. Membranous nephropathy

C. Minimal Change Disease

D. IgA nephropathy

E. Post-streptococcal GN

  1. Histological triad of MCD:

A. Normal LM, Granular IF, Subepithelial deposits

B. Normal LM, Negative IF, Diffuse FPE on EM

C. Sclerosis LM, IgM IF, FPE EM

D. Proliferation LM, Full-house IF, Deposits EM

E. Normal LM, Linear IF, Normal EM

  1. Proteinuria in MCD is characteristically:

A. Non-selective

B. Selective (albumin selective index <0.1)

C. Only tubular proteinuria

D. Bence Jones proteinuria

E. Haemoglobinuria

  1. Steroid response rate in childhood MCD:

A. 50–60%

B. 70–80%

C. >90%

D. 95–100%

E. <50%

  1. Paediatric steroid induction (ISKDC):

A. Pred 1mg/kg × 8 weeks

B. Pred 60 mg/m²/day × 4–6 weeks → 40 mg/m² alternate days × 4–6 weeks

C. Pulse methylprednisolone × 3 days

D. Pred 2mg/kg × 12 weeks

E. CNI first-line

  1. Frequent relapser definition (KDIGO):

A. 1 relapse in 6 months

B. ≥2 relapses in 6 months OR ≥4 in 12 months

C. Any relapse

D. Relapse during taper only

E. Steroid-dependent only

  1. First-line steroid-sparing agent for frequent relapser (KDIGO):

A. Cyclophosphamide

B. Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF)

C. Levamisole

D. Tacrolimus

E. Rituximab

  1. Levamisole use in MCD:

A. 1st line for adults

B. 2nd line for children only

C. 1st line for all

D. Only for steroid-resistant

E. Prophylaxis for thrombosis

  1. Steroid-resistant MCD (no remission after 4 weeks pred 60mg/m²):

A. Continue steroids higher dose

B. Add CNI

C. Re-biopsy (likely FSGS)

D. Start rituximab

E. Transplant referral

  1. Complication prophylaxis in MCD with albumin <20g/L:

A. Prophylactic anticoagulation + pneumococcal vaccine

B. Prophylactic anticoagulation if additional risk factors + vaccines

C. Therapeutic anticoagulation for all

D. No prophylaxis needed

E. Only IVIG

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 4-year-old boy, acute onset periorbital oedema, proteinuria 50mg/mmol (albumin selective), normal BP, normal Cr. Most likely diagnosis:

A. FSGS

B. Minimal Change Disease

C. Membranous nephropathy

D. Post-streptococcal GN

E. IgA nephropathy

  1. Same child, started on prednisolone 60mg/m²/day. Remission achieved at day 10. Next step:

A. Stop steroids immediately

B. Continue 60mg/m²/day until remission × 3 days, then 40mg/m² alternate days × 4–6 weeks

C. Switch to MMF

D. Taper over 2 weeks

E. Pulse steroids

  1. Adult MCD, remission achieved after 8 weeks pred 1mg/kg. Taper schedule:

A. Stop immediately

B. Reduce by 10mg every 1–2 weeks over 4–6 months

C. Alternate day dosing immediately

D. Alternate day 40mg × 4 weeks then stop

E. Switch to MMF

  1. Childhood MCD, 3 relapses in 6 months on steroids. Best next step:

A. Increase steroid dose

B. Start MMF 600–1000 mg/m² BD

C. Cyclophosphamide 12 weeks

D. Levamisole 2.5mg/kg alternate days

E. Rituximab

  1. Child on prednisolone, develops varicella. Management:

A. Stop steroids immediately

B. IV aciclovir + continue steroids (do not stop abruptly); consider VZIG if recent exposure

C. Switch to MMF

D. Plasma exchange

E. IVIG only

  1. MCD, albumin 15g/L, history of DVT. Anticoagulation:

A. Prophylactic dose

B. Therapeutic dose (treatment)

C. No anticoagulation

D. Aspirin only

E. IVC filter

  1. 12-year-old, no remission after 8 weeks pred 60mg/m²/day. Biopsy shows segmental sclerosis. Diagnosis:

A. Steroid-resistant MCD

B. FSGS

C. Membranous nephropathy

D. IgA nephropathy

E. Alport syndrome

  1. MCD remission maintenance — levamisole:

A. 1st line adult

B. 2nd line paediatric only

C. 1st line all

D. Only for steroid-resistant

E. Prophylaxis for infection

  1. Cyclophosphamide in frequent relapser MCD:

A. Continuous for 2 years

B. Single course 8–12 weeks (2–3mg/kg/day)

C. Monthly pulses × 6

D. Alternate day × 6 months

E. Not used in children

  1. Rituximab in MCD:

A. Only for steroid-resistant

B. Sustained remission in 60–70% frequent relapsers/steroid-dependent

C. 1st line for all

D. Only in adults

E. Replaces vaccines

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest childhood nephrotic cause?

A: Minimal Change Disease (70–90%)

  • Q: MCD histology triad?

A: Normal LM + Negative IF + Diffuse FPE on EM

  • Q: Proteinuria selectivity in MCD?

A: Selective (albumin selective index <0.1)

  • Q: Steroid response childhood MCD?

A: >90%

  • Q: Steroid induction ISKDC (kids)?

A: Pred 60mg/m²/day × 4–6wk → 40mg/m² alt days × 4–6wk

  • Q: Steroid induction adults?

A: Pred 1mg/kg/day × 8–16wk

  • Q: Frequent relapser definition?

A: ≥2 relapses/6mo OR ≥4/12mo

  • Q: Steroid-sparing 1st line (KDIGO)?

A: MMF

  • Q: Levamisole use?

A: 2nd line, children only

  • Q: Cyclophosphamide course?

A: Single 8–12 week course

  • Q: Steroid-resistant MCD →?

A: Re-biopsy (likely FSGS)

  • Q: MCD complications?

A: Infection, Thrombosis (albumin<20), Steroid toxicity

  • Q: Thrombosis prophylaxis?

A: Albumin <20g/L + risk factors

  • Q: Vaccines in MCD?

A: Pneumococcal, Hib, MenACWY, varicella (pre-steroids if possible)

  • Q: Rituximab in MCD?

A: Sustained remission 60–70% frequent relapsers

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — MCD = commonest childhood nephrotic syndrome (70–90%)
  2. B — Triad: Normal LM, Negative IF, Diffuse FPE on EM
  3. B — Selective proteinuria (charge/size barrier relatively preserved)
  4. C — >90% children respond to steroids
  5. B — ISKDC protocol: 60mg/m² daily × 4–6wk → 40mg/m² alternate days × 4–6wk
  6. B — KDIGO: frequent relapser = ≥2/6mo or ≥4/12mo
  7. B — MMF 1st line per KDIGO for frequent relapsers
  8. B — Levamisole = 2nd line, children only
  9. C — Steroid-resistant = NOT MCD; re-biopsy for FSGS
  10. B — Albumin <20 + risk factors → prophylactic anticoagulation; vaccines for all

SBAs

  1. B — Child + selective proteinuria + normal BP/Cr = MCD
  2. B — ISKDC: continue daily until remission × 3 days, then alternate day taper
  3. B — Adult taper: reduce 10mg every 1–2 weeks over 4–6 months
  4. B — Frequent relapser (≥2/6mo) → MMF 1st line
  5. B — Varicella on steroids: IV aciclovir + continue steroids (avoid abrupt stop) + VZIG if recent exposure
  6. B — Prior DVT = therapeutic anticoagulation
  7. B — Steroid-resistant + segmental sclerosis = FSGS
  8. B — Levamisole = 2nd line paediatric only
  9. B — Cyclophosphamide = single 8–12 week course
  10. B — Rituximab: sustained remission 60–70% in frequent relapsers/steroid-dependent

22. Summary

Minimal Change Disease is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Commonest childhood nephrotic (70–90%). Triad: Normal LM + Negative IF + Diffuse FPE on EM. Selective proteinuria. Steroid response >90% kids, ~80% adults. Induction: kids 60mg/m² × 4–6wk; adults 1mg/kg × 8–16wk. Relapse: 50–75%. Frequent relapser (≥2/6mo) → MMF 1st line (KDIGO). Steroid-resistant = re-bioopsy (FSGS). Complications: infection (vaccines!), thrombosis (albumin<20), steroid toxicity.

Exam focus: Triad, selective proteinuria, steroid protocols, relapse classification, MMF 1st line, steroid-resistant = FSGS, complications.

Clinical relevance: High relapse rate requires steroid-sparing strategy; vaccination critical before immunosuppression.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.6 Lupus Nephritis
Topic: Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Lupus Nephritis — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — ANCA-Associated Vasculitis]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Diabetic Nephropathy]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Amyloidosis]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Lupus Nephritis = renal manifestation of SLE. Affects 40–60% of SLE patients. ISN/RPS 2003 Class I–VI guides treatment. Class III/IV = proliferative (induction: MMF or CYC + steroids); Class V = membranous (add MMF/CNI); Class VI = advanced sclerosis (no immunosuppression).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify lupus nephritis using ISN/RPS 2003 classification (Class I–VI)
  • Correlate histology with clinical presentation and treatment
  • Apply induction and maintenance regimens per class
  • Monitor response and manage flares

2. Epidemiology & Risk Factors

FeatureDetail
Prevalence40–60% of SLE patients develop LN
DemographicsYoung women (9:1 F:M); higher severity in African, Asian, Hispanic ancestry
Risk FactorsMale sex, young age at SLE onset, high anti-dsDNA, low complement, hypertension, delay in treatment
GeneticsHLA-DR2/DR3, complement deficiencies (C1q, C4), FCGR polymorphisms, APOL1 (African ancestry)

3. ISN/RPS 2003 Classification (Light Microscopy)

ClassLight MicroscopyImmunofluorescenceElectron MicroscopyClinical FeaturesTreatment
Class IMinimal mesangialMesangial IgG, IgM, C3, C1qMesangial depositsAsymptomatic, normal renal functionNone (monitor)
Class IIMesangial proliferativeMesangial deposits (full-house)Mesangial depositsMild proteinuria, haematuriaSupportive; steroids if proteinuria >1g
Class IIIFocal (<50% glomeruli) endocapillary/proliferativeGranular: IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q (capillary + mesangial)Subendothelial + mesangialActive sediment, proteinuria, haematuria, renal impairmentInduction: MMF or CYC + steroids
Class IVDiffuse (≥50% glomeruli) endocapillary/proliferativeGranular "full-house" (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q)Subendothelial + mesangialSevere: nephritic/nephrotic, active sediment, renal impairmentInduction: MMF or CYC + steroids
Class IV-S / IV-GIV-S: Segmental (>50% tuft); IV-G: Global—Subendothelial + mesangialIV-G worse prognosisSame as Class IV
Class VMembranous (diffuse GBM thickening)Granular capillary wall (IgG, C3)Subepithelial depositsNephrotic syndrome; may overlap III/IV (III+V, IV+V)Class V alone: steroids + MMF/CNI; Mixed: treat as III/IV
Class VIAdvanced sclerosis (>90% globally sclerosed)VariableGlobal sclerosisAdvanced CKD, poor responseNo immunosuppression (renal replacement)

4. Activity & Chronicity Indices

Activity Index (AI)Chronicity Index (CI)
Endocapillary hypercellularityGlomerulosclerosis
Neutrophils/karyorrhexisFibrous crescents
Wire loopsTubular atrophy
Cellular crescentsInterstitial fibrosis
Hyaline deposits
Max AI = 24Max CI = 12
[!key]
High AI = active inflammation = responds to immunosuppression. High CI = irreversible damage = poor response.

5. Clinical Presentation by Class

ClassPresentation
I/IIAsymptomatic, microscopic haematuria, subnephrotic proteinuria (<1g)
IIIProteinuria 1–3g, active sediment (RBC casts), mild renal impairment
IVNephritic/nephrotic, heavy proteinuria (>3g), active sediment, HTN, renal impairment
VNephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria, oedema, hypoalbuminaemia); may have active sediment if mixed
VIAdvanced CKD, small kidneys, minimal proteinuria

6. Serology & Monitoring

MarkerSignificance
Anti-dsDNA↑ titre correlates with disease activity (esp. Class III/IV)
Complement (C3, C4)Low C3/C4 = active disease; normalisation = response
Anti-C1qHighly specific for LN; correlates with activity
UrinalysisProteinuria (UPCR), active sediment (RBC casts, WBC casts)
Renal biopsyGold standard for classification; repeat for flare assessment

7. Treatment — Induction (Class III/IV ± V)

RegimenDose/DurationNotes
