MMF (Preferred)MMF 2–3 g/day (target 2–3g) + Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (max 60mg) × 6moALMS trial: MMF non-inferior to CYC; better safety; 1st line per KDIGO/EULAR
Cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus)IV CYC 500 mg × 6 fortnightly (low-dose) + steroidsEuro-Lupus: equally effective, less gonadal toxicity; preferred in Caucasians
Cyclophosphamide (NIH)IV CYC 0.5–1g/m² monthly × 6mo + steroidsHigher dose; more toxicity; used in severe/refractory
Add-onHydroxychloroquine (all SLE), ACEi/ARB, BP control, statinHCQ reduces flares, improves survival
[!key]
Induction 6 months. Assess response at 3mo (proteinuria ↓50%) and 6mo (remission).

8. Treatment — Maintenance (Class III/IV)

AgentDoseDuration
MMF1–2 g/dayMinimum 3 years (longer if high-risk)
Azathioprine1.5–2 mg/kg/dayAlternative if MMF intolerant
CNI (Tac/CsA)Tac 0.05–0.1 mg/kg/dayOption for membranous overlap
PrednisoloneTaper to ≤5–7.5 mg/dayLowest effective dose
Hydroxychloroquine200–400 mg/dayLifelong (reduces flares, thrombosis, damage)

9. Class V (Membranous) LN Treatment

ScenarioTreatment
Pure Class V (nephrotic)Prednisolone + MMF (or CNI) × 6mo induction → MMF maintenance
Mixed III+V / IV+VTreat as Class III/IV (MMF/CYC + steroids)
Subnephrotic proteinuriaACEi/ARB + HCQ + monitor

10. Flare Management

Flare TypeDefinitionManagement
Renal Flare↑Proteinuria >50% from baseline, new active sediment, ↑CrRe-biopsy (class may change); restart induction regimen
Extra-renal FlareArthritis, rash, serositis, cytopeniasTreat per SLE guidelines (steroids, HCQ, immunosuppressants)

11. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- LN affects 40–60% SLE; ISN/RPS Class I–VI guides treatment
- Class III/IV = proliferative → MMF or CYC + steroids (6mo induction)
- MMF 1st line (ALMS trial); Euro-Lupus CYC (low-dose, less toxicity)
- Class V = membranous: Pure = steroids + MMF/CNI; Mixed = treat as III/IV
- Class VI = advanced sclerosis → no immunosuppression
- Full-house IF = IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q (pathognomonic for SLE)
- Activity vs Chronicity Index: AI = treatable; CI = irreversible
- Monitor: anti-dsDNA, C3/C4, UPCR, active sediment
- Maintenance: MMF 1–2g/day × ≥3 years + HCQ lifelong
- Flare: re-biopsy (class may change); restart induction

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All LN classes need immunosuppressionClass I/II = supportive; Class VI = no immunosuppression
Class V = always pure membranousOften mixed (III+V, IV+V) → treat as proliferative
CYC = always high-dose NIHEuro-Lupus low-dose (500mg × 6) equally effective, less toxicity
MMF = only maintenanceMMF = 1st line INDUCTION (ALMS trial)
Anti-dsDNA = always correlatesNot 100%; some flares without rise; complement more reliable
Flare = same classRe-biopsy essential — class often changes (e.g., IV → III, III → V)
HCQ = optionalHCQ mandatory all SLE (reduces flares, thrombosis, organ damage)
Maintenance = 1 yearMinimum 3 years (high relapse if stopped early)

13. Mnemonics

  • ISN/RPS Classes: I = Ignorable, II = Mild, III = Focal, IV = Diffuse, V = Membranous, VI = Sclerotic = IMFDMS
  • Full-house IF: IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q = III-CC
  • Induction: MMF or CYC + Steroids = MCS
  • Euro-Lupus: Euro = Easy CYC (500mg × 6)
  • Maintenance: MMF ≥ 3 years = M3
  • Monitor: Anti-dsDNA, Complement, UPCR, Sediment = ACUS

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Lupus Nephritis))
    Classification
      Class_I[Minimal mesangial]
      Class_II[Mesangial proliferative]
      Class_III[Focal proliferative <50%]
      Class_IV[Diffuse proliferative ≥50%]
      Class_IV_SG[IV-S segmental, IV-G global]
      Class_V[Membranous]
      Class_VI[Advanced sclerosis >90%]
    Histology
      LM[ISN/RPS]
      IF[Full-house: IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q]
      EM[Subendothelial (III/IV), Subepithelial (V)]
      Indices[Activity vs Chronicity]
    Treatment
      Induction[MMF or CYC + Steroids 6mo]
      Maintenance[MMF 1-2g 3yr + HCQ lifelong]
      Class_V[Pure: MMF/CNI; Mixed: as III/IV]
      Class_VI[No immunosuppression]
    Monitoring
      Anti_dsDNA[Activity]
      Complement[C3/C4]
      UPCR[Proteinuria]
      Sediment[RBC casts]
    Flare
      Rebiopsy[Class may change]
      Restart_Induction[MMF/CYC + steroids]

15. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. ISN/RPS Classes I–VI and which need immunosuppression
  2. Full-house IF components
  3. Class III vs IV distinction (focal <50% vs diffuse ≥50%)
  4. Induction: MMF 2–3g/day + steroids × 6mo (1st line)
  5. Euro-Lupus CYC: 500mg × 6 fortnightly
  6. Class V treatment (pure vs mixed)
  7. Class VI = no immunosuppression
  8. Maintenance ≥3 years + HCQ lifelong
  9. Flare = re-biopsy + restart induction

16. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

17. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴ISN/RPS classes, full-house IF, Class III/IV induction (MMF/CYC), Class V pure vs mixed, Class VI no immunosuppression, maintenance duration, flare = re-biopsy
Should Know 🟡Activity/Chronicity indices, Euro-Lupus vs NIH CYC, ALMS trial, monitoring markers, hydroxychloroquine mandate
Nice to Know 🟢Genetic risk factors, anti-C1q, novel therapies (belimumab, voclospirin), pregnancy in LN, T2LN (tubulointerstitial)

18. MCQs (10)

  1. ISN/RPS Class IV lupus nephritis is defined as:

A. Focal proliferative <50% glomeruli

B. Diffuse proliferative ≥50% glomeruli

C. Membranous pattern

D. Advanced sclerosis >90%

E. Minimal mesangial changes

  1. "Full-house" immunofluorescence in lupus nephritis includes:

A. IgG, C3 only

B. IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q

C. IgG, IgM, C3

D. C3, C1q only

E. IgA, C3 only

  1. First-line induction therapy for Class III/IV lupus nephritis (per KDIGO/EULAR):

A. Cyclophosphamide (NIH regimen) + steroids

B. Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 2–3g/day + steroids

C. Rituximab + steroids

D. Tacrolimus + steroids

E. Plasma exchange + steroids

  1. Euro-Lupus cyclophosphamide regimen:

A. IV CYC 0.5–1g/m² monthly × 6mo

B. IV CYC 500mg every 2 weeks × 6 doses

C. Oral CYC 2mg/kg/day × 3mo

D. IV CYC 1g/m² quarterly × 4

E. Pulse CYC 1g × 3 days

  1. Class V (membranous) lupus nephritis — pure form treatment:

A. CYC + steroids

B. Steroids + MMF (or CNI)

C. Rituximab alone

D. Plasma exchange

E. Supportive only

  1. Class III/IV + V (mixed) lupus nephritis — treatment:

A. Treat as pure Class V

B. Treat as Class III/IV (MMF/CYC + steroids)

C. Supportive only

D. CNI only

E. Rituximab only

  1. Class VI lupus nephritis management:

A. MMF induction

B. CYC induction

C. No immunosuppression (advanced sclerosis >90%)

D. Rituximab

E. Plasma exchange

  1. Minimum maintenance therapy duration after induction for Class III/IV LN:

A. 6 months

B. 1 year

C. 2 years

D. 3 years

E. Lifelong

  1. Activity Index (AI) includes:

A. Glomerulosclerosis, fibrous crescents, tubular atrophy

B. Endocapillary hypercellularity, neutrophils, karyorrhexis, wire loops, cellular crescents, hyaline deposits

C. Interstitial fibrosis only

D. Global sclerosis only

E. Tubular atrophy only

  1. Lupus nephritis flare — mandatory step:

A. Increase steroids only

B. Repeat renal biopsy (class may change)

C. Switch to rituximab

D. Plasma exchange

E. Stop immunosuppression

  1. Hydroxychloroquine in SLE — mandate:

A. Optional

B. Only if cutaneous involvement

C. Mandatory for all SLE patients (reduces flares, thrombosis, damage)

D. Only during pregnancy

E. Contraindicated in renal disease

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 28-year-old woman, SLE 3 years, proteinuria 4g/day, active sediment (RBC casts), Cr 140, low C3, high anti-dsDNA. Biopsy: diffuse endocapillary proliferation in 70% glomeruli, full-house IF, subendothelial deposits. Class and induction:

A. Class III — MMF + steroids

B. Class IV — MMF 2–3g/day + steroids × 6mo

C. Class V — steroids + CNI

D. Class IV — CYC NIH regimen

E. Class II — supportive only

  1. 35-year-old man, SLE, nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria 6g/day), normal Cr, low C3. Biopsy: diffuse GBM thickening, granular capillary wall IgG/C3, subepithelial deposits. No endocapillary proliferation. Class and treatment:

A. Class III — MMF + steroids

B. Class V pure — steroids + MMF (or CNI)

C. Class IV — CYC + steroids

D. Class II — ACEi/ARB only

E. Class VI — dialysis

  1. Same patient as Q2 but biopsy also shows focal endocapillary proliferation in 30% glomeruli. Class and treatment:

A. Class V — steroids + MMF

B. Class III+V (mixed) — treat as Class III (MMF/CYC + steroids)

C. Class IV — CYC + steroids

D. Class II — supportive

E. Class VI — no immunosuppression

  1. 40-year-old woman, SLE 10 years, Cr 350, small kidneys on US, proteinuria 0.5g/day. Biopsy: >90% globally sclerosed glomeruli. Class and management:

A. Class IV — MMF induction

B. Class III — CYC induction

C. Class VI — no immunosuppression, prepare for RRT

D. Class V — CNI + steroids

E. Class II — monitor

  1. Class IV LN, completed 6mo MMF induction, remission achieved (UPCR <0.5, normal Cr, normal C3/C4). Maintenance:

A. Stop all immunosuppression

B. MMF 1–2g/day × ≥3 years + HCQ lifelong + low-dose pred

C. Switch to CYC maintenance

D. Rituximab maintenance

E. MMF 6 months only

  1. LN patient on maintenance MMF 1g/day, presents with proteinuria rise from 0.3 to 2g/day, new RBC casts, Cr rise 100→150. Best next step:

A. Increase MMF dose

B. Repeat renal biopsy (class may have changed)

C. Add rituximab

D. Pulse steroids

E. Plasma exchange

  1. Euro-Lupus CYC regimen vs NIH CYC regimen — key difference:

A. Euro-Lupus uses oral CYC

B. Euro-Lupus: lower cumulative dose (3g vs 6–8g), less gonadal toxicity

C. Euro-Lupus uses rituximab

D. Euro-Lupus is for Class V only

E. No difference

  1. ALMS trial compared:

A. MMF vs Rituximab

B. MMF vs Cyclophosphamide (induction)

C. MMF vs Azathioprine (maintenance)

D. MMF vs CNI

E. MMF vs Placebo

  1. Anti-dsDNA and complement in LN monitoring:

A. Anti-dsDNA always rises with flare

B. Low C3/C4 more reliable than anti-dsDNA for activity; both used together

C. Complement never changes

D. Only anti-dsDNA matters

E. Only complement matters

  1. Pregnancy in lupus nephritis — key principle:

A. Stop all immunosuppression

B. Continue HCQ, AZA, steroids; stop MMF/CYC/CNI (teratogenic); plan pregnancy in remission ≥6mo

C. Only HCQ allowed

D. MMF safe in pregnancy

E. CYC safe in pregnancy

20. Flashcards

  • Q: LN prevalence in SLE?

A: 40–60%

  • Q: ISN/RPS Class IV definition?

A: Diffuse proliferative ≥50% glomeruli

  • Q: Class III vs IV?

A: III = focal <50%; IV = diffuse ≥50%

  • Q: Full-house IF?

A: IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q

  • Q: Class III/IV induction 1st line?

A: MMF 2–3g/day + steroids × 6mo

  • Q: Euro-Lupus CYC?

A: IV 500mg × 6 fortnightly

  • Q: Class V pure treatment?

A: Steroids + MMF (or CNI)

  • Q: Class III+V/IV+V treatment?

A: Treat as Class III/IV (MMF/CYC + steroids)

  • Q: Class VI management?

A: No immunosuppression; RRT

  • Q: Maintenance duration?

A: ≥3 years

  • Q: Flare management?

A: Re-biopsy + restart induction

  • Q: Activity Index components?

A: Endocapillary hypercellularity, neutrophils, karyorrhexis, wire loops, cellular crescents, hyaline deposits

  • Q: Chronicity Index components?

A: Glomerulosclerosis, fibrous crescents, tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis

  • Q: HCQ in SLE?

A: Mandatory all patients (reduces flares, thrombosis, damage)

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Class IV = diffuse ≥50% glomeruli involvement
  2. B — Full-house = IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q
  3. B — ALMS trial: MMF non-inferior to CYC, safer; 1st line per KDIGO/EULAR
  4. B — Euro-Lupus: low-dose IV CYC 500mg × 6 (cumulative 3g vs NIH 6–8g)
  5. B — Pure Class V = steroids + MMF/CNI
  6. B — Mixed III+V/IV+V = treat as proliferative (Class III/IV)
  7. C — Class VI = >90% sclerosis = no immunosuppression
  8. D — Maintenance minimum 3 years
  9. B — AI = endocapillary hypercellularity, neutrophils, karyorrhexis, wire loops, cellular crescents, hyaline deposits
  10. B — Re-biopsy mandatory (class often changes)
  11. C — HCQ mandatory all SLE

SBAs

  1. B — 70% glomeruli = Class IV; MMF 1st line induction
  2. B — Pure membranous pattern = Class V; steroids + MMF/CNI
  3. B — Focal proliferation (30%) + membranous = Class III+V → treat as Class III
  4. C — >90% sclerosis = Class VI; no immunosuppression
  5. B — Maintenance: MMF 1–2g/day ≥3 years + HCQ lifelong + low-dose pred
  6. B — Flare = re-biopsy essential (class may change)
  7. B — Euro-Lupus: 3g cumulative vs 6–8g NIH; less gonadal toxicity
  8. B — ALMS: MMF vs CYC induction (non-inferior)
  9. B — C3/C4 more reliable; both used together
  10. B — Pregnancy: HCQ/AZA/steroids safe; MMF/CYC/CNI teratogenic; remission ≥6mo pre-conception

22. Summary

Lupus Nephritis is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: ISN/RPS Class I–VI. Full-house IF (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q) pathognomonic. Class III/IV = proliferative → MMF 2–3g/day + steroids × 6mo (1st line, ALMS); Euro-Lupus CYC 500mg × 6 alternative. Class V = membranous: pure = steroids + MMF/CNI; mixed = treat as III/IV. Class VI = no immunosuppression. Maintenance ≥3 years (MMF 1–2g/day) + HCQ lifelong. Flare = re-biopsy + restart induction. Monitor anti-dsDNA, C3/C4, UPCR, sediment.

Exam focus: Classification, full-house IF, induction regimens (MMF vs CYC), Class V pure vs mixed, Class VI, maintenance duration, flare = re-biopsy, HCQ mandate.

Clinical relevance: Early biopsy-guided treatment prevents ESRD; MMF improved safety profile; HCQ reduces long-term damage.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.7 Anca Vasculitis Gpa Mpa Egpa
Topic: Secondary Glomerulonephritides — ANCA-Associated Vasculitis (GPA, MPA, EGPA) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Lupus Nephritis]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Diabetic Nephropathy]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Amyloidosis]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
ANCA-Associated Vasculitis (AAV) = pauci-immune crescentic GN. Three subtypes: GPA (c-ANCA/PR3+, granulomas, ENT/lung), MPA (p-ANCA/MPO+, renal/lung), EGPA (asthma, eosinophilia, p-ANCA/MPO+ ~40%). Renal = pauci-immune necrotising crescentic GN. Induction: Rituximab or CYC + steroids; PLEX for severe renal (Cr >500) or pulmonary haemorrhage (PULSE trial).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Distinguish GPA, MPA, EGPA by clinical features, ANCA pattern, histology
  • Recognise pauci-immune crescentic GN on biopsy
  • Apply induction (Rituximab vs CYC) and maintenance regimens
  • Know PLEX indications (PULSE trial)
  • Monitor for relapse (ANCA rise, clinical features)

2. Classification & ANCA Patterns

DiseaseANCA PatternTarget AntigenKey Clinical Features
GPA (Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis, Wegener's)c-ANCA (cytoplasmic)PR3 (proteinase 3)Granulomatous inflammation: ENT (sinusitis, saddle nose, subglottic stenosis), lung (nodules, cavities), renal (pauci-immune GN)
MPA (Microscopic Polyangiitis)p-ANCA (perinuclear)MPO (myeloperoxidase)No granulomas: renal (pauci-immune GN), lung (alveolar haemorrhage), skin (purpura), nerves (mononeuritis multiplex)
EGPA (Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis, Churg-Strauss)p-ANCA ~40% (MPO+)MPOAsthma, eosinophilia (>1.5×10⁹/L), granulomas, neuropathy, cardiac, skin; renal less common/severe

3. Renal Pathology (All AAV)

ModalityFindings
Light MicroscopyNecrotising crescentic GN: fibrinoid necrosis, cellular crescents (often >50% = RPGN)
ImmunofluorescencePauci-immune: scant/no immunoglobulins, no complement (distinguishes from immune complex GN)
Electron MicroscopyNo electron-dense deposits; fibrin in capillary loops; podocyte foot process effacement
[!key]
Pauci-immune necrotising crescentic GN = hallmark of AAV renal involvement. No IF deposits = "pauci-immune".

4. Clinical Presentation by Subtype

FeatureGPAMPAEGPA
RenalPauci-immune GN (common)Pauci-immune GN (very common)Less common, milder
LungNodules, cavities, haemorrhageAlveolar haemorrhage (common)Infiltrates, less haemorrhage
ENTSinusitis, saddle nose, subglottic stenosisRareSinusitis, nasal polyps
NervesMononeuritis multiplexMononeuritis multiplexMononeuritis multiplex
SkinPurpura, ulcersPurpuraPurpura, nodules
HeartRareRareCardiomyopathy, pericarditis
EosinophiliaRareRare>1.5×10⁹/L (defining)
AsthmaNoNoYes (defining, often severe)

5. Diagnostic Criteria (ACR/EULAR 2017)

DiseaseKey Criteria
GPAPR3-ANCA + granulomatous inflammation (biopsy or imaging) + ENT/lung involvement
MPAMPO-ANCA + pauci-immune GN + no granulomas + no asthma/eosinophilia
EGPAAsthma + eosinophilia >1.5×10⁹/L + granulomatous inflammation + neuropathy + p-ANCA/MPO+ (~40%)

6. Induction Therapy (Severe Disease)

RegimenDoseIndication
Rituximab (Preferred)375 mg/m² weekly × 4 (or 1g × 2 doses 2wks apart) + pulse methylprednisolone 500–1000mg × 3d → oral pred 1mg/kgRAVE/ RITUXVAS trials: non-inferior to CYC; 1st line per KDIGO/EULAR for new-onset/relapsing; preferred in young (fertility), PR3+
Cyclophosphamide (CYC)IV CYC 0.5–1g/m² monthly × 3–6mo (or oral 2mg/kg/day) + steroidsClassic regimen; preferred if severe alveolar haemorrhage, renal Cr >500 (with PLEX), or rituximab contraindicated
Plasma Exchange (PLEX)7 exchanges over 14 days (1.5 plasma volumes) + steroids + CYC/rituximabPULSE trial: Severe renal (Cr >500 μmol/L) or pulmonary haemorrhage
[!key]
PULSE trial: PLEX reduced ESRD risk in severe renal AAV (Cr >500) but increased infections. Indicated: Cr >500 or pulmonary haemorrhage.

7. Maintenance Therapy

AgentDoseDuration
Rituximab500 mg every 6 months (or 1g × 2 doses 6mo apart)MAINRITSAN trials: superior to AZA for relapse prevention; preferred maintenance
Azathioprine1.5–2 mg/kg/dayAlternative if rituximab unavailable/contraindicated
Mycophenolate mofetil1–2 g/dayOption (esp. if AZA intolerant)
Methotrexate15–25 mg/weekNon-severe disease (no renal/pulmonary involvement)
PrednisoloneTaper to ≤5–7.5 mg/day by 6moLowest effective dose

8. Monitoring & Relapse

ParameterFrequencySignificance
ANCA titreq3–6moRising titre predicts relapse (~60–70% PPV); but relapse can occur without rise
Urinalysisq1–3moNew haematuria/proteinuria = renal flare
eGFRq1–3moRising Cr = flare
FBC, LFT, renalq1–3moDrug toxicity (rituximab: IgG, infections; AZA: myelosuppression)
Chest imagingIf respiratory symptomsLung flare

9. Relapse Management

ScenarioManagement
Mild (non-renal)Increase steroids ± add methotrexate/AZA
Severe (renal/pulmonary)Re-induction: Rituximab (or CYC) + steroids ± PLEX (if Cr >500 or pulmonary haemorrhage)
ANCA rise without clinical flareMonitor closely; do NOT treat based on ANCA alone

10. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- AAV = pauci-immune crescentic GN (no IF deposits)
- GPA = c-ANCA/PR3+ + granulomas + ENT/lung + renal
- MPA = p-ANCA/MPO+ + no granulomas + renal/lung haemorrhage
- EGPA = asthma + eosinophilia >1.5×10⁹/L + p-ANCA/MPO+ (~40%)
- Induction: Rituximab 1st line (RAVE trial); CYC alternative
- PLEX: Cr >500 or pulmonary haemorrhage (PULSE trial)
- Maintenance: Rituximab 500mg q6mo (MAINRITSAN) preferred
- Relapse: rising ANCA predicts but not 100%; treat clinical flare only
- Fertility: Rituximab preferred in young; CYC = gonadal toxicity
- Granulomas: GPA (yes), EGPA (yes), MPA (no)

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All AAV = c-ANCAGPA = c-ANCA/PR3; MPA/EGPA = p-ANCA/MPO
EGPA = always ANCA+Only ~40% ANCA+ (p-ANCA/MPO); ANCA- EGPA = more cardiac/neuro, less renal
Pauci-immune = no immune depositsScant IgM/C3 may be trapped; no significant immune complexes
PLEX for all severe AAVOnly Cr >500 or pulmonary haemorrhage (PULSE)
Rituximab = only for relapse1st line INDUCTION for new-onset (RAVE)
CYC = always IV monthlyEuro-Lupus low-dose (500mg × 6 fortnightly) also used
ANCA rise = always treatTreat clinical flare only; ANCA rise alone = monitor
EGPA renal = severeEGPA renal involvement = less common, milder than GPA/MPA
Maintenance = 1 yearMinimum 2 years; often 5+ years for PR3+ GPA (high relapse)

12. Mnemonics

  • ANCA patterns: c-ANCA = PR3 = GPA; p-ANCA = MPO = MPA/EGPA = cPR3-GPA, pMPO-MPA/EGPA
  • GPA: Granulomas, PR3, ANCA = GPA
  • MPA: MPO, Pauci-immune, Alveolar haemorrhage = MPA
  • EGPA: Eosinophilia, Granulomas, Polyangiitis, Asthma = EGPA
  • PULSE: Plasma exchange for Uraemia/Severe renal (Cr>500) + Lung haemorrhage = PULSE
  • Induction: Rituximab First (RAVE) = RF
  • Maintenance: Rituximab Q6mo = RQ

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((ANCA-Associated Vasculitis))
    Subtypes
      GPA[c-ANCA/PR3+, Granulomas, ENT/Lung]
      MPA[p-ANCA/MPO+, No granulomas, Alveolar haemorrhage]
      EGPA[Asthma, Eosinophilia>1.5, p-ANCA~40%]
    Renal Pathology
      LM[Necrotising crescentic GN]
      IF[Pauci-immune]
      EM[No deposits]
    Induction
      Rituximab[375mg/m2 x4 + steroids 1st line]
      CYC[IV monthly x3-6mo + steroids]
      PLEX[Cr>500 or Pulm haemorrhage]
    Maintenance
      Rituximab[500mg q6mo preferred]
      AZA[Alternative]
      MMF[Option]
    Monitoring
      ANCA[Rising predicts relapse]
      Urine[Haematuria/proteinuria]
      eGFR[Rising Cr]

14. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Three AAV subtypes and their ANCA patterns (GPA=c-ANCA/PR3, MPA=p-ANCA/MPO, EGPA=p-ANCA/MPO~40%)
  2. GPA triad: granulomas, ENT, renal
  3. MPA: alveolar haemorrhage, renal
  4. EGPA: asthma, eosinophilia >1.5, ANCA 40%
  5. Renal biopsy: pauci-immune necrotising crescentic GN
  6. Induction: Rituximab 1st line (RAVE); CYC alternative
  7. PLEX: Cr >500 or pulmonary haemorrhage (PULSE)
  8. Maintenance: Rituximab 500mg q6mo (MAINRITSAN)
  9. Relapse: treat clinical flare only, not ANCA rise alone

15. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

16. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Three subtypes + ANCA patterns, pauci-immune crescentic GN, induction (Rituximab 1st line), PLEX indications, maintenance (Rituximab q6mo), relapse management
Should Know 🟡EGPA ANCA-negative variant, PULSE trial details, RAVE/RITUXVAS/MAINRITSAN trials, fertility considerations, drug monitoring
Nice to Know 🟢Avacopan (C5aR inhibitor), novel biomarkers, long-term outcomes, pregnancy in AAV, vaccines

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Pauci-immune crescentic GN is characteristic of:

A. Lupus nephritis Class IV

B. ANCA-associated vasculitis

C. Post-streptococcal GN

D. IgA nephropathy

E. Anti-GBM disease

  1. GPA (Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis) ANCA pattern:

A. p-ANCA / MPO

B. c-ANCA / PR3

C. c-ANCA / MPO

D. p-ANCA / PR3

E. ANCA negative

  1. EGPA (Churg-Strauss) defining features:

A. c-ANCA + granulomas

B. Asthma + eosinophilia >1.5×10⁹/L

C. Alveolar haemorrhage + renal

D. p-ANCA + no asthma

E. Sinusitis + renal only

  1. Renal biopsy in AAV shows immunofluorescence:

A. Granular capillary wall IgG

B. Linear GBM IgG

C. Pauci-immune (scant/no immunoglobulins)

D. Full-house (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q)

E. Mesangial IgA

  1. First-line induction for severe ANCA-associated vasculitis (RAVE trial):

A. Cyclophosphamide + steroids

B. Rituximab + steroids

C. Plasma exchange + steroids

D. Methotrexate + steroids

E. Mycophenolate + steroids

  1. PULSE trial — plasma exchange indicated for:

A. All AAV with Cr >200

B. Cr >500 μmol/L OR pulmonary haemorrhage

C. Any alveolar haemorrhage

D. ANCA titre >1:640

E. EGPA only

  1. Preferred maintenance therapy per MAINRITSAN trials:

A. Azathioprine

B. Mycophenolate mofetil

C. Rituximab 500mg every 6 months

D. Methotrexate

E. Low-dose cyclophosphamide

  1. EGPA ANCA positivity rate:

A. >90%

B. 70–80%

C. ~40% (p-ANCA/MPO)

D. <10%

E. 100%

  1. GPA vs MPA — key distinguishing feature:

A. ANCA pattern only

B. Granulomatous inflammation (GPA) vs absent (MPA)

C. Renal involvement (GPA) vs absent (MPA)

D. Lung involvement (GPA) vs absent (MPA)

E. Age of onset

  1. ANCA titre rising without clinical symptoms:

A. Start rituximab immediately

B. Increase steroids

C. Monitor closely; do NOT treat based on ANCA alone

D. Plasma exchange

E. Repeat biopsy

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 25-year-old man, haemoptysis, haematuria, Cr 200, c-ANCA positive (PR3+), sinusitis. Biopsy: necrotising crescentic GN, pauci-immune. Diagnosis:

A. MPA

B. GPA

C. EGPA

D. Anti-GBM disease

E. Lupus nephritis

  1. 60-year-old woman, rapidly progressive GN (Cr 600), pulmonary haemorrhage, p-ANCA positive (MPO+). No asthma, no granulomas. Induction:

A. Rituximab alone

B. CYC + steroids + Plasma Exchange (PULSE: Cr>500 + pulm haemorrhage)

C. Rituximab + steroids (no PLEX)

D. Methotrexate + steroids

E. Supportive only

  1. 35-year-old woman, asthma since childhood, eosinophilia 3×10⁹/L, mononeuritis multiplex, p-ANCA negative. Renal: mild proteinuria, no active sediment. Diagnosis:

A. GPA

B. MPA

C. EGPA (ANCA-negative variant)

D. Hypereosinophilic syndrome

E. Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis

  1. GPA patient completed rituximab induction, in remission. Maintenance:

A. Azathioprine 2mg/kg/day

B. Rituximab 500mg every 6 months

C. Mycophenolate 2g/day

D. Methotrexate 20mg/week

E. Stop immunosuppression

  1. MPA patient on maintenance rituximab 6-monthly. Presents with rising MPO-ANCA titre (1:80 → 1:640) but normal Cr, no haematuria, no symptoms. Management:

A. Increase rituximab frequency

B. Pulse steroids

C. Monitor closely; do NOT treat based on ANCA alone

D. Re-induction with CYC

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 25-year-old woman with GPA desires future fertility. Induction choice:

A. CYC IV monthly (gonadal toxicity high)

B. Rituximab (preferred — less gonadal toxicity)

C. Methotrexate (teratogenic)

D. CYC oral daily

E. Plasma exchange only

  1. Anti-GBM disease vs AAV — distinguishing feature on IF:

A. Both pauci-immune

B. Anti-GBM: linear GBM IgG; AAV: pauci-immune

C. Anti-GBM: granular IgG; AAV: linear

D. Both: full-house

E. No difference

  1. EGPA cardiac involvement:

A. Rare, <5%

B. Common: cardiomyopathy, pericarditis, heart failure

C. Only pericarditis

D. Only arrhythmias

E. Not seen

  1. PULSE trial result for severe renal AAV (Cr >500):

A. PLEX increased mortality

B. PLEX reduced ESRD risk but increased serious infections

C. PLEX no benefit

D. PLEX replaced CYC

E. PLEX only for pulmonary haemorrhage

  1. Relapse rate in PR3+ GPA vs MPO+ MPA:

A. PR3+ lower relapse

B. PR3+ higher relapse rate

C. Equal relapse rates

D. PR3+ never relapses

E. MPO+ never relapses

19. Flashcards

  • Q: AAV renal biopsy IF?

A: Pauci-immune (scant/no immunoglobulins)

  • Q: GPA ANCA?

A: c-ANCA / PR3

  • Q: MPA ANCA?

A: p-ANCA / MPO

  • Q: EGPA ANCA?

A: p-ANCA / MPO ~40%

  • Q: GPA triad?

A: Granulomas, ENT, Renal

  • Q: MPA hallmark?

A: Alveolar haemorrhage + pauci-immune GN

  • Q: EGPA defining?

A: Asthma + eosinophilia >1.5×10⁹/L

  • Q: Induction 1st line?

A: Rituximab + steroids (RAVE trial)

  • Q: CYC induction?

A: IV 0.5–1g/m² monthly × 3–6mo + steroids

  • Q: PLEX indication (PULSE)?

A: Cr >500 or pulmonary haemorrhage

  • Q: Maintenance preferred?

A: Rituximab 500mg q6mo (MAINRITSAN)

  • Q: ANCA rise alone?

A: Monitor; do NOT treat

  • Q: EGPA ANCA negative?

A: ~60% ANCA-; more cardiac/neuro, less renal

  • Q: GPA vs MPA granulomas?

A: GPA = yes; MPA = no

  • Q: Fertility preservation?

A: Rituximab preferred over CYC

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Pauci-immune crescentic GN = hallmark of AAV
  2. B — GPA = c-ANCA/PR3+
  3. B — EGPA = asthma + eosinophilia >1.5×10⁹/L (defining)
  4. C — Pauci-immune = scant/no immunoglobulins
  5. B — RAVE/RITUXVAS: Rituximab non-inferior to CYC, 1st line
  6. B — PULSE: Cr >500 OR pulmonary haemorrhage
  7. C — MAINRITSAN: Rituximab 500mg q6mo superior to AZA
  8. C — EGPA ANCA+ ~40% (p-ANCA/MPO)
  9. B — GPA = granulomas; MPA = no granulomas
  10. C — Treat clinical flare only; ANCA rise alone = monitor

SBAs

  1. B — c-ANCA/PR3 + sinusitis + pauci-immune GN = GPA
  2. B — Cr 600 + pulmonary haemorrhage = PLEX indicated (PULSE) + CYC/rituximab + steroids
  3. C — Asthma + eosinophilia + ANCA-negative = EGPA (ANCA-negative variant)
  4. B — MAINRITSAN: Rituximab 500mg q6mo preferred maintenance
  5. C — ANCA rise without clinical flare = monitor only
  6. B — Rituximab preferred for fertility preservation (less gonadal toxicity)
  7. B — Anti-GBM = linear GBM IgG; AAV = pauci-immune
  8. B — EGPA cardiac: cardiomyopathy, pericarditis common
  9. B — PULSE: PLEX reduced ESRD but increased infections
  10. B — PR3+ GPA has higher relapse rate than MPO+ MPA

21. Summary

ANCA-Associated Vasculitis is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Three subtypes: GPA (c-ANCA/PR3+, granulomas, ENT/lung), MPA (p-ANCA/MPO+, alveolar haemorrhage, no granulomas), EGPA (asthma, eosinophilia >1.5, p-ANCA~40%). Renal = pauci-immune necrotising crescentic GN. Induction: Rituximab 1st line (RAVE); CYC alternative. PLEX for Cr >500 or pulmonary haemorrhage (PULSE). Maintenance: Rituximab 500mg q6mo (MAINRITSAN). Relapse = treat clinical flare only; ANCA rise alone = monitor.

Exam focus: Subtype differentiation, ANCA patterns, pauci-immune GN, induction regimens, PLEX indications, maintenance, EGPA ANCA-negative variant, fertility.

Clinical relevance: Rituximab transformed management; early PLEX in severe renal saves kidneys; long maintenance prevents relapse.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.8 Diabetic Nephropathy
Topic: Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Diabetic Nephropathy — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Lupus Nephritis]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — ANCA-Associated Vasculitis (GPA, MPA, EGPA)]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Amyloidosis]], [[Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Diabetic Nephropathy = commonest cause of ESRD worldwide (~40–50%). Type 1: microalbuminuria → macroalbuminuria → declining GFR. Type 2: more variable, often CKD with albuminuria. Key: early detection (UACR), glycaemic control, BP control (ACEi/ARB), SGLT2i (game-changer), GLP-1 RA. Staging: KDIGO GFR + albuminuria categories.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define diabetic kidney disease (DKD) and staging (KDIGO)
  • Recognise natural history (hyperfiltration → microalbuminuria → macroalbuminuria → ESRD)
  • Apply comprehensive management (glycaemia, BP, RAAS blockade, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA)
  • Differentiate DKD from non-diabetic kidney disease in diabetes

2. Epidemiology & Significance

FeatureDetail
Prevalence~40% of T1DM, ~20–30% of T2DM develop DKD
ESRD CauseCommonest cause of ESRD worldwide (~40–50%)
Type 1 vs Type 2T1DM: more predictable progression; T2DM: heterogeneous, older, more comorbidities
Risk FactorsPoor glycaemic control, hypertension, smoking, dyslipidaemia, family history, ethnicity (South Asian, African, Hispanic higher risk)

3. Pathogenesis

MechanismDetail
HaemodynamicHyperfiltration → glomerular hypertension → sclerosis (early)
MetabolicAdvanced glycation end-products (AGEs), PKC activation, polyol pathway, oxidative stress
InflammatoryCytokines (TGF-β, VEGF), macrophage infiltration
StructuralGBM thickening, mesangial expansion (Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules), arteriolar hyalinosis (afferent + efferent), tubulointerstitial fibrosis

4. Natural History (Mogensen Stages — Type 1)

StageGFRAlbuminuriaPathology
1: Hyperfiltration↑↑ (130–150)NormalReversible with glycaemic control
2: SilentNormal/HighNormalGBM thickening, mesangial expansion
3: Incipient (Microalbuminuria)Normal30–300 mg/day (UACR 3–30 mg/mmol)Reversible with ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i
4: Overt (Macroalbuminuria)Declining>300 mg/day (UACR >30 mg/mmol)Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules, progressive
5: ESRD<15VariableGlobal glomerulosclerosis, fibrosis
[!key]
Type 2 DM: no clear stages; often CKD with albuminuria at diagnosis; screen annually from diagnosis.

5. Screening & Diagnosis

TestFrequencyThreshold
UACR (Urine Albumin:Creatinine Ratio)Annually from diagnosis (T2DM); 5yr post-diagnosis (T1DM)Microalbuminuria: 3–30 mg/mmol (30–300 mg/day); Macroalbuminuria: >30 mg/mmol (>300 mg/day)
eGFRAnnually (more often if CKD)KDIGO categories G1–G5
Confirm2 of 3 specimens in 3–6 months (exclude transient causes: UTI, fever, exercise, HF, menstruation)

KDIGO Staging (GFR + Albuminuria)

GFR CategoryG1 (≥90)G2 (60–89)G3a (45–59)G3b (30–44)G4 (15–29)G5 (<15)
A1 (<3 mg/mmol)LowLowModerateHighVery HighVery High
A2 (3–30 mg/mmol)LowModerateHighVery HighVery HighVery High
A3 (>30 mg/mmol)ModerateHighVery HighVery HighVery HighVery High

6. Differential: DKD vs Non-Diabetic Kidney Disease in Diabetes

FeatureTypical DKDSuggests Non-Diabetic Kidney Disease
RetinopathyPresent (especially T1DM)Absent (esp. T1DM)
Albuminuria OnsetGradual, progressiveSudden, nephrotic range
HaematuriaAbsent/minimalActive sediment (RBC casts)
Renal SizeNormal/large early; small lateSmall (chronic) or large (infiltrative)
ProgressionGradual GFR declineRapid decline
SerologyNegativeANA, ANCA, complement, paraprotein +ve
Biopsy IndicationAtypical featuresUnexplained AKI, nephrotic syndrome, haematuria, no retinopathy (T1DM)
[!key]
No retinopathy + T1DM = biopsy strongly considered (DKD unlikely without retinopathy).

7. Management — Multifactorial Intervention

1. Glycaemic Control

TargetAgent Preference
HbA1c ~7% (53 mmol/mol)SGLT2i (1st line if eGFR ≥20) → GLP-1 RA → Metformin (if eGFR ≥30) → DPP-4i → Insulin
IndividualiseAvoid hypoglycaemia (esp. elderly, CKD G4–5)
AgentRenal BenefiteGFR Cutoff
SGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin, canagliflozin)↓ ESRD, ↓ CV death, ↓ HF (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY)≥20 (initiate); continue to dialysis
GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide)↓ Albuminuria, ↓ CV eventsNo lower limit (dose adjust)
MetforminNeutral≥30 (caution 30–45); contraindicated <30
InsulinNeutralAll stages (dose reduce in CKD)
DPP-4i (linagliptin safe)NeutralAll stages

2. Blood Pressure & RAAS Blockade

TargetAgent
<130/80 (KDIGO 2024)ACEi/ARB (1st line) — max tolerated dose
Add-onThiazide-like (G1–G3), loop diuretic (G4–5), CCB, β-blocker
Dual RAAS BlockadeContraindicated (ACEi + ARB = ↑ hyperkalaemia, AKI)
[!key]
ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i = cornerstone of DKD management. Dual RAAS blockade prohibited.

3. Lipid Management

AgentIndication
StatinAll DKD ≥50yo or CVD risk >10% (or all CKD per some guidelines)
EzetimibeIf statin intolerant or LDL not at target

4. Lifestyle

InterventionTarget
Salt<2g Na/day (5g salt)
Protein0.8 g/kg/day (avoid >1.3)
WeightBMI <25
SmokingCessation
Exercise150 min/week moderate

8. SGLT2 Inhibitors — Landmark Trials

TrialPopulationKey Result
CREDENCET2DM + DKD (UACR 300–5000, eGFR 30–90)Canagliflozin: ↓ 30% ESRD/CV death/renal death
DAPA-CKDCKD (incl. non-diabetic) (UACR 200–5000, eGFR 25–75)Dapagliflozin: ↓ 39% ESRD/death
EMPA-KIDNEYCKD (incl. non-diabetic, low albuminuria) (eGFR 20–45 or 45–90 + UACR ≥200)Empagliflozin: ↓ 28% ESRD/CV death
[!key]
SGLT2i benefit extends to non-diabetic CKD (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY). Initiate if eGFR ≥20.

9. Finerenone (Non-Steroidal MRA)

TrialPopulationResult
FIDELIO-DKDT2DM + DKD (UACR 30–5000, eGFR 25–60) + ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i↓ 18% ESRD/CV death
FIGARO-DKDHigher eGFR (≥60)↓ 13% CV events
UseAdd to ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i if eGFR ≥25, K+ ≤4.8, albuminuria persists

10. Advanced DKD — Renal Replacement

StagePreparation
G4 (eGFR 15–29)Refer nephrology; AV fistula planning; transplant assessment; conservative care discussion
G5 (eGFR <15)Dialysis (HD/PD) or Transplant (pre-emptive ideal); SGLT2i continue if tolerated

11. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- DKD = commonest cause ESRD worldwide (~40–50%)
- Screen: UACR annually (T2DM from diagnosis; T1DM 5yr post-diagnosis)
- Microalbuminuria: UACR 3–30 mg/mmol (30–300 mg/day)
- Macroalbuminuria: UACR >30 mg/mmol (>300 mg/day)
- No retinopathy + T1DM = suspect non-diabetic kidney disease
- BP target <130/80; ACEi/ARB 1st line; dual RAAS blockade contraindicated
- SGLT2i = game-changer (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY) — initiate eGFR ≥20
- GLP-1 RA add-on for CV/renal benefit
- Finerenone add-on if albuminuria persists on ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i
- G4: refer nephrology (AV fistula, transplant); G5: RRT
- Metformin: safe ≥30; caution 30–45; stop <30

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Microalbuminuria = 30–300 mg/dayAlso UACR 3–30 mg/mmol (both used)
ACEi + ARB = betterContraindicated (ONTARGET, VA NEPHRON: ↑ hyperkalaemia, AKI)
SGLT2i only for diabetesBenefit in non-diabetic CKD (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY)
SGLT2i stop at eGFR <30Initiate ≥20; continue to dialysis
All diabetes + CKD = DKDRule out non-diabetic: no retinopathy (T1DM), haematuria, rapid decline, nephrotic syndrome
Finerenone = spironolactoneNon-steroidal MRA; less hyperkalaemia, no anti-androgen effects
GLP-1 RA only for diabetesCV/renal benefit in DKD; use regardless of HbA1c if indicated
Kimmelstiel-Wilson = only DKDAlso in other diseases (rare), but pathognomonic for DKD

13. Mnemonics

  • DKD Screening: UACR Annually from Diagnosis (T2DM) = UAD
  • Albuminuria: Micro = 3–30; Macro = >30 = M3/M30
  • Retinopathy: T1DM no retinopathy = biopsy = T1-NR-BX
  • BP: <130/80 = 130/80
  • ACEi/ARB: Dual Blockade Contraindicated = DBC
  • SGLT2i: CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY = CDE
  • SGLT2i eGFR: Initiate ≥20, continue to dialysis = 20-dialysis
  • KDIGO: GFR + Albuminuria = GA matrix

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Diabetic Nephropathy))
    Epidemiology
      Commonest_ESRD[40-50% worldwide]
      T1DM[40%], T2DM[20-30%]
    Pathogenesis
      Haemodynamic[Hyperfiltration]
      Metabolic[AGEs, PKC, oxidative]
      Structural[GBM thickening, K-W nodules]
    Stages_Mogensen
      Stage1[Hyperfiltration]
      Stage2[Silent]
      Stage3[Microalbuminuria]
      Stage4[Macroalbuminuria]
      Stage5[ESRD]
    Screening
      UACR[Annual]
      eGFR[Annual]
      Confirm[2 of 3 in 3-6mo]
    Differential
      DKD[Retinopathy+, gradual]
      Non-DKD[No retinopathy, haematuria, rapid]
    Management
      Glycaemia[SGLT2i 1st, GLP-1 RA, Metformin]
      BP[<130/80, ACEi/ARB, NO dual]
      Lipids[Statin]
      Lifestyle[Salt, protein, weight, smoking]
    SGLT2i
      Trials[CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY]
      NonDM[Benefit in non-diabetic CKD]
      eGFR[≥20 initiate, continue to dialysis]
    Finerenone
      Add_on[ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + Finerenone]
      FIDELIO[↓ ESRD/CV death]
    RRT
      G4[Refer nephrology, AV fistula]
      G5[Dialysis/Transplant]

15. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Commonest cause ESRD worldwide (DKD ~40–50%)
  2. Screening: UACR annually (T2DM from diagnosis; T1DM 5yr post)
  3. Microalbuminuria: UACR 3–30 mg/mmol; Macro: >30 mg/mmol
  4. KDIGO staging: GFR (G1–G5) + Albuminuria (A1–A3) matrix
  5. DKD vs non-diabetic: retinopathy (T1DM), haematuria, rapid decline
  6. BP <130/80; ACEi/ARB 1st line; NO dual RAAS blockade
  7. SGLT2i: CREDENCE/DAPA-CKD/EMPA-KIDNEY; initiate eGFR ≥20; continue to dialysis
  8. GLP-1 RA add-on; Finerenone add-on if albuminuria persists
  9. No retinopathy + T1DM = biopsy
  10. G4: refer nephrology; G5: RRT

16. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

17. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Screening (UACR), albuminuria thresholds, KDIGO staging, DKD vs non-diabetic, ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i (trials, eGFR cutoff), BP target, dual RAAS contraindicated
Should Know 🟡Mogensen stages, GLP-1 RA, finerenone (FIDELIO/FIGARO), metformin dosing in CKD, Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules, referral criteria
Nice to Know 🟢Novel agents (endothelin antagonists, CCR2 inhibitors), genetic risk (APOL1), biopsy indications, conservative care pathway

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest cause of end-stage renal disease worldwide:

A. Hypertension

B. Glomerulonephritis

C. Diabetic kidney disease (~40–50%)

D. Polycystic kidney disease

E. Obstructive uropathy

  1. Microalbuminuria threshold (UACR):

A. <3 mg/mmol

B. 3–30 mg/mmol

C. 30–300 mg/mmol

D. >300 mg/mmol

E. >30 mg/mmol

  1. Macroalbuminuria threshold (UACR):

A. 3–30 mg/mmol

B. >30 mg/mmol

C. >300 mg/mmol

D. <3 mg/mmol

E. 30–300 mg/mmol

  1. Screening for DKD in Type 2 diabetes starts:

A. 5 years after diagnosis

B. At diagnosis

C. At age 40

D. When eGFR <60

E. When hypertension diagnosed

  1. Which finding strongly suggests NON-diabetic kidney disease in a Type 1 diabetic?

A. Retinopathy present

B. Retinopathy absent

C. Gradual GFR decline

D. Microalbuminuria

E. Hypertension

  1. Blood pressure target in DKD (KDIGO 2024):

A. <140/90

B. <130/80

C. <120/70

D. <150/90

E. <160/100

  1. Dual RAAS blockade (ACEi + ARB) in DKD:

A. Recommended for all

B. Recommended if proteinuria >1g

C. Contraindicated (↑ hyperkalaemia, AKI)

D. Only in Type 1 DM

E. Only in Type 2 DM

  1. SGLT2 inhibitors in DKD — eGFR threshold for INITIATION:

A. ≥60

B. ≥45

C. ≥30

D. ≥20

E. ≥15

  1. SGLT2 inhibitor landmark trials showing renal benefit:

A. EMPA-REG, CANVAS, DECLARE

B. CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY

C. DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced, SOLOIST

D. VERTIS-CV, SCORED, FIGARO

E. PIONEER, SUSTAIN, AWARD

  1. Finerenone — class and indication in DKD:

A. SGLT2i; 1st line

B. Non-steroidal MRA; add-on to ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i if albuminuria persists

C. GLP-1 RA; instead of SGLT2i

D. ARB; replace ACEi

E. Statin; for lipids

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 55-year-old man, T2DM 10 years, HbA1c 8.5%, UACR 45 mg/mmol (macroalbuminuria), eGFR 55, on metformin + gliclazide. BP 145/90. Best next step:

A. Increase gliclazide

B. Start SGLT2i (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin) + ACEi/ARB

C. Start GLP-1 RA only

D. Start insulin

E. Refer for biopsy

  1. 30-year-old woman, T1DM 20 years, no retinopathy, UACR 50 mg/mmol, eGFR 50, active sediment (RBC casts). Management:

A. Start SGLT2i + ACEi

B. Renal biopsy (atypical: no retinopathy + active sediment)

C. Increase insulin

D. Start finerenone

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 65-year-old man, T2DM, DKD (UACR 300 mg/mmol, eGFR 35), on ACEi + SGLT2i, persistent albuminuria, K+ 4.5. Add:

A. ARB (dual blockade)

B. Finerenone

C. Spironolactone 25mg

D. Increase SGLT2i dose

E. Stop ACEi

  1. DKD patient, eGFR 25, on dapagliflozin. eGFR drops to 18. SGLT2i management:

A. Stop immediately

B. Continue (continue to dialysis if tolerated)

C. Reduce dose

D. Switch to empagliflozin

E. Stop and restart at eGFR >20

  1. Type 1 DM, 15 years, microalbuminuria (UACR 15 mg/mmol), eGFR 95, retinopathy present. Management:

A. Biopsy

B. ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + optimise glycaemia/BP

C. Finerenone

D. Cyclophosphamide

E. Monitor only

  1. Metformin in CKD — maximum eGFR for SAFE use:

A. 60

B. 45

C. 30 (caution 30–45; contraindicated <30)

D. 15

E. No limit

  1. KDIGO risk category: G3a (eGFR 50) + A3 (UACR 50 mg/mmol):

A. Low

B. Moderate

C. High

D. Very High

E. Not classifiable

  1. Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules are pathognomonic for:

A. FSGS

B. Membranous nephropathy

C. Diabetic nephropathy

D. Amyloidosis

E. Hypertensive nephrosclerosis

  1. Non-diabetic CKD also benefits from SGLT2i — proven in:

A. CREDENCE only

B. DAPA-CKD and EMPA-KIDNEY

C. EMPA-REG only

D. CANVAS only

E. DECLARE only

  1. 60-year-old man, T2DM, DKD G4 (eGFR 22), referred to nephrology. Key preparation:

A. Start dialysis immediately

B. AV fistula planning + transplant assessment + conservative care discussion

C. Stop all medications

D. Only dietary restriction

E. Increase diuretics

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest cause ESRD worldwide?

A: Diabetic Kidney Disease (~40–50%)

  • Q: DKD screening T2DM?

A: UACR annually from diagnosis

  • Q: DKD screening T1DM?

A: UACR annually from 5 years post-diagnosis

  • Q: Microalbuminuria UACR?

A: 3–30 mg/mmol (30–300 mg/day)

  • Q: Macroalbuminuria UACR?

A: >30 mg/mmol (>300 mg/day)

  • Q: KDIGO staging?

A: GFR (G1–G5) + Albuminuria (A1–A3) matrix

  • Q: DKD vs non-diabetic key feature T1DM?

A: Retinopathy absent = suspect non-diabetic

  • Q: BP target DKD?

A: <130/80

  • Q: ACEi/ARB dual blockade?

A: Contraindicated

  • Q: SGLT2i trials renal benefit?

A: CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY

  • Q: SGLT2i initiation eGFR?

A: ≥20

  • Q: SGLT2i continue to?

A: Dialysis

  • Q: Finerenone?

A: Non-steroidal MRA; add-on to ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i

  • Q: GLP-1 RA in DKD?

A: CV/renal benefit; add-on

  • Q: Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules?

A: DKD pathognomonic

  • Q: Metformin CKD safe eGFR?

A: ≥30 (caution 30–45); stop <30

  • Q: G4 referral?

A: AV fistula, transplant assessment, conservative care

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — DKD = commonest cause ESRD worldwide (~40–50%)
  2. B — Microalbuminuria = UACR 3–30 mg/mmol
  3. B — Macroalbuminuria = UACR >30 mg/mmol
  4. B — T2DM: screen from diagnosis
  5. B — T1DM without retinopathy = suspect non-diabetic kidney disease (biopsy considered)
  6. B — KDIGO 2024: <130/80
  7. C — ONTARGET/VA NEPHRON: dual RAAS blockade ↑ hyperkalaemia, AKI
  8. D — SGLT2i initiate eGFR ≥20 (CREDENCE ≥30, DAPA-CKD ≥25, EMPA-KIDNEY ≥20)
  9. B — CREDENCE (canagliflozin), DAPA-CKD (dapagliflozin), EMPA-KIDNEY (empagliflozin)
  10. B — Finerenone = non-steroidal MRA; add-on if albuminuria persists on ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i

SBAs

  1. B — Macroalbuminuria + eGFR 55 → SGLT2i + ACEi/ARB (cornerstone)
  2. B — T1DM + no retinopathy + active sediment = atypical → biopsy
  3. B — Persistent albuminuria on ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i → add finerenone (FIDELIO)
  4. B — SGLT2i continue to dialysis if tolerated (EMPA-KIDNEY)
  5. B — T1DM + retinopathy + microalbuminuria = typical DKD → ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i
  6. C — Metformin: safe ≥30; caution 30–45; contraindicated <30
  7. D — G3a + A3 = Very High (KDIGO matrix)
  8. C — Kimmelstiel-Wilson = DKD pathognomonic
  9. B — DAPA-CKD + EMPA-KIDNEY included non-diabetic CKD
  10. B — G4: AV fistula planning, transplant assessment, conservative care discussion

22. Summary

Diabetic Nephropathy is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Commonest ESRD cause. Screen: UACR annually (T2DM from diagnosis, T1DM 5yr post). Micro: 3–30 mg/mmol; Macro: >30 mg/mmol. KDIGO G+A matrix for risk. No retinopathy + T1DM = biopsy. BP <130/80; ACEi/ARB 1st line; NO dual blockade. SGLT2i game-changer (CREDENCE/DAPA-CKD/EMPA-KIDNEY) — initiate eGFR ≥20, continue to dialysis. GLP-1 RA add-on. Finerenone add-on if albuminuria persists. Metformin safe ≥30. G4: refer nephrology (AV fistula, transplant); G5: RRT.

Exam focus: Screening, albuminuria thresholds, KDIGO staging, DKD vs non-diabetic, ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i trials/cutoffs, dual RAAS contraindicated, finerenone/GLP-1 RA, referral criteria.

Clinical relevance: Early SGLT2i + ACEi/ARB dramatically slows progression; comprehensive multifactorial management reduces CV/renal events.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.3.9 Renal Amyloidosis
Topic: Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Amyloidosis — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Lupus Nephritis]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — ANCA-Associated Vasculitis (GPA, MPA, EGPA)]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Diabetic Nephropathy]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Renal Amyloidosis = extracellular deposition of misfolded protein fibrils (β-pleated sheets). AL (light chain) = commonest renal; AA (amyloid A) = chronic inflammation; Hereditary (ATTR, AFib, etc.). Renal: nephrotic syndrome, CKD. Diagnosis: biopsy + Congo red + apple-green birefringence + typing (immunohistochemistry/mass spec). Treat underlying cause.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify amyloid types (AL, AA, hereditary, dialysis-related)
  • Recognise renal and systemic manifestations
  • Apply diagnostic algorithm (biopsy, Congo red, typing)
  • Manage per type (AL: chemotherapy; AA: treat inflammation; hereditary: TTR stabilisers/silencers)

2. Classification & Aetiology

TypePrecursor ProteinSourceClinical Context
AL (Amyloid Light Chain)Immunoglobulin light chain (κ or λ)Plasma cell dyscrasia (MGUS, myeloma, Waldenström's)Commonest renal amyloidosis; median age 60–70; λ > κ
AA (Amyloid A)Serum amyloid A (SAA)Chronic inflammation (RA, JIA, IBD, FMF, TB, bronchiectasis)Secondary to chronic inflammatory disease
ATTR (Transthyretin)TransthyretinHereditary (ATTRv: Val30Met, etc.) or Wild-type (ATTRwt: elderly, cardiac)Cardiac + neuropathy ± renal
AFib (Fibrinogen Aα)Fibrinogen Aα-chainHereditaryRenal dominant (amyloid nephropathy)
Aβ2M (β2-Microglobulin)β2-MicroglobulinDialysis-related (long-term HD >10–15yr)Carpal tunnel, arthropathy, bone cysts; renal not affected (already ESRD)

3. Renal Manifestations

FeatureAL AmyloidosisAA AmyloidosisHereditary (AFib, ATTR)
ProteinuriaNephrotic range commonNephrotic range commonVariable
Nephrotic Syndrome~70%~50%Common (AFib)
Renal ImpairmentCommon at presentationVariableProgressive CKD
HaematuriaRareRareRare
HypertensionUncommon (vs diabetic)VariableVariable
Kidney SizeNormal/Large (infiltration)Normal/LargeNormal/Small late
[!key]
Normal/Large kidneys + nephrotic syndrome + no retinopathy + low BP = think amyloidosis.

4. Systemic Manifestations (AL)

SystemFeatures
HeartRestrictive cardiomyopathy (thickened walls, preserved EF, low voltage ECG), arrhythmias, HFpEF
NervesPeripheral neuropathy (painful, autonomic), carpal tunnel
GIMacroglossia (pathognomonic), GI dysmotility, bleeding, malabsorption
LiverHepatomegaly, elevated ALP
SkinPurpura (periorbital, "raccoon eyes"), waxy plaques, nail changes
Soft TissueShoulder pads (arthropathy), jaw claudication
BleedingFactor X deficiency (adsorption to amyloid) → coagulopathy

5. Diagnosis

1. Suspicion

  • Nephrotic syndrome + normal/large kidneys + no hypertension + systemic features (heart, nerve, GI, skin)
  • AL: Age >50, monoclonal protein, cardiac involvement
  • AA: Chronic inflammatory disease
  • Hereditary: Family history, neuropathy/cardiac (ATTR), renal dominant (AFib)

2. Biopsy (Gold Standard)

StainFinding
Congo RedApple-green birefringence under polarised light (β-pleated sheets)
Thioflavin TFluorescence (sensitive)
Electron MicroscopyNon-branching fibrils 7–10 nm (random orientation)

3. Typing (Essential for Treatment)

MethodDetail
Immunohistochemistryκ, λ, AA, TTR, fibrinogen — widely available, cross-reactivity possible
Laser Microdissection + Mass Spectrometry (LMD-MS)Gold standard — definitive typing
Serum Free Light Chains (sFLC)AL: abnormal κ/λ ratio + involved FLC elevated
Serum/Urinary ImmunofixationDetect monoclonal protein
Bone Marrow BiopsyPlasma cell % (MGUS <10%, myeloma ≥10%)
SAA LevelAA: elevated in active inflammation
[!key]
Typing MUST be done before treatment — AL vs AA vs hereditary = completely different management.

6. AL Amyloidosis — Staging & Treatment

Staging (Mayo 2012)

StageCriteriaMedian OS
INT-proBNP <1800, Troponin T <0.025, Involved FLC <180~94 months
II1 criterion elevated~40 months
III2 criteria elevated~14 months
IV3 criteria elevated~6 months

Treatment (Plasma Cell Directed)

RegimenIndication
Daratumumab + CyBorD (Dara-CyBorD)New standard 1st line (ANDROMEDA trial: ↑ haematologic CR, ↑ organ response)
CyBorD (Cyclophosphamide + Bortezomib + Dexamethasone)1st line if daratumumab unavailable
Bortezomib-basedRenal impairment (bortezomib not renally cleared)
ASCT (Autologous Stem Cell Transplant)Eligible patients (age <70, stage I–II, adequate organ function) — consolidation post-induction
Venetoclaxt(11;14) translocation (BCL2 high)

Response Criteria (Haematologic)

ResponseCriteria
CRInvolved FLC normal + normal κ/λ ratio + negative immunofixation + <5% marrow plasma cells
VGPRInvolved FLC <40 mg/L
PRInvolved FLC ↓ >50%
Organ Response↓ Proteinuria >50% (renal), ↓ NT-proBNP >30% (cardiac), ↓ liver span/ALP (hepatic)

7. AA Amyloidosis — Treatment

PrincipleDetail
Treat underlying inflammationControl SAA <10 mg/L (target) → halts amyloid deposition, may regress
Anti-inflammatoryDMARDs (methotrexate, sulfasalazine), biologics (anti-TNF, anti-IL-1, anti-IL-6), colchicine (FMF)
ColchicineFMF: lifelong prophylaxis (prevents amyloidosis)
No chemotherapyNot plasma cell disorder

8. Hereditary Amyloidosis — Treatment

TypeTreatment
ATTRv (Hereditary TTR)TTR stabilisers: Tafamidis, Diflunisal; Gene silencers: Patisiran (siRNA), Inotersen (ASO), Vutrisiran (siRNA)
ATTRwt (Wild-type)Tafamidis (cardiomyopathy); supportive
AFibLiver transplant (source of fibrinogen) ± kidney transplant; TTR silencers not effective
OtherOrgan-specific; genetic counselling

9. Dialysis-Related Amyloidosis (Aβ2M)

FeatureDetail
Causeβ2-microglobulin retention (long-term HD >10–15yr; low-flux membranes)
ManifestationsCarpal tunnel syndrome, destructive arthropathy (shoulders, hips), bone cysts, pathological fractures, flexor tenosynovitis
PreventionHigh-flux dialysers, longer dialysis, convective therapies (HDF)
TreatmentSymptomatic; kidney transplant (resolves)

10. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Renal amyloidosis: AL (commonest renal), AA (chronic inflammation), Hereditary (ATTR, AFib), Aβ2M (dialysis)
- AL = plasma cell dyscrasia (MGUS/myeloma); λ > κ
- Renal: Nephrotic syndrome, normal/large kidneys, no hypertension
- AL Systemic: Heart (restrictive CM, low voltage ECG), GI (macroglossia), nerves, liver, skin (purpura), Factor X deficiency
- Diagnosis: Biopsy + Congo red + apple-green birefringence + TYPING (IHC/LMD-MS)
- AL Typing: sFLC (abnormal κ/λ ratio), immunofixation, bone marrow
- AL Staging: Mayo 2012 (NT-proBNP, Troponin, involved FLC)
- AL Treatment: Dara-CyBorD 1st line (ANDROMEDA); ASCT eligible; Bortezomib-based if renal impairment
- AA Treatment: Treat inflammation → SAA <10 mg/L; colchicine for FMF
- ATTR: Tafamidis (stabiliser), Patisiran/Inotersen/Vutrisiran (silencers)
- AFib: Liver transplant ± kidney transplant
- Aβ2M: Long-term HD; carpal tunnel, arthropathy; high-flux dialysers prevent

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All amyloid = ALAA, hereditary, dialysis-related = different treatment
Congo red = diagnosis completeTyping essential — AL vs AA vs hereditary = different treatment
AL = myelomaAL = plasma cell dyscrasia (MGUS, smouldering, myeloma); most have MGUS
AA = only RAAny chronic inflammation (IBD, FMF, TB, bronchiectasis, etc.)
Amyloid kidneys = smallNormal/LARGE (infiltration) — distinguishes from diabetic/chronic
AL treatment = only ASCTDara-CyBorD 1st line (ANDROMEDA); ASCT = consolidation for eligible
SAA = only for AA diagnosisSAA <10 = treatment target for AA
ATTR = only cardiacATTRv = neuropathy + cardiac + renal; ATTRwt = cardiac dominant
Aβ2M = renal amyloidosisKidneys already ESRD; deposits in joints/tendons/bone
Factor X deficiency = only ALCan occur in any amyloid (adsorption to fibrils) but commonest in AL

12. Mnemonics

  • Amyloid Types: AL (Light chain), AA (Amyloid A), ATTR (Transthyretin), AFib (Fibrinogen), Aβ2M (β2-Microglobulin) = AL-AA-ATTR-AFib-Aβ2M
  • AL Source: Plasma Cell Dyscrasia = PCD
  • AL Renal: Nephrotic, Normal/Large kidneys, No HT = NNLNH
  • AL Systemic: Heart, GI (macroglossia), Nerves, Liver, Skin, Bleeding = HGNLSB
  • Diagnosis: Congo Red + Apple-Green + Typing = CRAG-T
  • AL Staging: NT-proBNP, Troponin, FLC = NTF
  • AL Treatment: Dara-CyBorD 1st line = DC1
  • AA Target: SAA <10 = S<10
  • ATTR: Tafamidis (stabiliser), Patisiran/Inotersen/Vutrisiran (silencers) = T-PIV
  • Aβ2M: High-flux Dialysers Prevent = HDP

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Amyloidosis))
    Types
      AL[Plasma cell dyscrasia, λ>κ]
      AA[Chronic inflammation, SAA]
      ATTRv[Hereditary TTR, neuropathy+cardiac]
      ATTRwt[Wild-type TTR, elderly cardiac]
      AFib[Hereditary fibrinogen, renal dominant]
      Aβ2M[Dialysis-related, >10-15yr HD]
    Renal
      Nephrotic[Common]
      Kidneys[Normal/Large]
      HT[Absent/Low]
    Systemic_AL
      Heart[Restrictive CM, low voltage ECG]
      GI[Macroglossia]
      Nerves[Peripheral, autonomic]
      Liver[Hepatomegaly]
      Skin[Purpura, raccoon eyes]
      Bleeding[Factor X deficiency]
    Diagnosis
      Biopsy[Congo red + apple-green birefringence]
      EM[7-10nm non-branching fibrils]
      Typing[IHC / LMD-MS (gold standard)]
      AL_Markers[sFLC ratio, immunofixation, bone marrow]
      AA_Markers[SAA]
    Treatment
      AL[Dara-CyBorD 1st line; ASCT eligible; Bortezomib if renal]
      AA[Control inflammation; SAA<10; Colchicine FMF]
      ATTR[Tafamidis; Patisiran/Inotersen/Vutrisiran]
      AFib[Liver transplant ± kidney]
      Aβ2M[High-flux dialysers; transplant resolves]

14. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Amyloid types: AL, AA, ATTR, AFib, Aβ2M
  2. AL = plasma cell dyscrasia (MGUS/myeloma); λ > κ
  3. Renal: nephrotic + normal/large kidneys + no HT
  4. AL systemic: heart (restrictive CM, low voltage), macroglossia, nerves, Factor X deficiency
  5. Diagnosis: Congo red + apple-green birefringence + TYPING (essential)
  6. AL typing: sFLC ratio, immunofixation, bone marrow
  7. AL staging: Mayo (NT-proBNP, Troponin, involved FLC)
  8. AL treatment: Dara-CyBorD 1st line (ANDROMEDA); ASCT consolidation
  9. AA treatment: treat inflammation → SAA <10; colchicine FMF
  10. ATTR: tafamidis (stabiliser), patisiran/inotersen/vutrisiran (silencers)
  11. AFib: liver transplant ± kidney
  12. Aβ2M: long HD; carpal tunnel, arthropathy; high-flux dialysers

15. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

16. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Types (AL/AA/hereditary/dialysis), renal features (nephrotic, large kidneys, no HT), AL systemic features, diagnosis (Congo red + typing), AL staging, AL treatment (Dara-CyBorD), AA treatment (SAA<10), ATTR treatments
Should Know 🟡ANDROMEDA trial, Mayo staging details, organ response criteria, hereditary types details, Aβ2M prevention, Factor X deficiency
Nice to Know 🟢Novel therapies (anti-fibril antibodies, SAP clearance), genetic counselling, pregnancy in amyloidosis, cost-effectiveness

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest type of renal amyloidosis:

A. AA amyloidosis

B. AL amyloidosis

C. ATTR amyloidosis

D. AFib amyloidosis

E. Aβ2M amyloidosis

  1. Precursor protein in AL amyloidosis:

A. Serum amyloid A

B. Transthyretin

C. Immunoglobulin light chain (κ or λ)

D. Fibrinogen Aα

E. β2-microglobulin

  1. Classic renal presentation of amyloidosis:

A. Nephritic syndrome, small kidneys, hypertension

B. Nephrotic syndrome, normal/large kidneys, no hypertension

C. Asymptomatic haematuria, normal kidneys

D. AKI, normal kidneys

E. Tubulointerstitial nephritis

  1. Congo red stain under polarised light shows:

A. Red birefringence

B. Apple-green birefringence

C. Blue fluorescence

D. Yellow birefringence

E. No birefringence

  1. AL amyloidosis — typing is essential because:

A. Determines prognosis only

B. Treatment differs completely (AL vs AA vs hereditary)

C. Only for research

D. Needed for transplant listing

E. Determines dialysis modality

  1. Mayo 2012 staging for AL amyloidosis uses:

A. Creatinine, albumin, proteinuria

B. NT-proBNP, Troponin T, Involved FLC

C. Age, cardiac EF, liver size

D. Bone marrow %, M-protein, FLC

E. SAA, CRP, ESR

  1. First-line treatment for AL amyloidosis (ANDROMEDA trial):

A. CyBorD

B. Daratumumab + CyBorD (Dara-CyBorD)

C. Bortezomib + dexamethasone

D. ASCT upfront

E. Melphalan + prednisone

  1. AA amyloidosis — treatment target for SAA:

A. <50 mg/L

B. <10 mg/L

C. <100 mg/L

D. Normalise CRP only

E. No specific target

  1. Hereditary ATTR amyloidosis — disease-modifying treatments:

A. Chemotherapy

B. Tafamidis (stabiliser); Patisiran/Inotersen/Vutrisiran (gene silencers)

C. Colchicine

D. Daratumumab

E. Plasma exchange

  1. Dialysis-related amyloidosis (Aβ2M) — prevention:

A. Low-flux dialysers

B. High-flux dialysers / HDF

C. Shorter dialysis sessions

D. Peritoneal dialysis only

E. No prevention possible

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 65-year-old man, nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria 8g/day), normal BP, normal-sized kidneys on US, echocardiogram shows thickened walls, preserved EF, low voltage ECG. Serum free light chains: κ 120, λ 5 (ratio 24). Bone marrow: 8% plasma cells. Diagnosis:

A. AA amyloidosis

B. AL amyloidosis (MGUS)

C. Diabetic nephropathy

D. Membranous nephropathy

E. ATTR amyloidosis

  1. Same patient — biopsy done, Congo red positive with apple-green birefringence. Next crucial step:

A. Start CyBorD

B. Typing (immunohistochemistry or LMD-MS) to confirm AL

C. Start daratumumab

D. Refer for ASCT

E. Bone marrow biopsy only

  1. 50-year-old woman, rheumatoid arthritis 20 years, nephrotic syndrome, elevated CRP, SAA 150 mg/L. No monoclonal protein. Diagnosis:

A. AL amyloidosis

B. AA amyloidosis

C. ATTR amyloidosis

D. AFib amyloidosis

E. Lupus nephritis

  1. AA amyloidosis — target for treatment:

A. CRP <5

B. SAA <10 mg/L

C. ESR <20

D. Proteinuria <1g

E. No specific target

  1. 40-year-old man, family history of neuropathy and cardiac disease, presents with cardiomyopathy and carpal tunnel. Genetic testing: TTR Val30Met. Treatment:

A. CyBorD

B. Tafamidis or Patisiran/Inotersen/Vutrisiran

C. Colchicine

D. Daratumumab

E. Liver transplant

  1. Hereditary fibrinogen Aα-chain amyloidosis (AFib) — definitive treatment:

A. Tafamidis

B. Patisiran

C. Liver transplant (± kidney transplant)

D. Daratumumab

E. Colchicine

  1. Long-term haemodialysis patient (15 years), carpal tunnel syndrome, shoulder pain, bone cysts on X-ray. Diagnosis:

A. AL amyloidosis

B. AA amyloidosis

C. ATTR amyloidosis

D. Aβ2M amyloidosis (dialysis-related)

E. AFib amyloidosis

  1. AL amyloidosis — Factor X deficiency causes:

A. Thrombosis

B. Bleeding diathesis

C. No clinical effect

D. Heparin resistance

E. Warfarin resistance

  1. AL amyloidosis — cardiac staging biomarker NOT in Mayo 2012:

A. NT-proBNP

B. Troponin T

C. Troponin I

D. Involved FLC

E. None (all included)

  1. ASCT in AL amyloidosis — eligible patients:

A. All patients

B. Age <70, Stage I–II, adequate organ function (post-induction consolidation)

C. Only Stage III–IV

D. Only if daratumumab fails

E. Never indicated

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest renal amyloidosis?

A: AL amyloidosis

  • Q: AL precursor protein?

A: Immunoglobulin light chain (κ/λ)

  • Q: AA precursor protein?

A: Serum amyloid A (SAA)

  • Q: Renal amyloid classic triad?

A: Nephrotic syndrome + normal/large kidneys + no hypertension

  • Q: AL systemic features?

A: Heart (restrictive CM, low voltage ECG), macroglossia, neuropathy, hepatomegaly, purpura, Factor X deficiency

  • Q: Congo red microscopy?

A: Apple-green birefringence under polarised light

  • Q: Amyloid typing methods?

A: Immunohistochemistry, LMD-MS (gold standard)

  • Q: AL markers?

A: sFLC (abnormal κ/λ ratio), immunofixation, bone marrow plasma cells

  • Q: Mayo 2012 staging?

A: NT-proBNP, Troponin T, Involved FLC → Stage I–IV

  • Q: AL treatment 1st line?

A: Dara-CyBorD (ANDROMEDA trial)

  • Q: AA treatment target?

A: SAA <10 mg/L

  • Q: ATTR treatments?

A: Tafamidis (stabiliser); Patisiran/Inotersen/Vutrisiran (silencers)

  • Q: AFib treatment?

A: Liver transplant ± kidney transplant

  • Q: Aβ2M prevention?

A: High-flux dialysers / HDF

  • Q: Factor X deficiency in amyloid?

A: Bleeding diathesis

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — AL = commonest renal amyloidosis
  2. C — AL = immunoglobulin light chain (κ/λ)
  3. B — Nephrotic + normal/large kidneys + no HT = amyloid classic
  4. B — Congo red = apple-green birefringence (pathognomonic)
  5. B — AL vs AA vs hereditary = completely different treatments
  6. B — Mayo 2012: NT-proBNP, Troponin T, involved FLC
  7. B — ANDROMEDA: Dara-CyBorD superior to CyBorD
  8. B — AA: SAA <10 mg/L target
  9. B — ATTR: tafamidis (stabiliser), patisiran/inotersen/vutrisiran (silencers)
  10. B — Aβ2M: high-flux dialysers/HDF prevent

SBAs

  1. B — Nephrotic + normal kidneys + no HT + cardiac (low voltage, thickened) + monoclonal FLC (κ 120, λ 5, ratio 24) + 8% marrow = AL (MGUS)
  2. B — Typing essential before treatment (AL vs AA vs hereditary)
  3. B — RA + nephrotic + elevated SAA + no monoclonal protein = AA
  4. B — AA: SAA <10 mg/L
  5. B — ATTR Val30Met = tafamidis or gene silencers (patisiran, etc.)
  6. C — AFib = fibrinogen from liver → liver transplant ± kidney
  7. D — Long-term HD + carpal tunnel + arthropathy + bone cysts = Aβ2M
  8. B — Factor X adsorption → bleeding
  9. C — Mayo uses Troponin T (not I)
  10. B — ASCT: age <70, Stage I–II, adequate organs; consolidation post-induction

21. Summary

Amyloidosis is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Types: AL (plasma cell dyscrasia, λ>κ, commonest renal), AA (chronic inflammation, SAA), Hereditary (ATTRv/wt, AFib), Aβ2M (long-term HD). Renal: nephrotic + normal/large kidneys + no HT. AL systemic: heart (restrictive CM, low voltage), macroglossia, neuropathy, Factor X deficiency. Diagnosis: Biopsy + Congo red (apple-green) + TYPING (essential). AL: sFLC ratio, immunofixation, marrow. Mayo staging: NT-proBNP, Troponin, involved FLC. AL Tx: Dara-CyBorD 1st line (ANDROMEDA); ASCT eligible. AA: SAA <10 target. ATTR: tafamidis, patisiran/inotersen/vutrisiran. AFib: liver transplant. Aβ2M: high-flux dialysers.

Exam focus: Types, renal triad, AL systemic features, Congo red, typing essential, AL staging, Dara-CyBorD, AA SAA target, ATTR treatments, AFib liver transplant, Aβ2M prevention.

Clinical relevance: Early typing directs life-saving therapy; Dara-CyBorD improved AL outcomes; SAA control halts AA progression.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.4 18.4 15
▸ 18.4.1 Acute Interstitial Nephritis Ain
Topic: Tubulointerstitial Diseases — Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Tubulointerstitial Diseases — Chronic Interstitial Nephritis]], [[Tubulointerstitial Diseases — Drug-Induced Tubulointerstitial Injury]], [[Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
AIN = acute interstitial inflammation + oedema. Drug-induced (70%): NSAIDs, antibiotics (β-lactams, quinolones), PPIs, diuretics. Infections (legionella, strep), autoimmune (Sjögren's, TINU), idiopathic. Classic triad: fever, rash, eosinophilia (only ~10–30%). Diagnosis: biopsy (interstitial oedema, mononuclear infiltrate, eosinophils). Treatment: stop offending drug ± steroids (1mg/kg if severe).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise clinical presentations (drug-induced, infectious, autoimmune)
  • Identify classic triad and its limitations
  • Apply diagnostic approach (urine eosinophils, biopsy indications)
  • Manage with drug withdrawal and steroid therapy
  • Differentiate from ATN, GN, other AKI causes

2. Aetiology

CategoryExamplesNotes
Drugs (70%)NSAIDs (commonest), Antibiotics (β-lactams, quinolones, sulfonamides, rifampicin), PPIs (omeprazole, lansoprazole), Diuretics (thiazides, furosemide), Allopurinol, ICIs (immune checkpoint inhibitors)Latency: NSAIDs/PPIs weeks–months; antibiotics/allopurinol days–weeks
InfectionsLegionella, Streptococcus, Mycobacteria, Viruses (Hantavirus, CMV, EBV, HIV), LeptospiraOften part of systemic illness
Autoimmune/SystemicSjögren's syndrome, TINU (Tubulointerstitial Nephritis + Uveitis), Sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease, SLE, ANCA vasculitisTINU = young women, uveitis precedes/follows renal
Idiopathic~10–15%Diagnosis of exclusion

3. Clinical Presentation

FeatureDrug-InducedNSAID-InducedInfectious/Autoimmune
OnsetDays–weeks (antibiotics); weeks–months (NSAIDs/PPIs)Insidious (weeks–months)Acute/subacute
Fever~30–50%RareCommon
Rash~15–30% (maculopapular)RareVariable
Eosinophilia~10–30% (peripheral)RareVariable
Eosinophiluria~50–80% (Hansel's stain)~30%Variable
Urine OutputNon-oliguric AKI commonNon-oliguricVariable
ExtrarenalArthralgia, myalgiaNephrotic syndrome (minimal change overlap)Uveitis (TINU), sicca (Sjögren's)
[!key]
Classic triad (fever, rash, eosinophilia) = ONLY ~10–30% of cases. Do not exclude AIN if triad absent.

4. Urine Findings

FindingSignificance
Eosinophiluria (Hansel's stain)Sensitivity ~50–80%, Specificity ~70–80%; also in atheroembolic, pyelo, GN, obstruction
WBC castsInterstitial inflammation (AIN, pyelonephritis)
Sterile pyuriaCommon
ProteinuriaUsually subnephrotic (<1g); NSAIDs can cause nephrotic range (MCD overlap)
HaematuriaMicroscopic common
Tubular proteinuriaLow molecular weight proteins (β2-microglobulin, NAG)

5. Diagnostic Approach

StepAction
1. Drug HistoryMeticulous: all drugs (prescribed, OTC, herbal) in past 3 months; focus on NSAIDs, antibiotics, PPIs, diuretics
2. Stop Suspected DrugImmediate withdrawal — single most important intervention
3. Urine TestsMicroscopy (WBC casts, eosinophils), eosinophiluria (Hansel's)
4. Blood TestsEosinophilia, CRP, IgE, complement (usually normal), ANCA, ANA, SPEP
5. ImagingUS: normal/large kidneys, loss of corticomedullary differentiation
6. Renal BiopsyGold standard if: diagnosis uncertain, no response to drug withdrawal in 5–7 days, severe AKI (Cr >300), need for steroids

6. Histopathology (Biopsy)

ModalityFindings
Light MicroscopyInterstitial oedema, mononuclear infiltrate (lymphocytes, plasma cells, macrophages), eosinophils (prominent in drug-induced); tubulitis; no glomerular involvement
ImmunofluorescenceUsually negative; occasional IgM/C3 in tubular basement membrane (non-specific)
Electron MicroscopyTubular basement membrane disruption; no deposits

7. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Feature
ATNMuddy brown casts, FeNa >2%, no eosinophilia/rash/fever, no WBC casts
Acute GNHaematuria, RBC casts, proteinuria, hypertension, low complement
PyelonephritisFever, flank pain, bacteriuria, WBC casts, positive culture
Atheroembolic DiseaseEosinophilia, eosinophiluria, livedo reticularis, post-vascular procedure, hypertension
Obstructive UropathyHydronephrosis on US, anuria/fluctuating output
NSAID-induced MCDNephrotic syndrome, selective proteinuria, minimal change on biopsy

8. Management

StepAction
1. Stop Offending DrugImmediate — most important step; ~50–70% recover with withdrawal alone
2. SupportiveFluids, avoid nephrotoxins, monitor electrolytes, manage complications
3. Steroids (if indicated)Indications: Severe AKI (Cr >300), no improvement after 5–7 days drug withdrawal, significant extrarenal features
Steroid RegimenPrednisolone 1 mg/kg/day (max 60–80mg) × 2–4 weeks → taper over 4–8 weeks; total 6–12 weeks
4. MonitorSerial Cr, eGFR; if no improvement by 2 weeks → re-evaluate (biopsy if not done)
[!key]
Early steroid initiation (within 1–2 weeks) → better renal recovery. Delay >2–4 weeks = worse outcome.

9. Prognosis & Outcomes

FactorBetter PrognosisWorse Prognosis
Timing of steroid<1–2 weeks>4 weeks
Drug typeAntibiotics, PPIsNSAIDs (often incomplete recovery)
Baseline CKDNonePre-existing CKD
SeverityNon-oliguric, Cr <300Oliguric, Cr >400
Interstitial fibrosis on biopsyMinimalSevere (T score equivalent)
[!key]
NSAID-AIN: often incomplete recovery, higher risk of chronic interstitial nephritis.

10. Special Syndromes

SyndromeFeatures
TINU (Tubulointerstitial Nephritis + Uveitis)Young women; bilateral uveitis ± renal; steroids for both
Sjögren's SyndromeSicca + renal (AIN, RTA type 1, nephrolithiasis); ANA+, anti-Ro/La+
IgG4-Related DiseaseIgG4+ plasma cells on biopsy, storiform fibrosis, obliterative phlebitis; multi-organ
Checkpoint Inhibitor NephritisICI (anti-PD1/PD-L1/CTLA4); AIN histology; high steroids; hold ICI

11. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- AIN = drug-induced (70%): NSAIDs (commonest), antibiotics, PPIs, diuretics
- Classic triad (fever, rash, eosinophilia) = only 10–30% — do not rely on it
- Eosinophiluria (Hansel's): sensitivity 50–80%, not specific
- WBC casts = interstitial inflammation
- Biopsy: interstitial oedema + mononuclear infiltrate + eosinophils
- Step 1: Stop drug — 50–70% recover with withdrawal alone
- Steroids: Pred 1mg/kg × 2–4wk → taper 4–8wk if severe/no improvement in 5–7d
- Early steroids (<2 weeks) = better recovery
- NSAID-AIN: often nephrotic overlap (MCD), incomplete recovery
- TINU: young women, uveitis + AIN
- Checkpoint inhibitor nephritis: AIN histology, hold ICI, high-dose steroids

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
No triad = not AINTriad only 10–30%; most have NONE
Eosinophiluria = specific for AINAlso in atheroembolic, pyelo, GN, obstruction
NSAID-AIN = same as other drug AINNSAID-AIN: insidious, often nephrotic (MCD overlap), higher chronicity
All AIN need steroidsMild/improving with drug withdrawal → no steroids
Steroids can be delayedEarly (<2 weeks) = better recovery; delay = worse
Biopsy always neededNot if clear drug history + improvement with withdrawal
Checkpoint inhibitor = ATNUsually AIN histology; needs steroids
AIN = always reversibleCan progress to chronic interstitial nephritis/CKD

13. Mnemonics

  • AIN Drugs: NSAIDs, Antibiotics, PPIs, Diuretics = NAPD
  • Triad: Fever, Rash, Eosinophilia = FRE (only 10–30%)
  • Diagnosis: Stop drug, Eosinophiluria, WBC casts, Biopsy = SEWB
  • Steroids: Early (<2wk) = Better = EB
  • NSAID-AIN: Nephrotic, Insidious, Chronic = NIC
  • TINU: Tubulointerstitial Nephritis + Uveitis = young women
  • Sjögren's: Sicca + AIN + RTA + Stones = SARS

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Acute Interstitial Nephritis))
    Aetiology
      Drugs[NSAIDs, Antibiotics, PPIs, Diuretics]
      Infections[Legionella, Strep, Viruses]
      Autoimmune[Sjögren's, TINU, IgG4, Sarcoid]
      Checkpoint_ Inhibitors[Anti-PD1/PD-L1]
    Clinical
      Triad[Fever, Rash, Eosinophilia ~10-30%]
      Drug_Latency[Abx days; NSAIDs/PPIs months]
      Urine[Eosinophiluria, WBC casts, sterile pyuria]
    Diagnosis
      Drug_History[Meticulous 3 months]
      Stop_Drug[Immediate]
      Biopsy[If uncertain/severe/no improvement]
    Histology
      LM[Interstitial oedema, mononuclear + eosinophils]
      IF[Negative]
      EM[TBM disruption]
    Management
      Stop_Drug[50-70% recover]
      Steroids[Pred 1mg/kg 2-4wk if severe/no improvement]
      Early[<2 weeks = better recovery]

15. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Commonest cause AIN: NSAIDs
  2. Classic triad (fever, rash, eosinophilia) only 10–30%
  3. Drug latency: antibiotics days–weeks; NSAIDs/PPIs weeks–months
  4. Urine: eosinophiluria (Hansel's), WBC casts, sterile pyuria
  5. Step 1: STOP OFFENDING DRUG (50–70% recover)
  6. Steroids: pred 1mg/kg × 2–4wk if severe/no improvement 5–7d
  7. Early steroids (<2 weeks) = better recovery
  8. NSAID-AIN: insidious, nephrotic overlap, higher chronicity
  9. TINU: young women, uveitis + AIN
  10. Checkpoint inhibitor nephritis: AIN histology, hold ICI, steroids

16. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

17. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Drug causes (NSAIDs, antibiotics, PPIs), triad limitations, eosinophiluria/WBC casts, stop drug first, steroid indications/regimen, early steroid timing, NSAID-AIN features
Should Know 🟡TINU, Sjögren's, IgG4-RD, checkpoint inhibitor nephritis, biopsy indications, prognosis factors
Nice to Know 🟢Novel biomarkers, genetic susceptibility, long-term outcomes, pregnancy

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest cause of drug-induced AIN:

A. Antibiotics

B. NSAIDs

C. PPIs

D. Diuretics

E. Allopurinol

  1. Classic triad of AIN (fever, rash, eosinophilia) is present in:

A. >80% of cases

B. 50–60% of cases

C. ~10–30% of cases

D. <5% of cases

E. Only NSAID-induced AIN

  1. Urine finding suggestive of AIN:

A. Muddy brown granular casts

B. WBC casts

C. RBC casts

D. Hyaline casts

E. Fatty casts

  1. Eosinophiluria (Hansel's stain) sensitivity for AIN:

A. 90–95%

B. ~50–80%

C. 30–40%

D. <20%

E. 100%

  1. First and most important step in management of suspected drug-induced AIN:

A. Start steroids immediately

B. Stop the offending drug

C. Renal biopsy

D. Plasma exchange

E. Dialysis

  1. Steroid regimen for AIN (if indicated):

A. Pulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3 days

B. Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day × 2–4 weeks → taper 4–8 weeks

C. Prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day × 12 weeks

D. Pulse cyclophosphamide + steroids

E. Rituximab

  1. NSAID-induced AIN — characteristic features:

A. Acute onset, fever, rash, eosinophilia

B. Insidious onset (weeks–months), often nephrotic range proteinuria (MCD overlap)

C. Prominent eosinophilia >90%

D. Rapid recovery with drug withdrawal

E. Never requires steroids

  1. TINU syndrome — demographics and features:

A. Elderly men, unilateral uveitis

B. Young women, bilateral uveitis + AIN

C. Children, no uveitis

D. Middle-aged, only renal

E. Associated with NSAIDs

  1. Checkpoint inhibitor (anti-PD-1/PD-L1) nephritis — typical histology:

A. ATN

B. AIN (interstitial oedema, mononuclear infiltrate, eosinophils)

C. Glomerulonephritis

D. Thrombotic microangiopathy

E. Minimal change disease

  1. Prognosis factor for WORSE outcome in AIN:

A. Antibiotic cause, early steroids

B. NSAID cause, delayed steroids >4 weeks, pre-existing CKD, interstitial fibrosis on biopsy

C. Young age, no comorbidities

D. Eosinophilia >20%

E. Non-oliguric presentation

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 65-year-old man, recent omeprazole for dyspepsia (3 months), presents with AKI (Cr 280, baseline 90), sterile pyuria, eosinophiluria+. No fever, no rash. Best next step:

A. Start prednisolone 1mg/kg

B. Stop omeprazole, monitor Cr (50–70% recover with withdrawal)

C. Renal biopsy immediately

D. Pulse methylprednisolone

E. Continue omeprazole, add antihistamine

  1. 40-year-old woman, recent ciprofloxacin for UTI (10 days), AKI (Cr 350), fever 38.5°C, maculopapular rash, eosinophilia 0.8×10⁹/L. Urine: WBC casts+, eosinophiluria+. Cr not improving after 7 days off antibiotic. Management:

A. Continue observation

B. Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day × 2–4 weeks → taper

C. Pulse cyclophosphamide

D. Plasma exchange

E. Dialysis only

  1. 70-year-old woman, chronic NSAID use for osteoarthritis, presents with nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria 5g/day, albumin 20g/L) + AKI (Cr 200). Urine: selective proteinuria. Biopsy: AIN + minimal change features. Diagnosis:

A. Primary MCD

B. NSAID-induced AIN with MCD overlap

C. Membranous nephropathy

D. Amyloidosis

D. Diabetic nephropathy

  1. 25-year-old woman, bilateral uveitis 2 months ago, now AKI, sterile pyuria, WBC casts. Biopsy: interstitial nephritis. Diagnosis:

A. Sjögren's syndrome

B. TINU syndrome

C. IgG4-related disease

D. Sarcoidosis

E. Drug-induced AIN

  1. 55-year-old man on pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) for melanoma, develops AKI (Cr 300). Biopsy: interstitial oedema, lymphocytic infiltrate, eosinophils. Management:

A. Continue pembrolizumab, start ACEi

B. Hold pembrolizumab, start prednisolone 1mg/kg

C. Pulse cyclophosphamide

D. Plasma exchange

E. Continue pembrolizumab, monitor

  1. AIN — eosinophiluria is also seen in:

A. Only AIN

B. Atheroembolic disease, pyelonephritis, GN, obstruction

C. Only pyelonephritis

D. Only GN

E. Only obstruction

  1. AIN — WBC casts indicate:

A. Glomerular inflammation

B. Tubulointerstitial inflammation

C. Tubular necrosis

D. Obstruction

E. Normal variant

  1. NSAID-AIN vs antibiotic-AIN — key difference:

A. NSAID = more eosinophilia

B. NSAID = insidious, nephrotic overlap, higher chronicity; antibiotic = acute, classic triad more common

C. NSAID = never needs steroids

D. Antibiotic = always needs steroids

E. No difference

  1. 26-year-old woman, Sjögren's syndrome, AKI, urine pH 6.5 (inappropriately high for acidosis), hypokalaemia, nephrolithiasis. Renal manifestation:

A. AIN

B. Distal RTA (Type 1) + AIN + nephrolithiasis

C. Proximal RTA

D. Fanconi syndrome

E. Minimal change disease
