  1. Best indicator of renal recovery in AIN:

A. Resolution of fever

B. Early steroid initiation (<2 weeks from onset)

C. Eosinophiluria resolution

D. Rash resolution

E. Drug withdrawal alone

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest drug causing AIN?

A: NSAIDs

  • Q: AIN classic triad prevalence?

A: Only 10–30%

  • Q: Drug latency antibiotics vs NSAIDs/PPIs?

A: Abx days–weeks; NSAIDs/PPIs weeks–months

  • Q: AIN urine findings?

A: Eosinophiluria (Hansel's), WBC casts, sterile pyuria, subnephrotic proteinuria

  • Q: Step 1 management AIN?

A: STOP offending drug

  • Q: Steroid indication AIN?

A: Severe AKI (Cr>300), no improvement 5–7d off drug, significant extrarenal

  • Q: Steroid regimen AIN?

A: Pred 1mg/kg/day × 2–4wk → taper 4–8wk

  • Q: Early steroids timing?

A: <2 weeks = better recovery

  • Q: NSAID-AIN features?

A: Insidious, nephrotic overlap (MCD), higher chronicity

  • Q: TINU?

A: Young women, uveitis + AIN

  • Q: Sjögren's renal?

A: AIN, distal RTA type 1, nephrolithiasis

  • Q: Checkpoint inhibitor nephritis?

A: AIN histology; hold ICI + steroids

  • Q: Eosinophiluria also in?

A: Atheroembolic, pyelo, GN, obstruction

  • Q: WBC casts = ?

A: Tubulointerstitial inflammation

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — NSAIDs = commonest drug causing AIN
  2. C — Classic triad only in ~10–30% of cases
  3. B — WBC casts = tubulointerstitial inflammation (AIN, pyelonephritis)
  4. B — Eosinophiluria sensitivity ~50–80%
  5. B — Stop offending drug = most important step; 50–70% recover
  6. B — Pred 1mg/kg × 2–4wk → taper 4–8wk
  7. B — NSAID-AIN: insidious, nephrotic overlap (MCD), higher chronicity
  8. B — TINU = young women, bilateral uveitis + AIN
  9. B — Checkpoint inhibitor nephritis = AIN histology
  10. B — NSAID cause, delayed steroids >4wk, pre-existing CKD, fibrosis = worse

SBAs

  1. B — PPI-AIN, no severe features, improving likely → stop drug, monitor (50–70% recover)
  2. B — Severe AKI (Cr 350), triad present, no improvement 7d off drug → steroids
  3. B — Chronic NSAID + nephrotic + AIN biopsy = NSAID-AIN with MCD overlap
  4. B — Uveitis + AIN = TINU (young women)
  5. B — ICI nephritis = hold ICI + pred 1mg/kg
  6. B — Eosinophiluria also in atheroembolic, pyelo, GN, obstruction
  7. B — WBC casts = tubulointerstitial inflammation
  8. B — NSAID = insidious, nephrotic, chronic; antibiotic = acute, triad more common
  9. B — Sjögren's = AIN + distal RTA type 1 + nephrolithiasis
  10. B — Early steroid initiation (<2 weeks) = best predictor of recovery

22. Summary

Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN) is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: 70% drug-induced (NSAIDs commonest, then antibiotics, PPIs, diuretics). Classic triad (fever, rash, eosinophilia) only 10–30%. Urine: eosinophiluria, WBC casts, sterile pyuria. Step 1: STOP DRUG — 50–70% recover. Steroids (pred 1mg/kg × 2–4wk → taper) if severe/no improvement 5–7d. Early steroids (<2wk) = better recovery. NSAID-AIN: insidious, nephrotic overlap, higher chronicity. TINU = young women + uveitis. Checkpoint inhibitor nephritis = AIN histology. Sjögren's = AIN + distal RTA + stones.

Exam focus: Drug causes, triad limitations, eosinophiluria/WBC casts, stop drug first, steroid indications/regimen/timing, NSAID-AIN features, TINU, checkpoint inhibitor nephritis.

Clinical relevance: Early recognition and drug withdrawal prevents irreversible fibrosis; steroids time-sensitive.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.4.10 Adtkd Inherited Tubulointerstitial
Topic: ADTKD and Other Inherited Tubulointerstitial Diseases — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD, ARPKD)]], [[Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)]], [[Renal Tubular Acidosis (RTA Types 1, 2, 4)]], [[Bartter and Gitelman Syndromes]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
ADTKD = Autosomal Dominant Tubulointerstitial Kidney Disease. Bland urine sediment (no protein/haematuria), progressive CKD, normal-sized kidneys. Four main genes: UMOD (gout), MUC1, REN (anaemia), HNF1B (MODY5). Formerly "Medullary Cystic Kidney Disease (MCKD)" & "Familial Juvenile Hyperuricaemic Nephropathy (FJHN)".

1. Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate ADTKD subtypes (UMOD, MUC1, REN, HNF1B, SEC61A1)
  • Recognise clinical clues (gout, anaemia, MODY, bland urine)
  • Apply genetic testing indications
  • Distinguish from ADPKD (no massive cysts) and other inherited diseases
  • Manage conservatively (uric acid, BP, CKD progression, transplant counselling)

2. ADTKD Subtypes Comparison

GeneProteinOld NameKey FeaturesExtrarenal
UMODUromodulin (Tamm-Horsfall)FJHN / MCKD2Early-onset gout (teens-30s), hyperuricaemia, CKD, bland urine—
MUC1Mucin-1MCKD1CKD, no gout, bland urine, indistinguishable from UMOD clinically—
RENRenin—Childhood anaemia, hypotension, hyperkalaemia, CKD—
HNF1BTranscription factorMODY5 / RCADMODY5 diabetes, renal cysts, genital anomalies, hyperuricaemiaPancreas, liver, GU tract
SEC61A1Translocon subunit—CKD, anaemia, neurodevelopmental delayBrain

3. ADTKD-UMOD (Uromodulin-Associated)

Genetics

  • UMOD (chr16p12.3) → Uromodulin (Tamm-Horsfall protein) — most abundant urinary protein
  • Autosomal dominant, high penetrance
  • Mutations → misfolding → ER retention → tubular cell stress → interstitial fibrosis

Clinical Features

FeatureTypical
GoutOnset teens-30s (often first manifestation), tophi may develop
HyperuricaemiaMarked (↓ fractional excretion of urate)
CKDSlow progression, ESRD 40–70yr
UrineBland (no proteinuria, no haematuria)
KidneysNormal or slightly small (no massive cysts like ADPKD)
Family historyStrong, vertical transmission
Uromodulin normally inhibits NKCC2 in TAL → mutations → ↑ urate reabsorption → hyperuricaemia/gout.

4. ADTKD-MUC1 (Mucin-1-Associated)

Genetics

  • MUC1 (chr1q22) → Mucin-1 (transmembrane glycoprotein)
  • Cytosine insertion in VNTR → frameshift → toxic frameshift protein
  • Autosomal dominant

Clinical Features

FeatureTypical
GoutAbsent (key distinguisher from UMOD)
HyperuricaemiaMild/absent
CKDSimilar progression to UMOD
UrineBland
DiagnosisGenetic testing required (no commercial protein assay)
No gout = think MUC1. If gout present = think UMOD.

5. ADTKD-REN (Renin-Associated)

Genetics

  • REN (chr1q32) → Renin (rate-limiting RAAS enzyme)
  • Autosomal dominant

Clinical Features

FeatureDetail
AnaemiaChildhood-onset (↓ EPO from ↓ renin → ↓ Ang II → ↓ EPO stim)
HypotensionLow BP (↓ Ang II)
Hyperkalaemia↓ Aldo (↓ renin → ↓ Ang II → ↓ aldo)
CKDProgressive, ESRD 40–60yr
Hyperuricaemia/GoutMay occur
Childhood anaemia + hypotension + hyperkalaemia + CKD = ADTKD-REN.

6. ADTKD-HNF1B (HNF1B-Associated = MODY5 / RCAD)

Genetics

  • HNF1B (chr17q12) → Hepatocyte Nuclear Factor 1β (transcription factor)
  • Autosomal dominant; de novo mutations common (~50%)

Clinical Features (RCAD = Renal Cysts And Diabetes)

SystemManifestation
RenalCysts (variable, often cortical), hypoplastic kidneys, single kidney, CAKUT
PancreasMODY5 diabetes (early-onset, insulin-deficient, not insulin-resistant)
GenitalMüllerian aplasia (absent uterus/fallopian tubes in females), hypospadias
LiverElevated LFTs, hepatic cysts
OtherHypomagnesaemia (renal Mg wasting), hyperuricaemia, gout
MODY5 + renal cysts + genital anomalies = HNF1B. Most pleiotropic ADTKD.

7. ADTKD-SEC61A1

  • SEC61A1 (chr3q24) → Translocon subunit
  • CKD, anaemia, neurodevelopmental delay, intellectual disability
  • Rare

8. Diagnostic Approach

StepTestFinding
1UrinalysisBland (no protein, no haematuria) — key clue
2Serum urate↑ in UMOD, HNF1B
3Kidney USNormal/small kidneys, cortical cysts (HNF1B), no massive cysts (vs ADPKD)
4Family historyAutosomal dominant vertical transmission
5Genetic panelUMOD, MUC1, REN, HNF1B, SEC61A1
ADTKD vs ADPKD: ADTKD = bland urine, no massive cysts, normal kidneys; ADPKD = massive cysts, haematuria/proteinuria, enlarged kidneys.

9. Management

AspectApproach
BP controlACEi/ARB (renoprotective)
Hyperuricaemia/Gout (UMOD/HNF1B)Allopurinol/febuxostat (target urate <360 μmol/L)
Anaemia (REN)ESA if symptomatic; avoid NSAIDs
Diabetes (HNF1B)Insulin early (not insulin-resistant)
Genital (HNF1B)Gynaecology referral (Müllerian anomalies)
CKD progressionSGLT2i (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY), standard CKD care
TransplantExcellent outcomes; recurrence rare (genetic, not in donor kidney)
Genetic counselling50% transmission; prenatal/preimplantation available

10. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- ADTKD = bland urine + CKD + autosomal dominant + normal kidneys.
- UMOD = gout (early) + hyperuricaemia; MUC1 = no gout.
- REN = childhood anaemia + hypotension + hyperkalaemia.
- HNF1B = MODY5 diabetes + renal cysts + genital anomalies (Müllerian aplasia).
- No massive cysts = distinguishes from ADPKD.
- Genetic testing confirms; transplant curative (no recurrence).
- Former names: MCKD (medullary cystic kidney disease), FJHN (familial juvenile hyperuricaemic nephropathy) = now ADTKD-UMOD/MUC1.

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
ADTKD = ADPKDADTKD: bland urine, normal kidneys, no massive cysts
All ADTKD have goutOnly UMOD & HNF1B have gout; MUC1 = no gout
REN = hypertensionREN = HYPOTENSION (low renin → low Ang II)
HNF1B = only diabetesMulti-system: renal cysts, genital (Müllerian), liver, Mg wasting
Medullary cysts on US = ADTKDMedullary cysts may be absent; cortical cysts in HNF1B
ADTKD recurs in transplantNo recurrence (genetic defect in native tissue, not donor)
All gout + CKD = UMODAlso HNF1B, classic gout, Lesch-Nyhan, tumour lysis
Bland urine = no kidney diseaseADTKD = bland urine BUT progressive CKD

12. Mnemonics

  • ADTKD genes: UMOD = Uric acid (gout); MUC1 = Minus gout; REN = Red cells low (anaemia); HNF1B = Hyperglycaemia (MODY) + Hysterectomy (Müllerian); SEC61 = Slow development
  • ADTKD vs ADPKD: ADTKD = Almost Normal kidneys, Bland urine; ADPKD = All Cysts, Haematuria
  • UMOD gout: UMOD = Urate Metabolism Overload → Distal Kidney Defect

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((ADTKD))
    UMOD[Uromodulin]
      Features[Gout (teens), Hyperuricaemia, Bland urine, CKD]
      Gene[UMOD chr16]
    MUC1[Mucin-1]
      Features[No gout, Bland urine, CKD]
      Gene[MUC1 chr1]
    REN[Renin]
      Features[Childhood anaemia, Hypotension, HyperK, CKD]
      Gene[REN chr1]
    HNF1B[HNF1B]
      Features[MODY5 DM, Renal cysts, Müllerian aplasia, Gout, HypoMg]
      Gene[HNF1B chr17]
    SEC61A1[SEC61A1]
      Features[CKD, Anaemia, Neurodevelopmental delay]
    Diagnosis
      Urine[Bland - KEY]
      US[Normal/small kidneys, Cortical cysts (HNF1B)]
      Genetics[Panel: UMOD, MUC1, REN, HNF1B, SEC61A1]
    Management[ACEi/ARB, Allopurinol (UMOD/HNF1B), ESA (REN), Insulin (HNF1B), Transplant = curative]

14. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. ADTKD definition (bland urine, AD, CKD, normal kidneys)
  2. UMOD vs MUC1 (gout vs no gout)
  3. REN triad (anaemia, hypotension, hyperK)
  4. HNF1B = MODY5 + renal cysts + genital anomalies
  5. ADTKD vs ADPKD differences
  6. Genetic testing panel

15. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

16. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴ADTKD definition, three main subtypes (UMOD gout, MUC1 no gout, REN anaemia, HNF1B MODY5), bland urine clue, transplant = curative
Should Know 🟡SEC61A1, genetic counselling, BP/uric acid management, differential from ADPKD, former names MCKD/FJHN
Nice to Know 🟢Novel genes (DNAJB11, etc.), uric acid transporters, HNF1B chromatin remodeling, MODY classification

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Hallmark urinalysis finding in ADTKD:

A. Nephrotic proteinuria

B. Bland (no protein, no haematuria)

C. RBC casts

D. Heavy haematuria

E. Pyuria

  1. ADTKD subtype with early-onset gout:

A. MUC1

B. REN

C. UMOD

D. HNF1B

E. SEC61A1

  1. ADTKD subtype WITHOUT gout:

A. UMOD

B. MUC1

C. HNF1B

D. Both UMOD and HNF1B

E. All subtypes

  1. ADTKD-REN characteristic triad:

A. Hypertension, hypokalaemia, alkalosis

B. Anaemia, hypotension, hyperkalaemia

C. Diabetes, cysts, gout

E. Proteinuria, oedema, hypertension

  1. HNF1B (MODY5) features include all EXCEPT:

A. Early-onset diabetes (insulin-deficient)

B. Renal cysts

C. Hypertension (common)

D. Müllerian aplasia (absent uterus/tubes)

E. Hypomagnesaemia

  1. Kidney ultrasound in ADTKD vs ADPKD:

A. Massive bilateral cysts in both

B. ADTKD: normal/small kidneys; ADPKD: massive enlarged cystic kidneys

C. ADTKD: enlarged; ADPKD: small

D. Both have normal kidneys

E. Only ADPKD has cysts

  1. Genetic testing in ADTKD — panel includes:

A. PKD1, PKD2

B. UMOD, MUC1, REN, HNF1B, SEC61A1

C. NPHS1, NPHS2, WT1

D. CTNS, PKHD1

E. COL4A3, COL4A4, COL4A5

  1. ADTKD recurrence post-renal transplant:

A. High (>50%)

B. No recurrence (genetic, native tissue)

C. Moderate (20–30%)

D. Only in UMOD

E. Only in HNF1B

  1. Former name for ADTKD-UMOD:

A. MCKD1

B. FJHN (Familial Juvenile Hyperuricaemic Nephropathy)

C. ADPKD

D. ARPKD

E. Alport syndrome

  1. ADTKD-HNF1B renal cysts vs ADPKD cysts:

A. Cortical cysts (HNF1B) vs diffuse massive cysts (ADPKD)

B. Medullary in both

C. No cysts in HNF1B

D. Same distribution

E. Only medullary in HNF1B

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 25-year-old man, gout since age 18, CKD stage 3, bland urine, father similar. Father died ESRD 55. Most likely gene:

A. PKD1

B. UMOD

C. MUC1

D. HNF1B

E. REN

  1. 30-year-old woman, CKD stage 3, bland urine, NO gout. Mother CKD, no gout. US: normal kidneys, cortical cysts. Gene:

A. UMOD

B. MUC1

C. HNF1B

D. REN

E. PKD1

  1. 12-year-old boy, anaemia (Hb 85), hypotension, hyperkalaemia (K+ 5.8), CKD stage 3. Father similar. Gene:

A. UMOD

B. MUC1

C. REN

D. HNF1B

E. PKD1

  1. 20-year-old woman, diabetes (diagnosed 16, on insulin), renal cysts on US, absent uterus on pelvic US. Gene:

A. UMOD

B. MUC1

C. REN

D. HNF1B

E. PKD1

  1. ADTKD patient asks about transplant recurrence risk. Counselling:

A. High risk, avoid living donor

B. No recurrence — genetic defect in native tissue, donor kidney unaffected

C. 30% risk, consider genetic testing of donor

D. Only if UMOD mutation

E. Only if HNF1B mutation

  1. 40-year-old man, CKD, gout, hyperuricaemia. US: kidneys 10cm, no cysts. Bland urine. Differentiate from ADPKD:

A. ADPKD = massive cysts, haematuria, enlarged kidneys

B. ADPKD = normal kidneys, bland urine

C. No difference

D. ADPKD = only medullary cysts

E. ADPKD = presents in childhood

  1. Asymptomatic 18-year-old, father ADTKD-UMOD. Wants genetic testing. Counselling:

A. Test now — 50% chance

B. Defer until adulthood with counselling — 50% risk, implications for insurance/family planning

C. Not available

D. Only if symptomatic

E. Test mother instead

  1. Management of hyperuricaemia in ADTKD-UMOD:

A. Avoid allopurinol (nephrotoxic)

B. Allopurinol/febuxostat to target urate <360

C. Rasburicase

E. Probenecid

E. No treatment needed

  1. HNF1B female with Müllerian aplasia — fertility counselling:

A. Natural conception possible

B. IVF with gestational carrier (absent uterus)

C. Adoption only

D. Uterine transplant

E. Surrogacy illegal

  1. ADTKD key diagnostic clue vs other CKD:

A. Heavy proteinuria

B. Bland urine sediment

C. RBC casts

D. Nephrotic syndrome

E. Active sediment

19. Flashcards

  • Q: ADTKD urinalysis?

A: Bland (no protein, no blood)

  • Q: ADTKD-UMOD hallmark?

A: Early gout + hyperuricaemia

  • Q: ADTKD-MUC1 hallmark?

A: NO gout, bland urine, CKD

  • Q: ADTKD-REN triad?

A: Childhood anaemia, hypotension, hyperK

  • Q: ADTKD-HNF1B = ?

A: MODY5 DM + renal cysts + Müllerian aplasia

  • Q: ADTKD vs ADPKD?

A: ADTKD = bland urine, normal kidneys; ADPKD = massive cysts, haematuria

  • Q: ADTKD transplant recurrence?

A: None

  • Q: Former name ADTKD?

A: MCKD / FJHN

  • Q: HNF1B genital?

A: Müllerian aplasia (♀), hypospadias (♂)

  • Q: UMOD gout age?

A: Teens-30s

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Bland urine (no proteinuria, no haematuria) is the hallmark of ADTKD.
  2. C — UMOD mutations → uromodulin defect → ↑ urate reabsorption → early gout.
  3. B — MUC1 = CKD, bland urine, no gout (key distinguisher from UMOD).
  4. B — REN = low renin → low Ang II → hypotension, low aldo → hyperK, low EPO → anaemia.
  5. C — HNF1B does not typically cause hypertension; hypotension more common in childhood.
  6. B — ADTKD: normal/small kidneys, cortical cysts if any; ADPKD: massive bilateral cystic enlargement.
  7. B — ADTKD genes: UMOD, MUC1, REN, HNF1B, SEC61A1. PKD1/2 = ADPKD.
  8. B — No recurrence in transplant (genetic defect in host tissue, not in donor kidney).
  9. B — FJHN (Familial Juvenile Hyperuricaemic Nephropathy) = old name for ADTKD-UMOD.
  10. A — HNF1B cysts typically cortical; ADPKD cysts diffuse throughout cortex/medulla.

SBAs

  1. B — Young gout + hyperuricaemia + CKD + bland urine + AD family = ADTKD-UMOD.
  2. B — CKD + bland urine + NO gout + cortical cysts + AD = ADTKD-MUC1.
  3. C — Childhood anaemia + hypotension + hyperK + CKD + AD = ADTKD-REN.
  4. D — Diabetes + renal cysts + Müllerian aplasia = HNF1B (RCAD/MODY5).
  5. B — Genetic defect in native kidney; donor kidney has normal genes → no recurrence.
  6. A — ADPKD: massive cysts, haematuria, enlarged kidneys; ADTKD: none of these.
  7. B — Predictive testing in asymptomatic at-risk requires genetic counselling (insurance, family planning, psychological).
  8. B — Allopurinol/febuxostat standard; target urate <360 μmol/L (6 mg/dL).
  9. B — Absent uterus = gestational carrier needed for biological child (IVF + surrogacy).
  10. B — Bland urine sediment = key clue distinguishing ADTKD from glomerular diseases, ADPKD, vasculitis.

21. Summary

ADTKD & Other Inherited Tubulointerstitial Diseases is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Definition: Autosomal dominant, bland urine, progressive CKD, normal/small kidneys (no massive cysts).

Subtypes: UMOD = gout/hyperuricaemia (FJHN); MUC1 = no gout (MCKD1); REN = anaemia/hypotension/hyperK; HNF1B = MODY5 + renal cysts + genital anomalies; SEC61A1 = CKD + neuro delay.

Diagnosis: Bland urine (key), US (normal kidneys ± cortical cysts), genetic panel.

Management: ACEi/ARB, allopurinol (UMOD/HNF1B), ESA (REN), insulin (HNF1B), transplant curative.

Vs ADPKD: ADTKD = bland urine, normal kidneys; ADPKD = massive cysts, haematuria.

Exam focus: Bland urine clue, UMOD vs MUC1 (gout), REN triad, HNF1B multi-system, transplant no recurrence.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.4.11 Cystinosis
Topic: Cystinosis — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Bartter and Gitelman Syndromes (plus Liddle)]], [[Fanconi Syndrome]], [[Renal Tubular Acidosis (RTA Types 1, 2, 4)]], [[Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)]], [[Kidney Transplantation (Indications, Workup, Immunosuppression, Complications)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Cystinosis = autosomal recessive lysosomal cystine storage disorder (CTNS gene, chr17p13). Cystine accumulates in lysosomes → multi-organ damage. Three forms: Nephropathic infantile (95%, severe), Late-onset juvenile/adult (milder, no Fanconi), Ocular (corneal crystals only). Most common cause of childhood Fanconi syndrome.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise classic infantile nephropathic cystinosis presentation (Fanconi, rickets, photophobia)
  • Differentiate three clinical forms
  • Diagnose via leukocyte cystine assay, genetic testing, corneal slit-lamp
  • Manage with cysteamine (cystine-depleting), supportive Fanconi care, transplant
  • Monitor for extra-renal complications (thyroid, diabetes, myopathy, CNS)
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP paediatric nephrology vignettes

2. Genetics & Pathogenesis

GeneProteinFunctionMutation Effect
CTNS (chr17p13)CystinosinLysosomal cystine/H+ symporter (exports cystine from lysosome)Loss-of-function → cystine accumulation in lysosomes → crystallisation → cellular damage
  • Autosomal recessive
  • Carrier frequency ~1:100 (higher in French Canadians)
  • Cystine = dimer of cysteine; poorly soluble → crystallises in lysosomes

3. Clinical Forms

FormAge OnsetKey FeaturesPrognosis
Nephropathic (Infantile)6–12 monthsFanconi syndrome, rickets, polyuria, failure to thrive, photophobiaESRD ~10yr if untreated; transplant → long survival
Late-onset (Juvenile/Adult)>12 yearsProteinuria/CKD (no/focal Fanconi), milderESRD 20–40yr
Ocular (Benign)Any ageCorneal cystine crystals only (photophobia)No renal disease

4. Nephropathic Infantile Cystinosis (Classic)

Presentation (6–18 months)

SystemFeatures
RenalFull Fanconi syndrome: polyuria, polydipsia, dehydration, glycosuria (normal glucose), aminoaciduria, phosphaturia (rickets), bicarbonaturia (RTA Type 2), hypouricaemia, low-molecular-weight proteinuria
GrowthFailure to thrive, short stature
BoneRickets (phosphate wasting, 1,25-Vit D deficiency) — resistant to Vit D
EyesPhotophobia, blepharospasm (corneal cystine crystals) — by age 2–3yr
ThyroidHypothyroidism (later)
PancreasDiabetes mellitus (insulin-deficient, later)
MuscleMyopathy, swallowing difficulty (later)
CNSNeurocognitive impairment (rare, late)
Fanconi + photophobia + rickets in infant = cystinosis until proven otherwise.

5. Late-Onset Cystinosis

  • Onset >12 years (often 15–30yr)
  • Proteinuria/CKD predominant
  • No/mild Fanconi, no rickets
  • Corneal crystals present
  • ESRD ~25–40yr

6. Ocular (Benign) Cystinosis

  • Corneal crystals only (slit-lamp)
  • Photophobia
  • No renal involvement, normal kidney function
  • Adult diagnosis common

7. Diagnosis

TestFinding
Leukocyte cystine assayGold standard — elevated cystine (nmol ½ cystine/mg protein): Infantile >500, Juvenile 150–500, Carrier 50–150, Normal <50
Genetic testing (CTNS)Confirms; 57kb founder deletion common in Northern Europeans
Slit-lamp examCorneal cystine crystals (needle-shaped, refractile) — pathognomonic
UrinalysisFanconi pattern (glycosuria, phosphaturia, aminoaciduria, bicarbonaturia)
Renal biopsyNot needed; shows cystine crystals in tubular cells (electron-dense)
Leukocyte cystine assay = diagnostic standard. Slit-lamp = quick screen.

8. Management

Cysteamine (Cystine-Depleting Agent)

FormDoseNotes
Cysteamine bitartrate (Cystagon)1.3–1.95 g/m²/day divided q6hStart ASAP — delays ESRD by 10+ years
Enteric-coated (Procysbi)Equivalent dose q12hBetter GI tolerability
Ophthalmic drops (0.55% / 0.1%)q1h waking hoursDissolves corneal crystals, prevents photophobia
Cysteamine enters lysosome → reacts with cystine → forms cysteine + cysteine-cysteamine mixed disulfide → exits via cationic amino acid transporter.

Supportive Fanconi Management

IssueTreatment
RTA Type 2 (bicarbonaturia)NaHCO₃/NaCitrate 5–15 mEq/kg/day (high dose)
Hypophosphataemia/RicketsPhosphate supplements + 1,25-Vit D (calcitriol)
HypokalaemiaKCl supplements
Dehydration/PolyuriaFree water access, avoid dehydration
GrowthGH therapy (if growth failure despite metabolic control)
ThyroidLevothyroxine if hypothyroid
DiabetesInsulin (insulin-deficient)

Transplant

  • Kidney transplant = definitive for ESRD
  • Cysteamine continues post-transplant → prevents extra-renal complications
  • No recurrence in allograft (genetic defect in host, not kidney)
  • Combined liver-kidney not needed (liver not major site)

9. Extra-Renal Complications (Monitor Post-Transplant)

SystemComplicationScreening
EyesCorneal crystals, band keratopathy, retinopathyAnnual slit-lamp
ThyroidHypothyroidism (50% by age 30)Annual TSH
PancreasDiabetes mellitus (insulin-deficient)Annual HbA1c
MuscleDistal myopathy, swallowing dysfunctionClinical review
CNSNeurocognitive decline, calcificationsMRI if symptomatic
GonadalMale infertility (obstructive azoospermia)Semen analysis
SkinPhotosensitivity, hyperpigmentation—

10. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Cystinosis = CTNS mutation → lysosomal cystine accumulation.
- Most common cause of childhood Fanconi syndrome.
- Triad: Fanconi + rickets + photophobia (corneal crystals).
- Leukocyte cystine assay = gold standard diagnosis.
- Slit-lamp = corneal cystine crystals (needle-shaped).
- Cysteamine = disease-modifying (start early, continue lifelong).
- Enteric-coated (Procysbi) = better GI tolerance.
- Eye drops = dissolve corneal crystals.
- Transplant curative for renal disease; cysteamine continues for extra-renal protection.
- No recurrence in transplant.
- Extra-renal: hypothyroidism, diabetes, myopathy, male infertility.

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Cystinosis = only kidneysMulti-system: eyes, thyroid, pancreas, muscle, CNS, gonads
All cystinosis = FanconiLate-onset = proteinuria/CKD, no Fanconi; Ocular = eyes only
Cysteamine cures everythingCysteamine delays ESRD, dissolves corneal crystals — but extra-renal complications still need monitoring
Stop cysteamine post-transplantContinue lifelong — prevents extra-renal complications
Corneal crystals = only cystinosisAlso in tyrosinaemia, hypercholesterolaemia, drug-induced (chloroquine, amiodarone), Fabry
Cystinosis = only childrenLate-onset presents in teens/adults with proteinuria
Leukocyte cystine = urinary cystineLeukocyte cystine = diagnostic; urinary cystine = screening only
All Fanconi = cystinosisAlso Wilson's, Lowe, tyrosinaemia, galactosaemia, drugs (tenofovir, ifosfamide), myeloma

12. Mnemonics

  • Cystinosis triad: Fanconi + Rickets + Photophobia = FRP
  • CTNS = Cystine Transporter Not Storing
  • Cysteamine: Cystine Eater Amines → Cysteine
  • Three forms: Nephropathic (infant), Late (juvenile), Ocular (benign) = NLO
  • Fanconi mnemonic: Glucose, Amino acids, Phosphate, Uric acid, HCO3- = GAPUH

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Cystinosis))
    Gene[CTNS chr17p13 → Cystinosin]
    Pathogenesis[Lysosomal cystine accumulation → crystallisation → cell damage]
    Forms
      Infantile[6-18mo: Full Fanconi, Rickets, Photophobia, FTT]
      Late[>12yr: Proteinuria/CKD, No Fanconi, Corneal crystals]
      Ocular[Any age: Corneal crystals only, Photophobia]
    Diagnosis
      Gold[Leukocyte cystine assay]
      Genetic[CTNS sequencing (57kb deletion common)]
      Eye[Slit-lamp: needle-shaped corneal crystals]
      Urine[Fanconi pattern]
    Management
      Cysteamine[Oral (Cystagon/Procysbi) lifelong + Eye drops]
      Fanconi_Rx[Alkali, Phosphate, Vit D, K+, GH]
      Transplant[Curative renal; continue cysteamine]
    Extra_Renal
      Eyes[Corneal crystals, band keratopathy]
      Thyroid[Hypothyroidism]
      Pancreas[Diabetes]
      Muscle[Myopathy, dysphagia]
      CNS[Calcifications, cognitive]
      Gonadal[Male infertility]

14. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. CTNS gene → cystinosin function
  2. Three forms (infantile, late, ocular)
  3. Classic infantile triad (Fanconi, rickets, photophobia)
  4. Gold standard diagnosis (leukocyte cystine assay)
  5. Slit-lamp finding (corneal needle-shaped crystals)
  6. Cysteamine mechanism, dosing, eye drops
  7. Transplant = curative renal, continue cysteamine for extra-renal
  8. Extra-renal complications monitoring

15. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

16. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴CTNS gene, three forms, infantile triad (Fanconi/rickets/photophobia), leukocyte cystine assay, slit-lamp, cysteamine (oral + eye drops), transplant curative
Should Know 🟡Extra-renal monitoring (thyroid, diabetes, myopathy, CNS, gonadal), late-onset vs ocular forms, GI side effects of cysteamine, Procysbi advantages
Nice to Know 🟢57kb founder deletion, corneal crystal dissolution kinetics, novel delivery systems, gene therapy trials, cystinosin structure/function

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Cystinosis gene:

A. CTNS (chr17p13)

B. SLC12A3

C. KCNJ1

D. CLCNKB

E. SCNN1B

  1. Classic infantile cystinosis triad:

A. Proteinuria, hypertension, oedema

B. Fanconi syndrome, rickets, photophobia

C. Haematuria, RBC casts, hypertension

D. Nephrotic syndrome, hyperlipidaemia, oedema

E. AKI, oliguria, hyperkalaemia

  1. Gold standard diagnostic test for cystinosis:

A. Urinary cystine excretion

B. Leukocyte cystine assay

C. Slit-lamp examination

D. Renal biopsy

E. Genetic testing only

  1. Slit-lamp finding in cystinosis:

A. Kayser-Fleischer rings

B. Needle-shaped corneal cystine crystals

C. Cataracts

D. Retinal haemorrhages

E. Optic neuritis

  1. Cysteamine mechanism:

A. Blocks cystine synthesis

B. Enters lysosome → reacts with cystine → forms cysteine + mixed disulfide → exits via cationic AA transporter

C. Inhibits CTNS transcription

D. Enhances lysosomal exocytosis

E. Chelates cysteine in blood

  1. Cysteamine dosing (Cystagon):

A. 100 mg/day

B. 1.3–1.95 g/m²/day divided q6h

C. 500 mg/day

D. 2 g/day single dose

E. Weight-based 10 mg/kg/day

  1. Ophthalmic cysteamine drops — purpose:

A. Treat cataract

B. Dissolve corneal cystine crystals, prevent photophobia

C. Lower IOP

D. Treat dry eye

E. Prevent retinal detachment

  1. Late-onset (juvenile) cystinosis — distinguishing feature from infantile:

A. Severe Fanconi

B. Proteinuria/CKD without Fanconi

C. Early rickets

D. No corneal crystals

E. Earlier ESRD

  1. Cystinosis post-kidney transplant — cysteamine:

A. Stop (renal disease cured)

B. Continue lifelong (prevents extra-renal complications)

C. Reduce dose by 50%

D. Switch to eye drops only

E. Stop if no corneal crystals

  1. Extra-renal complications of cystinosis include all EXCEPT:

A. Hypothyroidism

B. Diabetes mellitus

C. Cardiomyopathy

D. Myopathy

E. Male infertility

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 8-month-old infant, polyuria, failure to thrive, rickets, photophobia. Urine: glycosuria (normal glucose), phosphaturia, aminoaciduria, bicarbonaturia. Slit-lamp: corneal crystals. Most likely diagnosis:

A. Lowe syndrome

B. Nephropathic cystinosis

C. Wilson's disease

D. Tyrosinaemia

E. Galactosaemia

  1. 15-year-old with proteinuria, CKD stage 3, photophobia. No rickets, no Fanconi. Slit-lamp: corneal crystals. Leukocyte cystine 300 nmol/mg. Diagnosis:

A. Infantile cystinosis

B. Late-onset (juvenile) cystinosis

C. Ocular cystinosis

D. Fabry disease

E. Wilson's disease

  1. Cystinosis patient on cysteamine bitartrate. Severe GI nausea/vomiting. Best alternative:

A. Stop cysteamine

B. Enteric-coated cysteamine (Procysbi) q12h

C. Increase dose

D. Switch to IV cysteamine

E. Add ondansetron only

  1. 6-year-old cystinosis patient, post-kidney transplant 1 year. Mother asks if cysteamine can stop. Advice:

A. Yes, transplant cured disease

B. No — continue lifelong to prevent extra-renal complications

C. Reduce to eye drops only

D. Stop if leukocyte cystine normal

E. Continue only if corneal crystals persist

  1. 25-year-old man with cystinosis (diagnosed infancy), now post-transplant 5 years. Routine monitoring should include:

A. Annual slit-lamp only

B. Annual TSH, HbA1c, slit-lamp, neuro review, semen analysis

C. Annual creatinine only

D. Annual cystine level only

E. No extra monitoring needed

  1. Parents of cystinosis child ask recurrence risk for next pregnancy:

A. 25% (autosomal recessive)

B. 50%

C. 0%

D. 100%

E. Depends on gender

  1. Infantile cystinosis — RTA type present:

A. Type 1 (distal)

B. Type 2 (proximal) — bicarbonaturia

C. Type 4 (hypoaldosteronism)

D. Type 3 (combined)

E. No RTA

  1. Cystinosis corneal crystals vs other causes — differential:

A. Tyrosinaemia, hypercholesterolaemia, chloroquine/amiodarone, Fabry

B. Wilson's, Gaucher, Niemann-Pick

C. Only cystinosis causes corneal crystals

D. Diabetes, hypertension

E. SLE, vasculitis

  1. Cysteamine dosing schedule — reason for q6h (Cystagon):

A. Short half-life (<2h)

B. To maintain continuous lysosomal cystine depletion

C. Food interactions

D. Renal clearance

E. Avoid peak toxicity

  1. Genetic counselling for cystinosis carrier parents — prenatal diagnosis:

A. Not possible

B. Leukocyte cystine assay on chorionic villi OR CTNS mutation analysis

C. Ultrasound only

D. Amniotic fluid cystine only

E. Parental leukocyte cystine only

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Cystinosis gene?

A: CTNS (chr17p13)

  • Q: Cystinosin function?

A: Lysosomal cystine/H+ symporter

  • Q: Three forms?

A: Infantile (nephropathic), Late-onset (juvenile), Ocular (benign)

  • Q: Infantile classic triad?

A: Fanconi + rickets + photophobia

  • Q: Gold standard diagnosis?

A: Leukocyte cystine assay

  • Q: Slit-lamp finding?

A: Needle-shaped corneal cystine crystals

  • Q: Cysteamine mechanism?

A: Enters lysosome, reacts with cystine → cysteine + mixed disulfide → exits

  • Q: Cysteamine oral dose?

A: 1.3–1.95 g/m²/day q6h

  • Q: Eye drops purpose?

A: Dissolve corneal crystals, prevent photophobia

  • Q: Late-onset feature?

A: Proteinuria/CKD, NO Fanconi

  • Q: Ocular form?

A: Corneal crystals only, no renal disease

  • Q: Transplant + cysteamine?

A: Continue lifelong for extra-renal protection

  • Q: Extra-renal complications?

A: Hypothyroidism, diabetes, myopathy, CNS, male infertility

  • Q: Inheritance?

A: Autosomal recessive

  • Q: Carrier leukocyte cystine?

A: 50–150 nmol/mg (normal <50, infantile >500)

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — CTNS (chr17p13) encodes cystinosin, the lysosomal cystine transporter.
  2. B — Classic triad: Fanconi syndrome, rickets (phosphate wasting), photophobia (corneal crystals).
  3. B — Leukocyte cystine assay = quantitative gold standard. Normal <50, carrier 50–150, infantile >500 nmol ½ cystine/mg protein.
  4. B — Needle-shaped, refractile cystine crystals in cornea (pathognomonic).
  5. B — Cysteamine enters lysosome → thiol-disulfide exchange with cystine → cysteine + cysteamine-cysteine mixed disulfide → exits via cationic AA transporter (LYS2).
  6. B — Cystagon: 1.3–1.95 g/m²/day divided q6h (short half-life). Procysbi (enteric) q12h.
  7. B — 0.55% (or 0.1%) cysteamine drops q1h waking → dissolve corneal crystals, relieve photophobia.
  8. B — Late-onset: proteinuria/CKD predominates, Fanconi absent/minimal, corneal crystals present.
  9. B — Continue cysteamine lifelong post-transplant to prevent thyroid, pancreas, muscle, CNS, gonadal complications.
  10. C — Cardiomyopathy not typical; hypothyroidism, diabetes, myopathy, male infertility are.

SBAs

  1. B — Infantile cystinosis: Fanconi + rickets + photophobia + corneal crystals + elevated leukocyte cystine.
  2. B — Juvenile cystinosis: Proteinuria/CKD, NO Fanconi, corneal crystals, intermediate leukocyte cystine.
  3. B — Procysbi (enteric-coated) q12h = better GI tolerability, same efficacy.
  4. B — Cysteamine lifelong post-transplant prevents extra-renal complications (thyroid, diabetes, myopathy, CNS).
  5. B — Multi-system monitoring: thyroid (TSH), pancreas (HbA1c), eyes (slit-lamp), neuro, gonadal.
  6. A — AR inheritance → 25% recurrence risk.
  7. B — Fanconi includes proximal RTA (Type 2) = bicarbonaturia.
  8. A — Differential for corneal crystals: tyrosinaemia, hypercholesterolaemia, drug-induced (chloroquine, amiodarone), Fabry.
  9. B — Short half-life; q6h maintains continuous lysosomal cystine depletion.
  10. B — Prenatal: CTNS mutation analysis on CVS/amniocytes OR leukocyte cystine on fetal blood/CVS.

21. Summary

Cystinosis is a Must Know 🔴 paediatric nephrology topic.

Genetics: CTNS (chr17p13) → cystinosin (lysosomal cystine transporter) defect → cystine accumulation. AR.

Forms: Infantile (95%) = Fanconi + rickets + photophobia (6–18mo); Late-onset = proteinuria/CKD, no Fanconi; Ocular = corneal crystals only.

Diagnosis: Leukocyte cystine assay (gold standard), slit-lamp (corneal needle-shaped crystals), CTNS sequencing.

Treatment: Cysteamine (Cystagon q6h / Procysbi q12h) + ophthalmic drops (q1h). Start early, continue lifelong. Supportive Fanconi care (alkali, phosphate, Vit D, K+).

Transplant: Curative for ESRD; continue cysteamine for extra-renal protection. No recurrence.

Extra-renal: Hypothyroidism, diabetes, myopathy, CNS, male infertility — monitor annually.

Exam focus: Infantile triad, leukocyte cystine assay, slit-lamp, cysteamine (oral + eye drops), three forms, transplant + continue cysteamine, extra-renal monitoring.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.4.12 Nephrocalcinosis
Topic: Nephrocalcinosis — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Bartter and Gitelman Syndromes (plus Liddle)]], [[Renal Tubular Acidosis (RTA Types 1, 2, 4)]], [[Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD, ARPKD)]], [[Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)]], [[Urolithiasis (Renal Stones)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Nephrocalcinosis = generalised calcium deposition in renal parenchyma (cortex ± medulla). Distinct from nephrolithiasis (discrete stones). Causes: hypercalciuria, hypercalcaemia, hypocitraturia, alkaline urine, renal tubular defects. Medullary = most common (distal RTA, Bartter, loop diuretics, medullary sponge kidney). Cortical = severe (cortical necrosis, acute phosphate nephropathy, tumour lysis, Alport). Can cause CKD.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate nephrocalcinosis vs nephrolithiasis
  • Classify by distribution (medullary vs cortical) and aetiology
  • Identify underlying causes (RTA Type 1, Bartter, hyperparathyroidism, medullary sponge kidney, etc.)
  • Diagnose via imaging (US, CT) and metabolic workup
  • Manage underlying cause + prevent progression
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP radiology and metabolic vignettes

2. Definition & Distinction

FeatureNephrocalcinosisNephrolithiasis
DefinitionDiffuse calcium deposition in renal parenchymaDiscrete stones in collecting system (calyces, pelvis, ureter)
ImagingUS/CT: echogenic foci in parenchymaUS/CT: stones in lumen with acoustic shadowing
PathologyTubular basement membrane / interstitiumUrinary supersaturation → crystal aggregation
Renal functionOften impaired (CKD)Preserved until obstruction/infection
AssociationCan coexist with stonesMay occur without parenchymal calcification

3. Classification by Distribution

1. Medullary Nephrocalcinosis (Most Common)

CauseMechanismKey Features
Distal RTA (Type 1)Alkaline urine + hypocitraturia + hypercalciuriaClassic — bilateral, symmetric
Bartter SyndromeHypercalciuria (TAL Ca²⁺ wasting) + hypocitraturiaChildren, nephrocalcinosis + hypokalaemic alkalosis
Primary HyperparathyroidismHypercalcaemia → hypercalciuria↑ PTH, ↑ Ca²⁺, ↓ PO₄
Medullary Sponge KidneyDilated collecting ducts → stasis → Ca²⁺ precipitationAsymptomatic or stones/UTI; "paintbrush" calyces on IVU
Loop Diuretics (chronic)↓ TAL Ca²⁺ reabsorption → hypercalciuriaReversible if stopped early
Sarcoidosis / Vit D excessHypercalcaemia → hypercalciuria↑ 1,25-Vit D (granulomas)
Renal Tubular Acidosis Type 2 (Proximal)Bicarbonaturia → alkaline urine + hypocitraturiaLess common than Type 1
Hypocitraturia syndromes↓ Citrate (inhibitor of Ca crystallisation)Idiopathic, RTA, K⁺ depletion

2. Cortical Nephrocalcinosis (Rarer, More Severe)

CauseMechanismKey Features
Cortical NecrosisIschaemia → calcification of dead tubulesObstetric (abruption, sepsis), HUS, snake bite
Acute Phosphate NephropathyOral Na⁺ phosphate bowel prep → Ca-PO₄ precipitationElderly, CKD, volume depletion
Tumour Lysis SyndromeHyperphosphataemia → Ca-PO₄ precipitationPost-chemo, high tumour burden
Alport SyndromeGBM abnormality → cortical calcificationHaematuria, hearing loss, lenticonus
Chronic GlomerulonephritisEnd-stage scarring → dystrophic calcificationSmall kidneys, advanced CKD
Renal TBCaseous necrosis → calcification"Putty kidney"
OxalosisPrimary/enteric hyperoxaluria → CaOx depositionESRD, systemic oxalosis

4. Diagnostic Workup

Imaging

ModalityFinding
UltrasoundMedullary: multiple echogenic foci in pyramids (no shadowing); Cortical: echogenic cortex
Non-contrast CTGold standard — detects calcifications <1mm; distinguishes medullary vs cortical
IVU / CTU"Paintbrush" calyces (medullary sponge kidney)
Plain X-ray (KUB)Less sensitive; may show nephrocalcinosis as faint opacities over renal outline

Metabolic Workup (Essential)

TestPurpose
Serum Ca²⁺, PO₄, Mg²⁺, AlbuminCorrected Ca²⁺, hyper/hypocalcaemia, hyperphosphataemia
PTHPrimary hyperparathyroidism (↑ Ca, ↑ PTH) vs sarcoid (↑ Ca, ↓ PTH)
1,25-Vit DSarcoidosis, lymphoma (↑), Vit D toxicity
Urine 24h Ca²⁺, Citrate, Oxalate, PO₄, CreatinineHypercalciuria, hypocitraturia, hyperoxaluria, hyperphosphaturia
Urinalysis (pH)Alkaline → RTA Type 1; Acidic → other
Blood gasMetabolic acidosis (RTA) vs alkalosis (Bartter/Gitelman)
Serum electrolytes (K⁺, HCO₃⁻)Hypokalaemia (RTA1, Bartter, Gitelman)
Thyroid functionHyperthyroidism → hypercalciuria
ACE levelSarcoidosis

5. Aetiology-Specific Clues

CauseAgeKey Clues
Distal RTAChild/Young adultNAGMA, hypokalaemia, urine pH >5.5, nephrocalcinosis
BartterInfant/ChildPolyhydramnios, FTT, hypokalaemic alkalosis, nephrocalcinosis
HyperparathyroidismAdult"Stones, bones, groans, moans" + nephrocalcinosis
Medullary Sponge KidneyYoung adultRecurrent stones/UTI, asymptomatic screening, "paintbrush" calyces
SarcoidosisYoung adult↑ 1,25-Vit D, hypercalcaemia, hilar lymphadenopathy
Loop diureticsElderly/CHFChronic high-dose furosemide
Cortical necrosisPeripartum / SepsisAnuria, history of massive haemorrhage/shock
Phosphate nephropathyElderlyRecent oral phosphate bowel prep

6. Management

General Principles

PrincipleApproach
Treat underlying causeEssential — stops progression
Hydration2.5–3 L/day urine output
DietaryNormal Ca²⁺ intake (↓ oxalate absorption), low Na⁺, low animal protein
Citrate supplementationK-citrate/Na-citrate (↑ urinary citrate → inhibits crystallisation)
ThiazidesIf hypercalciuria persists (↑ proximal Ca²⁺ reabsorption) — avoid in RTA1
AvoidExcess Vit D, Ca²⁺ supplements (unless hypocalcaemic), high oxalate foods

Cause-Specific

CauseSpecific Management
RTA Type 1Alkali (NaHCO₃/Na-citrate) — corrects acidosis → ↑ citrate → ↓ Ca²⁺ crystallisation
BartterIndomethacin + K⁺ + K⁺-sparing
HyperparathyroidismParathyroidectomy (if indicated)
Medullary Sponge KidneyStone prevention (hydration, citrate), treat UTI
SarcoidosisSteroids, low Ca²⁺/Vit D diet
Loop diureticsReduce dose, switch to thiazide if possible
Cortical necrosisSupportive; may need dialysis; often irreversible
OxalosisPyridoxine (Type 1), dialysis, combined liver-kidney transplant

7. Prognosis

ScenarioOutcome
Early RTA/Bartter treatedNephrocalcinosis reversible (especially if caught early)
Long-standing untreatedIrreversible → interstitial fibrosis → CKD
Cortical nephrocalcinosisUsually irreversible, progressive CKD
Medullary sponge kidneyBenign course; recurrent stones main issue
Hyperparathyroidism post-opOften improves/regresses

8. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Nephrocalcinosis = parenchymal calcification (vs stones in lumen).
- Medullary = most common: RTA1, Bartter, hyperparathyroidism, medullary sponge kidney, loop diuretics.
- Cortical = severe: cortical necrosis, phosphate nephropathy, tumour lysis, oxalosis, Alport, TB.
- RTA Type 1 = classic medullary nephrocalcinosis (alkaline urine + hypocitraturia + hypercalciuria).
- Bartter = infantile nephrocalcinosis + hypokalaemic alkalosis.
- Medullary sponge kidney = "paintbrush" calyces, recurrent stones/UTI.
- Imaging: US (screening), CT = gold standard (detects <1mm).
- Workup: Ca²⁺, PO₄, PTH, 1,25-Vit D, 24h urine Ca/citrate/oxalate, urinary pH, blood gas.
- Treatment: Treat cause + hydration + citrate + thiazide (if hypercalciuria).
- RTA1 alkali reverses nephrocalcinosis by ↑ citrate.

9. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Nephrocalcinosis = kidney stonesNephrocalcinosis = parenchymal; Stones = collecting system
All nephrocalcinosis = irreversibleEarly RTA/Bartter treated = reversible
Citrate causes stonesCitrate INHIBITS Ca²⁺ crystallisation (chelate Ca²⁺)
Low Ca²⁺ diet prevents nephrocalcinosisNormal Ca²⁺ diet (binds oxalate in gut); low Ca²⁺ → ↑ oxalate absorption
Loop diuretics = hypocalciuriaLoop diuretics = HYPERcalciuria (blocks TAL Ca²⁺ reabsorption)
Thiazides = hypercalciuriaThiazides = HYPOcalciuria (↑ proximal Ca²⁺ reabsorption)
Medullary sponge kidney = medullary nephrocalcinosis alwaysMSK = dilated ducts → stones/stasis; nephrocalcinosis not always present
Cortical nephrocalcinosis = same as medullaryCortical = worse prognosis, different causes (necrosis, phosphate, oxalosis)
Paintbrush calyces = RTAPaintbrush = Medullary Sponge Kidney
Nephrocalcinosis only in adultsChildren: RTA, Bartter, hyperoxaluria, Alport

10. Mnemonics

  • Medullary causes: RTA1, Bartter, Hyperparathyroidism, Medullary sponge kidney, Loop diuretics, Sarcoid, Proximal RTA = RBHLMSP
  • Cortical causes: Cortical necrosis, Phosphate nephropathy, Tumour lysis, Oxalosis, Alport, TB = CPT OAT
  • RTA1 nephrocalcinosis: Alkaline urine + Hypocitraturia + Hypercalciuria = AHH
  • Bartter vs Gitelman Ca: Bartter = Big Ca²⁺ (nephrocalcinosis); Gitelman = Good Ca²⁺ (hypocalciuria)
  • Citrate: Citrate = Chelates Calcium = Crystallisation Inhibitor

11. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Nephrocalcinosis))
    Definition[Parenchymal Ca deposition ≠ Stones]
    Medullary[Most common]
      RTA1[Type 1 Distal: Alkaline urine, Hypocitraturia, Hypercalciuria]
      Bartter[TAL defect: Hypercalciuria, Hypokalaemic alkalosis]
      Hyperpara[Primary: HyperCa, ↑PTH]
      MSK[Medullary Sponge Kidney: Paintbrush calyces]
      LoopDiur[Chronic furosemide: Hypercalciuria]
      Sarcoid[↑1,25-VitD, HyperCa]
      RTA2[Proximal: Bicarbonaturia]
    Cortical[Severe, often irreversible]
      Cortical_Necrosis[Obstetric, HUS, Sepsis]
      Phosphate_Nephropathy[Oral phosphate prep]
      Tumour_Lysis[HyperPO4 → CaPO4]
      Oxalosis[Primary/enteric hyperoxaluria]
      Alport[GBM defect]
      TB[Putty kidney]
    Workup[Ca, PO4, PTH, VitD, 24h urine Ca/Cit/Ox, pH, Gas]
    Imaging[US screening, CT gold standard]
    Management
      Cause[Treat underlying]
      Hydration[2.5-3L/day]
      Citrate[K-citrate/Na-citrate]
      Thiazide[If hypercalciuria]
      Diet[Normal Ca, low Na, low animal protein]

12. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Nephrocalcinosis vs nephrolithiasis
  2. Medullary causes (RTA1, Bartter, Hyperpara, MSK, Loop diuretics)
  3. Cortical causes (Cortical necrosis, Phosphate nephropathy, Oxalosis, Alport)
  4. RTA1 mechanism (alkaline urine + hypocitraturia + hypercalciuria)
  5. Imaging: US vs CT
  6. Metabolic workup (Ca, PTH, Vit D, 24h urine, pH)
  7. Citrate inhibits crystallisation
  8. Thiazide = hypocalciuric; Loop = hypercalciuric
  9. Paintbrush calyces = MSK
  10. RTA1 alkali reverses nephrocalcinosis

13. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

14. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Medullary vs cortical, RTA1/Bartter/Hyperpara/MSK as medullary causes, cortical necrosis/phosphate nephropathy/oxalosis as cortical, CT gold standard, metabolic workup, citrate/thiazide Rx
Should Know 🟡Reversibility in early RTA/Bartter, medullary sponge kidney features, phosphate nephropathy risk factors, oxalosis management, Alport cortical calcification
Nice to Know 🟢Quantitative CT scoring, novel inhibitors (phytate, magnesium), genetic hypercalciurias, nephrocalcinosis in transplant

15. MCQs (10)

  1. Nephrocalcinosis vs Nephrolithiasis — key distinction:

A. Nephrocalcinosis = stones in ureter

B. Nephrocalcinosis = parenchymal calcification; Nephrolithiasis = collecting system stones

C. Both are the same

D. Nephrocalcinosis = only cortical

E. Nephrolithiasis = parenchymal

  1. Most common cause of medullary nephrocalcinosis in children:

A. Hyperparathyroidism

B. Distal RTA (Type 1) / Bartter Syndrome

C. Medullary sponge kidney

D. Loop diuretics

E. Sarcoidosis

  1. RTA Type 1 causes nephrocalcinosis via all EXCEPT:

A. Alkaline urine

B. Hypocitraturia

C. Hypercalciuria

D. Hyperphosphaturia

E. Impaired NH₄⁺ excretion

  1. "Paintbrush" calyces on IVU are characteristic of:

A. Distal RTA

B. Medullary Sponge Kidney

C. Bartter Syndrome

D. Hyperparathyroidism

E. Cortical necrosis

  1. Loop diuretics cause nephrocalcinosis by:

A. Increasing citrate excretion

B. Decreasing TAL Ca²⁺ reabsorption → hypercalciuria

C. Causing metabolic alkalosis

D. Increasing urinary pH

D. Decreasing oxalate excretion

  1. Cortical nephrocalcinosis causes include all EXCEPT:

A. Cortical necrosis

B. Acute phosphate nephropathy

C. Tumour lysis syndrome

D. Medullary sponge kidney

E. Oxalosis (primary/enteric)

  1. Medullary sponge kidney — classic imaging:

A. Nephrocalcinosis in cortex

B. Dilated collecting ducts → "paintbrush" calyces

C. Staghorn calculi

D. Small kidneys

E. Hydronephrosis

  1. Thiazide diuretics in nephrocalcinosis — indication:

A. All types routinely

B. Hypercalciuria (↑ proximal Ca²⁺ reabsorption)

C. Hypocalciuria

D. Cortical nephrocalcinosis only

E. Contraindicated always

  1. Reversibility of nephrocalcinosis — best chance:

A. Cortical necrosis

B. Early distal RTA / Bartter with alkali/indomethacin treatment

C. Long-standing hyperparathyroidism

D. Phosphate nephropathy

E. Oxalosis with ESRD

  1. Metabolic workup for nephrocalcinosis — essential tests:

A. Only serum creatinine

B. Ca²⁺, PO₄, PTH, 1,25-Vit D, 24h urine Ca/Citrate/Oxalate, urinary pH, blood gas

C. Only urinary pH

D. Only PTH

E. Only 24h urine calcium

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 6-year-old boy, failure to thrive, polyuria, hypokalaemia, metabolic alkalosis, renal US shows medullary nephrocalcinosis. Genetic test pending. Most likely diagnosis:

A. Distal RTA

B. Bartter Syndrome

C. Gitelman Syndrome

D. Medullary Sponge Kidney

E. Primary Hyperparathyroidism

  1. 45-year-old woman, recurrent calcium phosphate stones, medullary nephrocalcinosis on CT. Urine pH 7.2, hypokalaemia, bicarbonate 16 (normal anion gap). Serum Ca normal, PTH normal. Diagnosis:

A. Primary hyperparathyroidism

B. Distal RTA (Type 1)

C. Bartter Syndrome

D. Medullary Sponge Kidney

E. Renal tubular acidosis Type 2

  1. 30-year-old man, asymptomatic, incidentally found medullary nephrocalcinosis. IVU: "paintbrush" calyces. Recurrent UTIs. Best preventive strategy:

A. Parathyroidectomy

B. Hydration 2.5–3L/day + potassium citrate

C. Indomethacin

D. Thiazide diuretic

E. Allopurinol

  1. 70-year-old woman, recent oral sodium phosphate bowel prep for colonoscopy. Now oliguric, AKI. CT: diffuse cortical nephrocalcinosis. Most likely diagnosis:

A. Cortical necrosis

B. Acute phosphate nephropathy

C. Tumour lysis syndrome

D. Oxalosis

E. Chronic glomerulonephritis

  1. Distal RTA with nephrocalcinosis — specific treatment that reverses calcification:

A. Hydrochlorothiazide

B. Alkali therapy (NaHCO₃/Na-citrate) — corrects acidosis → ↑ citrate → inhibits Ca²⁺ crystallisation

C. Allopurinol

D. Low calcium diet

E. Magnesium supplements

  1. Bartter syndrome vs Gitelman syndrome — nephrocalcinosis:

A. Bartter: YES; Gitelman: NO (hypocalciuria)

B. Both yes

C. Both no

D. Gitelman: YES; Bartter: NO

E. Only with loop diuretics

  1. Cortical nephrocalcinosis in a 25-year-old man with haematuria, hearing loss, lenticonus. Genetic condition:

A. ADPKD

B. Alport Syndrome (COL4A5)

C. ADTKD

D. Fabry Disease

E. Thin Basement Membrane Disease

  1. Nephrocalcinosis workup — hypocitraturia found. Best treatment to increase citrate:

A. Thiazide

B. Potassium citrate / Sodium citrate

C. Allopurinol

D. High animal protein diet

E. Loop diuretic

  1. Primary hyperparathyroidism with nephrocalcinosis — definitive treatment:

A. Cinacalcet

B. Parathyroidectomy (if criteria met)

C. Bisphosphonates

C. Hydration only

E. Alkali therapy

  1. Acute phosphate nephropathy — risk factors:

A. Young age, normal renal function

B. Elderly, CKD, volume depletion, ACEi/ARB, high phosphate dose

C. Pregnancy

D. Hyperthyroidism

E. Bartter syndrome

17. Flashcards

  • Q: Nephrocalcinosis vs stones?

A: Parenchymal vs collecting system

  • Q: Medullary nephrocalcinosis causes?

A: RTA1, Bartter, Hyperpara, MSK, Loop diuretics, Sarcoid

  • Q: Cortical nephrocalcinosis causes?

A: Cortical necrosis, Phosphate nephropathy, Tumour lysis, Oxalosis, Alport, TB

  • Q: RTA1 mechanism?

A: Alkaline urine + hypocitraturia + hypercalciuria

  • Q: Bartter Ca urine?

A: High (hypercalciuria) → nephrocalcinosis

  • Q: Gitelman Ca urine?

A: Low (hypocalciuria) → NO nephrocalcinosis

  • Q: MSK imaging?

A: Paintbrush calyces

  • Q: Citrate role?

A: Inhibits Ca crystallisation (chelate Ca)

  • Q: Thiazide effect on Ca?

A: Hypocalciuric (↑ proximal reabsorption)

  • Q: Loop diuretic effect on Ca?

A: Hypercalciuric (blocks TAL reabsorption)

  • Q: Phosphate nephropathy risk?

A: Elderly, CKD, volume depletion, oral phosphate prep

  • Q: Paintbrush calyces = ?

A: Medullary Sponge Kidney

  • Q: Acute phosphate nephropathy?

A: Cortical calcification post oral phosphate bowel prep

  • Q: RTA1 alkali reverses?

A: Yes — ↑ citrate → inhibits crystallisation

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Nephrocalcinosis = diffuse parenchymal Ca deposition; Nephrolithiasis = discrete stones in calyces/pelvis/ureter.
  2. B — Children: Distal RTA (Type 1) and Bartter Syndrome are the classic medullary nephrocalcinosis causes.
  3. D — RTA1: alkaline urine, hypocitraturia, hypercalciuria cause nephrocalcinosis. Hyperphosphaturia not a mechanism.
  4. B — "Paintbrush" calyces = dilated collecting ducts in Medullary Sponge Kidney (contrast pooling in cystic ducts).
  5. B — Loop diuretics block NKCC2 in TAL → ↓ Ca²⁺ reabsorption → hypercalciuria → medullary nephrocalcinosis.
  6. D — MSK is a medullary cause (dilated ducts), not cortical. Cortical causes: necrosis, phosphate, tumour lysis, oxalosis, Alport, TB.
  7. B — MSK: dilated collecting ducts → contrast fills cystic ducts → "paintbrush" appearance on IVU/CTU.
  8. B — Thiazides ↑ proximal Ca²⁺ reabsorption → ↓ urinary Ca²⁺ (hypocalciuria) → used for hypercalciuric nephrocalcinosis.
  9. B — Early RTA1 (alkali) and Bartter (indomethacin) can reverse medullary nephrocalcinosis before fibrosis.
  10. B — Complete workup: serum Ca/PO₄/PTH/1,25-VitD, 24h urine Ca/Cit/Ox, urinary pH, blood gas.

SBAs

  1. B — Child + FTT + hypokalaemic alkalosis + medullary nephrocalcinosis = Bartter (TAL defect).
  2. B — Adult + medullary nephrocalcinosis + alkaline urine + NAGMA + normal Ca/PTH = Distal RTA Type 1.
  3. B — MSK asymptomatic with stones/UTI: hydration + citrate prevents new stones.
  4. B — Elderly + recent oral phosphate prep + AKI + cortical nephrocalcinosis = Acute phosphate nephropathy.
  5. B — Alkali corrects acidosis → ↑ urinary citrate (citrate reabsorption ↑ in alkalosis) → citrate chelates Ca²⁺ → inhibits crystallisation.
  6. A — Bartter: hypercalciuria → nephrocalcinosis; Gitelman: hypocalciuria → no nephrocalcinosis.
  7. B — Young male + haematuria + hearing loss + lenticonus + cortical nephrocalcinosis = Alport (COL4A5 X-linked).
  8. B — Citrate supplementation (K-citrate/Na-citrate) directly increases urinary citrate → inhibits Ca²⁺ crystallisation.
  9. B — Symptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism with nephrocalcinosis → parathyroidectomy per guidelines.
  10. B — Risk factors: elderly, CKD, volume depletion, ACEi/ARB, high phosphate dose/slow clearance.

19. Summary

Nephrocalcinosis is a Must Know 🔴 metabolic/renal topic.

Definition: Diffuse Ca²⁺ deposition in renal parenchyma (vs stones in collecting system).

Medullary (common): RTA1 (alkaline urine + hypocitraturia + hypercalciuria), Bartter, Hyperparathyroidism, Medullary Sponge Kidney (paintbrush calyces), Loop diuretics, Sarcoidosis.

Cortical (severe): Cortical necrosis, Acute phosphate nephropathy, Tumour lysis, Oxalosis, Alport, TB.

Imaging: US (screening), CT (gold standard).

Workup: Ca²⁺, PO₄, PTH, 1,25-VitD, 24h urine Ca/Citrate/Oxalate, urinary pH, blood gas.

Management: Treat cause + hydration (2.5–3L) + citrate (inhibits crystallisation) + thiazide if hypercalciuria. RTA1 alkali reverses via ↑ citrate.

Key discriminators: Bartter = nephrocalcinosis YES; Gitelman = nephrocalcinosis NO (hypocalciuria). MSK = paintbrush calyces. Phosphate nephropathy = elderly + oral phosphate prep. Cortical necrosis = obstetric/HUS.

Exam focus: Medullary vs cortical, RTA1 mechanism, Bartter vs Gitelman Ca²⁺, MSK imaging, citrate/thiazide roles, reversibility.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.4.13 Renal Tubular Acidosis Rta 1 2 4
Topic: Renal Tubular Acidosis (RTA Types 1, 2, 4) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Functional Anatomy and Physiology of the Kidney and Urinary Tract]], [[Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)]], [[Blood Tests in Renal Disease]], [[Electrolyte, Water, and Acid-Base Disorders]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
RTA = failure of renal tubular acidification → normal anion gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA). Three main types: Type 1 (Distal) = H+ secretion defect; Type 2 (Proximal) = HCO3- reabsorption defect; Type 4 (Hypoaldosteronism) = aldosterone deficiency/resistance. Type 3 (combined) = historical/rare.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate RTA types by pathophysiology, labs, and clinical features
  • Apply diagnostic approach (urine pH, anion gap, K+, urine anion gap, bicarbonate loading)
  • Manage each type (alkali therapy +/- thiazide, fludrocortisone)
  • Identify associations (Sjögren's, autoimmune, drugs, obstructive uropathy)
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP clinical vignettes

2. Overview: RTA Classification

TypeSitePrimary DefectUrine pHK+Urine Anion Gap
Type 1 (Distal)α-Intercalated CDH+ ATPase defect — cannot acidify urine>5.5 (inappropriately alkaline)Low (hypokalaemia)Positive
Type 2 (Proximal)Proximal tubuleNHE3/CA defect — impaired HCO3- reabsorption<5.5 (can acidify if HCO3- low)Low (hypokalaemia)Positive
Type 4 (Hypoaldo)Principal CDAldosterone deficiency/resistance — ↓ ENaC → ↓ H+/K+ secretion<5.5 (can acidify)High (hyperkalaemia)Positive

3. Type 1 (Distal) RTA

Pathophysiology

  • α-Intercalated cell defect: H+ ATPase (or H+/K+ ATPase) dysfunction → cannot secrete H+ into lumen
  • Result: Failure to generate new HCO3- → NAGMA; urine pH >5.5 despite systemic acidosis
  • Hypokalaemia: K+ secretion ↑ (luminal negativity maintained by H+ secretion failure)

Aetiology

CategoryCauses
Primary/GeneticATP6V1B1, ATP6V0A4 (autosomal recessive, sensorineural hearing loss), SLC4A1 (AE1, autosomal dominant, Southeast Asian ovalocytosis)
AutoimmuneSjögren's syndrome (most common adult cause), SLE, RA
Drug-inducedAmphotericin B, lithium, ifosfamide, tenofovir, toluene (glue sniffing)
Obstructive/NephrocalcinosisMedullary sponge kidney, hypercalciuria, nephrocalcinosis
OtherTransplant rejection, sickle cell, hypergammaglobulinaemia

Clinical Features

FeatureDetail
PresentationNAGMA, hypokalaemia (weakness, paralysis), nephrocalcinosis, renal stones (CaP)
ChildrenFailure to thrive, rickets (vit D resistance), polyuria
Hearing lossATP6V1B1/ATP6V0A4 mutations

Diagnosis

TestType 1 Finding
Urine pH>5.5 (cannot acidify despite acidosis)
Urine Anion GapPositive (UAG = UNa + UK - UCl > 0) — ↓ NH4+ excretion
Serum K+Low (hypokalaemia)
Bicarbonate loading↑ Urine HCO3- excretion (distal defect)
NH4+Cl load testUrine pH fails to drop <5.5

4. Type 2 (Proximal) RTA

Pathophysiology

  • Proximal tubule defect: Impaired HCO3- reabsorption (NHE3, carbonic anhydrase) → HCO3- wasting
  • Threshold phenomenon: At low serum HCO3- (<15–18), distal reabsorption compensates → urine can acidify (pH <5.5)
  • Fanconi syndrome = generalised proximal dysfunction (glucose, AA, PO4, uric acid, HCO3-)

Aetiology

CategoryCauses
IsolatedSLC4A4 (NBCe1), CA2 (carbonic anhydrase II — also osteopetrosis, cerebral calcification)
Fanconi SyndromeCystinosis (children), Wilson's, Lowe syndrome, Tyrosinaemia, Galactosaemia, Fructose intolerance
Drugs/ToxinsTenofovir, ifosfamide, cisplatin, aminoglycosides, lead, cadmium, outdated tetracycline
Autoimmune/InfiltrativeSjögren's, SLE, myeloma (light chains), amyloidosis
TransplantCalcineurin inhibitor toxicity

Clinical Features

FeatureDetail
PresentationNAGMA, hypokalaemia, renal HCO3- wasting (fractional excretion HCO3- >15%)
FanconiRickets/osteomalacia (PO4 wasting), glycosuria (normal glucose), aminoaciduria, hypouricaemia
Children (Cystinosis)Failure to thrive, photophobia (corneal crystals), Fanconi syndrome

Diagnosis

TestType 2 Finding
Urine pH<5.5 (if serum HCO3- low; >7 if HCO3- infused)
Fractional Excretion HCO3->15% (at normal serum HCO3-)
Urine Anion GapPositive
Serum K+Low
Fanconi screenGlucose, PO4, uric acid, amino acids in urine

5. Type 4 (Hypoaldosteronism) RTA

Pathophysiology

  • Aldosterone deficiency or resistance → ↓ ENaC activity in CD → ↓ lumen-negative PD → ↓ H+ & K+ secretion
  • Result: Hyperkalaemia (hallmark), NAGMA, urine pH <5.5 (can acidify if H+ gradient intact)
  • "Hyperkalaemic distal RTA" — most common RTA in adults

Aetiology

CategoryCauses
HypoaldosteronismHyporeninaemic hypoaldosteronism (diabetic nephropathy, interstitial disease, NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, heparin)
Aldosterone ResistanceObstructive uropathy, sickle cell, lupus, amyloidosis, transplant, K+-sparing diuretics (spiro, amiloride, triamterene), trimethoprim, pentamidine
PseudohypoaldosteronismType 1 (ENaC mutation — severe, neonatal), Type 2 (WNK1/4 mutation — hypertension, hyperkalaemia = Gordon's syndrome)

Clinical Features

FeatureDetail
PresentationHyperkalaemia (often asymptomatic), mild NAGMA, usually older adults with diabetes/CKD
Urine pH<5.5 (distal acidification intact)
AssociatedDiabetic nephropathy (most common), CKD, ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs

Diagnosis

TestType 4 Finding
Serum K+High (hyperkalaemia)
Urine pH<5.5 (can acidify)
Urine Anion GapPositive
AldosteroneLow (if hypoaldosteronism) or high (if resistance)
ReninLow (hyporeninaemic) or high (resistance)
Acute K+ load testImpaired K+ excretion (TTKG <7)

6. Diagnostic Algorithm (NAGMA)

NAGMA (Normal AG Metabolic Acidosis)
    │
    ├─ Urine pH >5.5 → **Type 1 (Distal)**
    │     └─ Hypokalaemia → Genetic (ATP6V1B1, SLC4A1), Sjögren's, Drugs, Obstructive
    │
    ├─ Urine pH <5.5 + Hypokalaemia → **Type 2 (Proximal)**
    │     └─ Fanconi screen → Cystinosis, Myeloma, Drugs, CA2 mutation
    │
    ├─ Urine pH <5.5 + Hyperkalaemia → **Type 4 (Hypoaldo)**
    │     └─ Check renin/aldosterone, review drugs (ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs, K+-sparing), DM
    │
    └─ Urine Anion Gap = UNa + UK - UCl
          Positive (>0) → Renal cause (RTA)
          Negative (<-20) → GI loss (diarrhoea)

7. Management Summary

TypeAlkali TherapySpecific Therapy
Type 1NaHCO3 / NaCitrate / KCl-Citrate 1–3 mEq/kg/day — large doses (10–20 mEq/kg) due to urinary lossesK+ replacement (hypokalaemia); treat underlying (Sjögren's, stop drugs); thiazide ↑ proximal reabsorption
Type 2NaHCO3 / NaCitrate 5–15 mEq/kg/day — very large doses (HCO3- wasting)Thiazide (induces mild volume contraction → ↑ proximal HCO3- reabsorption); K+ replacement; treat Fanconi cause
Type 4NaHCO3 1–2 mEq/kg/day (milder acidosis)Stop offending drugs (ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs, K+-sparing); fludrocortisone 0.1–0.5mg/day (if hypoaldo); dietary K+ restriction; loop diuretic (↑ K+ excretion); patiromer/sodium zirconium cyclosilicate
Na-Citrate preferred (also provides K+ citrate for stones in Type 1). Avoid NaHCO3 in heart failure/Na+ overload.

8. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- RTA = NAGMA + Positive Urine Anion Gap (vs diarrhoea = negative UAG).
- Type 1: Urine pH >5.5, hypokalaemia, nephrocalcinosis, stones. Causes: Sjögren's, genetic (ATP6V1B1/0A4/SLC4A1), amphotericin, lithium.
- Type 2: Urine pH <5.5 (if HCO3- low), hypokalaemia, FE HCO3- >15%, Fanconi syndrome. Causes: Cystinosis, tenofovir, myeloma, CA2 mutation.
- Type 4: Urine pH <5.5, hyperkalaemia (hallmark). Most common adult RTA. Causes: Diabetic nephropathy, ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs, K+-sparing diuretics, obstructive uropathy.
- Urine Anion Gap = UNa + UK - UCl. Positive = renal (RTA); Negative = GI (diarrhoea).
- Type 1 stones = CaP (alkaline urine); Type 4 = no stones.
- Sjögren's = Type 1 (distal) + Type 2 (proximal) both possible.
- Cystinosis = childhood Fanconi (Type 2) + corneal crystals.

9. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All RTA = hypokalaemiaType 4 = HYPERKALAEMIA (most common adult RTA)
Urine pH <5.5 = not RTAType 2 & 4 can acidify (pH <5.5) — use UAG, K+, FE HCO3-
Type 1 = only geneticSjögren's = most common adult cause; drugs (amphotericin, lithium) common
Fanconi = only cystinosisAlso Wilson's, Lowe, myeloma, amyloidosis, drugs (tenofovir, ifosfamide)
Type 4 = aldosterone deficiency onlyAlso aldosterone resistance (obstructive uropathy, drugs, Gordon's syndrome)
ACEi/ARB cause Type 1ACEi/ARB → Type 4 (hyporeninaemic hypoaldosteronism)
NaHCO3 dose same for allType 2 needs massive doses (5–15 mEq/kg/day); Type 4 mild (1–2)
Fludrocortisone for allOnly Type 4 (hypoaldosteronism)
UAG negative = RTAUAG positive = RTA; negative = diarrhoea (GI loss)
Nephrocalcinosis = all RTAType 1 only (alkaline urine + hypercalciuria + hypocitraturia)

10. Mnemonics

  • RTA Types: 1 = Distal (H+); 2 = Proximal (HCO3-); 4 = Aldosterone (HyperK) = DPA
  • Urine pH: Type 1 >5.5 (can't acidify); Types 2/4 <5.5 (can acidify)
  • Potassium: Types 1/2 = LOW (hypoK); Type 4 = HIGH (hyperK)
  • Urine Anion Gap: Positive = Renal (RTA); Negative = GI (diarrhoea) = PRNG
  • Type 1 stones: CaP (alkaline urine) = Calcium Phosphate
  • Sjögren's RTA: SS = Spiping (Type 1 distal) + Sieving (Type 2 proximal)
  • Fanconi mnemonic: Glucose, Amino acids, Phosphate, Uric acid, HCO3- = GAPUH

11. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Renal Tubular Acidosis))
    NAGMA[Normal AG Metabolic Acidosis]
      UAG[Urine Anion Gap: Positive = Renal; Negative = GI]
    Type1[Type 1: Distal]
      Defect[H+ ATPase (α-intercalated)]
      Urine_pH[>5.5 (cannot acidify)]
      K[Hypokalaemia]
      Features[Nephrocalcinosis, CaP stones, Rickets]
      Causes[Sjögren's, Genetic (ATP6V1B1, ATP6V0A4, SLC4A1), Amphotericin, Lithium, Obstructive]
      Rx[Alkali (large doses), K+ replacement, Thiazide]
    Type2[Type 2: Proximal]
      Defect[NHE3/CA (HCO3- reabsorption)]
      Urine_pH[<5.5 (if HCO3- low)]
      K[Hypokalaemia]
      FE_HCO3[>15%]
      Fanconi[Glucose, AA, PO4, UA, HCO3- wasting]
      Causes[Cystinosis, CA2, Tenofovir, Myeloma, Drugs, Sjögren's]
      Rx[Alkali (massive doses), Thiazide, K+, Treat Fanconi cause]
    Type4[Type 4: Hypoaldosteronism]
      Defect[Aldosterone deficiency/resistance → ↓ ENaC]
      Urine_pH[<5.5 (can acidify)]
      K[HYPERkalaemia]
      Causes[Diabetic nephropathy, ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs, K+-sparing, Obstructive, Transplant]
      Rx[Stop drugs, Fludrocortisone, Loop diuretic, K+ binders, Mild alkali]

12. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Three RTA types with site, urine pH, K+ status
  2. Urine Anion Gap formula and interpretation
  3. Type 1: urine pH >5.5, hypokalaemia, nephrocalcinosis
  4. Type 2: FE HCO3- >15%, Fanconi syndrome
  5. Type 4: hyperkalaemia, urine pH <5.5, diabetic/ACEi/ARB
  6. Sjögren's = Type 1 (and Type 2)
  7. Cystinosis = childhood Fanconi (Type 2)
  8. Alkali dosing differences by type

13. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

14. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Three types differentiation (site, pH, K+, UAG), Type 4 = hyperkalaemia most common, Sjögren's = Type 1, Cystinosis = Fanconi, alkali dosing
Should Know 🟡Genetic causes (ATP6V1B1, ATP6V0A4, SLC4A1, CA2, SLC4A4), drug causes (amphotericin, lithium, tenofovir), fludrocortisone use, thiazide in Type 2
Nice to Know 🟢Pseudohypoaldosteronism (Type 1/2), Gordon's syndrome (WNK), CA2 osteopetrosis, novel K+ binders (patiromer, SZC) in Type 4

15. MCQs (10)

  1. Hallmark of Type 4 RTA:

A. Hypokalaemia

B. Urine pH >5.5

C. Hyperkalaemia

D. Nephrocalcinosis

E. Fanconi syndrome

  1. Urine pH in Type 1 (Distal) RTA:

A. <5.5

B. >5.5

C. 7.0

D. Variable

E. Always 5.5

  1. Urine Anion Gap in RTA (renal NAGMA):

A. Positive (>0)

B. Negative (<0)

C. Zero

D. Not useful

E. Only in Type 1

  1. Most common cause of Type 1 RTA in adults:

A. Genetic mutations

B. Sjögren's syndrome

C. Amphotericin B

D. Lithium

E. Obstructive uropathy

  1. Fractional Excretion of HCO3- in Type 2 RTA:

A. <5%

B. >15%

C. 5–10%

D. 10–15%

D. Not measurable

  1. Fanconi syndrome includes all EXCEPT:

A. Glycosuria (normal glucose)

B. Aminoaciduria

C. Phosphaturia

D. Hyperuricaemia

E. Bicarbonaturia

  1. Drug causing Type 4 RTA (hyperkalaemic):

A. Amphotericin B

B. Spironolactone (K+-sparing diuretic)

C. Lithium

D. Tenofovir

E. Acetazolamide

  1. Childhood Fanconi syndrome — most common genetic cause:

A. Wilson's disease

B. Lowe syndrome

C. Cystinosis

D. Tyrosinaemia

E. Galactosaemia

  1. Alkali therapy dose — highest requirement in:

A. Type 1

B. Type 2 (proximal — massive HCO3- wasting)

C. Type 4

D. All equal

E. Type 3

  1. Urine Anion Gap formula:

A. UCl - (UNa + UK)

B. UNa + UK - UCl

C. UNa - UK + UCl

D. UK + UCl - UNa

E. UNa - UCl

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 45-year-old woman, Sjögren's, NAGMA, urine pH 6.2, K+ 2.8. Diagnosis:

A. Type 2 RTA

B. Type 1 (Distal) RTA

C. Type 4 RTA

D. Diarrhoea

E. Acetazolamide effect

  1. 60-year-old diabetic on lisinopril, spironolactone. NAGMA, K+ 6.2, urine pH 5.0. Best initial step:

A. Sodium bicarbonate

B. Stop spironolactone (and review ACEi)

C. Fludrocortisone

D. Haemodialysis

E. Insulin/dextrose

  1. 8-year-old boy, failure to thrive, rickets, glycosuria (normal glucose), phosphaturia, aminoaciduria, NAGMA. Genetic test:

A. ATP6V1B1

B. SLC4A1

C. CTNS (cystinosis)

D. CA2

E. WNK1

  1. 30-year-old man, glue sniffing (toluene), NAGMA, K+ 2.5, urine pH 6.5. Most likely:

A. Type 2 RTA

B. Type 1 RTA (toluene → distal toxin)

C. Type 4 RTA

D. Diarrhoea

E. Bartter syndrome

  1. Type 2 RTA — thiazide rationale:

A. ↓ K+ wasting

B. Induces volume contraction → ↑ proximal HCO3- reabsorption

C. ↑ Distal acidification

D. Blocks carbonic anhydrase

E. ↑ Aldosterone

  1. Fludrocortisone indicated in:

A. Type 1 RTA

B. Type 2 RTA

C. Type 4 RTA (hypoaldosteronism)

D. All types

E. None

  1. CA2 mutation (carbonic anhydrase II) causes:

A. Type 1 RTA only

B. Type 2 RTA + osteopetrosis + cerebral calcification

C. Type 4 RTA

D. Bartter syndrome

E. Gitelman syndrome

  1. Urine Anion Gap negative in NAGMA suggests:

A. Type 1 RTA

B. Diarrhoea (GI bicarbonate loss)

C. Type 2 RTA

D. Type 4 RTA

E. Renal cause

  1. Nephrocalcinosis characteristic of:

A. Type 2 RTA

B. Type 1 RTA (alkaline urine + hypercalciuria)

C. Type 4 RTA

D. All types

E. Type 3 RTA

  1. Pseudohypoaldosteronism Type 2 (Gordon's syndrome):

A. Hypotension, hypokalaemia

B. Hypertension, hyperkalaemia (WNK1/4 mutation)

C. Normotension, hyperkalaemia

D. Hypertension, hypokalaemia

E. Neonatal onset, salt wasting

17. Flashcards

  • Q: RTA = NAGMA + ?

A: Positive Urine Anion Gap

  • Q: Type 1 urine pH?

A: >5.5 (cannot acidify)

  • Q: Type 1 K+?

A: Low (hypokalaemia)

  • Q: Type 1 stones?

A: CaP (nephrocalcinosis)

  • Q: Type 1 adult cause?

A: Sjögren's (most common)

  • Q: Type 2 FE HCO3-?

A: >15%

  • Q: Type 2 Fanconi?

A: Glucose, AA, PO4, UA, HCO3- wasting

  • Q: Type 2 childhood cause?

A: Cystinosis

  • Q: Type 4 hallmark?

A: Hyperkalaemia

  • Q: Type 4 urine pH?

A: <5.5 (can acidify)

  • Q: Type 4 common causes?

A: DM, ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs, K+-sparing, obstructive

  • Q: UAG formula?

A: UNa + UK - UCl

  • Q: UAG positive?

A: Renal NAGMA (RTA)

  • Q: UAG negative?

A: GI loss (diarrhoea)

  • Q: Type 1 alkali dose?

A: 1–3 mEq/kg/day (large)

  • Q: Type 2 alkali dose?

A: 5–15 mEq/kg/day (massive)

  • Q: Type 4 alkali dose?

A: 1–2 mEq/kg/day (mild)

  • Q: Fludrocortisone for?

A: Type 4 (hypoaldosteronism)

  • Q: Thiazide for Type 2?

A: Volume contraction → ↑ prox HCO3- reabsorption

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — Type 4 RTA = hyperkalaemic distal RTA (aldosterone deficiency/resistance).
  2. B — Type 1 (distal) = H+ ATPase defect → cannot acidify urine → pH >5.5.
  3. A — UAG = UNa + UK - UCl. Positive = renal (↓ NH4+ excretion); Negative = GI (diarrhoea).
  4. B — Sjögren's syndrome is the most common cause of Type 1 RTA in adults.
  5. B — Type 2 (proximal) = impaired HCO3- reabsorption → FE HCO3- >15% (at normal serum HCO3-).
  6. D — Fanconi: urinary losses of glucose, amino acids, phosphate, uric acid (HYPOuricaemia), HCO3-.
  7. B — K+-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, amiloride, triamterene) cause aldosterone resistance → Type 4 RTA (hyperkalaemia).
  8. C — Cystinosis (CTNS mutation) = most common childhood Fanconi syndrome.
  9. B — Type 2 = massive proximal HCO3- wasting → requires 5–15 mEq/kg/day alkali.
  10. B — UAG = UNa + UK - UCl. Positive = renal; Negative = GI.

SBAs

  1. B — Sjögren's + NAGMA + urine pH >5.5 + hypokalaemia = Type 1 (Distal) RTA.
  2. B — Diabetic on ACEi + spironolactone + hyperkalaemia + NAGMA + urine pH <5.5 = Type 4 RTA. Stop K+-sparing diuretic first.
  3. C — Childhood Fanconi + failure to thrive + rickets + glycosuria + phosphaturia = Cystinosis (CTNS).
  4. B — Toluene (glue sniffing) = distal tubular toxin → Type 1 RTA (hypokalaemia, urine pH >5.5).
  5. B — Thiazide in Type 2: induces mild volume contraction → ↑ proximal Na+/HCO3- reabsorption → ↓ HCO3- wasting.
  6. C — Fludrocortisone replaces aldosterone in hypoaldosteronism (Type 4).
  7. B — CA2 mutation = Type 2 RTA + osteopetrosis + cerebral calcification (autosomal recessive).
  8. B — UAG negative = GI bicarbonate loss (diarrhoea); UAG positive = renal cause (RTA).
  9. B — Type 1: alkaline urine (pH >5.5) + hypercalciuria + hypocitraturia → CaP stones/nephrocalcinosis.
  10. B — Gordon's syndrome (Pseudohypoaldosteronism Type 2) = WNK1/4 mutation → hypertension + hyperkalaemia + normal GFR.

19. Summary

Renal Tubular Acidosis is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Classification: NAGMA + Positive UAG = RTA. Three types by site, pH, K+.

Type 1 (Distal): α-intercalated H+ ATPase defect → urine pH >5.5, hypokalaemia, nephrocalcinosis, CaP stones. Causes: Sjögren's, genetic (ATP6V1B1/0A4, SLC4A1), amphotericin, lithium. Rx: High-dose alkali, K+ replacement.

Type 2 (Proximal): NHE3/CA defect → HCO3- wasting, FE HCO3- >15%, urine pH <5.5 if HCO3- low, hypokalaemia. Fanconi = generalised proximal loss. Causes: Cystinosis, CA2, tenofovir, myeloma. Rx: Massive alkali, thiazide, K+ replacement.

Type 4 (Hypoaldosteronism): Aldosterone deficiency/resistance → ↓ ENaC → hyperkalaemia (hallmark), urine pH <5.5, mild NAGMA. Most common adult RTA. Causes: Diabetic nephropathy, ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs, K+-sparing diuretics, obstructive uropathy. Rx: Stop drugs, fludrocortisone, loop diuretic, K+ binders, mild alkali.

Diagnostic key: UAG (UNa+UK-UCl) positive = renal; negative = GI. Urine pH >5.5 = Type 1; <5.5 + hypoK = Type 2; <5.5 + hyperK = Type 4.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.4.14 Bartter Gitelman Liddle
Topic: Bartter and Gitelman Syndromes (plus Liddle) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Functional Anatomy and Physiology of the Kidney and Urinary Tract]], [[Renal Tubular Acidosis (RTA Types 1, 2, 4)]], [[Electrolyte, Water, and Acid-Base Disorders]], [[Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Bartter & Gitelman = inherited salt-wasting tubulopathies with hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. Bartter = TAL defect (NKCC2/ROMK/CLCNKB/BSND/MAGED2) → polyhydramnios, premature, nephrocalcinosis. Gitelman = DCT defect (NCC/SLC12A3) → hypomagnesaemia, hypocalciuria, later onset. Liddle = ENaC gain-of-function → hypertension, hypokalaemia, alkalosis, low renin/aldo.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate Bartter vs Gitelman vs Liddle by site, labs, and clinical features
  • Apply genetic classification (Bartter Types 1–5, Gitelman, Liddle)
  • Diagnose using urine electrolytes, renin/aldosterone, genetic testing
  • Manage each syndrome (NSAIDs/K+ for Bartter, Mg/K+ for Gitelman, amiloride for Liddle)
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP paediatric and adult vignettes

2. Bartter Syndrome

Genetics & Classification

TypeGeneProteinLocationFeatures
Type 1SLC12A1NKCC2TALAntenatal, severe, polyhydramnios
Type 2KCNJ1ROMK (Kir1.1)TALAntenatal, severe, transient neonatal hyperK
Type 3CLCNKBClC-KbTALClassic/classic, later onset, variable
Type 4BSNDBarttin (β-subunit ClC-Ka/ClC-Kb)TAL + inner earSensorineural deafness
Type 5MAGED2MAGE-D2TAL (X-linked)Transient antenatal, resolves postnatally
All Bartter types = TAL salt wasting → ↑ prostaglandin E2 → polyuria, polyhydramnios.

Pathophysiology (TAL Defect)

  • TAL = Na⁺-K⁺-2Cl⁻ cotransporter (NKCC2) + ROMK + ClC-Kb/Barttin
  • Defect → ↓ NaCl reabsorption → salt wasting → volume depletion → ↑ renin/aldo
  • Consequences: Polyuria (↓ medullary gradient), hypokalaemia, metabolic alkalosis, hypercalciuria → nephrocalcinosis

Clinical Presentation

FeatureAntenatal (Types 1,2,4)Classic (Type 3)
PresentationPrenatal: polyhydramnios, prematurityChildhood/adult: failure to thrive, polyuria
Polyuria/PolydipsiaSevere (neonatal)Moderate-severe
GrowthFailure to thriveShort stature
NephrocalcinosisCommon (hypercalciuria)Common
DeafnessType 4 (BSND) only—
HyperkalaemiaType 2 (KCNJ1) transient neonatal—

Biochemical Profile

ParameterBartter
K+Low (hypokalaemia)
Cl-Low
HCO3-High (metabolic alkalosis)
ReninHigh
AldosteroneHigh
BPNormal/Low (volume depletion)
Urine Ca2+High (hypercalciuria) → nephrocalcinosis
Urine Mg2+Normal/mildly ↑
Urine PGE2High

Management

InterventionDetails
NSAIDs (Indomethacin)First-line — inhibits PGE2 → ↑ NaCl reabsorption in TAL, ↓ renin/aldo, ↑ K+
K+ supplementsKCl (high dose, often 50–100 mmol/day)
K+-sparing diureticsSpironolactone/Amiloride (add-on)
ACEi/ARBSometimes used (↓ Ang II)
Growth hormoneIf severe growth failure
AvoidLoop diuretics (worsen wasting)

3. Gitelman Syndrome

Genetics

  • SLC12A3 (chr16q13) → NCC (Na-Cl cotransporter) in DCT
  • Autosomal recessive
  • Prevalence ~1:40,000 (higher in East Asia)

Pathophysiology (DCT Defect)

  • NCC loss-of-function → ↓ NaCl reabsorption in DCT → mild volume depletion → ↑ renin/aldo
  • Paradoxical Mg2+ wasting (DCT = major Mg reabsorption site via TRPM6)
  • Hypocalciuria (↑ proximal Ca2+ reabsorption due to volume depletion)

Clinical Presentation

FeatureTypical
Age at onsetAdolescence/adulthood (often asymptomatic until stress)
SymptomsFatigue, muscle cramps, tetany (hypomagnesaemia), salt craving
BPNormal/Low
GrowthNormal (vs Bartter failure to thrive)
NephrocalcinosisAbsent (hypocalciuria)
ChondrocalcinosisAdult: CPPD deposition (pseudogout)

Biochemical Profile

ParameterGitelman
K+Low (hypokalaemia)
Cl-Low
HCO3-High (metabolic alkalosis)
ReninHigh
AldosteroneHigh
Mg2+Low (hypomagnesaemia — hallmark)
Urine Ca2+Low (hypocalciuria — distinguishes from Bartter)
Urine Mg2+High (renal Mg wasting)

Management

InterventionDetails
Mg2+ supplementationOral Mg (glycinate/chloride) — mainstay; IV if severe
K+ supplementsKCl (moderate doses)
K+-sparing diureticsAmiloride (blocks ENaC → ↓ K+ wasting); Spironolactone
NSAIDsLess effective than Bartter (PGE2 not as central)
High salt dietLiberal NaCl intake
AvoidThiazides (target is NCC — already defective), loop diuretics

4. Liddle Syndrome (Pseudohyperaldosteronism)

Genetics

  • SCNN1B or SCNN1G (chr16p12) → ENaC β/γ subunits gain-of-function
  • Autosomal dominant
  • Constitutive ENaC activation → Na+ reabsorption independent of aldosterone

Pathophysiology

  • ENaC always open → ↑ Na+ reabsorption in CD → volume expansion → hypertension
  • ↓ Renin, ↓ Aldosterone (suppressed)
  • ↑ K+ & H+ secretion → hypokalaemia, metabolic alkalosis

Clinical Presentation

FeatureLiddle
HypertensionEarly-onset, severe, resistant
HypokalaemiaMarked
