24h urine: Volume >2.5L/day target; Ca <7.5mmol/day (M) / <6.2 (F); Oxalate <0.5mmol/day; Citrate >3mmol/day; Urate <3.5mmol/day; pH 6.0–6.5 (uric acid target >6.5).

7. Acute Management (Renal Colic)

StepAction
1. AnalgesiaNSAID 1st line (Diclofenac 75mg IM/PR; Ibuprofen 400–600mg PO) — superior to opioids, less nausea; Opioid (morphine) if NSAID contraindicated
2. AntiemeticMetoclopramide / ondansetron
3. HydrationOral >2.5L/day (IV if vomiting); do NOT overhydrate acutely (↑ intraluminal pressure)
4. Medical Expulsive Therapy (MET)Tamsulosin 400mcg OD (alpha-blocker) for distal ureteric stones <10mm — ↑ spontaneous passage, ↓ time, ↓ analgesia
5. Admission IndicatorsInfection (fever, sepsis), solitary kidney, bilateral obstruction, intractable pain/vomiting, AKI, pregnancy

8. Definitive Stone Management

Stone Factor1st LineAlternatives
<10mm, distal ureterMET (tamsulosin) + analgesiaESWL, URS
<10mm, proximal ureterESWLURS
10–20mm, renal/upper ureterESWL (if favourable: <1000 HU, <10mm, lower pole <30°)URS (flexible), PCNL
>20mm / Staghorn / ComplexPCNLStaged URS, ESWL (adjunct)
Lower pole 10–20mmURS (flexible)ESWL (if <10mm, favourable anatomy)
Uric Acid StonesAlkalinisation (K-citrate, target urine pH 6.5–7.0) → dissolutionESWL, URS
Cystine StonesHigh fluid (>3L), alkalinisation (pH >7.5), thiol drugs (D-penicillamine, tiopronin)ESWL (poor fragmentation), URS, PCNL
Struvite (Infection)Complete surgical removal (PCNL/URS) + culture-directed antibioticsESWL alone contraindicated (residual fragments = recurrent infection)

9. Prevention (All Stone Formers)

MeasureTarget
Fluid Intake>2.5–3L/day (urine output >2–2.5L) — single most effective
DietNormal Ca (1000–1200mg) — do NOT restrict; Low salt (<5g/day); Low animal protein; High fruit/veg (citrate); Avoid excess oxalate (spinach, nuts, tea) if hyperoxaluria
Urine pHCaOx/CaP: 6.0–6.5; Uric acid: >6.5 (alkalinisation); Cystine: >7.5
PharmacologicalThiazide (hypercalciuria); Potassium citrate (hypocitraturia, uric acid, cystine); Allopurinol/febuxostat (hyperuricosuria/gout); D-penicillamine/tiopronin (cystinuria)

10. Special Populations

PopulationConsiderations
PregnancyUS only (no CT); conservative (stent/nephrostomy if obstruction); avoid NSAIDs 3rd trimester; MET controversial
PaediatricUS first; metabolic workup mandatory (cystinuria, hypercalciuria, RTA); ESWL/URS preferred
Obesity / BMI >30CT KUB still gold; PCNL technically harder; ESWL less effective (skin-to-stone distance >10cm)
AnticoagulationHold for surgery; ESWL/URS possible with bridging; stent if urgent
Solitary Kidney / TransplantLower threshold for intervention; stent/nephrostomy for obstruction; MET cautious

11. Complications

ComplicationManagement
Infected Obstruction (Pyonephrosis)Emergency decompression (nephrostomy/stent) + IV antibiotics
Ureteric Injury (iatrogenic)Stent 4–6 weeks; ureteric reimplantation if complete
Steinstrasse (stone street)After ESWL; stent, URS for large fragments
Recurrence50% at 5 years, 70% at 10 years without prevention

12. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Calcium oxalate = commonest (80%)
- Risk: low fluid, high salt/protein, hypercalciuria, hypocitraturia, obesity, DM
- Renal colic: sudden loin→groin pain, restlessness, haematuria; NSAID 1st line (diclofenac IM)
- CT KUB = gold standard (size, location, HU, hydronephrosis)
- Uric acid stones: radiolucent on X-ray, low HU (<400), low pH (<5.5) → alkalinisation dissolves
- Struvite = UTI with urease-producers (Proteus); staghorn; complete removal + antibiotics
- Cystine = genetic (AR); high fluid, alkalinisation pH >7.5, thiol drugs
- MET: tamsulosin 400mcg for distal ureteric <10mm
- ESWL: <10mm proximal, favourable anatomy; PCNL >20mm/staghorn
- Prevention: >2.5L urine/day, normal Ca diet, low salt, potassium citrate (hypocitraturia), thiazide (hypercalciuria)
- 24h urine metabolic workup: recurrent, high-risk, young, atypical stones

13. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Low Ca diet prevents stonesNormal Ca (1000–1200mg) protects (binds oxalate in gut); low Ca ↑ oxalate absorption
Uric acid stones = radiopaqueRadiolucent on X-ray; low HU on CT
ESWL for all stonesPCNL for >20mm/staghorn; ESWL fails for cystine, brushite, dense stones
MET for all stonesDistal ureteric <10mm only
Struvite = treat with antibiotics aloneComplete surgical removal required (residual = recurrent infection)
Cystine stones fragment well with ESWLPoor fragmentation; URS/PCNL preferred
24h urine = single collectionNeed 2 collections (variability)
Citrate only for hypocitraturiaAlso for uric acid (alkalinisation) and cystine
All stones need surgeryMany <5mm pass spontaneously; MET helps
Overhydration helps acute colicAvoid IV overhydration (↑ pressure); oral 2.5L/day

14. Mnemonics

  • Stone Types: CaOx (80%), CaP (15%), Uric (10%), Struvite (10–15%), Cystine (<1%) = CCUSC
  • Risk Factors: Low fluid, High salt/protein, Hypercalciuria, Hypocitraturia, Obesity, DM = LHHHOD
  • Renal Colic: Loin→Groin, Restlessness, Haematuria = LGRH
  • CT KUB: CT = Gold Standard = CGS
  • Uric Acid: Radiolucent, Low HU, Low pH = RLLH
  • MET: Tamsulosin Distal Ureter <10mm = TDU10
  • Struvite: Urease Proteus Staghorn = UPS
  • Prevention: Fluid >2.5L, Normal Ca, Low Salt, Citrate = FNLC
  • 24h Urine: Volume >2.5L, Ca <7.5, Ox <0.5, Cit >3, Ur <3.5 = VCOCU

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Urolithiasis))
    Types
      CaOx[80%, hypercalciuria, hypocitraturia]
      CaP[15%, alkaline pH, RTA]
      Uric_Acid[10%, low pH <5.5, radiolucent]
      Struvite[10-15%, UTI urease, staghorn]
      Cystine[<1%, AR genetic, cystinuria]
      Drug[Indinavir, triamterene, sulfa]
    Pathophysiology
      Supersaturation[Promoters vs Inhibitors]
      Randall_Plaque[Suburothelial CaP nidus]
      Inhibitors[Citrate, Mg, Pyrophosphate, THP]
    Acute_Management
      Analgesia[NSAID 1st line (Diclofenac IM)]
      MET[Tamsulosin distal <10mm]
      Hydration[Oral >2.5L; avoid IV overload]
      Admission[Infection, solitary kidney, bilateral, intractable]
    Definitive
      ESWL[<10mm proximal, favourable]
      URS[10-20mm, lower pole, failed ESWL]
      PCNL[>20mm, staghorn, complex]
      Medical[Uric acid alkalinisation, Cystine thiols]
    Prevention
      Fluids[>2.5L urine/day]
      Diet[Normal Ca, Low salt, Low animal protein]
      pH_Targets[CaOx 6-6.5, UA >6.5, Cystine >7.5]
      Drugs[Thiazide hypercalciuria, Citrate hypocitraturia/UA]
    Workup
      Imaging[CT KUB gold standard]
      Metabolic[2x 24h urine if recurrent/high-risk]
      Blood[Ca, PO4, Urate, PTH, VitD]

16. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Calcium oxalate = 80% commonest
  2. Risk: low fluid, high salt/protein, hypercalciuria, hypocitraturia
  3. Renal colic: loin→groin, restlessness, haematuria; NSAID 1st line (diclofenac IM)
  4. CT KUB = gold standard (size, location, HU, hydronephrosis)
  5. Uric acid: radiolucent, low HU <400, pH <5.5 → alkalinisation dissolves
  6. Struvite: UTI + urease (Proteus), staghorn; complete removal + abx
  7. Cystine: AR genetic; high fluid, pH >7.5, thiol drugs
  8. MET: tamsulosin 400mcg for distal ureteric <10mm
  9. ESWL <10mm proximal; PCNL >20mm/staghorn
  10. Prevention: >2.5L urine/day, normal Ca, low salt, citrate, thiazide
  11. 24h urine: recurrent, high-risk, young, atypical stones

17. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

18. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Stone types & %, risk factors, renal colic management (NSAID, MET), CT KUB, ESWL/URS/PCNL indications, uric acid alkalinisation, struvite complete removal, prevention (fluids, diet, citrate, thiazide), metabolic workup indications
Should Know 🟡Randall's plaque, drug stones, pregnancy management, paediatric workup, obesity considerations, anticoagulation, Steinstrasse, cost-effectiveness
Nice to Know 🟢Novel lithotripsy (burst wave, laser), microbiome, genetic testing panels, long-term outcomes, quality of life, emerging therapies

19. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest renal stone composition:

A. Uric acid

B. Struvite

C. Calcium oxalate (80%)

D. Cystine

E. Calcium phosphate

  1. Renal colic — first-line analgesia:

A. IV morphine

B. NSAID (diclofenac 75mg IM/PR)

C. Oral paracetamol

D. IM pethidine

E. Epidural

  1. Uric acid stones — characteristic features:

A. Radiopaque, high HU, alkaline urine

B. Radiolucent, low HU (<400), acidic urine (pH <5.5)

C. Radiopaque, low HU, alkaline urine

D. Radiolucent, high HU, acidic urine

E. Radiopaque, high HU, acidic urine

  1. Medical expulsive therapy (MET) — indication:

A. All ureteric stones

B. Proximal ureteric stones >10mm

C. Distal ureteric stones <10mm

D. Renal stones >20mm

E. Staghorn calculi

  1. Struvite stones — causative organisms & management:

A. E. coli; antibiotics alone

B. Proteus/Klebsiella (urease); complete surgical removal + antibiotics

C. Pseudomonas; ESWL only

D. Enterococcus; alkalinisation

E. Staph; observation

  1. Cystine stones — inheritance & urine pH target:

A. AD; pH >6.5

B. AR; pH >7.5

C. X-linked; pH >6.0

D. Mitochondrial; pH >7.0

E. AD; pH >7.5

  1. ESWL — best indication (favourable factors):

A. >20mm stone

B. <10mm, proximal ureter, <1000 HU, lower pole <30°

C. Cystine stone

D. Staghorn calculus

E. Brushite stone

  1. PCNL — primary indication:

A. <5mm distal ureteric

B. >20mm renal stone / staghorn / complex

C. Uric acid stone

D. All stones <10mm

E. Proximal ureteric <10mm

  1. Dietary calcium in stone formers:

A. Restrict to <500mg/day

B. Normal 1000–1200mg/day (binds oxalate in gut)

C. Supplement to >2000mg/day

D. No calcium needed

E. Only dairy calcium restricted

  1. 24-hour urine metabolic workup indicated for:

A. Single stone, asymptomatic

B. Recurrent stones (≥2), high-risk, young onset, atypical stone type

C. All stone formers

D. Only calcium stones

E. Only uric acid stones

20. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 35-year-old man, sudden severe left loin pain radiating to groin, nausea, microscopic haematuria. CT KUB: 5mm left distal ureteric stone, HU 600. No fever, normal renal function. Management:

A. Emergency URS

B. Tamsulosin 400mcg OD + NSAID + oral hydration (MET)

C. ESWL

C. PCNL

E. Open ureterolithotomy

  1. 50-year-old woman, recurrent UTIs, flank pain. CT: large branching stone filling renal pelvis and calyces (staghorn), urine culture: Proteus mirabilis. Management:

A. Long-term antibiotics

B. PCNL (complete removal) + culture-directed antibiotics

C. ESWL alone

D. Alkalinisation

E. URS only

  1. 40-year-old man, gout, history of stones, CT KUB: 8mm renal stone, HU 300, urine pH 5.0. Stone type & treatment:

A. CaOx; ESWL

B. Uric acid; alkalinisation (K-citrate target pH 6.5–7.0)

C. Struvite; antibiotics

D. Cystine; thiol drugs

E. CaP; thiazide

  1. 20-year-old man, recurrent stones since childhood, hexagonal crystals in urine, positive cyanide-nitroprusside test. Genetic defect:

A. SLC2A9 (urate transporter)

B. SLC3A1 / SLC7A9 (cystine transporter)

C. CASR (calcium sensing)

D. CLDN16 (paracellular)

E. SLC12A1 (NKCC2)

  1. 65-year-old woman, obesity (BMI 38), 12mm lower pole renal stone, HU 1200. Best definitive:

A. ESWL

B. Flexible URS (laser lithotripsy)

C. PCNL

D. MET

E. Observation

  1. Recurrent CaOx stone former, 24h urine: volume 1.2L, Ca 8.5mmol, oxalate 0.6mmol, citrate 1.5mmol, urate 4.0mmol, pH 5.8. Most important intervention:

A. Thiazide

B. Increase fluid intake to >2.5L urine/day

C. Potassium citrate

D. Allopurinol

E. Low oxalate diet only

  1. Pregnancy 24 weeks, right ureteric colic, 6mm stone on US, no infection, normal renal function. Management:

A. Diclofenac + tamsulosin

B. Conservative (analgesia safe in 2nd tri, hydration); stent/nephrostomy if obstruction/AKI/infection

C. ESWL

D. URS

E. PCNL

  1. Brushite (calcium phosphate monobasic) stone — characteristic:

A. Excellent ESWL fragmentation

B. Dense, resistant to ESWL; associated with incomplete distal RTA

C. Radiolucent

D. Dissolves with alkalinisation

E. Uric acid component

  1. Cystinuria — thiol drug mechanism:

A. Inhibit cystine synthesis

B. Form soluble mixed disulfide with cystine (penicillamine, tiopronin)

C. Increase cystine excretion

D. Alkalinise urine only

E. Bind cystine in gut

  1. Steinstrasse — definition & management:

A. Single large fragment; observation

B. Column of fragments in ureter post-ESWL; stent + URS for large fragments

C. Ureteric stricture; dilatation

D. Stone in bladder; cystolitholapaxy

E. Renal colic without stone; analgesia

21. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest stone?

A: Calcium oxalate (80%)

  • Q: Renal colic analgesia?

A: NSAID 1st line (diclofenac IM)

  • Q: Uric acid stone features?

A: Radiolucent, low HU <400, pH <5.5

  • Q: MET indication?

A: Distal ureteric <10mm (tamsulosin 400mcg)

  • Q: Struvite cause & Rx?

A: UTI + urease (Proteus); complete removal + abx

  • Q: Cystine inheritance?

A: AR (SLC3A1/SLC7A9)

  • Q: ESWL favourable?

A: <10mm, proximal, <1000 HU, lower pole <30°

  • Q: PCNL indication?

A: >20mm, staghorn, complex

  • Q: Dietary calcium?

A: Normal 1000–1200mg (binds oxalate)

  • Q: Uric acid treatment?

A: Alkalinisation K-citrate pH 6.5–7.0

  • Q: Cystine treatment?

A: Fluid >3L, pH >7.5, thiol drugs (penicillamine, tiopronin)

  • Q: 24h urine targets?

A: Vol >2.5L, Ca <7.5, Ox <0.5, Cit >3, Ur <3.5, pH 6–6.5

  • Q: Prevention key?

A: >2.5L urine/day

  • Q: Steinstrasse?

A: Fragment column post-ESWL; stent + URS

  • Q: Brushite?

A: Dense CaP, ESWL resistant, incomplete dRTA

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — Calcium oxalate = 80% of stones
  2. B — NSAID (diclofenac IM) = 1st line renal colic
  3. B — Uric acid: radiolucent, low HU, acidic urine
  4. C — MET = tamsulosin for distal ureteric <10mm
  5. B — Struvite = urease-producers (Proteus); complete surgical removal + antibiotics
  6. B — Cystine = AR; alkalinisation pH >7.5
  7. B — ESWL favourable: <10mm, proximal, <1000 HU, lower pole <30°
  8. B — PCNL = >20mm, staghorn, complex stones
  9. B — Normal Ca diet protects (binds oxalate); restriction worsens
  10. B — Metabolic workup: recurrent, high-risk, young, atypical stones

SBAs

  1. B — 5mm distal ureteric = MET (tamsulosin + NSAID + hydration)
  2. B — Staghorn + Proteus = PCNL complete removal + antibiotics
  3. B — Gout + low HU + acidic urine = uric acid; alkalinisation dissolves
  4. B — Hexagonal crystals + cyanide-nitroprusside + childhood onset = cystinuria (SLC3A1/7A9)
  5. B — Obesity + 12mm lower pole dense stone = flexible URS (ESWL less effective)
  6. B — Low volume (1.2L) = most important; increase to >2.5L
  7. B — Pregnancy: conservative; stent/nephrostomy if complications
  8. B — Brushite = dense CaP, ESWL resistant, associated with incomplete dRTA
  9. B — Thiol drugs form soluble mixed disulfide with cystine
  10. B — Steinstrasse = fragment column post-ESWL; stent + URS for large

23. Summary

Urolithiasis is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: CaOx 80% commonest. Risk: low fluid, high salt/protein, hypercalciuria, hypocitraturia. Renal colic: loin→groin, restlessness; NSAID 1st line (diclofenac IM). CT KUB gold standard (size, HU, hydronephrosis). Uric acid: radiolucent, low HU <400, pH <5.5 → alkalinisation dissolves. Struvite: UTI + urease (Proteus), staghorn; complete removal + antibiotics. Cystine: AR, hexagonal crystals; fluid >3L, pH >7.5, thiol drugs. MET: tamsulosin distal <10mm. ESWL: <10mm proximal favourable. PCNL: >20mm/staghorn. Prevention: >2.5L urine/day, normal Ca, low salt, citrate, thiazide. Metabolic workup: recurrent, high-risk, young, atypical stones.

Exam focus: Stone types, renal colic management, CT KUB, MET, ESWL/URS/PCNL indications, uric acid alkalinisation, struvite complete removal, cystine genetics, prevention, metabolic workup.

Clinical relevance: High recurrence (50% at 5yrs); dietary/fluid modification + targeted pharmacotherapy dramatically reduce recurrence; CT KUB avoids radiation of IVU.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.5.4 Urethral Stricture Disease
Topic: Urethral Stricture Disease — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Urinary Tract Obstruction]], [[Urinary Tract Infection (UTI, Pyelonephritis, Prostatitis, Catheter-Associated)]], [[Neurogenic Bladder]], [[Male Reproductive Tract Disorders (BPH, Prostate Cancer, Testicular Cancer)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Urethral stricture = fibrotic narrowing of urethral lumen. Anterior (bulbar/penile) > Posterior (membranous/prostatic). Aetiology: traumatic (iatrogenic, straddle), inflammatory (Lichen sclerosus/BXO, STI), idiopathic. Presentation: obstructive voiding symptoms, recurrent UTI, retention. Gold standard diagnosis: retrograde urethrogram (RGU) + VCUG. Management: dilation/urethrotomy (short bulbar), urethroplasty (definitive for long/recurrent).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify strictures by location (anterior vs posterior) and aetiology
  • Recognise clinical presentation (obstructive symptoms, UTI, retention)
  • Apply diagnostic imaging (RGU + VCUG = gold standard)
  • Differentiate management by stricture length, location, recurrence
  • Apply urethroplasty principles (anastomotic vs substitution)
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP urology vignettes

2. Anatomy & Classification

Urethral Segments

SegmentLocationLengthStricture Frequency
Anterior Urethra80–90%
‑ Fossa navicularisGlans penis~2cm10%
‑ Penile (pendulous)Penile shaftVariable15%
‑ BulbarPerineum (bulbospongiosus)~3–4cm50–60% (most common)
Posterior Urethra10–20%
‑ MembranousUrogenital diaphragm~1–2cm10%
‑ ProstaticThrough prostate~3–4cm5%

3. Aetiology

CategoryCausesTypical Location
Traumatic (Iatrogenic)Catheterisation, cystoscopy, TURP, hypospadias repairBulbar, membranous
Traumatic (External)Straddle injury (fall on crossbar), pelvic fracture (posterior)Bulbar (straddle), Membranous/prostatic (pelvic fracture)
InflammatoryLichen sclerosus / BXO (Balinitis Xerotica Obliterans), Gonorrhoea, Chlamydia, TB, SchistosomiasisMeatus (BXO), Bulbar/penile (STI)
IdiopathicNo identifiable causeAny
RadiationPelvic radiotherapy (prostate, rectal, cervical)Posterior (prostatic/membranous)
Lichen sclerosus (BXO) = white, atrophic, fibrotic meatal/preputial skin → meatal stenosis → pan-urethral stricture.

4. Clinical Presentation

SymptomMechanism
Weak streamNarrowed lumen → ↑ resistance
Straining to voidCompensatory detrusor effort
Intermittency / SplittingTurbulent flow
Post-void dribblingUrine trapped distal to stricture
Recurrent UTIStasis, incomplete emptying
Acute urinary retentionComplete occlusion / decompensation
HaematuriaTrauma, instrumentation
Urethral dischargeSTI-related (gonorrhoea)
Perineal painBulbar stricture, abscess

5. Diagnostic Workup

Essential Imaging (Gold Standard)

StudyViewsInformation
Retrograde Urethrogram (RGU)AP, obliqueStricture length, calibre, location, number, spongiofibrosis
Voiding Cystourethrogram (VCUG)AP, lateralPosterior urethra, bladder neck, reflux, emptying
RGU + VCUG together = complete anterior + posterior urethral assessment.

Adjunctive

TestRole
UroflowmetryObjective flow rate (Qmax <10 mL/s suggests obstruction)
Post-void residual (US)Quantifies retention
UrethroscopyDirect visualisation (calibre, mucosa, false passages)
MRI (Pelvic)Complex posterior strictures, pelvic fracture urethral distraction defects
Urethral ultrasoundSpongiofibrosis depth (pre-op planning)

6. Management Algorithm

1. Acute Retention

StepAction
1Suprapubic catheter (SPC) — safest, avoids false passages
2Urethral catheter — only if easy passage; gentle, coudé tip
3Definitive planning — RGU/VCUG once stable
Never force catheter through stricture — creates false passages, worsens fibrosis.

2. Stricture Characteristics → Treatment

Stricture TypeLengthLocationFirst-LineDefinitive
Short (≤1cm)≤1cmBulbarDirect Vision Internal Urethrotomy (DVIU)Urethroplasty if recurrence
Short (≤1cm)≤1cmPenileDVIU (higher recurrence)Urethroplasty
Short (≤1cm)≤1cmMembranousEndoscopic dilation/incisionAnastomotic urethroplasty
Long (>2cm)>2cmAnyUrethroplasty (not DVIU)Urethroplasty
Recurrent (post-DVIU)AnyAnyUrethroplasty—
Pan-urethral / Lichen sclerosusLongEntire urethraUrethroplasty (buccal mucosa graft)—
Pelvic fracture urethral distraction defect (PFUDD)>2cmMembranous/prostaticSuprapubic cystostomy → Delayed anastomotic urethroplasty (3–6mo)—

7. Urethroplasty Techniques

TechniqueIndicationPrincipleSuccess Rate
Anastomotic (Excision + Primary Anastomosis - EPA)Short (≤2cm) bulbar/membranousExcise stricture, spatulate ends, tension-free anastomosis90–95%
Augmented Anastomotic2–3cm bulbarPartial excision + dorsal/ventral graft85–90%
Substitution (Graft)Long (>2cm) or pan-urethralIncise stricture, lay graft (patch/onlay/ tubularised)80–85%
Two-Stage (Johanson)Complex, failed prior, severe spongiofibrosis, BXOStage 1: Urethrostomy + graft inlay; Stage 2: Tubularisation70–80%

Graft Sources

GraftAdvantagesDisadvantages
Buccal MucosaGold standard — thick, vascular, hairless, easy harvestOral morbidity (numbness, tightening)
Lingual MucosaSimilar to buccalTongue morbidity
Penile Skin (Flap)Local, reliable vascularityHair-bearing, contracture risk
Bladder MucosaNo external donor siterequires laparotomy, mucus
Buccal mucosa = preferred for substitution urethroplasty.

8. Post-Operative Care

AspectProtocol
CatheterSPC + urethral stent (14–21 days for anastomotic; 3–4 weeks for graft)
VCUGBefore catheter removal (confirm no leak/stricture)
Follow-upUroflowmetry q3mo × 1yr, then annually
Stricture recurrencePeak 3–12 months; DVIU if short, repeat urethroplasty if long

9. Special Scenarios

Pelvic Fracture Urethral Distraction Defect (PFUDD)

AspectDetail
MechanismType III pelvic fracture → membranous urethra distraction (prostate displaced cephalad)
AcuteSuprapubic cystostomy ONLY — do NOT attempt early realignment (↑ stricture rate)
DefinitiveDelayed anastomotic urethroplasty at 3–6 months (wait for fibrosis to mature)
ApproachPerineal ± abdominal (if prostate high)
Success85–90%

Lichen Sclerosus / BXO

AspectDetail
PathologyWhite, sclerotic, atrophic skin (meatus, prepuce, glans) → pan-urethral fibrosis
RiskSquamous cell carcinoma (SCC) — monitor
Meatal stenosisMeatotomy ± buccal graft
Pan-urethralTwo-stage buccal mucosa urethroplasty (Johanson)
CircumcisionPart of treatment (removes diseased prepuce)

10. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Stricture location: Bulbar (50–60%) > Penile > Membranous > Prostatic.
- Aetiology: Iatrogenic (catheter/cystoscopy) > Straddle > BXO > Idiopathic.
- Diagnosis: RGU + VCUG = gold standard.
- Acute retention: Suprapubic catheter (never force urethral).
- Short bulbar (≤1cm): DVIU first-line; longer/recurrent → Urethroplasty.
- Anastomotic (EPA) = gold standard for short bulbar/membranous (90–95% success).
- Substitution (Buccal graft) = for long (>2cm) or pan-urethral.
- PFUDD: SPC acutely → delayed anastomotic urethroplasty at 3–6mo.
- BXO (Lichen sclerosus): Meatal stenosis → pan-urethral → two-stage buccal urethroplasty; SCC risk.
- Success rates: EPA 90–95%, Graft 80–85%, Two-stage 70–80%.

11. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Urethral catheter for acute retentionSPC preferred — forcing catheter causes false passages
DVIU for all stricturesOnly short (≤1cm) bulbar; long/recurrent/penile → urethroplasty
Penile strictures = same as bulbarPenile = higher DVIU recurrence; graft urethroplasty preferred
PFUDD = immediate realignmentNO — SPC only; definitive at 3–6mo
Buccal graft = only optionAlso lingual, penile skin flap, bladder mucosa
BXO = just meatal stenosisPan-urethral; needs two-stage urethroplasty
Urethroplasty = no recurrenceRecurrence 10–20% (graft), 5–10% (EPA); surveillance needed
Anastomotic for allOnly short (≤2cm); long = graft/two-stage
VCUG not needed if RGU doneBoth needed — RGU = anterior; VCUG = posterior + bladder

12. Mnemonics

  • Stricture sites: Bulbar Penile Membranous Prostatic = BPMP
  • Aetiology: Iatrogenic Straddle BXO Idiopathic = ISBI
  • RGU + VCUG = Complete urethral picture
  • Short bulbar = DVIU; Long = Urethroplasty
  • PFUDD: SPC → Wait 3–6mo → Anastomotic
  • BXO: White Sclerotic Meatus = WSM

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Urethral Stricture))
    Anatomy
      Anterior[Bulbar 50-60%, Penile, Fossa]
      Posterior[Membranous, Prostatic]
    Aetiology
      Iatrogenic[Catheter, Cystoscopy, TURP]
      Straddle[Bulbar]
      BXO[Lichen sclerosus - pan-urethral]
      PFUDD[Pelvic fracture - membranous]
      Idiopathic
    Diagnosis
      RGU[Anterior: length, calibre, spongiofibrosis]
      VCUG[Posterior: bladder neck, reflux]
      Uroflow[Qmax <10 = obstruction]
    Management
      Acute[SPC - never force]
      Short_Bulbar[≤1cm: DVIU]
      Long_Recurrent[>2cm or recurrent: Urethroplasty]
      Short_Bulbar_EPA[Anastomotic EPA: 90-95%]
      Long_Graft[Buccal mucosa graft: 80-85%]
      PFUDD[SPC → 3-6mo → Anastomotic]
      BXO[Two-stage buccal]

14. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Stricture location frequency (Bulbar > Penile > Membranous)
  2. Aetiology (Iatrogenic, Straddle, BXO, PFUDD)
  3. Gold standard diagnosis (RGU + VCUG)
  4. Acute retention management (SPC)
  5. Short bulbar ≤1cm = DVIU; >2cm or recurrent = Urethroplasty
  6. EPA (anastomotic) for short bulbar/membranous
  7. Buccal mucosa graft for long/pan-urethral
  8. PFUDD: SPC → 3-6mo → Anastomotic
  9. BXO: two-stage buccal urethroplasty
  10. Success rates: EPA 95%, Graft 85%, Two-stage 75%

15. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

16. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Bulbar most common, RGU+VCUG, SPC for retention, DVIU vs urethroplasty criteria, EPA for short, graft for long, PFUDD delayed repair, BXO two-stage
Should Know 🟡Urethroplasty techniques (EPA, augmented, substitution, two-stage), buccal vs lingual vs penile skin, post-op surveillance, recurrence rates
Nice to Know 🟢Tissue-engineered urethra, cell-seeded scaffolds, dorsal vs ventral onlay technique comparison, quality of life outcomes, cost-effectiveness

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Most common site of urethral stricture:

A. Prostatic

B. Membranous

C. Penile

D. Bulbar

E. Fossa navicularis

  1. Gold standard imaging for urethral stricture:

A. Ultrasound

B. Retrograde Urethrogram (RGU) + Voiding Cystourethrogram (VCUG)

C. CT urogram

D. MRI

E. Uroflowmetry

  1. Acute urinary retention from stricture — best initial drainage:

A. Urethral catheter (coude tip)

B. Suprapubic catheter (SPC)

C. Intermittent catheterisation

D. Urethral dilation

E. Internal urethrotomy

  1. Short (≤1cm) bulbar stricture — first-line treatment:

A. Anastomotic urethroplasty

B. Buccal mucosa graft

C. Direct Vision Internal Urethrotomy (DVIU)

D. Dilation

E. Two-stage urethroplasty

  1. Anastomotic urethroplasty (EPA) — ideal indication:

A. Pan-urethral stricture

B. Short (≤2cm) bulbar or membranous

C. Penile stricture >2cm

D. Lichen sclerosus

E. Post-radiation stricture

  1. Pelvic fracture urethral distraction defect (PFUDD) — acute management:

A. Primary endoscopic realignment

B. Urethral catheter

C. Suprapubic cystostomy

D. Immediate anastomotic urethroplasty

D. DVIU

  1. PFUDD — timing of definitive repair:

A. Immediately

B. 2 weeks

C. 3–6 months (delayed)

D. 1 year

E. Never (permanent SPC)

  1. Definitive treatment for long (>2cm) or recurrent bulbar stricture:

A. Repeat DVIU

B. Urethroplasty (buccal mucosa graft)

C. Permanent stent

C. Dilation

E. SPC only

  1. Lichen sclerosus (BXO) urethral stricture — preferred definitive repair:

A. Single-stage anastomotic

B. DVIU

C. Two-stage buccal mucosa urethroplasty (Johanson)

D. Penile skin flap

E. Perineal urethrostomy

  1. Success rate of anastomotic urethroplasty (EPA) for short bulbar stricture:

A. 50–60%

B. 70–75%

C. 80–85%

D. 90–95%

E. 100%

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 30-year-old man, straddle injury, unable to void. Urethrogram: 2cm bulbar stricture. Best definitive management:

A. DVIU

B. Anastomotic urethroplasty (EPA)

C. Buccal graft urethroplasty

D. Urethral dilation

E. Stent

  1. 45-year-old man, TURP 2 years ago, now weak stream. RGU: 1.5cm bulbar stricture. Management:

A. DVIU (short bulbar)

B. Anastomotic urethroplasty

C. Buccal graft

D. Dilation

E. Stent

  1. 25-year-old man, pelvic fracture (Type III), urethral disruption. Acute: SPC placed. When to plan definitive repair?

A. 2 weeks

B. 6 weeks

C. 3–6 months

D. 1 year

E. Only if symptomatic

  1. 35-year-old man, BXO (lichen sclerosus), pan-urethral stricture, meatal stenosis. Best surgical approach:

A. Single-stage buccal graft

B. Two-stage buccal mucosa urethroplasty (Johanson)

C. Penile skin flap (Orandi)

D. DVIU

E. Perineal urethrostomy only

  1. Recurrent bulbar stricture after 2 DVIUs, now 3cm. Best option:

A. Third DVIU

B. Buccal mucosa graft urethroplasty

C. Permanent urethral stent

D. Dilation

E. SPC only

  1. PFUDD patient, 4 months post-injury, ready for repair. Urethral gap 3cm on imaging. Surgical approach:

A. Perineal anastomotic only

B. Perineal ± abdominal (if prostate high) anastomotic urethroplasty

C. Buccal graft

D. Two-stage

E. Endoscopic realignment

  1. Penile stricture 2cm, no prior surgery. Best definitive treatment:

A. DVIU

B. Buccal mucosa graft urethroplasty (penile = higher DVIU recurrence)

C. Anastomotic urethroplasty

D. Penoscrotal flap

E. Two-stage

  1. Post-urethroplasty surveillance — stricture recurrence peak:

A. 1 month

B. 3–12 months

C. 2 years

D. 5 years

E. 10 years

  1. Buccal mucosa graft — harvest complication:

A. Severe bleeding

B. Temporary oral numbness, tightness, salivary duct injury

C. Airway compromise

D. Facial nerve palsy

E. Mandibular fracture

  1. Anastomotic urethroplasty — maximum stricture length for tension-free repair:

A. 1cm

B. 2cm

C. 3cm

D. 4cm

E. 5cm

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Most common stricture site?

A: Bulbar (50-60%)

  • Q: Gold standard imaging?

A: RGU + VCUG

  • Q: Acute retention drainage?

A: SPC (suprapubic catheter)

  • Q: Short bulbar ≤1cm = ?

A: DVIU

  • Q: Short bulbar/membranous ≤2cm = ?

A: EPA (anastomotic urethroplasty)

  • Q: Long >2cm or recurrent = ?

A: Buccal mucosa graft urethroplasty

  • Q: PFUDD acute?

A: SPC only

  • Q: PFUDD definitive timing?

A: 3-6 months delayed

  • Q: BXO stricture = ?

A: Two-stage buccal urethroplasty

  • Q: EPA success rate?

A: 90-95%

  • Q: Graft success rate?

A: 80-85%

  • Q: Post-op recurrence peak?

A: 3-12 months

  • Q: Buccal graft complications?

A: Oral numbness, tightness, salivary duct injury

  • Q: Penile stricture DVIU recurrence?

A: High (graft preferred)

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. D — Bulbar urethra (50–60%) is the most common stricture site.
  2. B — RGU (anterior) + VCUG (posterior) = complete urethral evaluation gold standard.
  3. B — Forcing urethral catheter through stricture creates false passages; SPC is safest.
  4. C — Short (≤1cm) bulbar stricture: DVIU first-line (success 50–60% at 1yr).
  5. B — EPA (excision + primary anastomosis) for short (≤2cm) bulbar/membranous strictures.
  6. C — PFUDD acute: SPC only. Early realignment increases stricture rate.
  7. C — PFUDD definitive: delayed anastomotic urethroplasty at 3–6 months (fibrosis matured).
  8. B — Long (>2cm) or recurrent: substitution urethroplasty with buccal mucosa graft.
  9. C — BXO/lichen sclerosus: pan-urethral fibrosis → two-stage buccal (Johanson).
  10. D — EPA success 90–95% for short bulbar strictures.

SBAs

  1. B — Straddle injury, 2cm bulbar gap = EPA (anastomotic) ideal (short, bulbar).
  2. A — Post-TURP 1.5cm bulbar = DVIU appropriate (short, <2cm, first recurrence).
  3. C — PFUDD definitive at 3–6 months (fibrosis matured, prostate descended).
  4. B — BXO pan-urethral = two-stage buccal (Johanson) standard.
  5. B — Recurrent after 2 DVIUs, 3cm = graft urethroplasty (buccal mucosa).
  6. B — PFUDD 3cm gap: perineal approach ± abdominal if prostate high; anastomotic.
  7. B — Penile strictures have high DVIU recurrence; graft urethroplasty preferred.
  8. B — Stricture recurrence peaks at 3–12 months post-urethroplasty.
  9. B — Buccal harvest: temporary oral numbness, tightness, salivary duct injury risk.
  10. B — EPA maximum tension-free length ~2cm (bulbar mobilisation limited).

21. Summary

Urethral Stricture Disease is a Must Know 🔴 urology topic.

Epidemiology: Bulbar (50–60%) most common; aetiology: iatrogenic > straddle > BXO > idiopathic.

Diagnosis: RGU + VCUG = gold standard; uroflowmetry for functional assessment.

Acute Management: Suprapubic catheter (SPC) — never force urethral catheter.

Definitive Treatment by Stricture:

  • Short (≤1cm) bulbar → DVIU (first-line)
  • Short (≤2cm) bulbar/membranous → Anastomotic Urethroplasty (EPA) — 90–95% success
  • Long (>2cm) / Recurrent / Penile → Buccal Mucosa Graft Urethroplasty — 80–85% success
  • Pan-urethral / BXO (Lichen sclerosus) → Two-Stage Buccal (Johanson)
  • PFUDD → SPC acutely → Delayed Anastomotic Urethroplasty at 3–6mo

Post-op: VCUG before catheter removal; surveillance uroflowmetry q3mo (recurrence peaks 3–12mo).

Exam focus: Bulbar most common, RGU+VCUG, SPC for retention, DVIU vs EPA vs Graft criteria, PFUDD delayed repair, BXO two-stage, success rates.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.5.5 Urinary Incontinence
Topic: Urinary Incontinence — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Clinical Examination of the Kidney and Urinary Tract]], [[Urinary Tract Infection (UTI, Pyelonephritis, Prostatitis, Catheter-Associated)]], [[Male Reproductive Tract Disorders (BPH, Prostate Cancer, Testicular Cancer)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Urinary incontinence (UI) = involuntary leakage of urine. Types: Stress (SUI), Urge (UUI), Mixed (MUI), Overflow, Functional. Prevalence: Women > Men; increases with age. Impact: QoL, skin breakdown, falls, social isolation. Management: Conservative → Pharmacological → Surgical.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify types of urinary incontinence
  • Assess and diagnose UI (history, examination, investigations)
  • Apply conservative, pharmacological, and surgical management
  • Address special populations (elderly, post-prostatectomy, neurogenic)
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP clinical scenarios

2. Classification of Urinary Incontinence

TypeMechanismKey Features
Stress UI (SUI)↑ Intra-abdominal pressure > urethral closure pressureLeakage with cough, sneeze, exercise, lifting; no urgency
Urge UI (UUI)Detrusor overactivity (involuntary contractions)Urgency + frequency + nocturia + leakage; "key-in-lock" syndrome
Mixed UI (MUI)Combination of SUI + UUIFeatures of both; one may predominate
Overflow UIChronic urinary retention → bladder overdistensionConstant dribbling, poor stream, hesitancy, incomplete emptying; PV residual high
Functional UINormal lower urinary tract but physical/cognitive impairmentMobility issues, dementia, environmental barriers
Continuous UIFistula, ectopic ureter, sphincter destructionConstant leakage (no storage)

3. Prevalence & Risk Factors

PopulationPrevalence
Women25–45% (any UI); SUI commonest in young/middle; UUI increases with age
Men10–30% (post-prostatectomy SUI common; UUI with BPH)
Elderly (>65)30–50% (nursing home higher)
Risk FactorSUIUUI
Age↑↑↑
Parity/Vaginal Delivery↑↑↑
Menopause↑ (oestrogen loss)↑
Obesity↑↑↑
Chronic Cough/Constipation↑
Neurological Disease↑ (MS, stroke, Parkinson's)
BPH/Prostate Surgery↑ (post-TURP)↑ (BOO)
MedicationsDiuretics, α-blockersAnticholinergics, diuretics, caffeine

4. Assessment & Diagnosis

History

DomainQuestions
TypeLeakage with activity (SUI) vs urgency (UUI) vs constant (overflow)
SeverityPads/day, volume, interference with activities
ImpactQoL scores (ICIQ-UI SF, King's Health Questionnaire)
Voiding Diary3-day diary: frequency, volumes, leakage episodes, fluid intake
Red FlagsHaematuria, recurrent UTI, pain, neurological symptoms, pelvic mass

Examination

ComponentSUI FocusUUI/Overflow Focus
AbdominalMass, scars, distension
Pelvic (Women)Cough stress test (leakage with cough), pelvic organ prolapse (POP-Q), vaginal atrophy
Genital (Men)Penile/scrotal, meatusProstate (DRE: size, consistency, nodules)
NeurologicalPerineal sensation, anal tone, bulbocavernosus reflex, lower limbs
Post-Void Residual (PVR)Usually lowHigh (>100–150 mL) = retention/overflow

Investigations

TestIndication
Urinalysis + CultureExclude UTI, haematuria, glycosuria
UrodynamicsComplex/unclear diagnosis, pre-surgery, neurogenic, failed conservative tx
CystoscopyHaematuria, recurrent UTI, failed surgery, suspicion of malignancy/fistula
ImagingUS KUB (hydronephrosis, PVR), MRI (if fistula/sphincter)
Pad Test1-hour (standardised) or 24-hour (quantify leakage)

Urodynamics Key Parameters

ParameterSUIUUIOverflow
Valsalva Leak Point Pressure (VLPP)Low (<60 cmH2O) = intrinsic sphincter deficiencyNormal/highN/A
Detrusor Pressure at Max Flow (PdetQmax)NormalHigh (overactivity)Low (underactivity)
Maximum Urethral Closure Pressure (MUCP)LowNormalNormal
Bladder ComplianceNormalNormal/lowNormal
Detrusor OveractivityAbsentPresent (phasic/terminal)Absent

5. Management

Conservative (1st Line for All Types)

InterventionDetails
LifestyleWeight loss (BMI <25), smoking cessation, fluid management (1.5–2L/day, reduce caffeine/alcohol), treat constipation
Pelvic Floor Muscle Training (PFMT)SUI: 1st line; 8–12 weeks supervised; ⱗ "Knack" (pre-contraction before stress)
Bladder TrainingUUI: 1st line; scheduled voiding, urge suppression, progressive interval increase
Continence ProductsPads, sheaths (men), catheters (last resort)
Vaginal Oestrogen (Post-menopausal)Topical (creme/pessary/ring) — improves urethral mucosa, PFMT response

Pharmacological

Stress UI

DrugMechanismNotes
DuloxetineSNRI → ↑ urethral sphincter tone (Onuf's nucleus)Only drug approved for SUI; 20mg BD → 40mg BD; nausea common; avoid if hepatic/severe renal

Urge UI / OAB

DrugMechanismDoseNotes
AnticholinergicsM3 blockade → ↓ detrusor contractionsOxybutynin 5mg TDS/IR, 10mg OD/ER; Tolterodine 2mg BD/4mg OD; Solifenacin 5mg OD; Darifenacin 7.5mg OD; Fesoterodine 4–8mg OD1st line; side effects: dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, cognitive (avoid elderly)
β3-AgonistMirabegron → β3 relaxation → ↑ bladder capacity25mg OD → 50mg ODAlternative if anticholinergic intolerant; less cognitive; hypertension monitoring
CombinationAnticholinergic + β3-agonistSolifenacin + mirabegronRefractory OAB

Overflow / Neurogenic

ApproachDetail
Clean Intermittent Catheterisation (CIC)Gold standard for retention/neurogenic
Alpha-blockersTamsulosin (if BOO component)
BethanecholCholinergic agonist (if detrusor underactivity) — limited evidence

Surgical

Stress UI (Women)

ProcedureIndicationNotes
Retropubic Mid-Urethral Sling (TVT)Gold standard SUI>85% cure; mesh complications (erosion, pain, obstruction)
Transobturator Sling (TOT)AlternativeLess bladder/bowel injury; similar efficacy
Single-Incision Mini-SlingLess invasiveLower long-term data
Colposuspension (Burch)Open/laparoscopicHistorical; durable; more invasive
Bulking AgentsIntrinsic sphincter deficiency (low VLPP)Periurethral injection; lower durability; repeat needed
Artificial Urinary Sphincter (AUS)Men (post-prostatectomy SUI); failed slingsGold standard men; 3-component; mechanical failure ~10–15%/yr

Urge UI / OAB (Refractory)

ProcedureIndication
Botulinum Toxin A (Intradetrusor)Refractory OAB; 100U/200U; repeat q6–12mo; risk retention (CIC needed)
Sacral Neuromodulation (SNM)Refractory OAB/UUI; test phase → permanent; reversible
Percutaneous Tibial Nerve Stimulation (PTNS)Refractory OAB; weekly × 12 then maintenance
Augmentation CystoplastyEnd-stage small capacity bladder; enterocystoplasty

Overflow (BOO/Neurogenic)

ProcedureIndication
TURP / HoLEPBPH with overflow
UrethroplastyStricture
CICNeurogenic detrusor underactivity

6. Special Populations

Post-Prostatectomy Incontinence (Men)

TimelineManagement
0–3 monthsPFMT, lifestyle, pads; expect recovery
3–12 monthsPFMT + biofeedback; duloxetine; consider early sling if persistent
>12 monthsArtificial Urinary Sphincter (AUS) = gold standard; Adjuvant sling (male sling) if mild-moderate

Elderly / Nursing Home

ApproachDetail
Prompted VoidingScheduled toileting + positive reinforcement
Habit TrainingIdentify pattern → scheduled voiding
Indwelling CatheterLast resort (comfort care only); suprapubic preferred if long-term

Neurogenic Bladder

ConditionManagement
Suprasacral (UMN)Detrusor overactivity + DSD → anticholinergic + CIC; botulinum toxin
Sacral/Infrasacral (LMN)Detrusor underactivity + areflexia → CIC; bethanechol (limited)
Spinal Cord InjuryAutonomic dysreflexia risk; CIC + anticholinergic + botulinum

7. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- SUI: leakage with cough/sneeze/exercise; no urgency; women > men
- UUI: urgency + frequency + nocturia + leakage; "key-in-lock"
- MUI: both SUI + UUI features
- Overflow: constant dribbling + high PVR (>100–150mL)
- Functional: normal tract, impaired mobility/cognition
- Assessment: 3-day voiding diary, cough stress test, DRE, PVR
- SUI 1st line: PFMT (8–12wk); drug: duloxetine; surgery: TVT (women), AUS (men)
- UUI 1st line: bladder training; drug: anticholinergics (oxybutynin, solifenacin) or mirabegron; refractory: botox, SNM, PTNS
- Overflow: high PVR; CIC gold standard; treat cause (TURP, stricture)
- Post-prostatectomy: PFMT 0–3mo; AUS >12mo if persistent
- Vaginal oestrogen: post-menopausal adjunct
- Pad test: 1-hr or 24-hr quantify leakage

8. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
SUI = urgencySUI = NO urgency; UUI = urgency
PFMT only for womenMen post-prostatectomy: PFMT 1st line
Anticholinergics for SUIAnticholinergics for UUI/OAB; SUI = duloxetine
Mirabegron = anticholinergicβ3-agonist (different mechanism, less cognitive)
TVT = for menTVT = women; AUS = men (post-prostatectomy)
All incontinence = surgeryConservative 1st line for ALL types
Overflow = just dribblingMust confirm high PVR (>100–150mL)
Elderly = just padsPrompted voiding, habit training, treat reversible causes
Post-TURP SUI = immediate AUSWait 12 months; PFMT first; most recover
Urodynamics always neededOnly if complex/unclear, pre-surgery, neurogenic, failed conservative

9. Mnemonics

  • UI Types: Stress, Urge, Mixed, Overflow, Functional = SUMOF
  • SUI: Cough, Sneeze, Exercise = CSE (no urgency)
  • UUI: Urgency, Frequency, Nocturia = UFN
  • Overflow: High PVR, Dribbling = HPD
  • Assessment: Diary (3-day), Cough Stress, PVR, Urodynamics = DCS-PVU
  • SUI Tx: PFMT → Duloxetine → TVT/AUS = PDTA
  • UUI Tx: Bladder training → Anticholinergic/Mirabegron → Botox/SNM/PTNS = BAM-BSP
  • Overflow: PVR >100 → CIC → TURP = PCT
  • Post-Prostatectomy: 0–3mo PFMT, >12mo AUS = 03-12
  • Red Flags: Haematuria, Recurrent UTI, Pain, Neuro, Mass = HRPNM

10. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Urinary Incontinence))
    Types
      SUI[Leak with stress, no urgency]
      UUI[Urgency + leakage]
      MUI[Both SUI + UUI]
      Overflow[High PVR, dribbling]
      Functional[Normal tract, impaired mobility]
      Continuous[Fistula, ectopic ureter]
    Assessment
      Diary[3-day voiding diary]
      Exam[Cough stress, DRE, PVR, neuro]
      Urodynamics[VLPP, Pdet, MUCP, DO]
      Red_Flags[Haematuria, UTI, pain, mass]
    Conservative
      PFMT[SUI 1st line, 8-12wk]
      Bladder_Training[UUI 1st line]
      Vaginal_Oestrogen[Post-menopausal]
      Lifestyle[Weight, fluids, caffeine, constipation]
    Pharmacological
      SUI[Duloxetine (SNRI)]
      UUI[Anticholinergics, Mirabegron (β3)]
    Surgical
      SUI_Women[TVT/TOT (gold), Bulking, Colposuspension]
      SUI_Men[AUS (gold post-prostatectomy), Male sling]
      UUI_Refractory[Botox, SNM, PTNS, Augmentation]
      Overflow[CIC, TURP, Urethroplasty]
    Special
      Post_Prostatectomy[PFMT 0-3mo; AUS >1yr]
      Elderly[Prompted voiding, habit training]
      Neurogenic[UM=antichol+CIC+botox; LM=CIC]

11. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. UI types: SUI (cough/sneeze, no urgency), UUI (urgency+frequency), MUI, Overflow (high PVR), Functional
  2. Assessment: 3-day diary, cough stress test, DRE, PVR, urodynamics if complex
  3. SUI: women > men; leakage with activity; PFMT 1st line; duloxetine drug; TVT/TOT surgery (women), AUS (men)
  4. UUI: urgency + frequency + nocturia; bladder training 1st line; anticholinergics/mirabegron drugs; botox/SNM/PTNS refractory
  5. Overflow: constant dribbling + high PVR (>100–150mL); CIC gold standard; treat cause (TURP, stricture)
  6. Post-prostatectomy: PFMT 0–3mo; AUS >12mo if persistent
  7. Vaginal oestrogen: post-menopausal adjunct
  8. Urodynamics: VLPP low = ISD (SUI); detrusor overactivity = UUI; Pdet low = overflow

12. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

13. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Types (SUI/UUI/MUI/Overflow/Functional), assessment (diary, cough stress, PVR), SUI PFMT/duloxetine/TVT-AUS, UUI bladder training/anticholinergics/mirabegron/botox/SNM, overflow CIC, post-prostatectomy AUS
Should Know 🟡MUI management (treat predominant), vaginal oestrogen, neurogenic bladder protocols, augmentation cystoplasty, male slings, PTNS, prompt voiding elderly
Nice to Know 🟢Novel devices (urethral inserts, magnetic sphincter), stem cell therapy, urethral bulking agent comparisons, cost-effectiveness, QoL instruments, AI-assisted urodynamics

14. MCQs (10)

  1. Stress urinary incontinence is characterised by:

A. Urgency and frequency

B. Leakage with cough, sneeze, exercise; no urgency

C. Constant dribbling

D. Nocturia only

E. High post-void residual

  1. Urge urinary incontinence — key feature:

A. Leakage with lifting only

B. Urgency + frequency + nocturia + leakage

C. High PVR

C. No urgency

E. Only at night

  1. Post-void residual (PVR) elevated in:

A. SUI

B. UUI

C. Overflow incontinence

D. Functional incontinence

E. SUI + UUI

  1. First-line conservative management for SUI:

A. Bladder training

B. Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) 8–12 weeks

C. Anticholinergics

D. Mirabegron

E. Duloxetine

  1. First-line pharmacological treatment for UUI/OAB:

A. Duloxetine

B. Anticholinergics (oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin) or mirabegron

C. Duloxetine + anticholinergic

D. Alpha-blockers

E. Beta-3 agonists only

  1. Drug approved specifically for SUI:

A. Oxybutynin

B. Mirabegron

C. Duloxetine

D. Solifenacin

E. Tamsulosin

  1. Surgical gold standard for SUI in women:

A. Colposuspension

B. Retropubic mid-urethral sling (TVT)

C. Bulking agents

D. Artificial urinary sphincter

E. Single-incision mini-sling

  1. Surgical gold standard for post-prostatectomy SUI in men (>12 months):

A. Male sling

B. Artificial urinary sphincter (AUS)

C. TVT

D. Bulking agents

E. Colposuspension

  1. Refractory OAB — intradetrusor injection:

A. Duloxetine

B. Botulinum toxin A (100U/200U)

C. Mirabegron

D. Tamsulosin

E. Bulking agent

  1. Overflow incontinence — gold standard management:

A. Anticholinergics

B. Clean intermittent catheterisation (CIC)

C. TVT

D. Duloxetine

E. Mirabegron

  1. Post-prostatectomy incontinence — timeline for AUS:

A. Immediately

B. 3 months

C. 6 months

D. >12 months (after maximal conservative recovery)

E. Never

15. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 45-year-old woman, 2 vaginal deliveries, leaks urine when coughing, sneezing, exercising. No urgency. Cough stress test positive. PVR 30mL. Best initial management:

A. TVT sling

B. PFMT supervised × 8–12 weeks

C. Duloxetine 40mg BD

D. Oxybutynin 5mg TDS

E. Refer for urodynamics

  1. 65-year-old woman, urgency, frequency (10/day), nocturia ×3, leakage with urgency. PVR 40mL. Failed bladder training 8 weeks. Best next step:

A. TVT sling

B. Anticholinergic (solifenacin 5mg OD) or mirabegron 25mg OD

C. Duloxetine

D. CIC

E. Botulinum toxin

  1. 70-year-old man, post-radical prostatectomy 14 months, persistent SUI (3 pads/day). Failed PFMT × 6 months. Best surgical option:

A. Repeat prostatectomy

B. Artificial urinary sphincter (AUS)

C. TVT sling

C. Bulking agents

E. CIC

  1. 55-year-old man, BPH, constant dribbling, poor stream, hesitancy. PVR 350mL. US: hydronephrosis. Management:

A. Anticholinergics

B. CIC + TURP/HoLEP (relieve obstruction)

C. Mirabegron

D. Duloxetine

E. TVT

  1. 60-year-old woman, post-menopausal, SUI + vaginal atrophy. Adjunct to PFMT:

A. Oral oestrogen

B. Topical vaginal oestrogen (cream/pessary/ring)

C. Duloxetine

D. Oxybutynin

E. Mirabegron

  1. 75-year-old nursing home resident, dementia, mobility impaired, wet every 2 hours. No UTI. PVR 50mL. Best approach:

A. Anticholinergics

B. Prompted voiding (scheduled toileting + reinforcement)

C. CIC

D. TVT

E. Indwelling catheter

  1. Urodynamics — low Valsalva leak point pressure (VLPP <60 cmH2O) indicates:

A. Detrusor overactivity

B. Intrinsic sphincter deficiency (SUI)

C. Bladder outlet obstruction

D. Detrusor underactivity

E. Normal

  1. Mirabegron — mechanism of action:

A. M3 anticholinergic

B. β3-adrenoceptor agonist (detrusor relaxation)

C. SNRI

D. Alpha-blocker

E. Muscarinic agonist

  1. Neurogenic bladder — suprasacral (UMN) lesion pattern:

A. Detrusor underactivity + areflexia

B. Detrusor overactivity + detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia (DSD)

C. Normal detrusor, low compliance

D. Overflow only

E. Stress incontinence only

  1. Pad test — 1-hour standardised pad test >4g indicates:

A. No incontinence

B. Mild incontinence

C. Moderate-severe incontinence

D. Overflow only

E. UUI only

16. Flashcards

  • Q: SUI key feature?

A: Leakage with cough/sneeze/exercise; NO urgency

  • Q: UUI key feature?

A: Urgency + frequency + nocturia + leakage

  • Q: Overflow UI?

A: Constant dribbling + high PVR (>100–150mL)

  • Q: Functional UI?

A: Normal tract + impaired mobility/cognition

  • Q: SUI 1st line conservative?

A: PFMT 8–12 weeks supervised

  • Q: UUI 1st line conservative?

A: Bladder training

  • Q: SUI drug?

A: Duloxetine (SNRI)

  • Q: UUI/OAB drugs?

A: Anticholinergics (oxybutynin, solifenacin) or mirabegron (β3-agonist)

  • Q: SUI surgery women?

A: TVT/TOT (mid-urethral sling) gold standard

  • Q: SUI surgery men (post-prostatectomy)?

A: AUS (artificial urinary sphincter) gold standard >12mo

  • Q: UUI refractory?

A: Botox (intradetrusor), SNM, PTNS

  • Q: Overflow management?

A: CIC gold standard; treat cause (TURP, stricture)

  • Q: Post-prostatectomy AUS timing?

A: >12 months after maximal recovery

  • Q: Vaginal oestrogen?

A: Post-menopausal SUI/UUI adjunct

  • Q: Urodynamics VLPP low?

A: Intrinsic sphincter deficiency (SUI)

  • Q: Urodynamics detrusor overactivity?

A: UUI/OAB

  • Q: Mirabegron mechanism?

A: β3-agonist → detrusor relaxation

  • Q: Prompted voiding?

A: Scheduled toileting + reinforcement (elderly/dementia)

  • Q: CIC gold standard?

A: Overflow, neurogenic retention

  • Q: Pad test 1hr >4g?

A: Moderate-severe incontinence

17. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — SUI = leakage with stress, no urgency
  2. B — UUI = urgency + frequency + nocturia
  3. C — Overflow = high PVR
  4. B — SUI 1st line = PFMT
  5. B — UUI 1st line = anticholinergics or mirabegron
  6. C — Duloxetine = only drug approved for SUI
  7. B — TVT = gold standard SUI women
  8. B — AUS = gold standard post-prostatectomy men
  9. B — Botox = refractory OAB intradetrusor
  10. B — CIC = gold standard overflow

SBAs

  1. B — SUI + positive cough stress + low PVR = PFMT 1st line
  2. B — UUI + failed bladder training = anticholinergic/mirabegron
  3. B — Post-prostatectomy >12mo + failed PFMT = AUS
  4. B — High PVR + hydronephrosis = obstruction → CIC + TURP
  5. B — Post-menopausal + SUI + atrophy = vaginal oestrogen adjunct
  6. B — Dementia + mobility impaired = prompted voiding
  7. B — VLPP <60 = intrinsic sphincter deficiency
  8. B — Mirabegron = β3-agonist
  9. B — UMN = DO + DSD
  10. C — 1hr pad test >4g = moderate-severe

18. Summary

Urinary Incontinence is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: SUI: leakage with stress (cough/sneeze), no urgency; PFMT 1st line, duloxetine drug, TVT/TOT (women), AUS (men post-prostatectomy). UUI: urgency + frequency + nocturia; bladder training 1st line, anticholinergics/mirabegron, refractory = botox/SNM/PTNS. Overflow: constant dribbling + high PVR >100mL; CIC gold standard; treat cause. Assessment: 3-day diary, cough stress test, PVR, urodynamics if complex. Post-prostatectomy: PFMT 0–3mo, AUS >12mo. Vaginal oestrogen: post-menopausal adjunct. Elderly: prompted voiding, habit training.

Exam focus: Types & features, assessment algorithm, PFMT vs bladder training, duloxetine vs anticholinergics/mirabegron, TVT vs AUS, botox/SNM for refractory UUI, CIC for overflow, post-prostatectomy timeline, urodynamics interpretation.

Clinical relevance: UI massively impacts QoL; stepwise conservative → pharmacological → surgical approach; most patients improve with PFMT/bladder training; surgery reserved for refractory.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.5.6 Neurogenic Bladder
Topic: Neurogenic Bladder — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Urinary Tract Infection (UTI, Pyelonephritis, Prostatitis, Catheter-Associated)]], [[Urinary Incontinence]], [[Urinary Tract Obstruction]], [[Spinal Cord Injury]], [[Neurology]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Neurogenic bladder = bladder dysfunction due to CNS/PNS lesions. Classification by lesion level: Suprasacral (UMN) = detrusor overactivity + DSD; Sacral/Infrasacral (LMN) = areflexia/underactivity. Key: Urodynamics (pressure-flow) essential. Complications: UTI, stones, hydronephrosis, VUR, renal failure. Management: CIC, anticholinergics, botox, augmentation.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify neurogenic bladder by lesion level (UMN vs LMN)
  • Apply urodynamic interpretation (compliance, DSD, detrusor pressure)
  • Manage with clean intermittent catheterisation (CIC), pharmacotherapy, surgical options
  • Prevent upper tract deterioration (hydronephrosis, VUR, renal scarring)
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP spinal cord injury / myelomeningocele vignettes

2. Classification by Lesion Level

Lesion LevelTypeBladder BehaviourSphincterUrodynamics
Suprasacral (UMN)Hyperreflexic (Overactive)Detrusor overactivity (involuntary contractions)DSD (Detrusor-Sphincter Dyssynergia) — external sphincter contracts during detrusor contractionHigh detrusor pressure, low compliance, DSD
Sacral/Infrasacral (LMN)Areflexic (Underactive)Areflexia / Acontractility — no detrusor contractionFlaccid (no voluntary control)Low detrusor pressure, high capacity, no DSD
Conus MedullarisMixedVariable (often areflexic detrusor + DSD possible)MixedMixed
UMN (Suprasacral) = spastic bladder + DSD → high pressure → upper tract risk.
LMN (Sacral) = flaccid bladder → retention → overflow incontinence + UTI.

3. Common Aetiologies

LevelCauses
Suprasacral (UMN)Spinal cord injury (T12/L1), Multiple sclerosis, Transverse myelitis, Spinal tumours, Cerebral palsy, Stroke, Parkinson's
Sacral/Infrasacral (LMN)Cauda equina syndrome, Sacral tumours, Spina bifida/Myelomeningocele, Diabetic neuropathy, Pelvic surgery (rectal/prostate), Herpes zoster (S2–S4)

4. Clinical Presentation

Suprasacral (UMN) — Hyperreflexic

SymptomMechanism
Urgency, frequency, urge incontinenceDetrusor overactivity
Incomplete emptying, retentionDSD → functional obstruction
Recurrent UTIsStasis, high pressure
Autonomic dysreflexia (if lesion >T6)Bladder distension → massive sympathetic surge → hypertension, bradycardia, headache

Sacral/Infrasacral (LMN) — Areflexic

SymptomMechanism
Overflow incontinenceAcontractile detrusor → chronic retention → overflow
Stress incontinenceFlaccid sphincter + abdominal pressure
Recurrent UTIsStasis, incomplete emptying
Saddle anaesthesia, lax anusSacral root involvement

5. Urodynamics (Essential for Diagnosis & Management)

ParameterUMN (Suprasacral)LMN (Sacral)
Detrusor activityOveractive (involuntary contractions)Areflexic / Underactive
ComplianceLow (stiff bladder)High (flaccid)
Detrusor pressureHigh (>40 cmH₂O risk)Low
DSDPresent (EMG activity during contraction)Absent
Sphincter toneHypertonicFlaccid
Bladder capacityReducedIncreased
Maximum flow rateLow (obstructed by DSD)Low (no contractility)
Detrusor leak point pressure (DLPP) >40 cmH₂O = risk of upper tract damage (hydronephrosis, VUR).

6. Diagnostic Workup

TestPurpose
Urodynamics (video-urodynamics preferred)Gold standard — confirms type, measures pressures, DSD, compliance
Renal USScreen for hydronephrosis, cortical thinning, stones
DMSA scanRenal scarring (reflux nephropathy)
VCUGVesicoureteric reflux (VUR) grading
Post-void residual (PVR)Quantifies retention
CystoscopyAnatomy, stones, bladder trabeculation
MRI SpineIdentifies lesion level if unknown

7. Management Principles

1. Clean Intermittent Catheterisation (CIC) — Cornerstone

AspectDetail
IndicationPVR >100–150mL, inadequate emptying, high detrusor pressure
Frequencyq4–6h (target PVR <100mL)
TechniqueSterile vs clean (clean acceptable for chronic); hydrophilic catheters
ComplicationsUTI (most common), urethral stricture, false passages, haematuria
TeachingPatient/caregiver training essential; lifelong in most
CIC + Anticholinergic = standard for UMN to protect upper tracts.

2. Pharmacotherapy

Drug ClassIndicationDetails
Anticholinergics (Oxybutynin, Tolterodine, Solifenacin, Darifenacin, Fesoterodine)UMN — detrusor overactivity, low complianceOral/Transdermal; side effects: dry mouth, constipation, cognitive (elderly), retention
β₃-Agonist (Mirabegron)UMN (add-on or alternative)Relax detrusor via β₃; fewer anticholinergic SEs
α-Blockers (Tamsulosin, Alfuzosin)DSD (UMN) — ↓ sphincter resistanceHelps emptying with CIC
Botulinum Toxin A (OnabotulinumtoxinA)Refractory UMN100–200U intradetrusor q6–12mo; may need CIC post-injection
Baclofen (intrathecal)Severe spasticity/DSDIntrathecal pump for spasticity + bladder

3. Surgical Options (Refractory Cases)

ProcedureIndicationDetails
Augmentation Cystoplasty (ileum/colon/ileocaecal)Low compliance, high pressure refractory to meds↑ capacity, ↓ pressure; CIC mandatory lifelong; risks: stones, mucus, metabolic acidosis, malignancy (long-term)
Urinary Diversion (ileal conduit, continent pouch)Failed augmentation, patient preferenceStoma or catheterisable channel (Mitrofanoff)
Sacral NeuromodulationNon-neurogenic + selected neurogenicNot first-line for neurogenic; limited evidence
Sphincterotomy (endoscopic)Male UMN with DSD refractory↓ outlet resistance; incontinence → condom drainage
Artificial Urinary Sphincter (AUS)Stress incontinence (LMN)Gold standard for sphincteric incontinence; revision rate high

8. Upper Tract Surveillance (Critical)

ParameterFrequencyAction Threshold
Renal USq6–12moNew hydronephrosis → investigate
Serum Creatinine / eGFRq6–12moRising → urgent urodynamics
Urodynamicsq1–2yr (or change)DLPP >40 cmH₂O → intervene
DMSAIf VUTI / hydronephrosisScarring → aggressive management
Goal: Keep detrusor pressure <40 cmH₂O, compliance >20 mL/cmH₂O, PVR <100mL.

9. Complications

ComplicationPrevention
UTICIC technique, cranberry/methenamine, prophylactic abx (selected)
Bladder StonesHydration, CIC, augmentation surveillance
Hydronephrosis / VURLow detrusor pressure (anticholinergics, botox, augmentation)
Renal FailureEarly detection + aggressive pressure management
Bladder Cancer (↑ risk in augmentation)Annual cystoscopy + cytology 10yr post-augmentation

10. Special Populations

Spina Bifida / Myelomeningocele

  • LMN pattern (sacral agenesis) — areflexic bladder + flaccid sphincter
  • Early CIC (from infancy) + anticholinergics if high pressure
  • Lifelong surveillance — renal US q6mo, urodynamics q1–2yr

Spinal Cord Injury (SCI)

  • Acute phase (spinal shock) — areflexic bladder → indwelling catheter
  • Recovery phase (weeks-months) — UMN pattern emerges → transition to CIC
  • Autonomic dysreflexia risk if lesion >T6 — bladder/bowel distension triggers

Multiple Sclerosis

  • Variable (UMN common) — fluctuating course
  • Urodynamics at baseline + q1–2yr or symptom change

11. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Neurogenic bladder = bladder dysfunction from CNS/PNS lesion.
- UMN (Suprasacral) = detrusor overactivity + DSD → high pressure → upper tract risk.
- LMN (Sacral) = areflexic/underactive + flaccid sphincter → overflow incontinence.
- Urodynamics = gold standard (compliance, DSD, detrusor pressure, DLPP).
- DLPP >40 cmH₂O = danger threshold for upper tracts.
- CIC = cornerstone of management (q4–6h, target PVR <100mL).
- UMN: Anticholinergics + CIC ± Botox ± α-blocker for DSD.
- LMN: CIC ± α-blocker; AUS for stress incontinence.
- Augmentation cystoplasty = refractory low compliance; CIC lifelong; metabolic acidosis, stones, malignancy risk.
- Surveillance: US q6–12mo, urodynamics q1–2yr, DMSA if VUTI.
- Autonomic dysreflexia (SCI >T6): bladder distension → hypertensive crisis.

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All neurogenic bladder = UMNSacral/cauda equina = LMN (areflexic, overflow)
DSD = sphincter relaxes during voidingDSD = sphincter CONTRACTS during detrusor contraction (dyssynergia)
CIC not needed if continentPVR >150mL or high detrusor pressure = CIC needed even if continent
Anticholinergics for allLMN (areflexic) = anticholinergics WORSEN retention
Augmentation = no more CICCIC MANDATORY lifelong after augmentation (mucus, incomplete emptying)
β₃-agonist = same as anticholinergicMirabegron = different mechanism (β₃), fewer cognitive/dry mouth SEs
α-blocker = for DSD in UMNYes — tamsulosin ↓ sphincter tone → helps emptying with CIC
Autonomic dysreflexia = any SCIOnly lesions >T6 (T6 and above)
Urodynamics = one-time testRepeat q1–2yr or with symptom change

13. Mnemonics

  • UMN vs LMN: Upper = Unstable (overactive) + DSD; Lower = Limp (areflexic) + Flaccid
  • DSD: Detrusor Sphincter Dyssynergia = Don't Synergise During voiding
  • Neurogenic bladder Rx: CIC + Anticholinergic (UMN) = CA
  • DLPP: DLPP > 40 = Danger to upper tracts
  • Autonomic dysreflexia: Above T6 = Autonomic Dysreflexia risk

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Neurogenic Bladder))
    UMN[Suprasacral (UMN)]
      Lesion[SCI, MS, Stroke, Tumour]
      Bladder[Overactive, Low compliance]
      Sphincter[DSD - contracts with detrusor]
      Pressure[High, DLPP >40 risk]
      Management[CIC + Anticholinergics ± Botox ± α-blocker]
    LMN[Sacral/Infrasacral (LMN)]
      Lesion[Cauda equina, Spina bifida, Diabetic neuropathy]
      Bladder[Areflexic, High compliance]
      Sphincter[Flaccid]
      Management[CIC ± α-blocker; AUS for stress incontinence]
    Diagnosis
      Urodynamics[Gold standard: Compliance, DSD, DLPP, Capacity]
      Imaging[US, VCUG, DMSA]
      PVR[Post-void residual]
    Surveillance
      US[q6-12mo]
      Urodynamics[q1-2yr]
      Creatinine[q6-12mo]
    Complications
      UTI[Recurrent]
      Hydronephrosis[High pressure]
      VUR[Reflux]
      Stones[Stasis]
      Renal_Failure[End-stage]

15. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. UMN vs LMN classification (lesion level, bladder behaviour, sphincter)
  2. DSD definition and significance
  3. DLPP >40 cmH₂O = upper tract risk
  4. CIC indications and frequency
  5. UMN management: CIC + anticholinergics ± Botox
  6. LMN management: CIC ± AUS
  7. Augmentation cystoplasty indications + lifelong CIC
  8. Autonomic dysreflexia (lesion >T6, bladder trigger)
  9. Surveillance schedule (US, urodynamics, creatinine)

16. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

17. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴UMN/LMN classification, DSD, DLPP >40, CIC cornerstone, anticholinergics for UMN, augmentation indications, surveillance
Should Know 🟡Botox dosing, α-blockers for DSD, AUS for LMN stress incontinence, sacral neuromodulation limits, paediatric spina bifida management
Nice to Know 🟢Intrathecal baclofen, novel neuromodulation, tissue-engineered bladders, quality of life metrics, health economics

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Suprasacral (UMN) neurogenic bladder — key urodynamic feature:

A. Areflexic detrusor

B. Detrusor overactivity + DSD

C. High compliance

D. Flaccid sphincter

E. Low detrusor pressure

  1. Detrusor-Sphincter Dyssynergia (DSD) definition:

A. Sphincter relaxes during detrusor contraction

B. Sphincter contracts during detrusor contraction

C. Detrusor contracts without sphincter activity

D. Sphincter contracts without detrusor activity

E. Both relax simultaneously

  1. Danger threshold for upper tract damage (DLPP):

A. >20 cmH₂O

B. >30 cmH₂O

C. >40 cmH₂O

D. >50 cmH₂O

E. >60 cmH₂O

  1. Cornerstone management for neurogenic bladder:

A. Indwelling catheter

B. Clean Intermittent Catheterisation (CIC)

C. Suprapubic catheter

D. Condom drainage

E. Diapers

  1. CIC target post-void residual:

A. <50 mL

B. <100 mL

C. <200 mL

D. <300 mL

E. <500 mL

  1. Sacral (LMN) neurogenic bladder — typical presentation:

A. Urgency, frequency, DSD

B. Overflow incontinence, flaccid sphincter, high PVR

C. Autonomic dysreflexia

D. High detrusor pressure

E. Low bladder capacity

  1. Anticholinergics in LMN (areflexic) bladder:

A. First-line

B. Contraindicated (worsen retention)

C. Use β₃-agonist instead

D. Add α-blocker

E. Only with CIC

  1. Augmentation cystoplasty — mandatory lifelong:

A. Anticholinergics

B. CIC

C. Prophylactic antibiotics

D. α-blockers

E. Botox injections

  1. Autonomic dysreflexia risk — spinal lesion level:

A. >T10

B. >T6

C. >L1

D. >S2

E. Any level

  1. Surveillance renal US frequency in neurogenic bladder:

A. Monthly

B. q6–12 months

C. Yearly

D. q2 years

E. Only if symptomatic

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 25-year-old man, T4 complete SCI, 6 months post-injury. Urodynamics: detrusor overactivity, DSD, DLPP 55 cmH₂O. Management:

A. Indwelling catheter

B. CIC + oxybutynin (anticholinergic) ± tamsulosin

C. Suprapubic catheter only

D. Augmentation cystoplasty now

E. Botulinum toxin only

  1. 40-year-old woman, cauda equina syndrome (L1 fracture). Overflow incontinence, PVR 400mL, flaccid sphincter. Best initial management:

A. Oxybutynin

B. CIC q4–6h

C. Tamsulosin only

D. Artificial urinary sphincter

E. Augmentation cystoplasty

  1. Neurogenic bladder patient on CIC + anticholinergic. Repeat urodynamics: compliance 15 mL/cmH₂O, DLPP 45 cmH₂O, recurrent febrile UTIs. Next step:

A. Increase anticholinergic dose

B. Add intradetrusor botulinum toxin A (100–200U)

C. Switch to mirabegron

D. α-blocker only

E. Suprapubic catheter

  1. Male with UMN neurogenic bladder, failed CIC + anticholinergics + botox. Severe DSD. Surgical option:

A. Augmentation cystoplasty

B. Endoscopic sphincterotomy + condom drainage

C. Artificial urinary sphincter

D. Sacral neuromodulation

E. Ileal conduit only

  1. Spina bifida child, neurogenic bladder. Surveillance to prevent renal scarring:

A. US only

B. US q6mo + urodynamics q1–2yr + DMSA if VUTI/hydronephrosis

C. Annual creatinine only

D. VCUG yearly

E. No surveillance if continent

  1. T8 SCI patient develops sudden severe headache, hypertension (220/130), bradycardia, sweating above lesion. Trigger: bladder distension. Immediate action:

A. IV labetalol

B. Sit up, loosen clothes, immediate bladder drainage (catheterise)

C. IV nitroglycerin

D. Nifedipine SL

E. IV hydralazine

  1. Neurogenic bladder with augmentation cystoplasty (ileal). Long-term complication:

A. Hypertension

B. Metabolic acidosis, bladder stones, mucus, malignancy risk

C. Hyperkalaemia

D. Hypocalcaemia

E. Liver failure

  1. Stress incontinence in LMN (sacral) neurogenic bladder — gold standard surgical:

A. Sling

B. Artificial Urinary Sphincter (AUS)

C. Bulking agents

D. Tape

E. Colposuspension

  1. Mirabegron vs Anticholinergics — advantage:

A. More potent

B. Fewer cognitive/dry mouth/constipation side effects

C. Cheaper

D. Once weekly dosing

E. Works in LMN

  1. Refractory UMN neurogenic bladder — bladder pressure persistently >40 cmH₂O despite CIC + max anticholinergic + botox. Best definitive:

A. Long-term indwelling catheter

B. Augmentation cystoplasty

C. Sacral neuromodulation

D. α-blocker addition

E. Condom drainage

20. Flashcards

  • Q: UMN bladder = ?

A: Suprasacral, overactive, DSD, high pressure

  • Q: LMN bladder = ?

A: Sacral/infrasacral, areflexic, flaccid sphincter, overflow

  • Q: DSD = ?

A: Sphincter contracts DURING detrusor contraction

  • Q: DLPP danger threshold?

A: >40 cmH₂O

  • Q: CIC target PVR?

A: <100 mL

  • Q: UMN Rx?

A: CIC + anticholinergics ± botox ± α-blocker

  • Q: LMN Rx?

A: CIC ± AUS for stress incontinence

  • Q: Augmentation mandatory?

A: Lifelong CIC

  • Q: Autonomic dysreflexia lesion?

A: >T6

  • Q: Bladder trigger for AD?

A: Distension

  • Q: Botox dose?

A: 100–200U intradetrusor q6–12mo

  • Q: Surveillance US?

A: q6–12mo

  • Q: α-blocker in UMN?

A: For DSD (tamsulosin)

  • Q: mirabegron SEs?

A: Fewer cognitive/dry mouth than anticholinergics

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — UMN (suprasacral) = detrusor overactivity + DSD (dyssynergia) → high detrusor pressure.
  2. B — DSD = external urethral sphincter contracts IN SYNC with detrusor contraction (fails to relax).
  3. C — DLPP >40 cmH₂O = risk of VUR, hydronephrosis, renal deterioration.
  4. B — CIC is the gold standard for emptying; indwelling catheters cause more complications.
  5. B — Target PVR <100mL to minimise stasis, infection, stone risk.
  6. B — LMN (sacral) = areflexic detrusor → overflow incontinence, high PVR, flaccid sphincter.
  7. B — Anticholinergics further suppress detrusor → worsen retention in already areflexic bladder.
  8. B — Post-augmentation: mucus production, incomplete emptying → CIC mandatory lifelong.
  9. B — Autonomic dysreflexia: massive sympathetic surge from noxious stimulus below lesion >T6.
  10. B — Renal US q6–12mo standard surveillance for upper tract changes.

SBAs

  1. B — UMN with high DLPP: CIC + anticholinergic (oxybutynin) first-line; add α-blocker (tamsulosin) for DSD.
  2. B — LMN (cauda equina): areflexic bladder → CIC essential; anticholinergics contraindicated.
  3. B — Refractory low compliance + high DLPP + recurrent UTIs → intradetrusor botox 100–200U q6–12mo.
  4. B — Male UMN + severe DSD refractory → endoscopic sphincterotomy (reduces outlet resistance) + condom drainage.
  5. B — Spina bifida surveillance: US q6mo + urodynamics q1–2yr + DMSA if VUTI/hydronephrosis.
  6. B — Autonomic dysreflexia: immediate bladder drainage (catheterise) is FIRST and most effective step.
  7. B — Augmentation (ileum/colon): metabolic acidosis (HCO₃⁻ loss), mucus, stones, malignancy (long-term).
  8. B — AUS = gold standard for sphincteric stress incontinence (LMN, post-prostatectomy).
  9. B — Mirabegron (β₃-agonist): relaxes detrusor via cAMP; no antimuscarinic SEs (dry mouth, cognitive).
  10. B — Refractory high pressure despite maximal medical therapy → augmentation cystoplasty definitive.

22. Summary

Neurogenic Bladder is a Must Know 🔴 urology/neurology topic.

Classification: UMN (Suprasacral) = detrusor overactivity + DSD (sphincter contracts during voiding) → high pressure; LMN (Sacral/Infrasacral) = areflexic/underactive + flaccid sphincter → overflow.

Urodynamics: Gold standard. DLPP >40 cmH₂O = upper tract danger.

Management: CIC q4–6h (PVR <100mL) = cornerstone. UMN: CIC + anticholinergics/β₃-agonist ± Botox ± α-blocker (tamsulosin for DSD). LMN: CIC ± AUS for stress incontinence.

Refractory: Augmentation cystoplasty (ileum/colon) → ↑ capacity, ↓ pressure; lifelong CIC mandatory.

Surveillance: US q6–12mo, urodynamics q1–2yr, creatinine q6–12mo, DMSA if VUTI.

Autonomic dysreflexia: Lesion >T6, triggered by bladder/bowel distension → hypertensive crisis; immediate catheterisation first step.

Exam focus: UMN vs LMN, DSD, DLPP, CIC, anticholinergics UMN only, augmentation + CIC, autonomic dysreflexia >T6.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 18.5.7 Urological Malignancies Rcc Bladder Urothelial
Topic: Urological Malignancies (RCC, Bladder Cancer, Urothelial Cancer) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Male Reproductive Tract Disorders (BPH, Prostate Cancer, Testicular Cancer)]], [[Urinary Tract Obstruction]], [[Urinary Tract Infection (UTI, Pyelonephritis, Prostatitis, Catheter-Associated)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Urological malignancies: Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC) = commonest renal malignancy (3% adult cancers); Bladder Cancer = commonest urothelial malignancy; Upper Tract Urothelial Carcinoma (UTUC) = rare. Key: RCC = VHL mutation, clear cell histology; Bladder Ca = smoking, occupational; UTUC = Balkan nephropathy link. Staging = TNM; Treatment: surgery, systemic therapy (IO, TKI), intravesical (BCG).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Diagnose, stage, and manage Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC)
  • Diagnose, stage, and manage Bladder Cancer (NMIBC vs MIBC)
  • Diagnose, stage, and manage Upper Tract Urothelial Carcinoma (UTUC)
  • Apply staging systems (TNM, AJCC 8th) and risk stratification
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP clinical scenarios

2. Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC)

Epidemiology & Risk Factors

FactorAssociation
Incidence~3% of adult cancers; 60–70yo; M:F 1.5:1
Smoking2x risk (dose-dependent)
Obesity↑ risk (adipokines, insulin resistance)
HypertensionIndependent risk factor
Acquired Cystic Kidney DiseaseDialysis patients (30–50x risk)
GeneticVHL syndrome (3p25 deletion/mutation) → clear cell RCC; HLRCC (fumarate hydratase) → papillary type 2; BHD (FLCN) → chromophobe/oncocytoma
OccupationalTrichloroethylene, cadmium, asbestos

Histological Subtypes

Subtype%GeneticsPrognosis
Clear Cell70–80%VHL loss (3p), PBRM1, SETD2, BAP1Intermediate
Papillary Type 110–15%MET activationBetter
Papillary Type 25–10%Fumarate hydratase (HLRCC), NRF2Worse
Chromophobe5%TP53, PTEN, BHD (FLCN)Better
Collecting Duct<1%SMARCB1, SETD2Aggressive
Unclassified3–5%VariableVariable

Clinical Presentation

FeatureDetail
Classic TriadFlank pain, haematuria, palpable mass (now <10%)
Incidental>50% (CT/US for other reasons)
ParaneoplasticErythrocytosis (EPO), hypercalcaemia (PTHrP), hypertension (renin), amyloidosis, Stauffer syndrome (hepatic dysfunction)
MetastaticLung (50%), bone, liver, brain, adrenal

Staging (TNM AJCC 8th)

T StageDefinition
T1a≤4 cm, renal only
T1b4–7 cm, renal only
T2a7–10 cm, renal only
T2b>10 cm, renal only
T3aPerinephric fat / renal vein / IVC (infradiaphragmatic)
T3bIVC supradiaphragmatic / IVC wall
T3cIVC wall invasion
T4Beyond Gerota's fascia (adrenal, adjacent organs)
N1Regional nodes
M1Distant mets

Management by Stage

StageTreatment
T1a (≤4 cm)Active surveillance (elderly/comorbid), Ablation (RFA/cryo), Partial nephrectomy (gold standard if feasible)
T1b (4–7 cm)Partial nephrectomy (preferred), Radical nephrectomy
T2 (>7 cm)Radical nephrectomy
T3Radical nephrectomy + IVC thrombectomy if needed
T4Radical nephrectomy + multivisceral resection (if resectable)
Metastatic (M1)Cytoreductive nephrectomy (selected) + Systemic therapy
[!key]
Nephron-sparing (partial) = preferred for T1 if technically feasible — equivalent cancer control, better renal function.

Systemic Therapy (Metastatic RCC)

LineRegimen
1st Line (Clear Cell)IO + TKI: Pembrolizumab + axitinib, Nivolumab + cabozantinib, Avelumab + axitinib
IO + IO: Nivolumab + ipilimumab (intermediate/poor risk IMDC)
2nd LineTKI (cabozantinib, axitinib, lenvatinib+everolimus) if IO-naïve; IO if TKI-naïve
Non-Clear CellTKI (sunitinib, cabozantinib); clinical trials

IMDC Prognostic Model (Metastatic RCC)

FactorPoints
Hb < LLN1
Corrected Ca > ULN1
KPS <80%1
Time from diagnosis to treatment <1yr1
Neutrophils > ULN1
Platelets > ULN1
Risk GroupsFavourable (0), Intermediate (1–2), Poor (≥3)

3. Bladder Cancer

Epidemiology & Risk Factors

FactorDetail
Incidence4th commonest cancer in men; 70–80yo; M:F 3:1
Smoking#1 risk (50% cases); 2–4x risk
OccupationalAromatic amines (benzidine, β-naphthylamine) — dye, rubber, leather, textile, paint
ChemotherapyCyclophosphamide (acrolein), ifosfamide
RadiationPelvic RT
Chronic IrritationStones, indwelling catheter, schistosomiasis (S. haematobium → squamous cell)
GeneticLynch syndrome, RB1 mutation

Classification by Invasion

CategoryDefinition%
NMIBC (Non-Muscle Invasive)Ta, Tis (CIS), T175–80%
MIBC (Muscle Invasive)T2–T420–25%

TNM Staging (Bladder)

StageDefinition
TaNon-invasive papillary
Tis (CIS)Carcinoma in situ (flat, high-grade)
T1Lamina propria invasion (subepithelial connective tissue)
T2Muscularis propria invasion (T2a superficial, T2b deep)
T3Perivesical fat (T3a micro, T3b macro)
T4Adjacent organs (prostate, uterus, vagina, pelvic/abdominal wall)

NMIBC Risk Stratification (EAU/AUA)

Risk GroupCriteriaRecurrence/Progression Risk
LowSingle, primary, Ta, LG, <3cm, no CISRecur 15%, Prog <1%
IntermediateMultiple/recurrent Ta LG, or Ta HG <3cmRecur 30–50%, Prog 5–10%
HighAny T1, any HG, any CIS, multiple/large Ta HGRecur 50–70%, Prog 15–30%

Management

NMIBC

StepTreatment
TURBTComplete resection (including muscle for T1); Re-TURBT mandatory for T1, incomplete, no muscle in specimen
Low RiskSingle post-op instillation (mitomycin C/epirubicin/gemcitabine within 24h)
IntermediateInduction BCG (6 weekly) ± Maintenance BCG (3 weekly at 3,6,12,18,24,30,36mo)
High RiskBCG induction + maintenance (1–3yr); Radical cystectomy if BCG-unresponsive
BCG-UnresponsiveRadical cystectomy (gold standard); Intravesical alternatives (gemcitabine/docetaxel, pembrolizumab, nadofaragene firadenovec)

MIBC

StepTreatment
Localised (T2–T4a, N0)Radical cystectomy + pelvic lymphadenectomy + urinary diversion (ileal conduit, neobladder, continent diversion)
NeoadjuvantCisplatin-based chemo (GC/MVAC) → cystectomy (survival benefit)
AdjuvantIf not given neoadjuvant + high risk (pT3/T4, N+)
Trimodality (Bladder Preservation)Maximal TURBT + concurrent chemoradiation (selected patients)
MetastaticCisplatin-based chemo (GC) → IO maintenance (avelumab); IO if cisplatin-ineligible

4. Upper Tract Urothelial Carcinoma (UTUC)

FeatureDetail
Incidence5–10% of urothelial cancers; 70–80yo
Risk FactorsSmoking, aromatic amines, Balkan nephropathy (aristolochic acid), Lynch syndrome, analgesic nephropathy
PresentationHaematuria (90%), flank pain, UTI, incidental
DiagnosisCT urogram (gold standard), ureteroscopy + biopsy, urine cytology (low sensitivity)
StagingSame TNM as bladder (Ta–T4, N, M)
Risk StratLow: Ta LG, solitary, <2cm; High: T1, HG, CIS, multifocal, >2cm, hydronephrosis
TreatmentRadical nephroureterectomy (RNU) + bladder cuff (gold standard high-risk); Kidney-sparing (ureteroscopic ablation/laser, segmental resection) for low-risk
AdjuvantCisplatin-based chemo if pT3/T4 or N+ (controversial)
SurveillanceHigh risk of bladder recurrence — cystoscopy q3mo × 2yr

5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- RCC = commonest renal cancer; Clear cell 70–80%; VHL mutation
- RCC classic triad (flank pain, haematuria, mass) <10%; incidental >50%
- RCC paraneoplastic: erythrocytosis (EPO), hypercalcaemia (PTHrP)
- RCC staging: T1a ≤4cm → partial nephrectomy/ablation/surveillance; T1b 4–7 → partial; T2+ → radical
- Metastatic RCC: IO+TKI 1st line (pembro+axiti, nivo+cabo); IMDC risk stratification
- Bladder Ca: smoking #1; occupational aromatic amines; M>F 3:1
- NMIBC (75%): Ta, Tis, T1; MIBC (25%): T2+
- NMIBC risk: Low (single Ta LG) → single post-op chemo; High (T1/HG/CIS) → BCG induction+maintenance
- BCG-unresponsive → radical cystectomy
